# Thread: Sleep: the final frontier...(I need to talk)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### janagram — 2012-05-14T09:00:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23858>

Maybe Kruse could have saved Michael Jackson....it's sad that he was so desperate for sleep. I kinda know the feeling. Lately I've been waking up at 3:15 a.m. and staying awake till dawn, then drifting off for another hour or so....so the last couple of days I have been doing open studios (art show) with friends, and while I didn't eat a single incredible homemade choc. chip cookie, I did succumb to a few corn chips with salsa. I didn't drink the lemonade, but had some wine. I'm trying to say, in a tight spot I may not be perfect but I'm nowhere near SAD. (Eating chips is actually a little too close for comfort)....

I just needed to talk.....to sort out my feelings...guess its time to really tighten up and get to bed earlier, etc.

Thanks for listening.

### freesia — 2012-05-14T10:19:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23860>

Night waking like that makes things very hard.  You have my sympathy, and understanding.

I've been waking up at approx 3am too - every night for the past week. Same as you I get back to sleep much, much later. It's tough getting up for that 6.30am alarm clock.

I think, in my case at least, toxin/hormone dump is stressing adrenals more. i don't notice any negative through the day (thank you LR!) but nights are a write-off currently!

And yeah it can make you more vulnerable to reaching for pick-me-ups during the day. I've had 2 cups of coffee with my BAB 3 days out of the last 4.

Hang in there. Just think how great the next full night's sleep is going to feel. Just like when a baby finally decides to sleep right through the night!

### Glamorama — 2012-05-14T12:27:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23863>

Hey, sorry you're going through this. I've had the same, and every time it was supplements that caused it. MSM caused me 3 am wakings and wiredness once it was built up to a certain level in my blood. Any liver detox supplement, herbal and all, will cause me insomnia. CO, fish oil or Vit D in the PM will cause extreme insomnia. Tiger balm on sore muscle Before bed cause insomnia. As you can see, I don't cope well with stuff affecting my liver. Have you considered your supplements?

### vlynnb — 2012-05-14T17:22:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23902>

So, Glam, do you only take those liver detox supplements in the morning or is there a cut off time in the afternoon that your body is OK with in order for you to get your sleep? I never thought of any of those making a difference other than having read the Vit D might.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-14T18:32:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23917>

How old are you? My first thought is ... MENOPAUSE.

Or maybe perimenopause.

Anytime a woman starts talking about sleep issues, the very first thing their doc should consider is the above. Sleep disturbances and depression are hallmarks of menopause.

Ask me how I know. Go ahead. Ask me.

I have recommended these books before and I'll keep doing it until every woman I know has read them. TS Wiley's **Sex, lies & menopause and any of the late Dr. John R. Lee's books which includes What your doctor may not tell you about menopause**.

Going to bed earlier won't help. Getting your hormones back to where they were when you were in your 20's, will. Again. Ask me how I know.

Menopause is NOT a dirty word. It is nothing to shy away from. It doesn't mean you are shriveled and dried up and worthless. It is a natural course your body takes. We cannot stay young forever but we CAN stay youthful which is more about attitude than about birthdays. I mean, picture this. I'm zooming down the road in my minivan rockin' out to Lady Gaga. Now COME ON! I intend to rock it right straight to the grave. And I do hope it's a long ways off but I refuse to get OLD.

My unasked for advice? Get those hormone levels checked.

### DarleenMB — 2012-05-14T20:59:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-23959>

LOL. I am on the Wiley Protocol. Which means I use estradiol and progesterone cream applied in the same rhythm that my body USED to make them. The dose varies depending on which day of my "cycle" I'm on. I say it that way 'cause I lost my parts about 15 years ago. so I'm on a lunar cycle.

and that's it for the hormones. It's taken nearly 10 months but my libido appears to have woken up and slipped her leash. Huh. Interesting, eh? Last time it was checked my testosterone was zero so I figured that's was it for Lady L. Then I found myself checking out some long, tall gorgeous hunk in the checkout line. Um. hello? Where did THAT come from.

I wander.

I cannot recommend the Wiley protocol highly enough. I feel fabulous and my poor old brain is sorta working again. No more brain fog, really GREAT sleep (thanks to the addition of my blu-blockers at night). I may not be 20 anymore but I sure feel good for the shape I'm in.

### Birdy — 2012-08-25T19:06:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47044>

I have been doing very well with using a sleep mask for the first part of the night. When I put it on, all distractions seem to disappear. I will be sewing more sleep masks. Recently I went on a trip, and forgot my mask, and had a tough couple of nights depending on where I stayed. A couple of nights I was treated to a nice dark air-conditioned room and had wonderful sleep. But at other times, I was stuck with the hotel lights, the flashing green smoke detector, the red letters on the alarm clock, and some really annoying blue led light coming out of the bathroom. These lights are impossible to turn off, and I found myself like a kid, making a tent around my sleeping area to block it all out, 'course then it was hot.

I am becoming more and more picky about the bedroom. I have cleaned much of the clutter out of it so if I have to stumble out of bed at night I don't trip over anything. I make sure not to store anything dusty or in orange crates (they are coated with pesticides). I make sure I have enough water by the late afternoon. I have a stash of books and magazines, which along with music, I enjoy before bed. I even have special knitting projects. I have to pick out all the colors during the daytime, and this is also the time to figure out patterns, do the gage, and start the tricky parts. Eventually I hope to create a small area of the bedroom devoted to spinning, since it is such a wonderful craft that can be done pretty well with no light. But right now, I need to be considerate of the allergy issue. The other parts of my home are still occupied by modern peoples, and right now if I want to spin, I have to venture into the land of the fluorescents.

### Pamela — 2012-08-25T19:17:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47048>

> **Birdy;47468 wrote:** I will be sewing more sleep masks.

Whenever I've tried masks they are tight or feel bad on my head. Will you be offering them for sale?

### Birdy — 2012-08-25T19:47:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sleep-the-final-frontier-i-need-to-talk.1449/post-47059>

Oh, I don't think I would be that good. My current sleep mask is stretched out and so it is comfy. It doesn't block out all of the light, though. I think for me, putting the mask on is just like calming things down. When I wear it, I just want to keep my eyes closed even though I am wide awake. It also helps keep my eyes from drying out. After I have had a long day outside, I like to put drops in my eyes and then put on the mask.

For the ones that I am planning to make, they will be made out of cotton, and I might use just a bit of batting in it to keep it soft and flexible, but unfortunately the batting doesn't really block light as well. I don't really have that much light, just street lights and a hallway light when it is too hot to close the door. What I really need is something to encourage me to keep my eyes closed, and my eyelids will block the rest of the light. My plan is to make the mask part the same as the airline masks, and then crochet an over-the-head strap system similar to a headlamp. That way it will stay in place better without the need for tightness.

Try overstretching the elastic on your masks and see if that doesn't help. My fave mask has no stretch left at all. I have tied the excess elastic into a knot to get the perfect size. I put one band underneath my hair, and the other band around the upper part of my head.

# Thread: Ear Infections ugghh!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2012-05-14T16:37:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/post-23887>

Ok.. so ds is on the 2nd' round of abx - for a wicked ear infection - the only reason we knew about it was b/c he told us 3 friday's ago - about ear pain. went to our ped - and she told us it was the worst ear infection she's ever seen - head of the practice agreed...so we started w/amoxcillin - and a follow up 10days later...

so 10days later we go back and see the ped - he's still got it...now he's on another abx - cefedir - we go back in another 10days (actually 6 days b/c he's been on the new abx since Thursday)

Ped now thinks ds late talking, problems w/talking etc is all related to his hearing. she thinks he may have been having ear infections w/o showing symptoms since he was little. he always loves to pull/play w/his ears - so we can't rely on his playing w/his ears as an indication of an ear infection. he also doesn't get fevers when he has ear infections!

We see the ENT in 6wks (earliest apt) to check his hearing and see what the proper course of treatment is!

still waiting on our appt w/ Child Find for his speech eval...

we're pushing bone broths, soups, probiotics extra hard considering he's on serious abx .... the life of chasing after optimal health... not only for yourself but for your kids as well....

### Glamorama — 2012-05-15T02:36:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/post-24067>

Have you considered chiropractic treatment, and cutting out dairy for him? Both help immensely for kids with reoccurring ear-infections. The chiropractor adjusts the upper neck, and that normally releases the tension that might in turn affect the build-up of germs in his ear canal.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-15T04:38:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/post-24108>

The kids see a Chiro monthly. Ds has only been "out" 2xs in his 2yrs of life . As for dairy we limit it in general at the house & I've considered cutting it back at home but state law says he hast to have milk at Daycare unless he has an allergy. We' ll go to the ent then look at an allergist ugh. He only drink goats milk at dcp. He still nurses at home 2x/day. Ped thinks his etruscan (spelling??) tubes might be a bit faulty. Also thinks this might be why he's a late talker.

### MJ* — 2012-05-15T15:27:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/post-24176>

> **Shijin13;23969 wrote:** Ok.. so ds is on the 2nd' round of abx - for a wicked ear infection - the only reason we knew about it was b/c he told us 3 friday's ago - about ear pain. went to our ped - and she told us it was the worst ear infection she's ever seen - head of the practice agreed...so we started w/amoxcillin - and a follow up 10days later...
>
>
>
> so 10days later we go back and see the ped - he's still got it...now he's on another abx - cefedir - we go back in another 10days (actually 6 days b/c he's been on the new abx since Thursday)
>
>
>
> Ped now thinks ds late talking, problems w/talking etc is all related to his hearing. she thinks he may have been having ear infections w/o showing symptoms since he was little. he always loves to pull/play w/his ears - so we can't rely on his playing w/his ears as an indication of an ear infection. he also doesn't get fevers when he has ear infections!
>
>
>
> We see the ENT in 6wks (earliest apt) to check his hearing and see what the proper course of treatment is!
>
>
>
> still waiting on our appt w/ Child Find for his speech eval...
>
>
>
>
>
> we're pushing bone broths, soups, probiotics extra hard considering he's on serious abx .... the life of chasing after optimal health... not only for yourself but for your kids as well....

I had a feeling it could be the ears, from experience with students of mine...that's why I suggested getting that checked. The good thing is you know now and you can work with that. Once the ear health is cleared the speech may not be an issue, time will tell. It's so annoying to have to wait so long for an appointment...let us know how it goes.

### MJ* — 2012-05-15T15:30:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ear-infections-ugghh.1453/post-24177>

> **Shijin13;24190 wrote:** The kids see a Chiro monthly. Ds has only been "out" 2xs in his 2yrs of life . As for dairy we limit it in general at the house & I've considered cutting it back at home but state law says he hast to have milk at Daycare unless he has an allergy. We' ll go to the ent then look at an allergist ugh. He only drink goats milk at dcp. He still nurses at home 2x/day. Ped thinks his etruscan (spelling??) tubes might be a bit faulty. Also thinks this might be why he's a late talker.

Eustacian tubes...that's the part of the ear that fills with fluid, and some kids eustacian tubes fill because of how they lay in the ear. Once they get the fluid down, that's when you'll be able to tell about the speech.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-15T15:40:44+0300

yeah... that's why the ped thinks his delayed speech is 100% related to his hearing/ears. we'll see what the plan of attack is once we see the ENT ....

Ugh, sorry you (and baby shijin) are going through this. We had a ton of probs with baby related to this during months 1-18. We ended up having to sleep with one of us holding him upright. For 18 months. Baby couldn't lay down. Perhaps some mechanical relief for healing like figuring out how their head needs to be positioned with their layout. We got a nice recliner and just went with it.

Good news is one day it just started working right (18 months) and all was good. At first signs of infection after that we dripped a little garlic oil in ear and all was good.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-15T16:53:39+0300

We ended up having to sleep with one of us holding him upright. For 18 months. Baby couldn't lay down. ds had reflux - that he out grew around 13mths... but the ear infections - he doesn't really give clues things are going on - he doesn't present ear infections normally - as far as we know this is his 2nd one - but that may not be the actual case. since birth he's played w/his ears - we figure its a comfort thing - b/c he still does it when he goes to sleep or nurses. he also does when he's upset... so for him playing w/ears isn't an indicator. then there's the fact he only pops fevers when he's teething & bringing in 4 teeth at a time- and then its a whopper of a fever (104 for ~6 days or so!)

# Thread: Big Picture: Infradian Rhythm Applied to Vit-D/Carbs/CT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T18:51:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23921>

I've read almost everything I could on Leptin RX, Paleo Diet, CT and a lot of the comments (there are a ton!) but I haven't really found a complete guide that unifies all the theories and puts them in the context of **Infradian rhythms** as a complete annual system... Especially with regards to Vitamin D and CT.

There's a lot of discussion of circadian rhythms through out the site, but once you've mastered the basics and optimized yourself on the 24-hour cycle I think it's natural to turn our attention to the annual cycle...

1) Jack has already mentioned that he cycles carbs from low (zero) in the winter months to slightly higher during the summer (Jack's FB Yamsagna), but I'm still not sure what (Jack's) "higher carb" generally means... I would imagine it's something along Paul Jaminet's "Perfect Health Diet" with about 100-150 grams of carbs from "safe starches", however, I don't think Jack has gone into detail about the summer diet yet, has he? I mean, you could easily put away 200+ grams of carbs from that yamsagna alone (haha)... Did I just miss it?

2) It seems like we should also abide by an infradian rhythm of Vit-D as well, just like we do with cabs... Higher levels of Vit-D (supplemental/natural sun exposure) during the summer while consuming higher levels of carbs, lower levels of Vitamin D (No supplementation?) during the winter with zero carbs? In fact, according to an interesting article on Vit-D by [Dr. Grominak](http://http://drgominak.com/vitamin-d), the blood levels should vary with the seasons and diet; summer being the building and growing season and winter being the slow living on stores sleeping season... If we supplement Vit-D during the winter (low carb, CT) are we actually doing harm?

3) My final question involves infradian rhythms with CT... my impression from reading CT 1-12 is that you do this year round, but shouldn't we stop this during the summer, when our carb intake and Vit-D levels are higher in nature? Wouldn't this create a mis-match?

Thanks in advance for helping me clarify the "Big Picture", maybe Jack will chime in on a few of these ;-)

### Dan in Utah — 2012-05-14T20:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23957>

I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:

1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.

2) If your vitamin D deficient, supplement all year and get the extra sun in the summer. If you're not deficient, just cycle with the season. (In my experience, the vast majority are deficient, however.)

3) If you're obese and sickly, you need CT year round to reverse disease and calm inflammation. If you're completely healthy, maybe you can get by with less CT in the summer.

Nice "horizontal" thinking JJtitus.

Recently, I just started asking myself the following: what am I doing in my life currently that's not in balance with my genetic, hormonal, and hypothalamic biology and then I try to rectify the mismatches.

### NickieG — 2012-05-14T21:36:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23968>

> **Dan in Utah;24039 wrote:** I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:
>
>
>
> 1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.

Dan....would you say more about carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage? I don't understand what that means.

Thanks

Nickie

### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T21:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23977>

> **NickieG;24050 wrote:** Dan....would you say more about carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage? I don't understand what that means.
>
> Thanks
>
> Nickie

I think what Dan means by eating from a palette based on "genetic heritage" is to choose carb/starch sources that your ancestors traditionally ate... for instance, if you were of Irish heritage you might use potatoes preferentially, or if you were of Asian descent you might choose white rice. I think taking this approach with your diet in general is probably a good basis... That being said, I wouldn't exclude other foods entirely just because it isn't part of your particular ancestor's traditional diet.

### jjtitus — 2012-05-14T22:23:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23985>

> **Dan in Utah;24039 wrote:** I'll take a crack at your 3 issues:
>
>
>
> 1) If you have weight to lose, more carbs in summer might be okay, but don't go much over 100 grams or insulin will trigger fat storage. If your skinny, a little more might be okay, say 150ish. Choose the carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage the best.
>
>
>
> 2) If your vitamin D deficient, supplement all year and get the extra sun in the summer. If you're not deficient, just cycle with the season. (In my experience, the vast majority are deficient, however.)
>
>
>
> 3) If you're obese and sickly, you need CT year round to reverse disease and calm inflammation. If you're completely healthy, maybe you can get by with less CT in the summer.

Dan, that's similar to the question I've been asking myself lately and that led to my 3 infradian rhythm discussions... the article in #2 actually was the catalyst for all three, but I have been contemplating this issue for a while...

1) That's along the lines of what I thought too... although initially Jack made it seem like VLC + CT was the optimal state (for increasing autophagy, lengthening telomeres), it seems like that's shifting to a more seasonal diet.... but I've also heard that every gram of sugar your body uses for energy instead of fat will make you age faster (shorten telomeres). So, which is it? Does the seasonal increase in blood Vit-D protect you from the damaging effects of a higher carbohydrate (sugar) diet? Or, do we just take a bit of a hit on telomere length over the summer and it's no big deal? I feel like I'm still missing something here...

2) I completely agree that you should supplement AFTER getting your blood levels checked, aiming for the 60-80 ng/ml range... but I still think there might be something to having lower levels of Vit-D during the winter than summer, or maybe just higher (80 ish ng/ml) levels during the summer while you're consuming more carbs. From the article it seems like declining levels of Vit-D (shorter days, lower sunlight intensity) along with elevated carb intake during the Fall could facilitate putting on fat stores for the winter.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-05-14T22:59:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-23998>

> 1) That's along the lines of what I thought too... although initially Jack made it seem like VLC + CT was the optimal state (for increasing autophagy, lengthening telomeres), it seems like that's shifting to a more seasonal diet.... but I've also heard that every gram of sugar your body uses for energy instead of fat will make you age faster (shorten telomeres). So, which is it? Does the seasonal increase in blood Vit-D protect you from the damaging effects of a higher carbohydrate (sugar) diet? Or, do we just take a bit of a hit on telomere length over the summer and it's no big deal? I feel like I'm still missing something here...

I think these are essentially unanswerable at this juncture. Doubtless, Vitamin D is protective, but to what degree? Doubtless that advanced glycation endproducts are problematic. Until enough people have "before and after" telomeres, however, I feel a bit in the dark here, too. But if we take evolution as a guide, ketosis in winter, and near ketosis in the summer, is a reasonable target to shoot for. AGEs will bit your telomeres in the shorts, but so will ALEs. To me, it's a big battle against entropy. Eventually entropy is going to win out. Life is like a little air-pocket in a sea of entropy.

The best anyone can do is slow entropy down a bit by living in an optimal way. The essentials for me seem to be: night/day congruent sleep, low stress, paleo-style eating, cold exposure, sprinting and lifting and walking, with each component adapted to individual disease states and genetics.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-15T00:01:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-24022>

> **NickieG;24050 wrote:** Dan....would you say more about carbs from a palette that matches your genetic heritage? I don't understand what that means.
>
> Thanks
>
> Nickie

your genetics dont matter that much......your current environment dictate you genomic expression.......what you are doing now is all that matters. In our modern world we are constantly creating mismatches.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-15T00:06:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-24025>

Around June 21......I have eaten over 400 gms of carbs on occassion. This yr I wont be doing that.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-15T01:44:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-24056>

### Glamorama — 2012-05-15T01:53:25+0300

### jjtitus — 2012-05-15T17:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/big-picture-infradian-rhythm-applied-to-vit-d-carbs-ct.1455/post-24223>

> **Jack Kruse;24107 wrote: Around June 21......I have eaten over 400 gms of carbs on occassion. This yr I wont be doing that.
>
>
> This sounds like it's in line with what we've discussed... higher levels of carbs (even up to 400g/day) during the height of summer if you are lean and fit, less if you still have weight to lose... my question would still be, even if you are lean and fit and CAN eat 400g of carbs a day, should you? That leads me to Dan's post:
>
>
>
> Dan in Utah;24080 wrote:** I think these are essentially unanswerable at this juncture. Doubtless, Vitamin D is protective, but to what degree? Doubtless that advanced glycation endproducts are problematic. Until enough people have "before and after" telomeres, however, I feel a bit in the dark here, too. But if we take evolution as a guide, ketosis in winter, and near ketosis in the summer, is a reasonable target to shoot for. AGEs will bit your telomeres in the shorts, but so will ALEs. To me, it's a big battle against entropy. Eventually entropy is going to win out. Life is like a little air-pocket in a sea of entropy.

My question would be; for a given amount of calories, which metabolic pathway (Glycolysis, Ketosis) produces the fewest oxidative end products (AGEs and ALEs, respectively)? I'm guessing it's ketosis (in general), but I'm getting the impression this changes with the seasons and it seems like Vit-D might play an important part...

# Thread: Becoming cold adapted?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/becoming-cold-adapted.1456/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-05-14T19:13:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/becoming-cold-adapted.1456/post-23927>

For a while now I've been ale to easily handle an hour plus in 50 degree water. Just the past week I've started to notice that I'm cold in normal situations like I used to be. For instance yesterday at my moms I felt quite warm and mentioned that it was warm inside to my parents. My mom looked at me strange and said, it's only 70 degrees in here, I'm freezing and you usually are when I am. I was a bit uncomfortably warm though. Does this mean something is finally starting to happen in my body? Or am I just reading too much into this? What sorts of things have you noticed to tip you off that you're becoming cold adapted?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-14T21:40:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/becoming-cold-adapted.1456/post-23970>

My temperature, taken orally, goes up approximately two degrees now from when I jump in the tub to about 30 min into it. It took me a long time to get to that point with 55-58 water. Now my water is consistently in the high 50s (tap is in the mid-high 60s) and need more of those reusable ice cubes! They seem to work quite well. I also noticed I like the house temp lower than 68 (66) than I used to. But I also think I still have a ways to go to get totally cold adapted.

### PaleoMom — 2012-05-14T21:50:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/becoming-cold-adapted.1456/post-23973>

I'm comfy indoors down to about 62 now and outside down to 50 if it's sunny in a t-shirt, jeans and bare feet. I tend to get really hot at night sometimes. I sleep with the window open (outside temp mid 30's to low 40's at night) and for at least half the night I have only a sheet over me. I don't need that either, but I have a hard time sleeping without something over me (like the sheet is actually protecting from anything )

### TerrierMom — 2012-05-14T21:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/becoming-cold-adapted.1456/post-23974>

I think jack has defined it as when your temp goes up after being in the water (or cold air) -core temp- and a couple of degrees. In some folks it will go up \*in anticipation\* of getting In the water (very cold adapted) so if you're curious check your temp before during and after CT and see what your trends are..... Forget which blog or comment it was where I read this!

Also have heard him say in response to a ? That being cold adapted actually makes you tolerate heat better too, as well as cold (which I have already noticed) ....

Other then that could just be hormone changing or something else making you hot? I'm sure I've heard someone else report this and JK commented so check the CT series blog comments and CT threads....

# Thread: Hello from NH
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-05-14T22:56:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-23997>

Hi all,

I've been reading, re-reading, and re-re-reading Jack's blog posts, as well as the forums, since I stumbled into the information gem about 7 weeks ago. I had doing yet another google search about adrenal fatigue, or Hashimoto's, or biotoxin illness, or food sensitivities causing weight gain. I was tired of reading the same old information, and had been looking for a fresh approach for quite a while. Boy did I hit a goldmine!!

I've been officially doing the leptin reset for 6 weeks, but had allready incorporated everything except the BAB for a month prior to finding the leptin RX on my own. I started CTing about 4 weeks ago, and do daily 54ish degree baths for 30ish minutes almost fully immersed (I am stuggling to get much past the 30 minute mark). I also do cold showers almost daily. My afterdrop recovery times are improving, but I do still feel a strong afterdrop response getting out of the tub, so I guess I'm not cold adapted yet.

6 weeks into the leptin RX, I am still struggling with never feeling full/satisfied. I feel like I could keep eating and eating. I finally decided to come out the shadows of lurkdom and try to take a more active role on the forum.

### ealachan — 2012-05-14T23:07:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24001>

I wave to you from Vermont!

I was a bit confused by your post - you DO now incorporate the BAB, or you still do not?

### nonchalant — 2012-05-14T23:35:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24011>

Hi Bigknitwit, welcome to the forum!

You may want to think about increasing the amount of protein you are eating.

### SeaHorse — 2012-05-14T23:50:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24018>

Another wave from Vermont!

What foods are you sensitive to? I'm still trying to figure this out and it can make a big difference in bloating weight gain and appetite as I'm sure you know...I'm working with a really helpful ND here in Vermont if you are looking for somebody..

### bigknitwit — 2012-05-15T01:36:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24055>

> **ealachan;24083 wrote:** I wave to you from Vermont!
>
>
>
> I was a bit confused by your post - you DO now incorporate the BAB, or you still do not?

Yes, for the past 6 weeks I do the BAB. For a month prior to that, I was trying to reign in my appetite and had restricted myself to 3 low carb meals/day only.

### bigknitwit — 2012-05-15T01:53:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24058>

> **SeaHorse;24100 wrote:** Another wave from Vermont!
>
>
>
> What foods are you sensitive to? I'm still trying to figure this out and it can make a big difference in bloating weight gain and appetite as I'm sure you know...I'm working with a really helpful ND here in Vermont if you are looking for somebody..

Ughh... I used to be one of the those people who could eat anything she wanted and remain weight stable and slim. That disappeared after my 4th baby was born. I struggled to drop anything after she was born (which had been effortless with the older 3). Then I had a rapid weight loss with illness (13 pounds in 10 days) followed by an elimination diet down to 6 foods for 6 weeks, trying to figure about baby's allergies/food issues (she was nursing and having all kinds of skin reactions, especially around her mouth). After that, things were never the same for me. I'm not sure if it's an allergic reaction, an insulin issue, or both, but when I eat a food I cannot tolerate, I gain weight - like 2-3 pounds overnight. And the weight stays on and accumulates quickly. I gained 15 pounds in 3 weeks while trying to figure out the food issues. Now, I am left with meats (not cured, sadly), vegetables (no mushrooms or starchy veggies), cantaloupe, and a few other fruits in very small portions. Everything else causes the dramatic overnight water retention. It's very frustrating. I am seeing an environmental physician who has tested me and confirmed I have had biotoxin (mold) exposure. I just did more testing to quantify how much and then he will create a detox program for me. I am hopeful that this may be at the root of my issues. How's that for a long answer??!

### SeaHorse — 2012-05-15T14:55:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24165>

I can relate to this too....swelling hands and feet in the am are a sign something was off the day before. Have you looked at oxalates, histamine and salicylates? For me, it explained why certain foods were giving me a reaction...still is a pain to maintain such a limited range of meat and veg. but I'm hoping with the resets and CT that things will start to turn around. I can't take some of the liver support herbs because they are high oxalate...

Hope things start to improve for you soon!

### bigknitwit — 2012-05-15T23:31:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-from-nh.1459/post-24374>

Interesting... 18 months ago while on the elimination diet for my daughter, I was really suspect of salicylate foods causing her reactions. I could never really pin it down for me. It seems like foods that aggravate yeast overgrowth (like mushrooms and grapes) are a problem. I don't think the sals are an issue (cantaloupe is high and it doesn't cause the extreme water retention). I don't know about histamines.

I have a little theory that each time I've quickly dropped weight (once due to illness, once on a 5 day fast), I've actually just recirculated toxins and made things worse (more food sensitivities). I am doing CT, but in the back of my mind I'm wondering about my body's ability to excrete all that may be released.

# Thread: MSG...now I think I really get it
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/msg-now-i-think-i-really-get-it.1461/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-14T23:49:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/msg-now-i-think-i-really-get-it.1461/post-24016>

[http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/crux/2012/05/14/is-the-purpose-of-sleep-to-let-our-brains-defragment-like-a-hard-drive/](http://http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/crux/2012/05/14/is-the-purpose-of-sleep-to-let-our-brains-defragment-like-a-hard-drive/)

Is the Purpose of Sleep to Let Our Brains â€œDefragment,â€ Like a Hard Drive?

Yet this poses a problem for the brain. If LTP and synapse formation is constantly strengthening our synapses, and we are learning all our lives, might the synapses eventually reach a limit? Couldnâ€™t they â€œmax out,â€ so that they could never get any stronger?

**Worse, most of the synapses that strengthen during memory are based on glutamate. Glutamate is dangerous. Itâ€™s the most common neurotransmitter in the brain, and itâ€™s also a popular flavouring: â€œMSGâ€, monosodium glutamate. But in the brain, too much of it is toxic.

Glutamate works as a transmitter molecule by opening channels on the cells that receive it. The channels allow calcium into the cells on the receiving end, which activates them, allowing messages to go through. But too much glutamate can cause excess calcium to build up inside the very cells that receive the message, a harmful process called excitotoxicity.**

So, if our brains were constantly forming stronger glutamate synapses, we might eventually run into serious problems. One function of sleep, according to the theory, is to protect the brain against excitotoxicity or other â€œsynaptic overloadâ€ problems by pruning the synapses.

### chocolate — 2012-05-14T23:55:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/msg-now-i-think-i-really-get-it.1461/post-24021>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20968043](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20968043)

\[Effect of long-term monosodium glutamate administration on structure and functional state of the stomach and body weight in rats\].

Abstract

The influence of prolonged administration of monosodium glutamate (MSG) on basal gastric acid secretion, body weight and gastric mucosa in rats was studied. **We found that 10-, 20-, 30-days feeding by MSG in doses 15 to 30 mg/kg (equivalent to I and 2 g/person) leads to erosive and ulcerative lesions of the gastric mucosa and an increased secretion of hydrochloric acid and an increased body weight. It is concluded that the stimulating effect of MSG on the basal secretion of hydrochloric acid in the stomach may be implicated in the pathogenesis of a number of acid-dependent diseases. An excessive consumption of MSG can cause a "Chinese Restaurant Syndrome" and gastritis, gastric and duodenal ulcers. Therefore, the maximum dose of MSG should be reconsidered taking into account its influence on the secretory capacity of the stomach.** We also conclude that prolonged, excessive and systemic consumption of MSG causes obesity.

### chocolate — 2012-05-15T00:04:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/msg-now-i-think-i-really-get-it.1461/post-24024>

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/102/7/835.full.pdf](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/102/7/835.full.pdf)

Effects of Dietary Vitamin B6 on the Utilization of

Monosodium Glutamate by Rats '

CHI-PANG WEN AND STANLEY N. GERSHOFF

Department of Nutrition, Harvard School of Public Health

There appeared to be decreased clear

ance of intravenously administered gluta

mate in deficient animals. This might have

been the result of diminished tissue uptake

of the amino acid or of delayed catabolism

of the amino acid. Beaton et al. (13) have

reported that vitamin Be-deficient rats have

a decreased ability to deaminate amino

acids and that their utilization of glutamic

acid is impaired. It has also been shown

that vitamin Be deficiency depresses the

aminotransferase systems most important

to glutamic acid metabolism in rat liver

(14), blood (15), and heart (16). In this

study similar observations were obtained

on the intestinal aminotransferases. These

enzymes are of particular importance be

cause they determine to a considerable ex

tent the amount of glutamic acid which

enters the portal and systemic circulation

from the intestines.

**This article is from 1972**

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/msg-now-i-think-i-really-get-it.1461/post-26184>

> **chocolate;24098 wrote: [http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/crux/2012/05/14/is-the-purpose-of-sleep-to-let-our-brains-defragment-like-a-hard-drive/](http://http://blogs.discovermagazine.com/crux/2012/05/14/is-the-purpose-of-sleep-to-let-our-brains-defragment-like-a-hard-drive/)
>
> Is the Purpose of Sleep to Let Our Brains “Defragment,” Like a Hard Drive?
>
>
>
> Yet this poses a problem for the brain. If LTP and synapse formation is constantly strengthening our synapses, and we are learning all our lives, might the synapses eventually reach a limit? Couldn’t they “max out,” so that they could never get any stronger?
>
>
>
> Worse, most of the synapses that strengthen during memory are based on glutamate. Glutamate is dangerous. It’s the most common neurotransmitter in the brain, and it’s also a popular flavouring: “MSG”, monosodium glutamate. But in the brain, too much of it is toxic.
>
>
>
> Glutamate works as a transmitter molecule by opening channels on the cells that receive it. The channels allow calcium into the cells on the receiving end, which activates them, allowing messages to go through. But too much glutamate can cause excess calcium to build up inside the very cells that receive the message, a harmful process called excitotoxicity.**
>
>
>
> So, if our brains were constantly forming stronger glutamate synapses, we might eventually run into serious problems. One function of sleep, according to the theory, is to protect the brain against excitotoxicity or other “synaptic overload” problems by pruning the synapses.

This helps to explain why when exposed to MSG I get headache within 15 mins that eventually turns into a migraine, stomach cramps, cold sweats, nasuea, increased heart rate, etc. Damned MSG shouldn't be added to ANYTHING but it is indeed in EVERYTHING!

### kjgraffin@yahoo.com — 2012-05-17T16:54:14+0300

Does that play into my numbers at all? My lactoferrin is 2.91 (1.00-7.24) but I don't know if it supposed to be on the low end or the high end.

Thoughts?

# Thread: Lower freezing point with salt
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 21
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-05-15T14:23:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24157>

If I want to lower the freezing point of tap water, how much salt would I need to add?

For instance; lowering 150litres from 0c to -2c.

### Onum — 2012-05-15T20:57:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24324>

> **primat;24239 wrote:** If I want to lower the freezing point of tap water, how much salt would I need to add?
>
>
>
> For instance; lowering 150litres from 0c to -2c.

Seawater which on average (according to Wikipedia) is 3.5% dissolved solids has a typical fp of -2 deg.C - although this isn't just Sodium Chloride of course -should be a good starting off point.

That would make it 5.25kg Salt in your 150l If you wanted to be really swish there is a product sold for marine aquariums called "instant ocean" or some such which AFAIK is just salt from evaporated sea water and is thus a more "natural" combination of salts, but I'm guessing won't be as economical.

### Onum — 2012-05-15T22:35:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24343>

Is your next question going to be "How the heck do I cool 150l of salt water to below freezing??"

I'm genuinely curious about this, I see you're in Norway but the weather I see isn't conducive to passive chilling to those temps.

If using ice you're going to need a shed-load\* (and of course that's water so it will dilute the salt and raise the fp) - you could use ice made from the same concentration solution to avoid a dilution effect)

\*By my calcs\*\* you would need over 20kg of -20Â°C ice to cool 150l of nominal 10Â°C water to around -2Â°C and it would have all melted by this point.

Of course you may have a cunning plan...

\*\*Not to be trusted

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-15T22:42:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24349>

This is what I was curious about. It is one thing to lower the freezing point. Completely another thing to get it to that new temp.

### primat — 2012-05-16T09:11:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24545>

> **Onum;24425 wrote:** How the heck do I cool 150l of salt water to below freezing??

Hehe - well, since ice is not a viable product here (you can get 2kg bags at around $8 at 7-eleven) I had to get ice somewhere else. The local fishmarket has a supplier that sells crushed ice at an affordable price pr kilo. I'm picking up 40kg today, just for a test. I thought I needed around 50kg? Anyways it's all going in

I havent got hold of salt yet, so I'll do this with just ice. It's probably going to be cold enough for a first deep ct-dip

### primat — 2012-05-16T09:27:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24547>

> **August;24433 wrote:** I wonder if adding salt or epson salts to the CT water would affect the rate at which heat is transferred from your body . . (I think sodium and magnesium are both technically considered metals) . . . I would guess not enough to make it worthwhile . . but it might be worth finding out for sure. .

Hmm, If it also would lower freezing temp I wouldn't mind trying it. Seems our local farm supplier has [bittersalt](http://http://www.felleskjopet.no/landbruk/Documents/Interne/Kraftfor_Plantekultur/Gj%C3%B8dsel/Produktdatablad/MAGNESIUM%20SULPHATE%207-HYDRATE%20FCC%202010-11-16%20MA2415.pdf) for bulk sale. Have to stop by and check price and availability...

Update: ordered 2x25kg bags of MgSO4Â·7H2O for a total of $75. Getting 50kg from our friendly neighbourhood store would set me back 100x500g bags @$17.5 = $1750  Ok, the stuff is more finely grained...

### Onum — 2012-05-16T13:29:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24554>

> **primat;24627 wrote:** Hehe - well, since ice is not a viable product here (you can get 2kg bags at around $8 at 7-eleven) I had to get ice somewhere else. The local fishmarket has a supplier that sells crushed ice at an affordable price pr kilo. I'm picking up 40kg today, just for a test. I thought I needed around 50kg? Anyways it's all going in
>
>
>
> I havent got hold of salt yet, so I'll do this with just ice. It's probably going to be cold enough for a first deep ct-dip

Well, it really depends on the temperature of the water you're trying to cool, 40kg of ice should be sufficient to cool 160l of 20Â°C water to about 0Â°. If the inititial temp of the water is about 10Â°C you may have to wait for half of it to melt to get down to your desired temp - it's the phase change of the ice from solid to liquid that will cool the water, any excess ice will act as a buffer to maintain the temp whilst your body tries to warm it up.

Good luck!

### primat — 2012-05-16T15:33:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-24563>

Got to 0.9c after stirring and waiting a bit - I could have done with less ice too.

It felt less cold than the 8-10c tap water, that was a bit surprising. After 14 min I started shivering, and I got out at 18min. Warmed up faster than ever, maybe because duration was shorter than my usual 45 min @10c? 37.3c core temp before, 37.2c core temp after

That sent a chill through me just looking at it! Hahaha! Guess my 52 degree water @ 50 mins needs to bring it. Wish I could submerge that much in my tub...'tis very shallow. Anywho, that is very cool; might have to try that.

### primat — 2012-05-22T09:13:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lower-freezing-point-with-salt.1467/post-26287>

Sent from my U8860 using Tapatalk 2

# Thread: How can I put webinar on itunes?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/>
**Subforum:** Feedback/Suggestions
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-05-15T15:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/post-24168>

I purchased webinar which is in wmv format. Do you know how I can convert this so I can put the audio on my ipod? thanks

### admin — 2012-05-15T16:38:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/post-24208>

It's a video file, not an audio file.

### JC2K — 2012-05-15T16:57:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/post-24215>

What exactly are you looking for Dali? The audio or the video?

I'm assuming the WMV is not DRM protected. There's misc free websites that will convert WMV to MP3 if you just want the audio part. Depending on the size of the WMV file, they may want money. On the other hand, if it is an iPod that can play video, there are sites that will convert WMV to MP4 format to play on the iPod (again for money if it is too large). There is also software you can buy like Anysoft's Any Dvdconverter that will do it as well with tons of options (you have to pay for it).

Drag the resulting file to the correct area in iTunes and then sync it to your device.

I've used them all for Youtube videos for my iPhone and they've worked well enough, just search Google for them.

### Dali Dula — 2012-05-15T17:16:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/post-24224>

The webinar although in wmv actually has no video. I just want the audio on my ipod. I was hoping there was a way to do this without third party software, but it seems lke that is the only way. A podcast format of the webinar would be awesome.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T17:45:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-can-i-put-webinar-on-itunes.1469/post-24234>

> **Dali Dula;24306 wrote:** The webinar although in wmv actually has no video. I just want the audio on my ipod. I was hoping there was a way to do this without third party software, but it seems lke that is the only way. A podcast format of the webinar would be awesome.

I agree with this! I would love to listen on idevice without video. Much smaller audio file would be peachy.

# Thread: Tinnitus r us
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T15:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24186>

Through the years, I've had little blips of tinnitus that last for 1-3 mins. lately however, I've had a constant and louder ringing. Yesterday it was enough in interfere with sleep. And I love my sleep - very unwelcomed!

I've seen a few things about magnesium deficiency and hearing loss causes. For the record I've been supplementing like a champ for months. I do have some absorption issues - so that COULD be the issue.

I can't start trying to figure this out without at least looking at what I am doing differently NOW that might be triggering it.

I'm a good leptin RX girl, heavy seafood, CT, lots of supps. AND my friends - that is what worries me a little. Instead of "not enough of X", do any of you admirable minds have ideas on "too much of X"

I've got a doc appt tomorrow for lots of tests (yay! finally getting tests!) so I think I won't take any supps today and see if it backs off tonight.

I hate to be an internet whiner - but last night was a different tone in each ear and they were not nice 3rds, it was dischord. Can't have that!

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-15T15:56:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24189>

Cavemam, sorry to hear this. All this talk about tinnitus made me realize that mine has disappeared! Weird. I wish I knew what it was. I take a lot of mag too, but I've been doing that for a while. So, I'm not sure what clear it up! Sorry, wish I could help...how about coffee? Are you doing coffee?

### Melinda — 2012-05-15T16:14:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24197>

Too early in the morning for me to provide links but as I understand it, tinnitis is the result of hearing loss in spots along the frequency range where the brain adds in a "ghost" noise. You can go get brain training (link to follow later) that gets the brain to grab the deaf spots and train them to the neighboring spots that work/hear. That being said, why does it wax and wane? Mine buzzes when I'm hungry and other times. I'm still working on it.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T16:22:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24199>

> **Melinda;24279 wrote:** Too early in the morning for me to provide links but as I understand it, tinnitis is the result of hearing loss in spots along the frequency range where the brain adds in a "ghost" noise. You can go get brain training (link to follow later) that gets the brain to grab the deaf spots and train them to the neighboring spots that work/hear. That being said, why does it wax and wane? Mine buzzes when I'm hungry and other times. I'm still working on it.

Thanks Mel - I'm going to look into this brain training. I know I have hearing loss in one side, and since I spent the better part of 10 years in marching bands surrounded by blasting trombones and trumpets - not a total surprise.

It's something to look at, after only one night of lost sleep I can see how people would go nuts. I was envisioning having to go see Jack to dissconnect something in my brain so I could at least just be deaf and experience sweet relief. I daydream too much.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T16:23:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24201>

> **Jennifer;24271 wrote:** Cavemam, sorry to hear this. All this talk about tinnitus made me realize that mine has disappeared! Weird. I wish I knew what it was. I take a lot of mag too, but I've been doing that for a while. So, I'm not sure what clear it up! Sorry, wish I could help...how about coffee? Are you doing coffee?

Awesome that yours is gone. It's hopeful. I am going to get the rub-on mag that you guys have been raving about -stat. I am doing coffee. My day always starts with a great big cup of optimal.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-15T16:31:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24205>

> **Cavemam;24283 wrote:** Awesome that yours is gone. It's hopeful. I am going to get the rub-on mag that you guys have been raving about -stat. I am doing coffee. My day always starts with a great big cup of optimal.

It's too bad I didn't realize when it stopped, thenI might have a clue to offer. I was lucky that although it was annoying, it never interrupted my sleep. Thank god. The only radically different things I am doing are the BAB, 5 Htp and L-tyrosine, and I cut out eating O6 (mayo, blue cheese dressing, almonds). Oh and I haven't had coffee except for a few cups here and there. Good luck!

### nonchalant — 2012-05-15T16:57:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24216>

Hi Cavemam, sorry you're in distress. I had tinnitus that was getting fairly bad, but not enough to wake me up, before leaving SAD-land. All my senses were a bit wonky though.

Loud noises would irritate me; my family knew better than to slam the door or pop a balloon near me! I couldn't take bright lights, whether car headlights, a flashlight, or bright sunlight. I was also quite ticklish. Seems all my senses were irritable, except I remember I could barely smell if something was rancid or not. All that has changed for the better now. Not sure if my taste has changed any, but everything else has really calmed down.

CT this year has not aggravated my tinnitus. I have been taking various chelates of magnesium for well over a year, in increasing amounts. I seem to recall that it really helped, at the beginning when tinnitus was bothering me. What a relief! If you are having problems absorbing magnesium I would definitely go the skin route, including CT in the lakes nearby. I wonder if you can absorb Mg Threonate?

### ealachan — 2012-05-15T17:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24226>

> **Cavemam;24268 wrote:** Through the years, I've had little blips of tinnitus that last for 1-3 mins. lately however, I've had a constant and louder ringing. Yesterday it was enough in interfere with sleep. And I love my sleep - very unwelcomed!
>
>
>
> I've seen a few things about magnesium deficiency and hearing loss causes. For the record I've been supplementing like a champ for months. I do have some absorption issues - so that COULD be the issue.
>

> I can't start trying to figure this out without at least looking at what I am doing differently NOW that might be triggering it.
>

> I'm a good leptin RX girl, heavy seafood, CT, lots of supps. AND my friends - that is what worries me a little. Instead of "not enough of X", do any of you admirable minds have ideas on "too much of X"
>

>
>
>
> I hate to be an internet whiner - but last night was a different tone in each ear and they were not nice 3rds, it was dischord. Can't have that!

Do you supplement heavily with B12? I get once-in-a-while tinnitus, but the one thing that made me have it almost constantly was sublingual B-12 supplementation. No idea why. Third or fourth day of taking the supplement, the tinnitus cropped up and I had it for two days straight. By the third morning, I was a wreck. I stopped the B-12, and by that evening the tinnitus had faded considerably. The next morning I was pretty much back to normal.

Not saying B-12 would do that to everyone...just my weird tinnitus experience. I have weird ears anyway, for the record. Lots of ear infections as a kid (because I have enormous tonsils that block my eustacian tubes so that they don't drain properly), I've been able to hear my heart beating in my left ear for like 10 years now, and I have some hearing loss in one ear as well.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T17:31:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24228>

Hmm! I do actually. Since I have absorption issues I upped my b12 to 2X and sometimes 3X a day - and did that in the last few weeks. I'm not doing any supps today, I may stop them for tomorrow too and see what happens. I will n-1 for this, Thanks!

I seem to recall that it really helped, at the beginning when tinnitus was bothering me. What a relief! If you are having problems absorbing magnesium I would definitely go the skin route, including CT in the lakes nearby. I wonder if you can absorb Mg Threonate?

I have been taking the supp form of malate and chelated. So I'm on the hunt today for the skin kind - and I am going to start putting the epsom in the baths. I have heard it's not as effective in cold, but perhaps since I'm in the damn tub 6X a week it might be a plus.

Thanks all - this is great!

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-15T19:05:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24259>

I get this from allergies. Particularly supplements that have inactive ingredients that don't agree with me (it comes with heart palpitations, stuffy nose, and a face full of dermatitis).

### vlynnb — 2012-05-15T20:14:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24299>

Souldanzer, Which inactive ingredients bother you? I have been having louder tinnitus at night than usual. I am allergic to a lot of foods, pollen and animals but never connected supplement ingredients to the tinnitus.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-15T20:30:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-24309>

At this point I count them all unless it's MCT oil, olive oil. Worst offenders I am sure of:

Cellulose/microcrystalline cellulose/hypoallergenic cellulose

rice flour

magnesium stearate

natural flavors

whatever colors no matter how natural

pretty sure magnesium carbonate

silicon dioxide

titanium dioxide

....

I have not found a pill/tablet that I can tolerate. Glycerine in soft gels appears to be acceptable as long as they have absolutely no other ingredients. A few weeks ago I figured out I am allergic to toothpaste as well. Have switched to toothsoap now. I can't have store bought Kevita either... natural flavors. Easiest way to find out is to stop them all for a week and look for improvement.

### chocolate — 2012-06-24T06:15:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33251>

I read that histidine deficiency is the cause of nerve deafness and think tinnitus was mentioned.

### vlynnb — 2012-06-24T06:49:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33253>

Thanks, Souldanzer. Somehow I missed seeing your post before. I'm definitely going to have to stop most of my supplements to see if it makes a difference as my tinnitus is getting worse. It isn't too bad, I only notice it at night and it sounds like the water system is running outside, just a very low hum but it is getting louder lately.

And Chocolate, that is very interesting about the histamine. With all the allergies I have and the antihistamines I have taken that makes me wonder. Thanks.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-24T06:55:10+0300

I have been off all supps for 11 days. No CT either. Tonight my tinnitus is pretty annoying. Mine is like a high pitched ring. I wonder what the best medical pro is to see for this.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-24T07:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33257>

I think the underlying cause for tinnitus is reduced blood flow to the ear. For me laying of the allergens helps b/c it makes my headache better... which means increased blood flow. So whatever you need to do to increase blood going to that area might help. I still have tinnitus from my brain injury. Hoping to get that dealt with once I'm ready for HBOT which should make it go away.

### Glamazon — 2012-06-26T04:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33573>

I have had tinnitus for years.For awhile acupuncture helped but not anymore. Last year I thought about the vasodilation, that it might help. So I started taking increasingly higher doses of niacin. It did help but, annoyingly one day, I stood up and had lost all control of my bladder. Went all over my feet. I couldn't figure out what happened. Really scared me. The only new thing that I had added to my life at that time was increased niacin supplementation. I did not take the sustained release. I no longer had the flushing because I was taking so much, about three tabs of 600 throughout the day. Reading some side effects on the internet I found that a very small percentage of the population do lose bladder control with niacin supplementation. It was so bad it was like emptying out a cup. I could not feel any muscle contraction in the urethra. I stopped the supplementation immediately and it took almost two months for control to be re-established. It was awful. I had to do kiegels for awhile.

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-26T04:26:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33575>

> **Glamazon;33710 wrote:** I have had tinnitus for years.For awhile acupuncture helped but not anymore. Last year I thought about the vasodilation, that it might help. So I started taking increasingly higher doses of niacin. It did help but, annoyingly one day, I stood up and had lost all control of my bladder. Went all over my feet. I couldn't figure out what happened. Really scared me. The only new thing that I had added to my life at that time was increased niacin supplementation. I did not take the sustained release. I no longer had the flushing because I was taking so much, about three tabs of 600 throughout the day. Reading some side effects on the internet I found that a very small percentage of the population do lose bladder control with niacin supplementation. It was so bad it was like emptying out a cup. I could not feel any muscle contraction in the urethra. I stopped the supplementation immediately and it took almost two months for control to be re-established. It was awful. I had to do kiegels for awhile.

What a pisser!

### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-06-26T04:33:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33577>

I was a band geek, Trombone - and spent every year from about 6th grade through grad school surrounded by very loud and blatting brass instruments. I suppose hearing issues should not be a surprise. I do have hearing loss in the ear that rings the most. I am going to make an appointment at a hearing center here to get an opinion. From their website they seem to have some interesting technology.

[http://longmonthearing.com/tinnitus/](http://http://longmonthearing.com/tinnitus/)

From my best googling, tinnitus following hearing loss is explainable with my auditory history. This seems like such a textbook case - and I've tried every variation of supplement/natural food possibility so it's time to see if CW can do anything.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-26T05:26:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33594>

My dh is just like you except he played the drums. If something works, please let us know.

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-26T07:15:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33609>

> **Melinda;24279 wrote:** Too early in the morning for me to provide links but as I understand it, tinnitis is the result of hearing loss in spots along the frequency range where the brain adds in a "ghost" noise. You can go get brain training (link to follow later) that gets the brain to grab the deaf spots and train them to the neighboring spots that work/hear. That being said, why does it wax and wane? Mine buzzes when I'm hungry and other times. I'm still working on it.

Interested in the links Melinda. DH was also a band geek, and artillery in his USMC days didn't help either. Then last year at a baseball game I let out a whistle a bit louder than I meant to and BLammo! Instant ringing that hasn't stopped yet. He isn't a complainer so I only recently found out it's still happening.

He is supplementing with mg malate and threonate (like me) but not sure if it's getting into his system as he had a radical leg cramp last night after ocean CT of all things (should have had

Plenty of Mg right??). Put him in a warm Epsom salt bath and it subsided....

Interested in any and all potential things to try, ESP the brain retraining thing.

### Melinda — 2012-06-26T07:30:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-r-us.1470/post-33613>

It's late for me so I'll give you a better answer tomorrow. I think I got it from "Train yourself to survive on stress". Mine varies with my stress level, my glucose level and my blood pressure. Up, up, down.

# Thread: Ridin the wave. . .
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jeanie@truelongmont.com — 2012-05-15T16:16:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24198>

My microwave broke a couple of months ago. We've not replaced it. I wanted to see if we could go without - but to be honest, I haven't looked into the matter much. Some of our paleo/hippie type friends refuse to use them, but their reasons seem vague and silly sometimes (unlike sitting in ice, that's just plain cool  heh)

Aside from the superficial convention for "keeping up appearances" for professional reasons, most would think we've gone full hippie. No cosmetics, only homemade concoctionss. Outdoors earthing and attempting some sun with no sunscreen. Not letting our poor child have cake at parties. You, I think you know what I'm talkin about.

So is wave free a reasonable step? Ok in moderation? Never without aluminum foil hat?

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-15T16:28:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24202>

Never w/o a tinfoil hat, especially if you're watching ancient aliens....

seriously though - It depends on what works best for your family imo. We use a toaster oven to heat lots of stuff up...but sometimes its just easier/more convenient to use the microwave (especially when the sudden feed me now mommy monsters show up (patients not included in said monsters) also b/c both DH & I work full time... its hard to always have a fresh cooked meal w/in a reasonable time frame (dinner before 7pm) when we get home from work btwn 6-630!. I can prep meals on the weekend - or a day or two before hand...but then there's the whole reheat process... microwave makes dinner a little easier - probably not optimal - but it works for now...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-15T19:20:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24268>

We have a functioning microwave but I don't use it except to heat up water to put the environmentally safe "Oxy-Clean" in for the laundry! LOL I never use the micro for food any more. It's been over a year. We use the toaster oven as well when heating up food or even the stove top. Plus I eat food cold although the BF won't.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-15T19:31:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24272>

I store pots in my microwave and use it to melt coconut oil b/c it's warm in there.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-05-16T00:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ridin-the-wave.1471/post-24400>

No judgements on how bad the microwave is, but.... I pack all my husband's work lunches which he reheats in the microwave..... and I make sure they're always in glass, no plastic.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-16T01:46:47+0300

This is a bit on the geeky side, but a lot of the MMO games have Developer Trackers that will list any posts by the game developers with links to the actual post in the thread they responded to.

Example:

[http://www.swtor.com/community/devtracker.php](http://http://www.swtor.com/community/devtracker.php)

It would make me feel a bit stalkerlike, but can we get something like that for Jack? We can do something almost the same with the search functionality, but you can't see the new responses, and if the response is buried in a thread, you have to go through the whole thread to find the post to see if it is even something that you want to read (might not be pertinent to your interests).

# Thread: Use your mind
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-05-15T17:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24240>

reading about people who can start warming themselves up before actually getting in the water intrigues me. While I have never done official biofeedback, I have been able to change a few things at will, such as blood pressure, blood flow and pulse rate. Don't have any water? Can you just think your way to thermogenesis? Is there any literature out there on this, and/or have you tried it?

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-05-15T18:34:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24249>

And, should you try to concentrate/visualize that your body is freezing cold - so it will warm itself up? or should you imagine your furnace taking off and generating heat?

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-15T18:53:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24257>

It's a habituated response. Your nervous system and brain will do this for you if trained correctly.

> Classical conditioning (also Pavlovian conditioning or respondent conditioning) is a form of learning in which one stimulus, the conditioned stimulus or CS, comes to signal the occurrence of a second stimulus, the unconditioned stimulus or US.

The moment you hit the cold water your body reacts with turning on the furnace. Pair this moment with an unconditioned stimulus of your choice for a few times the moment you enter the cold (could be a mental image if you've got enough control of your mind for this or an actual stimulus... ring a bell, knead a stress ball, look at a particular picture... pick something that you don't encounter too often in daily life when it isn't followed by cold).

Within a few trials of pairing CS with UCS, your body should react with warming up to just the new stimulus (the mental image, the kneading of a stress ball, ...).

Happy classical conditioning. Works with dogs, too

### youthfuliz — 2012-05-15T19:44:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24275>

> **Souldanzer;24339 wrote:** It's a habituated response. Your nervous system and brain will do this for you if trained correctly.
>
>
>
> The moment you hit the cold water your body reacts with turning on the furnace. Pair this moment with an unconditioned stimulus of your choice for a few times the moment you enter the cold (could be a mental image if you've got enough control of your mind for this or an actual stimulus... ring a bell, knead a stress ball, look at a particular picture... pick something that you don't encounter too often in daily life when it isn't followed by cold).
>
>
>
> Within a few trials of pairing CS with UCS, your body should react with warming up to just the new stimulus (the mental image, the kneading of a stress ball, ...).
>
>
>
> Happy classical conditioning. Works with dogs, too

Ha, I have been playing the same CD for my CT sessions for two months now... Rod Stewart The Great American Songbook V. One. I should play it without going into the tub one day to see if my body fires up. Well, just thinking of it gets my heart pumping now... so it's working.

### janagram — 2012-05-16T02:43:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24441>

just google Tummo fire.....ancient Tibetan yoga. They could sit in snow and dry off wet sheets on their chests.

### Birdy — 2012-05-16T03:59:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24482>

> **villjamur\_stevenson;24331 wrote:** And, should you try to concentrate/visualize that your body is freezing cold - so it will warm itself up? or should you imagine your furnace taking off and generating heat?

visualize my furnace taking off and generating heat. I can do it with my hands, but not for very long

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-17T21:34:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24976>

it appears my body has gotten accustomed to the time of day I usually hit the tub... if I don't I start to get way too hot

### neaderthal — 2012-05-17T22:17:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24990>

This is an interesting thread as it somewhat captures something I've wondered about. I was surprised when I was able to sit in a bath of 55 degree water for over an hour the first time I tried CT with no problems--no shivering during or after. After a couple of times at 55 degrees, I got the water down to 50 degrees--again no problem. While I'm CTg, I'm always looking outside; so, my eyes are observing a hot & sunny environment. I believe the visual of "hot & sunny" is contributing to my ability to tolerate the cold bath so well. Although, I wonder if the "hot & sunny" visual could block the full potential of biochemical changes we are aiming for by CTg. Thoughts?

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-17T22:22:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24992>

> **neaderthal;25072 wrote:** This is an interesting thread as it somewhat captures something I've wondered about. I was surprised when I was able to sit in a bath of 55 degree water for over an hour the first time I tried CT with no problems--no shivering during or after. After a couple of times at 55 degrees, I got the water down to 50 degrees--again no problem. While I'm CTg, I'm always looking outside; so, my eyes are observing a hot & sunny environment. I believe the visual of "hot & sunny" is contributing to my ability to tolerate the cold bath so well. Although, I wonder if the "hot & sunny" visual could block the full potential of biochemical changes we are aiming for by CTg. Thoughts?

Nice. I doubt it hurts since your body still goes through the same biological changes to warm you up.

Looking for a nice picture to place right where I look while I'm in the tub....

### neaderthal — 2012-05-17T22:40:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/use-your-mind.1474/post-24997>

Souldanzer, yes, the "visual" seems to work quite well as it's hard to think one is going to get too cold as you are looking out towards beautiful green trees and mountains and sunshine. My roommate doesn't much like the fact that I keep removing the shower doors, but who wants a claustrophobic CT session when with a little tweaking of your bathroom and maybe your mind, you can be enjoying a cool bath out in nature?!
