# Thread: Epicatechin...its what's in chocolate and lots of other things
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-17T15:21:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-24858>

Cocoa 'Vitamin' Health Benefits Could Outshine Penicillin [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2007/03/070311202024.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2007/03/070311202024.htm)

ScienceDaily (Mar. 11, 2007) — The health benefits of epicatechin, a compound found in cocoa, are so striking that it may rival penicillin and anaesthesia in terms of importance to public health, reports Marina Murphy in Chemistry & Industry, the magazine of the SCI. Norman Hollenberg, professor of medicine at Harvard Medical School, told C&I that epicatechin is so important that it should be considered a vitamin.

Currently, there are only 13 essential vitamins. An increase in the number of vitamins would provide significant opportunity for nutritional companies to expand their range of products. **Flavanols like epicatechin are removed for commercial cocoas because they tend to have a bitter taste. So there is huge scope for nutritional companies to develop epicatechin supplements or capsules .

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120302132259.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/03/120302132259.htm)

Cocoa May Enhance Skeletal Muscle Function

ScienceDaily (Mar. 2, 2012) — A small clinical trial led by researchers at UC San Diego School of Medicine and VA San Diego Healthcare System (VASDHS) found that patients with advanced heart failure and type 2 diabetes showed improved mitochondrial structure after three months of treatment with epicatechin-enriched cocoa. Epicatechin is a flavonoid found in dark chocolate.

**"The cristae had been severely damaged and decreased in quantity in these patients," said one of the senior investigators, Francisco J. Villarreal, MD, PhD of UC San Diego's Department of Medicine's Division of Cardiology. "After three months, we saw recovery -- cristae numbers back toward normal levels, and increases in several molecular indicators involved in new mitochondria production."**

[http://jp.physoc.org/content/early/2...09924.abstract](http://http://jp.physoc.org/content/early/2...09924.abstract)

(-)-EPICATECHIN ENHANCES FATIGUE RESISTANCE AND OXIDATIVE CAPACITY IN MOUSE MUSCLE

The flavanol (-)-epicatechin, a component of cacao (cocoa), has been shown to have multiple health benefits in humans. Using one year old male mice we examined the effects of 15 days of (-)-epicatechin treatment and regular exercise on: 1) exercise performance; 2) muscle fatigue; 3) capillarity; and 4) mitochondrial biogenesis in mouse hindlimb and heart muscles. Twenty-five male mice (C57BL/6N) were randomized into four groups: 1) water; 2) water-exercise (W Ex); 3) (-)- epicatechin ((-)-Epi); and 4) (-)-epicatechin-exercise ((-)-Epi Ex). Animals received 1 mg∙kg 1 of (-)-epicatechin or water (vehicle) via oral gavage (twice daily). Exercise groups underwent 15 days of treadmill exercise. Significant increases in treadmill performance (≈50%) and enhanced in situ muscle fatigue resistance (≈30%) were observed with (-)-epicatechin. Component of oxidative phosphorylation complexes, mitofilin, porin, and Tfam as well as mitochondrial volume and cristae abundance were significantly higher with (-)-epicatechin treatment for hindlimb and cardiac muscles than exercise alone. In addition, there were significant increases in skeletal muscle capillarity. The combination of (-)-epicatechin and exercise resulted in further increases in oxidative phosphorylation complexes proteins, mitofilin, porin, and capillarity than (-)-epicatechin alone. These findings indicate that (-)-epicatechin _alone_ or in combination with exercise induces an integrated response that includes structural and metabolic changes in skeletal and cardiac muscles resulting in greater endurance capacity. These results, therefore, warrant the further evaluation of the underlying mechanism of action of (-)-epicatechin and its potential clinical application as an exercise mimetic.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-17T15:23:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-24859>

Mars corporation has had a team in the Amazon working on cocoa and heart ailments for 4 years now. There is a HG tribe there that has had no reported heart attack in over 400 yrs and all they do is put pure cocoa beans in all the water they drink. That is why Mars is there studying them.

### chocolate — 2012-05-17T15:25:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-24861>

[http://jp.physoc.org/content/early/2011/07/24/jphysiol.2011.209924.abstract](http://http://jp.physoc.org/content/early/2011/07/24/jphysiol.2011.209924.abstract)

(-)-EPICATECHIN ENHANCES FATIGUE RESISTANCE AND OXIDATIVE CAPACITY IN MOUSE MUSCLE

The flavanol (-)-epicatechin, a component of cacao (cocoa), has been shown to have multiple health benefits in humans. Using one year old male mice we examined the effects of 15 days of (-)-epicatechin treatment and regular exercise on: 1) exercise performance; 2) muscle fatigue; 3) capillarity; and 4) mitochondrial biogenesis in mouse hindlimb and heart muscles. Twenty-five male mice (C57BL/6N) were randomized into four groups: 1) water; 2) water-exercise (W Ex); 3) (-)- epicatechin ((-)-Epi); and 4) (-)-epicatechin-exercise ((-)-Epi Ex). Animals received 1 mg∙kg 1 of (-)-epicatechin or water (vehicle) via oral gavage (twice daily). Exercise groups underwent 15 days of treadmill exercise. Significant increases in treadmill performance (≈50%) and enhanced in situ muscle fatigue resistance (≈30%) were observed with (-)-epicatechin. Component of oxidative phosphorylation complexes, mitofilin, porin, and Tfam as well as mitochondrial volume and cristae abundance were significantly higher with (-)-epicatechin treatment for hindlimb and cardiac muscles than exercise alone. In addition, there were significant increases in skeletal muscle capillarity. The combination of (-)-epicatechin and exercise resulted in further increases in oxidative phosphorylation complexes proteins, mitofilin, porin, and capillarity than (-)-epicatechin alone. **These findings indicate that (-)-epicatechin _alone_ _or in combination with exercise induces an integrated response that includes structural and metabolic changes in skeletal and cardiac muscles resulting in greater endurance capacity. These results, therefore, warrant the further evaluation of the underlying mechanism of action of (-)-epicatechin and its potential clinical application as an exercise mimetic._**

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-17T15:36:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-24864>

Cocoa beans in water, sounds like a good idea

### chocolate — 2012-05-17T15:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-24868>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120509123858.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120509123858.htm)

Soybeans Soaked in Warm Water Naturally Release Key Cancer-Fighting Substance

Bean

Hari B. Krishnan and colleagues explain that the substance, Bowman-Birk Protease Inhibitor (BBI), has shown promise for preventing certain forms of cancer in clinical trials. Those human tests resulted from evidence of BBI's beneficial effects, including indications that BBI derived from the large amounts of soybeans in traditional Japanese diets might underpin low cancer mortality rates in Japan. However, the current method of extracting BBI from soybeans is time-consuming and involves harsh chemicals. The scientists set out to see if there might be a greener and more environmentally friendly way of obtaining BBI.

**They found that _soybean seeds incubated in water at 122 degrees Fahrenheit naturally release large amounts of BBI_ that can easily be harvested from the water. The protein appeared to be active, with tests showing that it stopped breast cancer cells from dividing in a laboratory dish. "The abundance of BBI in soybean seed exudates by incubating the seeds in warm water provides a simple and alternative method to isolate this low molecular weight protein," the researchers said.

The scientists acknowledge funding from the Agricultural Research Service of the U.S. Department of Agriculture.

I wonder if that's the magic temperature?

[http://swfrec.ifas.ufl.edu/docs/pdf/vegetable\_hort/trans\_ds2.pdf](http://http://swfrec.ifas.ufl.edu/docs/pdf/vegetable_hort/trans_ds2.pdf)

**Growers who are interested in a "chemical-free" alternative to fungicide treatment might want to try a hot-water soak. This

technique may be used on tomato seeds and cabbage seeds just before planting to destroy many diseases causing microorganisms

carried on and within seeds. Seeds should be soaked at 122 degrees F for 25 minutes. Use a large quantity of water with some type of

agitator to keep the water temperature as uniform as possible. Note: water temperatures and treatment times in excess of that

recommended may kill the embryo or reduce the percentage of seed germination. **Lower temperatures will not kill the disease

microorganisms. Immediately after this heat treatment, seed should be plunged into cold water to reduce the post treatment “cooking

effect”, then removed and planted promptly.**

Seed Treatments

### chocolate — 2012-07-29T17:53:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epicatechin-its-whats-in-chocolate-and-lots-of-other-things.1516/post-41795>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/11/101110141247.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2010/11/101110141247.htm)

> Why Chocolate Protects Against Heart Disease
>
>
>
> ScienceDaily (Nov. 12, 2010) â€” Numerous studies have shown that cocoa has a protective effect against cardiovascular diseases. The reason for this has now been uncovered by researchers at LinkÃ¶ping University in Sweden. When a group of volunteers devoured a good-sized piece of dark chocolate, it inhibited an enzyme in their bodies that is known to raise blood pressure.
>
>
>
> The findings, now being published in Journal of Cardiovascular Pharmacology, were revealed by a group of drug researchers headed by Ingrid Persson. "We have previously shown that green tea inhibits the enzyme ACE, which is involved in the body's fluid balance and blood pressure regulation. Now we wanted to study the effect of cocoa, since the active substances catechins and procyanidines are related," says Ingrid Persson.
>
>
>
> The researchers recruited 16 healthy volunteer subjects for the study. They were not tobacco users and were not allowed to take any pharmaceuticals for two weeks. During the last two days they were not allowed to eat chocolate or anything containing similar compounds, including many kinds of berries and fruits, nor could they drink coffee, tea, or wine.
>
>
>
> When the study took place, everyone in the group -- ten men and six women between the ages of 20 and 45 -- ate 75 grams of unsweetened chocolate with a cocoa content of 72 percent. To analyze what happened with the ACE enzyme, blood samples were taken in advance and then a half hour, one hour, and three hours afterward.
>
>
>
> **In the sample taken three hours afterward, there was a significant inhibition of ACE activity. The average was 18 percent lower activity than before the dose of cocoa, fully comparable to the effect of drugs that inhibit ACE and are used as a first-choice treatment for high blood pressure.**
>
>
>
> When the activities of the enzyme decline, the blood pressure goes down with time. As expected, no such effect was found in the subjects. To show this, the study would have to continue over a longer period.
>
>
>
> Even though Ingrid Persson is a drug researcher, the object of her studies is not to design new pharmaceuticals.
>
>
>
> "Our findings indicate that changes in lifestyle with the help of foods that contain large concentrations of catechins and procyaninides prevent cardiovascular diseases," she says.

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/08/110829070555.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/08/110829070555.htm)

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120531200822.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/05/120531200822.htm)

# Thread: Cure for a cold?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cold.1517/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### trishalinn@gmail.com — 2012-05-17T16:51:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cold.1517/post-24883>

I think I saw on here somewhere some ideas for fighting off sickness. I think it may have involved vitamin d or garlic.

Anyhow, im afraid my two babies and I are coming down with a cold. Theyve been congested and coughing the last couple of days. I woke up with a nasty sore throat today.

Any ideas, for me? For them? We leave on vacation Saturday morning and I want us all to feel well. Help!

### JC2K — 2012-05-17T17:36:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cold.1517/post-24907>

Vitamin D's been it for me. Ever since I started boosting my D last year, I've been fairly bulletproof (was in the teens when I started supplementing). I work in an office with several hundred people on my floor, and they tend to pass the creeping crud around from their kids fairly often. You hear the coughing, wheezing, and sneezing in stereo sometimes. Same with travel, used to always get something after business trips cross country. Hasn't happened since. I've been taking pretty much taking 10k iu D3 a day since last year, my last blood test for D was 73. I'm aiming to get it up to 80 before I cut down to 5k iu a day and see how it stabilizes.

Which doesn't necessarily help you in your situation though, since you're already coming down with something. Probably wouldn't hurt, but I don't know if it would help that fast. I've tried garlic before, didn't really notice a difference in the outcomes, and it was rough on the ole gastrointestinal tract. Not sure how it would work with the babies either.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-05-18T21:30:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cure-for-a-cold.1517/post-25289>

> **Trisha;24965 wrote:**
>
> Anyhow, im afraid my two babies and I are coming down with a cold. Theyve been congested and coughing the last couple of days. I woke up with a nasty sore throat today.
>
>
>
> Any ideas, for me? For them? We leave on vacation Saturday morning and I want us all to feel well. Help!

Hi Trisha,

I know how scary it is to have a sick baby. But two! Here is what I do for my girls along with a nourishing traditional diet. I don't know if you are open to trying homeopathy, but I use Aconite 30c at the earliest stages of colds. Works very well with young children and is safe:

[http://eatkamloops.org/healthy-household-comfort-for-colds/](http://http://eatkamloops.org/healthy-household-comfort-for-colds/)

I hope you have a healthy holiday.

Cheers,

Caroline

# Thread: Sugar Can Make You Dumb Warn Scientists
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-can-make-you-dumb-warn-scientists.1518/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### skywaykate — 2012-05-17T18:26:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-can-make-you-dumb-warn-scientists.1518/post-24930>

[http://news.yahoo.com/sugar-dumb-us-scientists-warn-190918006.html](http://http://news.yahoo.com/sugar-dumb-us-scientists-warn-190918006.html)

> "The DHA-deprived animals were slower, and their brains showed a decline in synaptic activity," said Fernando Gomez-Pinilla, a professor of neurosurgery at the David Geffen School of Medicine at UCLA.
>
>
>
> "Their brain cells had trouble signaling each other, disrupting the rats' ability to think clearly and recall the route they'd learned six weeks earlier."
>
>
>
> A closer look at the rat brains revealed that those who were not fed DHA supplements had also developed signs of resistance to insulin, a hormone that controls blood sugar and regulates brain function.
>
>
>
> "Because insulin can penetrate the blood-brain barrier, the hormone may signal neurons to trigger reactions that disrupt learning and cause memory loss," Gomez-Pinilla said.
>
>
>
> In other words, eating too much fructose could interfere with insulin's ability to regulate how cells use and store sugar, which is necessary for processing thoughts and emotions.
>
>
>
> "Insulin is important in the body for controlling blood sugar, but it may play a different role in the brain, where insulin appears to disturb memory and learning," Gomez-Pinilla said.
>
>
>
> "Our study shows that a high-fructose diet harms the brain as well as the body. This is something new."
>
>
>
> ....
>
>
>
> "Our findings illustrate that what you eat affects how you think," said Gomez-Pinilla.
>
>
>
> "Eating a high-fructose diet over the long term alters your brain's ability to learn and remember information. But adding omega-3 fatty acids to your meals can help minimize the damage."

We already are understanding this here, but I think it also shows that insulin can affect the brain even if it's not from sugar sources, this doesn't say that but you know it's there, it's only a matter of time before scientists study it, and in the meantime, we're living it!

Need to convince my friend who understands wheat isn't good for you, but all those GF mixes she's using aren't any better!

# Thread: Surgery + Antibiotics + Possible Infection?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-antibiotics-possible-infection.1519/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-17T19:15:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-antibiotics-possible-infection.1519/post-24942>

Hi Everyone!

I am scheduled for surgery in a few weeks to remove a wisdom tooth and a dentigerous cyst that developed out of the unerupted wisdom tooth. I know weird, huh? There's a 1% chance that someone would get this and I got it! I eat strict post-reset keto-paleo so no sugar or chance of sugar/grains in my house.I do CT 5-6 days a week for 45 min in 55-60 degree water. Have Hashimotos (take T3), a little hormonal imbalance (ED) and an hsCRP of around 2.0.

The doc has prescribed Keflex (Cephalexin) for any potential infection after the surgery....he wanted to script Penicillin but I'm allergic to it. I hate antibiotics and have been working on candida for over a month now and don't want to destroy and gains I have made.

My questions are:

1) Do I really need to take it?

2) If I take it what probiotics should I take (any particular strains or brands that are good)?

3) What about iodine? Has anyone used this as an antibiotic/antibacterial/antiviral med on a surgery site?

Thanks for your help!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-17T19:26:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-antibiotics-possible-infection.1519/post-24943>

Why not avoid the keflex and just use peridex mouthrinse. Likely a lot better for oral coverage and wont screw your gut up? Ask the surgeon.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-17T19:43:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/surgery-antibiotics-possible-infection.1519/post-24947>

Thank you Dr. Kruse! I'll ask the surgeon what he thinks.

I have been CTing the jaw area twice a day and will continue after surgery too. I'm hoping to NOT have to use the Percocet at all because of the ice.

# Thread: probiotics for dry eyes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-for-dry-eyes.1522/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-17T23:00:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/probiotics-for-dry-eyes.1522/post-25003>

[http://www.eyeworld.org/article.php?sid=4964](http://http://www.eyeworld.org/article.php?sid=4964)

Investigators diluted Lactobacillus acidophilus into a saline solution to create the eye-drop solution.

Symptoms in all six patients who completed the study (one patient dropped out) were significantly improved at two weeks and four weeks. Clinical signs were also significantly better after four weeks (although not at the two-week mark). The impression cytology, which was performed in three patients, showed a down regulation of ICAM-1 and TLR-4 in two patients. **â€œWe were fairly surprised by the fact that every patient, regardless of statistically significant clinical improvement, described a positive effect of Lactobacilli eye drops on their symptoms, and none of them reported discomfort or the occurrence of side effects,â€￾ Dr. Iovieno said. He and fellow investigators are now analyzing the effects of prolonged oral consumption of Lactobacilli for more mild forms of eye allergies.

A number of allergy studies, some of which also track eye symptoms, are finding good results associated with probiotic use. A review article published in the December 2008 issue of Annals of Allergy, Asthma, and Immunology analyzed 12 randomized controlled trials that studied the effects of probiotics in allergic rhinitis and asthma. **â€œNine of the 12 randomized controlled trails that evaluated clinical outcomes in allergic rhinitis showed an improvement due to the use of probiotics,â€￾ wrote lead investigator Harissios Vliagoftis, M.D., Department of Medicine, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta. â€œAll the \[trials\] that studied perennial allergic rhinitis showed lower symptom scoring with the use of probiotics compared with placebo. Also, five of the eight \[trials\] that referred to seasonal allergic rhinitis suggested an improvement in clinical outcomes,â€￾ he wrote. Four of the trials reviewed also tracked ocular allergy symptoms; all four of those studies reported decreased eye symptoms after probiotic use.** Although Dr. Vliagoftis and co-investigators wrote that the mechanism of action for probiotics in allergy** is not entirely clear, they may have an effect on immune regulation by altering the composition of gut flora. Others have postulated a connection to the so-called â€œhygiene hypothesis,â€￾ which is based on research that finds children exposed to more microbes earlier in life actually have a lower incidence of allergy and asthma than children who are exposed to fewer microbes.

# Thread: Quercetin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quercetin.1527/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-18T03:24:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quercetin.1527/post-25048>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22438067](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/22438067)

Quercetin increased bioavailability and decreased methylation of green tea polyphenols in vitro and in vivo.

The extensive methylation of green tea polyphenols (GTPs) in vivo may limit their chemopreventive potential. We investigated whether quercetin, a natural inhibitor of catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) and multidrug resistance proteins (MRPs), will differentially increase the intracellular concentration and decrease the methylation of GTPs in different cancer cell lines. Intrinsic COMT activity was lowest in lung cancer A549 cells, intermediate in kidney 786-O cells and highest in liver HepG2 cells. Quercetin increased the cellular absorption of epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) four-fold in A549 cells with a decreased methylation rate from 63 to 19%, 2-fold in 786-O cells with a decreased methylation from 97% to 56%, while no significant effect was observed in HepG2 cells. The combination significantly decreased the activity and protein expression of COMT and decreased the protein expression of MRP1 compared to individual treatments. The combination exhibited the strongest increase in antiproliferation in A549 cells, an intermediate effect in 786-O cells and lowest effect in HepG2 cells. The effect of quercetin on bioavailability and metabolism of GTPs was confirmed in vivo. Severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID) mice were administered brewed green tea (GT) and a diet supplemented with 0.4% quercetin alone or in combination for 2 weeks. We observed a 2- to 3-fold increase of total and non-methylated EGCG in lung and kidney and an increasing trend in liver. In summary, combining quercetin with GT provides a promising approach to enhance the chemoprevention of GT. Responses of different cancers to the combination may vary by tissue depending on the intrinsic COMT and MRP activity.

### chocolate — 2012-11-25T18:45:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/quercetin.1527/post-65592>

http://www.hindawi.com/journals/jbb/2009/419891/

The Phytoestrogen Quercetin Impairs Steroidogenesis and Angiogenesis in Swine Granulosa Cells In Vitro

Â 

AbstractExperimental evidence documents that nutritional phytoestrogens may interact with reproductive functions but the exact mechanism of action is still controversial. Since quercetin is one of the main flavonoids in livestock nutrition, we evaluated its possible effects on cultured swine granulosa cell proliferation, steroidogenesis, and redox status. Moreover, since angiogenesis is essential for follicle development, the effect of the flavonoid on VascularÂ  Endothelial Growth FactorÂ  output by granulosa cells was also taken into account. **Our data evidence that quercetin does not affect granulosa cell growth while it inhibits progesterone production and modifies estradiol
Â 
Â Â  Â Â Â  Â 
Â Â  Â Â Â  Â Â Â  Â 

Â Â  Â Â Â  Â 
Â Â  Â 
Â **production in a dose-related manner.** Additionally, the flavonoid interferes with the angiogenic process by inhibiting VEGF production as well as by altering redox status. Since steroidogenesis and angiogenesis are strictly involved in follicular development, these findings appear particularly relevant, pointing out a possible negative influence of quercetin on ovarian physiology.** Therefore, the possible reproductive impact ofÂ  the flavonoidÂ  should be carefully considered in animal nutrition.

### Eve — 2012-05-18T04:47:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-taking-so-long.1528/post-25091>

Why is it taking so long for my leaky gut to heal?

I've been on GAPS intro more or less since Feb 2011. Before that I was gluten free for 2 years (but tried restarting it in Fall 2010 and failed) and was trying my best to be corn-free until I realized that corn is in nearly everything.

I've been doing the Leptin Reset since Jan 2012, with only one cheat late Jan (several glasses of wine one weekend). For the past couple of months, I have been doing CT face-dunking (1st month 2-3 times a week) and spot CTing on my stomach every day or every other day for the past 3 weeks. I've tried taking a bath a couple of times with a wool hat but it was pretty unpleasant even at 65 degrees.

In the past month, I've been consistently getting glutathione IV once a week, although it's expensive. I've been taking glutamine powder since Feb but reacted to all other supplements.

Since adding CT my sleep is better. And in the past month, my skin has been consistently smooth. After starting GAPS last year, my skin has a nice glow to it. Headaches are less and reactions to food intolerances are not as bad but still debilitating.

However, now to the not so good stuff...My leaky gut is terrible! And I fear that it is never going to get better. It seems that I react to everything -- gluten, corn derivatives, all grains, dairy, nuts, chocolate, coconut butter etc....And if it's not that either everything has corn in it (corn derivatives) or I react to it because it's not organic. I would kill for some bacon or sausages but can't find a brand even natural ones that I don't react to. Plus I'm sensitive to fragrances -- makes me feel nauseous. I tried bovine colostrum and was repulsed by the taste. I was taking probiotics since Sep 2011 but realized a couple of days ago that they are not gluten-free and that I was having a reaction to them.

I'm going to make some sauerkraut soon though to supplement.

I've also more or less weighed the same the entire 5 months on Leptin Reset and I need to lose at least 25lbs. I did start losing weight after I removed the poultry I was reacting to but then I tried some 100% chocolate and nuts this week and am now almost 10lbs up.

My doctor won't test my estrogen or progesterone levels. I'm not overly concerned about my hormones because my last month's period was finally regular and mostly cramp free (vs. my previously heavy ones). My HS-CRP was 1.6 last month...which I would think would be higher because of all my leaky gut probs. My O6:O3 ratio was 10.8:1...but I tested at a time I was reacting to some poultry that likely had some corn in it or wasn't truly organic. Once I took the poultry out, my soft skin returned.

I was on T4 meds for my thyroid since Feb 2008 but last month I convinced my doc to switch me to natural dessicated thyroid. When I was on T4 meds, my labs last month were 0.03TSH and free t3 bottom of range and free t4 middle of range.

Is there any hope for me? I can't even give up because I can't eat anything else and have to eat only meat and veggies by default. Yes, this is a post out of desperation.

This is what I eat on a daily basis

breakfast: 6-8 eggs fried in coconut oil and ~1/2 cup organic, free-range ground pork or beef

late lunch/dinner: local zucchini/broccoli and garlic stir-fry in coconut oil and salmon or shrimp

And yes, I have cravings usually all the time for desserts and pizza. I'm prone to binging on foods that I react to. For example, I caved and had some nut butter (which I'm sensitive to) this week and ate nearly all the jar in one sitting.

I am going to check that kyolic garlic out and I also have some olive leaf extract I am going to start up again. I also may ask my doctor about Nyastin (sp?), which is a prescription anti-fungal that is recommended by the GAPS doctors. My doctor is also going to refer me to an internal medicine doctor.

### hazyjane — 2012-05-18T21:11:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-taking-so-long.1528/post-25282>

I'd ditch the eggs for a few weeks. They tend to be an issue for leaky gut.

Here are some supplements that will fast-track leaky gut healing: Sea Cure whitefish peptides, N-Acetyl Glucosamine (NAG), L-Reuteri (a specific probiotic strain that rebuilds the villi and gut lining) and possibly cat's claw (also antifungal and immune boosting. Nutricology and Saventaro are good brands). Stay on these for at least 2 months. I was able to reverse a ton of food sensitivities using these.

Candida doesn't always cause skin or vaginal symptoms. It often causes bloating, headaches, brain fog, etc.

If candida is an issue, I also recommend a month of Candex enzymes. They work faster than antifungals do and some strains of yeast are so resistant that they're really hard to kill with antifungals. Candex doesn't work via an antifungal mechanism; it's an enzyme that dissolves the cell wall of the yeast and implodes it. The yeast isn't able to become resistant. It's good to take an immune booster like cat's claw along with it to help the suppressed immune system to recover and be able to keep the yeast from coming back (it's always in us- it's just a matter of keeping it balanced with good bacteria and a healthy immune system).

If someone is having a really hard time killing yeast, consider a heavy metal cleanse. A naturopath I know discovered that all of his difficult yeast patients had a lot of heavy metals and when they were removed, the yeast cleared easily. The best product for heavy metals that I know of is HMD Heavy Metal Detox.

Great stuff, Hazy Jane. Thanks! I just saw a Func Med doc and she diagnosed me with leaky gut (it wasn't a huge leap) but I wasn't really thrilled with the medicinal food that she wanted me to take - Metagenics Ultra Clear Sustain. It has like 10g of sugar per serving and like 34 carbs. I do NOT want to take it and was going to have to research alternatives - SO THANK YOU.

I also really like how she explained the connection between leaky gut and AF. Basically, if your gut is leaky, your body is under constant attack and your adrenals will be working over time.

My understanding about eggs is that it's the white part that people react to because that is the part of the egg that has the "defense mechanism" in it. Here is a nice article on eggs -

[http://paleodietlifestyle.com/an-egg-yolk-a-day-keeps-the-doctor-away/](http://http://paleodietlifestyle.com/an-egg-yolk-a-day-keeps-the-doctor-away/)

This part:

Unlike most animals, eggs can’t defend themselves by bitting or escaping and have to rely on some of the same mechanisms that plants, grains and seeds use to survive. The white is solely responsible for this part while the yolk is busy becoming a full-fledged animal. 13 of the 14 proteins found in the white have anti-microbial properties. This usually deters microbes and viruses, but can also affect us, especially when consumed in high quantity or when our gut health is already compromised.

The protein lysozyme, which is usually harmless, can bind with proteins like ovomucoid or ovoinhibitor and inhibit trypsin, a protease enzyme, from doing its job of digesting proteins. Even more insidious, some of the compounds can pass through the intestinal wall and can agravate autoimmune problems or damaged guts. This is why egg whites are best avoided when dealing with autoimmune problems.

For example, with Bubbies I experienced my usual "corned" signs -- addiction to the food, headaches, extreme brain fog and fatigue, urinary frequency and insomnia. ### Eve — 2012-05-20T18:34:29+0300

I tried the Enzymedia Dig Enzymes too cause I thought they were corn-free. At first they seemed to help and then my reactions accumulated. It's possible you don't react to the Bubbies -- sometimes people with corn sensitivities only react to certain corn derivatives.

On the other hand, I find that if I've been corned by a certain food for days and days and have started to get addicted to it, I eventually start to get repulsed by it. Not sure if that applies to you either.

As for the L-glutamine...I guess that's out too  I did start taking colostrum again and it's not as bad I remembered the first time...well this time, I dilute in a whole glass of water rather than just a little. The past couple of days I've also started Olive Leaf extract before bed and both days I've woken up at 11:30am (yikes!). Hopefully that means it's working.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-05-20T23:45:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-taking-so-long.1528/post-25831>

> **Eve;25173 wrote:** Why is it taking so long for my leaky gut to heal?
>
>
>
> I've been on GAPS intro more or less since Feb 2011. Before that I was gluten free for 2 years (but tried restarting it in Fall 2010 and failed) and was trying my best to be corn-free until I realized that corn is in nearly everything.
>
> I've been doing the Leptin Reset since Jan 2012, with only one cheat late Jan (several glasses of wine one weekend). For the past couple of months, I have been doing CT face-dunking (1st month 2-3 times a week) and spot CTing on my stomach every day or every other day for the past 3 weeks. I've tried taking a bath a couple of times with a wool hat but it was pretty unpleasant even at 65 degrees.

Hi Eve,

I just listened to this lecture by Dr Natasha Campbell-McBride on mercola.com. I found it very good:

[http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2012/05/12/dr-campbell-mcbride-on-gaps.aspx?e\_cid=20120520\_WNL\_art\_1](http://http://articles.mercola.com/sites/articles/archive/2012/05/12/dr-campbell-mcbride-on-gaps.aspx?e_cid=20120520_WNL_art_1)

Eating more fermented foods might be helpful. If at all possible make your own fermented foods. If you make your own fermented foods you will get "wild culture" from your local area. It will help your immune system better than store bought fermented foods that are coming from God knows where.

Corn is very damaging for many people on SCD/GAPS. I will get massive blood blisters in my mouth after have popcorn. It makes no difference if the corn is organic and GMO free. My great grandfather used to say: "if the food does not agree with you, leave it alone." Good advice for everyone.

Cheers,

Caroline

### PaleoCowgirl — 2012-05-21T04:50:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-is-this-taking-so-long.1528/post-25886>

> **Jennifer;25370 wrote:** Great stuff, Hazy Jane. Thanks! I just saw a Func Med doc and she diagnosed me with leaky gut (it wasn't a huge leap) but I wasn't really thrilled with the medicinal food that she wanted me to take - Metagenics Ultra Clear Sustain. It has like 10g of sugar per serving and like 34 carbs. I do NOT want to take it and was going to have to research alternatives - SO THANK YOU.
>
>
>
> I also really like how she explained the connection between leaky gut and AF. Basically, if your gut is leaky, your body is under constant attack and your adrenals will be working over time.
>
> My understanding about eggs is that it's the white part that people react to because that is the part of the egg that has the "defense mechanism" in it. Here is a nice article on eggs -
>
> [http://paleodietlifestyle.com/an-egg-yolk-a-day-keeps-the-doctor-away/](http://http://paleodietlifestyle.com/an-egg-yolk-a-day-keeps-the-doctor-away/)
>
> This part:
>
> Unlike most animals, eggs canâ€™t defend themselves by bitting or escaping and have to rely on some of the same mechanisms that plants, grains and seeds use to survive. The white is solely responsible for this part while the yolk is busy becoming a full-fledged animal. 13 of the 14 proteins found in the white have anti-microbial properties. This usually deters microbes and viruses, but can also affect us, especially when consumed in high quantity or when our gut health is already compromised.
>
>
>
> The protein lysozyme, which is usually harmless, can bind with proteins like ovomucoid or ovoinhibitor and inhibit trypsin, a protease enzyme, from doing its job of digesting proteins. Even more insidious, some of the compounds can pass through the intestinal wall and can agravate autoimmune problems or damaged guts. This is why egg whites are best avoided when dealing with autoimmune problems.

I did two rounds of the UltraClear Sustain and found it did help me somewhat. I was not absorbing any nutrients for so long, and the mix finally gave me some much needed nutrition. You're right though -- huge downfall with the carbs and sugars. I didn't finish the second round because I started CT in February, and it felt like such a contradiction. I may finish it out this summer if I add more carbs into the mix.

There is an enzyme product HISTAME which can help breakdown histamine, but I'm staying away from the high histamine foods and oxalates for awhile and will try again later in the year after giving my gut a break. Can't do eggs at the moment either.

# Thread: chelation for cataracts
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chelation-for-cataracts.1532/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-18T16:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chelation-for-cataracts.1532/post-25161>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21271438](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/21271438)

Topical metal chelation therapy ameliorates oxidation-induced toxicity in diabetic cataract.

Zhang M, Shoeb M, Liu P, Xiao T, Hogan D, Wong IG, Campbell GA, Ansari NH.

Source

Department of Biochemistry & Molecular Biology, University of Texas Medical Branch, Galveston, Texas 77555-0647, USA.

Abstract

Oxidative stress plays a critical role in cataractogenesis, the leading cause of blindness worldwide. Since transition metals generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation, metal chelation therapy has been proposed for treatment of cataracts. However, the effectiveness of most chelators is limited by low tissue penetrability. **This study is the first to demonstrate **that the topically applied divalent metal chelator ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) combined with the carrier and _permeability enhancer methyl sulfonyl methane (MSM)_** ameliorates both oxidation-induced lens opacification and the associated toxic accumulation of protein-4-hydroxynonenal (HNE) adducts.** Both in vitro (rat lens culture) and in vivo (diabetic rats), EDTA-MSM (1) significantly reduced lens opacification by about 40-50%, (2) significantly diminished lens epithelial cell proliferation and fiber cell swelling in early stages of cataract formation in vivo, and (3) notably decreased the levels of protein-HNE adducts. These findings have important implications specifically for the treatment of cataract and generally for other diseases in which oxidative stress plays a key pathogenic role.

PMID:

21271438

\[PubMed - indexed for MEDLINE\]

### chocolate — 2012-06-24T02:08:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chelation-for-cataracts.1532/post-33232>

> Prologue
>
>
>
> Dr. Marios Kyriazis brings a wealth of medical expertise to one of the most important breakthroughs in recent times to help combat a disease called ‘senile cataract,’ a disease that affects one in five people over the age of 55.
>
>
>
> In the past, the only treatment available for this chronic condition was surgery, which by its very nature can lead to serious complications. We must also consider that the replacement of a natural lens with a plastic one leads to a loss in accommodation; in brief a plastic lens is not as good as a natural one.
>
>
>
> **But now other non-surgical treatments are available, in particular the Russian development of N-acetyl-carnosine. In clinical trials this eye-drop treatment has shown dramatic and impressive results in treating age related cataracts.**
>
>
>
> Furthermore, The Cataract Cure also covers other associated eye disorders including;
>
>
>
> Glaucoma
>
> Macular degeneration
>
> Infection of the cornea
>
> Dry eye syndrome
>
> Computer vision syndrome
>
> Eyestrain
>
> Blurred vision
>
>
>
> The Cataract Cure is a resource for all persons interested in knowing about alternative approaches to eye care, approaches that are still proven, scientific and based in clinical experience.
>
>
>
> Disclaimer
>
>
>
> The information contained within this book is based on the experience and research of the author. It is not intended to substitute for a medical consultation and any attempt to diagnose or treat a disease should only be done under the

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-20T14:58:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-the-clueless.1536/post-25666>

"an explanation for the way things are" it is. Believe me when I say Factor X is irrelevant for present life as it is now (not that it wouldn't be different if it hasn't been, and clearly it has been a special key in the puzzle for Dr. Kruse) and somewhat _disappointing (I am not the only one to even have talked about it without pointing it out with two letters)... not so its _consequences (I am not completely convinced about the adjective_ here, though) nowadays:

-   Much higher quantity of junk DNA on eutherians than in birds (I only have a comment in [this entry](http://http://scienceblogs.com/pharyngula/2010/05/junk_dna_is_still_junk.php)): yes, birds are quite more perfect

_

-   , with their superior mitochondria, but it seems that we eutherians have won being more adaptable.
-   Retrotransposons and its great abundance in primates: see for example [this paper](http://http://rcordaux.voila.net/pdfs/42.pdf).
    Junk DNA coming from viruses: does primates with their [GULO mutation](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L-gulonolactone_oxidase) are more susceptible to increase their junk DNA because of it? I know, I know... it would close the circle for me (and I am completely convinced of the [antiviral potency of vitamin C in high doses](http://http://perfecthealthdiet.com/2010/09/fighting-viral-infections-by-vitamin-c-at-bowel-tolerance/)). It is a quite testable hypothesis though: if frugivorous bats and guinea pigs had a higher amount of junk DNA than the rest of eutherians (like primates do), it would point toward it.

Just as Dr. Kruse says, we will have a lot to talk about the implications of the epigenetic speed up.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-21T11:59:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-the-clueless.1536/post-25913>

> **Jack Kruse;25920 wrote:** I think reptiles survived because they were bruminating in the fall and winter also underground.

Yes, that makes sense for small reptiles.

Nevertheless non-aquatic big ones seem to have survived mostly on the southern hemisphere, just as Komodo dragons (it must have helped that they can [persist as a species just from a single female](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parthenogenesis)).

All of the present Crocodilia order seem to have span from marine predecessors ([look at salt glands](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Crocodilia#Teeth_and_jaws)), so it is inconclusive where their ancestors survive the K/T event.

I am a completely amateur on these biology themes so perhaps my impression is wrong, though.

### AndrÃ©s — 2012-05-22T12:31:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-the-clueless.1536/post-26291>

I write a new comment so it shows as new.

Either [wikipedia page about the K/T event](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event) is completely wrong or you should reevaluate your secondary premises (not that it matters for the consequences you have exposed):

-   [Terrestial plants](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event#Terrestrial_plants):

    In high southern hemisphere latitudes, such as New Zealand and Antarctica the mass die-off of flora caused no significant turnover in species, but dramatic and short-term changes in the relative abundance of plant groups.\[[Amphibians](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event#Amphibians):

    There is no evidence of Kâ€“T boundary mass extinctions of amphibians, and there is strong evidence that most amphibians survived the event relatively unscathed.\[[Birds](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event#Birds):

    The only bird group which can confidently be said to have survived the Kâ€“T boundary are the Neornithines (though one Paleogene species, Qinornis paleocenica, may represent a surviving non-neornithine bird).\[[Mammals](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event#Mammals):

    All major Cretaceous mammalian lineages, including monotremes (egg-laying mammals), multituberculates, marsupials and placentals, dryolestoideans,\[[Continental water, rivers and/or lakes must have been](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cretaceous%E2%80%93Paleogene_extinction_event#Extinction_patterns):

    Mammals and birds that survived the extinction fed on insects, worms, and snails, which in turn fed on dead plant and animal matter. Scientists hypothesize that these organisms survived the collapse of plant-based food chains because they fed on detritus (non-living organic material).\[7\]\[11\]\[12\]

    In stream communities few animal groups became extinct because stream communities rely less directly on food from living plants and more on detritus that washes in from land, buffering them from extinction.\[13\]​

    I don't dispute placental mammals having evolved on the cold though.​

### chocolate — 2012-08-08T04:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thread-for-the-clueless.1536/post-43571>

Paddlefish's doubled genome may question theories on limb evolution

[http://phys.org/news/2012-08-paddlefish-genome-theories-limb-evolution.html](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-08-paddlefish-genome-theories-limb-evolution.html)

> "We found that paddlefish have had their own genome duplication," said Karen Crow, assistant professor of biology at San Francisco State University. "This creates extra genetic material that adds complexity to comparative studies. It may change the way we interpret studies on limb development." In order to study how human limbs develop, scientists compare the limb-building genes found in mice with fin-building genes found in fishes. Previous research on paddlefish has suggested that fishes possessed the genetic toolkit required to grow limbs long before the evolution of the four-limbed creatures (tetrapods) that developed into reptiles, birds, amphibians and mammals. In the last decade, paddlefish have become a useful benchmark in evolutionary studies because their position on the evolutionary tree makes them a reasonably good proxy for the ancestor of the bony fishes that evolved into tetrapods such as humans. However, the fact that paddlefish underwent a genome duplication could complicate what its genes tell us about the fin-to-limb transition, says Crow. "Our findings suggest that the results of previous studies using paddlefish as a comparative species may need to be re-interpreted," Crow said. Crow and colleagues sequenced chromosomal regions containing 19 Hox genes in the American paddlefish. Hox genes determine body shape and limb development, and have become prime candidates for detecting whole genome duplications. **Whole genome duplications are game-changing events in evolutionary history that give rise to new species or novel features within a species. They occur when a series of unlikely circumstances coincide, resulting in twin copies of every gene. When this happens, one scenario that could take place is that one gene in the pair keeps its designated function while the other is either lost or takes on a new purpose. "This extra genetic material provides the canvas for evolution to paint with," said Crow, who studies the evolution of novelty and diversity.**
>
>
>
> Read more at: [http://phys.org/news/2012-08-paddlefish-genome-theories-limb-evolution.html#jCp](http://http://phys.org/news/2012-08-paddlefish-genome-theories-limb-evolution.html#jCp)

# Thread: Summer, Carbs and CT

**Posts:** 1

### bigknitwit — 2012-05-18T18:46:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/summer-carbs-and-ct.1537/post-25218>

I am confused about how these three things mingle. I have been CTing with a keto paleo diet along with the leptin reset, and have achieved at least some level of cold adaptation (based on minimal shivering post-CT and fully recovered in less than 1 hour). However, I would like to enjoy the upcoming season of summer, including being outside in the hot sun for several hours a day with my kids, and enjoying some of the fruits of summer (the strawberries, sugar snap peas, raspberries, etc, all from my own garden). I imagine I will not maintain ketosis all the time, and probably not most of the time. So, should I continue to do my daily 50 degree CT session? I get that if I want to continue healing, I need to maintain winter (keto diet + cold) until healing is completed. But I already know that won't be possible (I can't stay inside all summer long with 4 kids at home lol!!) I'm wondering if there's any benefit to continuing the cold exposure all summer, or if it may be somehow damaging.

# Thread: San Francisco Ocean Dipping is a Blast!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/san-francisco-ocean-dipping-is-a-blast.1538/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 53
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### curves64 — 2012-05-18T19:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/san-francisco-ocean-dipping-is-a-blast.1538/post-25223>

Anyone interested in joining Rose and I for an Ocean Dip at Aquatic Park this **Saturday (tomorrow, 5/18) at 12:30 pm**? We've been twice already and had a blast. I've checked out other local swim spots but I like Aquatic Park because the area is buoyed off, and there are some people swimming (although we were the only ones in the water without a wet suit!) Both times Rose and I stayed in the water up to our necks for 35-40 minutes laughing the entire time. I know Dr. K's webinar is in the morning, but if you're local, 12:30 should give you enough time to get there. I look forward to meeting you!

[http://www.yelp.com/biz/aquatic-park-san-francisco](http://http://www.yelp.com/biz/aquatic-park-san-francisco)

### JoeBranca — 2012-07-17T22:33:20+0300

### Rosekelly — 2012-07-17T23:09:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/san-francisco-ocean-dipping-is-a-blast.1538/post-38838>

Ah, darn. Next time, go right through town down to the boardwalk and take a right. Park immediately. Meters are free until 10 a.m. I swim right across the street from where I park. You walk past the Dream Inn.

If you want free parking on a weekend after 10 a.m. there, fuggedaboutit. But before 10? The beach is your oyster.

I like this beach because there's no undertow, and it's calm, for the ocean.

### growltgc — 2012-07-23T21:09:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/san-francisco-ocean-dipping-is-a-blast.1538/post-40431>

I've been swimming at ocean beach. after the first minute, it's not that cold. I was surprised. The waves do throw me around a bit, which is kind of interesting, but it makes it so I can't swim that much. The other downside is that the National Park service, whistles you out of the water, if they see you... I play hide and seek, it only happened once to me.

The experience is really nice and, is the next day tomorrow? the bus ride would be worth it to meet other Ct'ers and be free of the waves and the NPS.

### hellojtm — 2012-08-01T04:02:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/san-francisco-ocean-dipping-is-a-blast.1538/post-42183>

I'd be wary of wading in ocean beach, personally. I've done it but I think the rangers are doing a good service because I think the currents are not intuitive there.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Hunger Signals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 33
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-05-18T21:27:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25287>

\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`\`

### ealachan — 2012-05-18T22:01:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25303>

> **Souldanzer;25369 wrote:** Okay, somebody please educate me
>
>
>
> I am the child of an anorexic mother and it is dawning on me what that really means. I have a lifetime of anorexia behind me so far, realizing that I think I never had proper hunger (leptin) signaling. I remember being 5 years old and wondering how somebody could stop eating sweets just like that.
>
>
>
> So, I don't remember any proper satiety cues at all, nope, nothing, zero, blank in my head. I don't feel hunger until I'm starving and even then it's often pretty easy for me to still avoid food for a while. I don't feel satiety until I'm way too full, which results in feeling badly, nauseous, sick to my stomach.
>
>
>
> I'm working on moving the two signals closer together  Eating when I'm hungry, not starving, and stopping when I'm full, not sickly. The stopping too late has nothing to do with binging, I just plain miss the earlier "full" signal.
>
>
>
> I've been making some progress over the last few months, I think. I never outright binge any longer, I never outright starve any longer. I had a few weeks when I was regularly feeling sick after eating and figured out that that meant too much food (duh, I know). So I slowly cut back my portions until I felt okay again. A few weeks later, the same thing now is happening again. I feel sick after eating what doesn't appear \*that\* much to me. I'm assuming it means again that I need less food.... but I'm having a hard time discerning the true satiety signal that comes before I feel sick.
>
>
>
> I need to know what I am looking for here. I noticed a very, very subtle change in taste sensation of the food I'm eating, which right now I'm using as my "stop" signal... but it's very minor and I need to pay a lot of attention while eating to not miss it.
>
>
>
> How do you know you've had enough? How do you know it's time to eat?

This is interesting to me, because I had the same issues with hunger and satiety growing up, but I grew up obese, in a family of other obese people.

One of the most helpful things I've used to help me learn satiety is "mindful eating". My therapist actually got me into mindfulness practices in general, but at one point she handed me a sheet about mindful eating, and it really made sense to me. Basically what you do is, take a bite of food and put your fork / spoon down. Really taste the food, feel it in your mouth. Close your eyes at first, if it helps (it helped me. Husband laughed, of course. Good thing I'm good-natured!). Once you've chewed and swallowed, pick up your fork / spoon, take another bite, and repeat the process. The idea is that not only do you become more mindful of what you're eating, but that eating more slowly gives your saliva more time to begin to break the food down, and possibly start to send signals to your gut and brain as to whether you want more or are full. The first few times I did it, it seriously took me like an hour to eat dinner...but it really helped me. Sometimes I catch myself just mindlessly shoveling food down now and then, and I try really hard to recognize that, put the mental brakes on, and make myself slow down and become more aware.

Just my two cents. Hope it helps.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-05-18T23:57:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25328>

Forgot where I learned this, wasn't in treatment, outpatient 3xweek for 16 weeks, years ago. I think I read this somewhere...

We have physical cues we have to look for, like a shifting/antsy/fidgety thing as we are sitting at our plate (you are sitting to eat, right?)

I usually give my plate a little push, not like across the table, but a barely noticeable push with my thumb. Or I let out a big sigh.

But you have to be looking for it. When I get it, THEN I pick up my plate and pack it up. When I remember, I tell myself I can always eat more later, if I get hungry. Now, eating this way, I tell myself if I get hungry later, I can just make it up at breakfast next morning.

Try to look for physical cues. Observing my family: DD puts her knife and fork on the plate, DH starts chewing very slowly and takes a lot longer in between bites, sometimes he gets up (the fidgety thing) We try NOT to be in the clean plate club. Which is hard, cause that is how I was raised.

### nicebeanst@yahoo.com — 2012-05-19T00:04:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25329>

> **Teenie Leek;25411 wrote:** When I get it, THEN I pick up my plate and pack it up

Meaning, right there I decide I'm done. I don't take anymore bites (well I try anyway LOL) I remember a little bit more now, author was talking about "honoring hunger" and in the same vein, to honor my body's gentle signals that I had enough. Honoring my fullness.

So feel, and LOOK, for tiny things. HTH

A few weeks later, the same thing now is happening again. I feel sick after eating what doesn't appear \*that\* much to me. ### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-19T07:21:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25444>

Souldanzer, I've sort of tried to tackle this issue in reverse. What I mean is, I, too, couldn't figure out my hunger/satiation cues so I worked backwards since going paleo/leptin RX. I started by hearing what others were saying about good amounts to eat in a meal (and of course the Rx with the BAB etc is pretty helpful in terms of that) and then I'd make it and eat it all. If I found I was really hungry before my next meal rolled around, I'd add more to the breakfast the next day. If I found I was too full, I'd reduce it. I think in the early stages I was still eating more than I needed because I wasn't quite clear what 'too full' felt like and I was worried I'd get hungry (and then binge). Now I eat somewhat less overall than I did and I don't have any trouble between meals with hunger. The main sign I'm now aware of it actual fullness in my stomach. I can feel when the stomach appears full and if I eat beyond that it's a feeling of pressure. I think eating slowly is important - it apparently takes 20 minutes for your brain to get the satiety signals from the stomach -but obviously too slowly is more about fear than it is about pacing yourself. I also put a lot of stock in the need to turn off the sympathetic system (fight or flight) and turn on the parasympathetic system (rest and digest) to keep cortisol low and allow for proper digestion. I try to use even just 30 seconds before I start eating to thank the animal who gave its life, to take a few deep breaths, to be grateful for having such a healthy meal. And, even though it is a hard habit to break, I don't listen to the radio or read, etc. If I'm alone, I just eat.

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-20T03:42:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-25620>

Fwiw I think it took me until I'd been on the LR to really identify hunger & satiety cues. Now I can eat a BAB or HAB depending on whats on the agenda for the day and not be hungry until close to 12 hrs later. It took a while to get here though. There are times when I'm detoxing a HAB won't cut it but a BAB will. Stick to the plan and it will fall into place. Many not as fast as some ppl but it will happen. The same for cravings for bad foods Eventually they go away Am I ready to do a 24 hr IF. I don't think so, but if I some how ended up in a situation where there's only crappy food it won't be a problem to IF until I can find an optimal choice. Could I have done this a yr ago. No. It's no longer an impossibility for me. Follow the LR & CT and you'll get there.

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T00:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-30204>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zinc\_deficiency](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zinc_deficiency)

> Hunger
>
>
>
> The influence of zinc on hunger is complex and likely depends upon the status of other nutrients, the developmental stage of the animal, and percentage body fat. Some research groups have argued for a role of zinc deficiency decreasing appetite, while others have shown zinc ingestion can reduce feelings of hunger by increasing leptin levels. There is evidence that the way zinc influences hunger depends on the sodium/osmotic status of the organism, **with low sodium/low zinc levels increasing hunger and high sodium/low zinc levels decreasing **it**. An organism with a low level of zinc has an increased susceptibility to hypoosmotic stress and cell rupture. Thus if the osmotic pressure is too low the organism may be inclined to eat to raise osmolality and prevent osmotic shock. It should be noted that zinc is known to affect osmolality by increasing sodium retention.**
>
>
>
> In rats, the "first visible sign" of zinc deficiency is a decreased appetite.\[28\]

This is a chaotic post, in that it seems to fit with the high altitude stuff. The sodium dumping and decreased appetite have me confused.

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T17:55:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-30284>

[http://www.arltma.com/CandidaDoc.htm](http://http://www.arltma.com/CandidaDoc.htm)

Zinc Deficiency

Zinc metabolism is closely related to Candida because 1) the zinc/copper balance is critical, and 2) zinc is required for many essential enzyme systems, including production of digestive enzymes and synthesis of all body proteins.

A zinc imbalance is indicated on a tissue mineral chart by a zinc level less than 12.0 mgs/% or greater than 20.0 mgs/%, or a zinc/copper ratio greater than 12.0 mgs/%. A phosphorus level greater than 16 or less than 12 may also indicate a zinc imbalance.

Deficiency of zinc is common for several reasons:

•

Use of superphosphate fertilizers and hybrid crops have contributed to widespread zinc deficiency in all foods.

•

Processing and refining further depletes foods of their zinc content. For example, zinc loss occurs in the conversion of whole wheat to white flour, in the conversion of sugar cane to white sugar, and in spraying of frozen and canned vegetables with EDTA to retain color.

•

Foods, relatively low in zinc, such as chicken and fish are being increasingly substituted for higher-zinc foods such as beef and red meats. Soy protein, commonly substituted for beef, is low in zinc.

•

Stress of any type results in zinc depletion.

•

Zinc deficiency is accentuated if copper exposure is high, because of a copper-zinc antagonism. Copper exposure is higher today for several reasons:

– Birth control pills raise tissue copper levels by raising estrogen levels.

– Copper is absorbed from the Copper-7 intrauterine device.

–

Water remaining in copper pipes, and **consumption of high-copper foods such as soy, avocado, and chocolate are sources of copper.**

– Stress causes copper levels to increase, by causing a zinc deficiency.

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hunger-signals.1541/post-30291>

[http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2FBJN%2FBJN62\_02%2FS000711458900125Xa.pdf&code=0799121739d2be1967d2aaa56a4c8e16](http://http://journals.cambridge.org/download.php?file=%2FBJN%2FBJN62_02%2FS000711458900125Xa.pdf&code=0799121739d2be1967d2aaa56a4c8e16)

Effect of experimental zinc deficiency and repletion on sodium,

potassium, copper and iron concentrations in guinea-pigs

BY R.P.GUPTA', P.C.VERMA'\*, J. R. SADANA'AND V. K. GUPTA2

University, Hisar-125004, India

' Department of Veterinary Pathology, Department of Soil Science, Haryana Agricultural

(Received 30 December I988 - Accepted 17 April 1989)

Zinc, sodium, potassium, copper and iron concentrations were analysed in serum and tissues of guineapigs

fed on a diet containing 1.25 mg Zn/kg diet over a period of 60 d. The response of the Zn-deficient

(ZnD) animals to Zn supplementation (100 mg Zn/kg diet) was also studied for 15 d. Serum studies in

the ZnD group revealed significant decreases in the concentrations of Zn and Na from 24 d, and increases

in the concentrations of Fe and K from 36 and 48 d onwards respectively; an increase in Cu was seen

on day 60 only. Zn deficiency caused significant reductions in Na, K and Zn and increases in Cu and Fe

contents of liver and kidney. In testis, significant decreases were noted only in Zn, K and Fe contents.

**Zn supplementation of the previously ZnD group resulted in marked improvements in serum and tissue

mineral levels. However, hepatic Cu and Fe and renal K did not appear to respond appreciably.**

If there are any tests you would recommend, I would gladly let my doctor know. She acts surprised to see me and doesn't hesitate to help all she can.

Cardo CPR 1.0 mg/L

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-20T03:09:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/would-love-help-with-my-lab-tests.1542/post-25614>

Your thyroid tests aren't complete. You need a FT3 and a rT3 (taken at the same time). Your FT. 4 look a little low so I suspect your FT3 will also be low.

Your sex hormones also look a little low. I know you said you are post menopausal and maybe you don't want to add exogenous hormones to your body so take this FWIW. DHEA should be 275-400 and Kruse has said anything below 160 is deficient so you are right there. Testosterone Total s/b 60-76 for optimal. Menopause maybe different though for your sex hormones. I know way more about thyroid than I do hormones I just have a listing of optimized ranges. Hope that helps!

estrone, estradiol, and estriol or E1, E2, and E3

You as woman, require over 10x of what I need in the blood.

Additionally from mine and others experience, there is a load up period and then you need to

You may start easily at 900mg/day

second is cortisol 4%

the remaining 0.5% is everything else.

or

to survive few bad days

long term you must have cortisol made by your own body with just in time delivery

with best possibly tuned Circadian Rhythm.

Start someplace, do blood tests monthly, keep adjusting.

Start with 50mg/day DHEA keep adjusting per blood tests

3 tabs MLM preg

4 hrs latter

100mg 7ketoDHEA

25mg DHEA

### Sem — 2012-05-18T23:21:47+0300

Hi everyone. I've been doing the Leptin Reset for the past few weeks. Over the last few days, I've been waking up with very sore knuckles. It feels sore and hurts a little when I bend my fingers - feels like they are swollen. Do you think this has anything to do with the diet? Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-18T23:24:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25325>

### JC2K — 2012-05-19T00:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25334>

Are you losing a lot of weight on the LR? First thing I think of is inflammation due to detoxing from the fat that's getting used up.

### Sem — 2012-05-19T03:12:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25355>

Sadly, I'm not losing weight on the LR. I am definitely eating more beef that usually do. The only other thing would be coconut oil, butter and heavy whipping cream (just with my coffee in the morning). Also, my knees hurt. So weird.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-19T03:21:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25356>

Maybe you are having a reaction to dairy, which would include the butter and HWC. Dairy can be very inflammatory in people with autoimmune diseases or if you are sensitive to it. Maybe make bulletproof coffee with ghee and whip it up in a blender. It tastes better than coffee w/HWC! Also maybe try CTing your hands. Maybe the swelling will go down. But that probably doesn't stop the cause just helps the symptoms.

### Sem — 2012-05-19T03:40:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25367>

Ugh. That's what I was afraid someone would say. I thought i would be safe because hwc and butter don't have lactose. Too sad.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-19T03:47:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25372>

take some krill oil and quercetin and see how it goes

### Sem — 2012-05-19T04:06:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25382>

Thanks Dr. Kruse. I'll try that.

### BJK77 — 2012-05-19T04:08:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25384>

I have this happen frequently too, but for me it's only one knuckle - the pointer finger on my left hand. It comes and goes and seems totally random. I don't eat any dairy, so that's definitely not it for me. I'll even notice that it turns slightly purple/dark like the vein is popping out! When it happens it's always worse as soon as I wake up and stretch my fingers out . . . so bizarre!

### PrimalPam — 2012-05-19T19:26:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25536>

I've always been a dairy freak, but ever since the LR, the dairy has just moved out of my life, and it's hardly noticeable. I put CO in my coffee, as well as CO milk, turmeric, and stevia. I cook in CO and use CO butter on my veggies. I have no more stiffness in any joints (I was having back issues), and I sleep like a baby. Try to replace your dairy products with CO products. The turmeric is anti-inflammatory, plus it gives my coffee a really nice taste. I also take krill, which I believe, as Dr. Jack says, really helps.

### Sem — 2012-05-20T00:35:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25588>

I think it is the dairy because I am also suddenly gaining weight and it corresponds with my increase in HWC and butter. Boo hoo! I'll try the coconut milk. I bought the coconut creamer without checking the ingredients until I got home. Big mistake. Guar gum and sugar. Lovely.

### Sem — 2012-05-20T19:23:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-25741>

### Jillm — 2012-05-31T08:54:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sore-knuckles.1543/post-28532>

Tumeric in coffee? That is very interesting. I am going to try that. I have been making bone broth. I have a cup each day instead of tea or coffee. A nice change.
