# Thread: hibernating fx
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-fx.1544/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-19T00:38:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-fx.1544/post-25339>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/18/hitting.snooze.molecular.clock.rabies.evolves.slower.hibernating.bats?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/18/hitting.snooze.molecular.clock.rabies.evolves.slower.hibernating.bats?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content)

Hitting snooze on the molecular clock: Rabies evolves slower in hibernating bats

Published: Friday, May 18, 2012 - 15:33 in Biology & Nature

The rate at which the rabies virus evolves in bats may depend heavily upon the ecological traits of its hosts, according to researchers at the University of Georgia, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and Katholieke Universiteit Leuven in Belgium. Their study, published May 17 in the journal PLoS Pathogens, found that the host's geographical location was the most accurate predictor of the viral rate of evolution. Rabies viruses in tropical and sub-tropical bat species evolved nearly four times faster than viral variants in bats in temperate regions. "Species that are widely distributed can have different behaviors in different geographical areas," said Daniel Streicker, a postdoctoral associate in the UGA Odum School of Ecology and the study's leader. "Bats in the tropics are active year-round, so more rabies virus transmission events occur per year. Viruses in hibernating bats, on the other hand, might lose up to six months' worth of opportunities for transmission."

Evolutionary biologists have long recognized that molecular evolution proceeds in a largely clock-like manner, with mutations accumulating at a fairly constant rate over time. This "molecular clock" allows for powerful inferences -- from dating the origins of species to the origins of epidemics. However, the rate at which the clock ticks varies dramatically among species; much research has focused on what causes these differences.

### AKMan — 2012-05-19T01:39:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-fx.1544/post-25347>

Very interesting. This goes along with something I have been reading today on Type 1 diabetes.

"The sweet thing about Type 1 diabetes:

A cryoprotective evolutionary adaptation"

"...Summary and discussion

In summary, we have provided evidence that certain metabolic perturbations associated with cold

exposure in animals, namely elevated glucose, increased production of glycerol and sorbitol, lipolysis,

and fatty acid oxidation, mimic those associated with Type 1 diabetes. Accordingly, we

hypothesize that genetic factors predisposing to Type 1 diabetes were selected for as an adaptive

measure to protect ancestral human populations from increasingly colder climates. We have provided

evidence to suggest that such selection might have begun approximately 14,000 years ago and

peaked during the Younger Dryas (12,600– 11,300 years ago) in both people from northern

Europe and Sardinia who share common palaeolithic ancestors, who are the inheritors of a unique

cold tolerant legacy, as well as express the highest worldwide prevalence of Type 1 diabetes. In light

of the fact that there are many examples of coldtolerant animals that have protective measures designed

to deal with the deleterious affects of high levels of glucose, the idea that humans have developed

similar adaptations is not without reason. Our hypothesis, that Type 1 diabetes arose as a

cryoprotective adaptation to cold environments, is compelling since it not only explains the unique

prevalence of this condition in certain populations, but it is yet another example by which we have

been, and continue to be, shaped by the environments in which we live. When life expectancy

was short and our ability to deal with the cold was limited, traits that conferred advantageous

cold tolerance far outweighed their deleterious effects. Examples of published descriptions of evolutionary

adaptations conferring selection advantages were previously limited to human

pathogens and reported as epidemic pathogenic selection (EPS). Such proposed examples include

the aforementioned cystic fibrosis mutations in the CFTR gene bestowing resistance to S. typhi

and hemochromatosis mutations conferring protection against iron-seeking intracellular pathogens.

This paper is one of the first accounts of a metabolic disorder providing a selection advantage not

to a pathogenic stressor alone, but rather due to climatic changes. We thus believe that the concept

of EPS should now include environmental factors that may be nonorganismal in nature; in so doing

we propose that factors resulting in Type 1 diabetes be considered a result of environmental pathogenic

selection (EnPS). In line with current conceptual models in evolutionary medicine we

should now focus our attention on the biological solutions employed by other cold tolerant animals

that deal successfully with the complications of high sugar and sugar alcohols, and in so doing, find

new ways in which to reduce the deleterious consequences seen in the long term clinical course of

Type 1 diabetes."

If you'd like a copy of the study, PM me, I have a zip file of the full-text article. Too big to post here.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-19T03:38:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-fx.1544/post-25365>

> **chocolate;25422 wrote:** [http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/18/hitting.snooze.molecular.clock.rabies.evolves.slower.hibernating.bats?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/18/hitting.snooze.molecular.clock.rabies.evolves.slower.hibernating.bats?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content)
>
> Hitting snooze on the molecular clock: Rabies evolves slower in hibernating bats

>
>
>
> The rate at which the rabies virus evolves in bats may depend heavily upon the ecological traits of its hosts, according to researchers at the University of Georgia, the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and Katholieke Universiteit Leuven in Belgium. Their study, published May 17 in the journal PLoS Pathogens, found that the host's geographical location was the most accurate predictor of the viral rate of evolution. Rabies viruses in tropical and sub-tropical bat species evolved nearly four times faster than viral variants in bats in temperate regions. "Species that are widely distributed can have different behaviors in different geographical areas," said Daniel Streicker, a postdoctoral associate in the UGA Odum School of Ecology and the study's leader. "Bats in the tropics are active year-round, so more rabies virus transmission events occur per year. Viruses in hibernating bats, on the other hand, might lose up to six months' worth of opportunities for transmission."
>
>
>
> Evolutionary biologists have long recognized that molecular evolution proceeds in a largely clock-like manner, with mutations accumulating at a fairly constant rate over time. This "molecular clock" allows for powerful inferences -- from dating the origins of species to the origins of epidemics. However, the rate at which the clock ticks varies dramatically among species; much research has focused on what causes these differences.

### chocolate — 2012-06-04T05:16:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hibernating-fx.1544/post-29320>

[http://jp.physoc.org/content/587/9/1863.full.pdf](http://http://jp.physoc.org/content/587/9/1863.full.pdf)

> A role for nuclear receptors in mammalian hibernation
>
> Clark J. Nelson, Jessica P. Otis and Hannah V. Carey
>
> Department of Comparative Biosciences, University of Wisconsin School of Veterinary Medicine, Madison, WI, USA
>
> Hibernation is one of the most dramatic examples of phenotypic plasticity in mammals.During
>
> periods of food shortage and/or reduced ambient temperatures hibernating mammals become
>
> heterothermic, allowing their body temperature to decreasewhile entering an energy-conserving
>
> torpid state. In order to survive the multi-month hibernation season many species engage in
>
> hyperphagy, dramatically increasing adipose stores prior to the onset of hibernation. Nuclear
>
> receptors are a superfamily of transcription factors many of which bind lipophilic molecules
>
> as ligands. They regulate a variety of processes including energy homeostasis, carbohydrate
>
> and lipid metabolism, inflammation and circadian rhythm. Given that lipids are integral in the
>
> hibernation phenotype theymay play important regulatory roles through their interactionswith
>
> nuclear receptors. Here we review current knowledge and suggest possible roles in mammalian
>
> hibernation for peroxisome proliferator-activated receptors (PPARs), farnesoid X receptors
>
> (FXRs), liver X receptors (LXRs), retinoid-related orphan receptors (RORs) and Rev-ERBs.

I just recently went to a Chiropractor/MD in Pastoral Medicine(something like that can't remember what it said? not sure what that is exactly)

[http://newleafhealthandwellness.com/custom\_content/c\_46432\_meet\_the\_doctor.html](http://http://newleafhealthandwellness.com/custom_content/c_46432_meet_the_doctor.html)

Info about her is here. Visit was quite interesting...that applied kinesiology thing...weird. Nevertheless it seemed like a good visit and even if I'm skeptical about certain things I obviously won't write off everything because of that.

Anyway she gave told me she wanted me to start with a 3-5 day fast with these guidelines.

(Fast Guidelines

The goal of this fast is to quickly cut the inflammation in your body and regulate your blood sugar. A minimum of three days is required for best results. During the fast, do not take any supplements. Limit your dietary intake to a drink that is made up of freshly squeezed lime and/or lemon (with pulp), water, organic maple syrup (grade B is preferred), and brewed green tea (optional). Make 1 gallon each morning. Take sips of the drink every 15 minutes throughout the day.

Makes 1/2 Gallon

12 Tablespoons lemon or lime juice (~3 lemons)

8 Tablespoons Grade B Maple Syrup

64 oz filtered water

Exact proportions are not critical and you can make the drink as sour or diluted as you like.

If you are taking sips all day you should be able to stabilize your blood sugar without any foods. Expect to feel the need to urinate frequently. If you truly become hungry, you are allowed to eat one food (or a fresh veggie drink), but it must be a vegetable you rarely eat, such as Brussels Sprouts, cauliflower, etc. Do not add anything to the vegetables such as spices or dressings.

The strict limitation of food is important because youâ€™re trying to decrease the load on your immune system. Many people are sensitive to the foods that they eat most often. The goal of the original three days is not to decrease calories, but rather to decrease the load of foods your body reacts to. The reason lime and lemon juice are used is due to its alkalinizing properties which are usually required. Organic grade B maple syrup is suggested because it is sweeter than other grades, and therefore less maple syrup is needed to counter the sour taste. Green tea can be added to the mixture if you prefer and you can drink hot green tea all day during the initial 3-5 days.)

I'd like to know what people think about this fast and also applied kinesiology. Also I asked a few questions of the fast which I will also ask here. I read that raw unfiltered honey is beneficial for allergies as well as being healthy all around as long its not overdone partly in an article by Mark Sisson on marksdailyapple.com.

Anyway I e-mailed her asking if I could substitute Honey for the Grade B Maple syrup and how much and if using lemon juice concentrate stuff would be ok or if I should make sure to buy lemon/lime and make it myself. The lemon and lime stuff I have includes a few preservatives, but I haven't researched enough yet on how bad if at all they are. Sodium Benzoate, Sodium Metabisulfite and sodium sulfite. Just the benzoate in the lime.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-19T02:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-physicians-network-advice.1545/post-25349>

The drink seems remarkably like the Master Cleanse diet that Beyonce went on to lose weight for her role in the movie Dreamgirls.

I do think applied kinesiology has its merits. Many people think its woo woo.

Personally I am skeptical of a plan that says it can reduce your inflammation that quickly...but you need to do whats right for you.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-19T03:24:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/paleo-physicians-network-advice.1545/post-25358>

I don't think its intended to cut inflammation completely or anything its supposed to be a boost at the start of a good diet. Start with 3-5 fast than Paleo after that. I don't think it will hurt me so I'll go ahead and do it. What I'm most curious about is if people think I can substitute honey for maple syrup and how much is the equivalent amount?

She mentioned a modified Leptin Reset thing after the fast I think. Not sure what modifications she means though. I'll see when she tells me more about it.

I don't see how this would harm you for a few days...give your digestive system and liver a rest and then segue into Paleo.

# Thread: Dry Ice to Cool CT Bath?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jim — 2012-05-19T04:47:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-25406>

As my input water temperature goes up, I am looking for more efficient ways to cool down my tub water. I was in my local grocery store and noticed that they are selling dry ice. That got me thinking that I could use that to cool my tub water. Does anyone have any experience using dry ice? How much should I use? Can I let it touch the water directly? What are the safety concerns if any?

Thanks!

### indigogirl — 2012-05-19T05:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-25417>

I don't know...dry ice can burn you...when you put it in a liquid it makes "fog" ...think a witch's cauldron on Halloween

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-05-19T06:05:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-25429>

Dry ice is frozen carbon dioxide (co2). It's been awhile since physical chemistry, but dry ice sublimates (goes from frozen to gaseous) at normal room barometric pressure, so there's a good chance you could asphyxiate (deprive oxygen) yourself if you try this. depending on how much you use and room ventilation of course. You could find yourself nodding off into a deep sleep for good.

### MJ* — 2012-05-19T06:46:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-25438>

> **incorky;25512 wrote:** Dry ice is frozen carbon dioxide (co2). It's been awhile since physical chemistry, but dry ice sublimates (goes from frozen to gaseous) at normal room barometric pressure, so there's a good chance you could asphyxiate (deprive oxygen) yourself if you try this. depending on how much you use and room ventilation of course. You could find yourself nodding off into a deep sleep for good.

Oh that seems too dangerous in a tub...no Jim no, we'd miss you...

### Nymue — 2012-05-23T15:42:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-26650>

I wanted to chime in on this back when I first saw the thread, but my account hadn't been activated yet. Better late than never! I tried the dry ice route, as it's available at my local grocery store. 5 lbs. of the stuff took about half an hour to fully melt, and that was with help from me, swirling it around first with a broomstick (talk about looking like a witch stirring her bubbling cauldron, ha!), then with rubber gloved hands. I knew I couldn't get in until it was fully dissolved. So, after waiting all of that time, 5 lbs. of dry ice cooled my 80Â° tap water down to..... 75Â°! How disappointing. It's a good thing though, it cost me $1.49/lb. and would've been a VERY expensive way to CT.

I've gone the route now of filling up my largest tupperware containers w/water and freezing them. That gets my water down to about 63Â°. If I bought a 5lb. bag of ice or 2 and dumped that in, I think I'd be able to get it into the 50s.

### Jim — 2012-05-23T16:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-26667>

> **Nymue;26742 wrote:** I wanted to chime in on this back when I first saw the thread, but my account hadn't been activated yet. Better late than never! I tried the dry ice route, as it's available at my local grocery store. 5 lbs. of the stuff took about half an hour to fully melt, and that was with help from me, swirling it around first with a broomstick (talk about looking like a witch stirring her bubbling cauldron, ha!), then with rubber gloved hands. I knew I couldn't get in until it was fully dissolved. So, after waiting all of that time, 5 lbs. of dry ice cooled my 80Â° tap water down to..... 75Â°! How disappointing. It's a good thing though, it cost me $1.49/lb. and would've been a VERY expensive way to CT.
>
> I've gone the route now of filling up my largest tupperware containers w/water and freezing them. That gets my water down to about 63Â°. If I bought a 5lb. bag of ice or 2 and dumped that in, I think I'd be able to get it into the 50s.
>
>
> Thanks for the information. I haven't tried it yet. I have been buying 10 lb bags of ice for $2.19. Three of those got my 64 F tap water down into the 40's. I was hoping the dry ice would work better than regular ice since it is much colder. It sublimates (goes from a solid to a gas) starting at -109 deg F.
>
>
>
> I found the following on [Wikipedia](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Freezing_mixture). I don't think I will be going the acetone route!!
>
>
>
> The simplest and cheapest cooling bath is an ice/water mixture, which maintains a temperature of 0 Â°C. For lower temperatures, three main types of cooling baths are typical:
>
>
>
> Ice
>
>
>
> A slurry of ice and an inorganic salt such as sodium chloride or calcium chloride can provide temperatures down to about −40 Â°C. The temperature depends on the amount and type of salt used, based on the freezing point depression effect.
>
>
>
> Dry ice
>
>
>
> A slurry of dry ice and a suitable organic solvent, such as acetone, can provide temperatures down to about −100 Â°C (with diethyl ether). Temperatures in the range of -12 Â°C to -78 Â°C can be conveniently generated with ethylene glycol / ethanol / dry ice mixtures.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-23T18:33:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-26712>

Do you have a spare cooler? You could put water in containers (water jugs, ziploc bags, ice trays, etc.) in the cooler along with the dry ice. That might be enough to freeze the water in the containers or at least get it good and chilled. When we had a 2-day power outage last fall, we bought dry ice and it stayed frozen in coolers for days. Dry ice isn't cheap but it might be more cost-effective than buying ice.

### Nymue — 2012-05-24T00:30:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-26846>

> **August;26807 wrote:** I think dry ice is considerable colder than what we are going for . . cold enough to cause major frostbite/tissue damage . . if the carbon dioxide does not do you in . . (which I think it might . . .)

Yup, this is why you'd have to wait for all of the dry ice to dissolve before getting into the tub. You want to make sure that it doesn't touch your skin and that you're not sitting there breathing in all of that carbon dioxide!

The cooler I grabbed it from at the store said that it is 5 times colder than ice. So I figured a 5 lb. block should be the equivalent of 25 lbs. of regular ice. No dice. Even so, 25 lbs. of ice would've been cheaper.

### Nymue — 2012-05-24T00:32:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-26847>

> **Lyndra;26804 wrote:** Do you have a spare cooler? You could put water in containers (water jugs, ziploc bags, ice trays, etc.) in the cooler along with the dry ice. That might be enough to freeze the water in the containers or at least get it good and chilled. When we had a 2-day power outage last fall, we bought dry ice and it stayed frozen in coolers for days. Dry ice isn't cheap but it might be more cost-effective than buying ice.

The issue with using dry ice this way is that it won't stay frozen for long! I initially bought 2 10 lb. bags of dry ice. I used 5 lbs. the first night and put the rest in the freezer, w/the intention of returning the one unopened bag to the grocery store after my experiment didn't go as planned. Well, it should have been obvious that my freezer is not cold enough to keep something that is -109Â° frozen! Duh moment on my part!! Expensive duh moment, too.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-29T06:34:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dry-ice-to-cool-ct-bath.1546/post-28141>

Bummer it didn't last. I wonder if your freezer actually heated up the dry ice and melted it? Your freezer is intended to stay at around 0 degrees so if you put something super cold in there, it might not work as intended. In our case, we had the dry ice in a portable cooler. Once we were done with it, I opened up the cooler and it took the better part of a day for it to melt/evaporate.

# Thread: Bladder cancer linked with diabetes drug
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bladder-cancer-linked-with-diabetes-drug.1547/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-19T17:04:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bladder-cancer-linked-with-diabetes-drug.1547/post-25484>

[http://networkedblogs.com/xPcoQ?a=share](http://http://networkedblogs.com/xPcoQ?a=share)

Bladder Cancer Linked With Diabetes Drugs in Thiazolidinedione Class

MedPage Today reports that thiazolidinediones (aka glitazones) are linked to development of bladder cancer. Pioglitazone is the most commonly used TZD in the U.S. From the article:

**The increased risk of bladder cancer associated with glitazones â€” which reached a relative increase of 72% in patients who started on the agents more than 5 years earlier â€” â€œappears to be a class effect,â€￾ the research team, led by Ronac Mamtani, MD, of the University of Pennsylvania in Philadelphia, conclude**d.

According to the National Cancer Institute, the U.S. has 74,000 new cases of bladder cancer yearly, and 15,000 annual deaths from bladder cancer.

If you take a thiazolidinedione, talk to your doctor about bladder cancer at your next visit.

Get ready for the attorney commercial if you watch tv.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-19T17:23:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bladder-cancer-linked-with-diabetes-drug.1547/post-25488>

That is pretty old news.....Actos was part of Shoemaker's lymph cleans both cold works better........and does not cause cancer.

# Thread: Coconut oil and the trots
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-and-the-trots.1550/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-20T01:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-and-the-trots.1550/post-25596>

Hi All,

If I eat dried coconut flakes or shredded coconut, I notice virtually no digestive disturbances. However, when I eat even a little bit of the coconut manna or coconut oil (extra virgin organic), I get "the trots", gas, bloating and all around misery.

What gives? Any thoughts? Thanks!.... Tom

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-20T04:25:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-and-the-trots.1550/post-25631>

Coconut oil is notorious for creating serious die-off - it is anti-microbial and kills the bad guys off. Part of its glory! Try starting off with a tsp of CO per day, then gradually working up to 1T/day, then slowly working up to 2 or 3 or 4 or whatever your goal is per day.

### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-20T17:32:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-and-the-trots.1550/post-25695>

Die-off!! Thanks, MamaGrok. I keep discovering that digestive issues = friendly and unfriendly bacteria in the gut. No wonder I bloat like a balloon. Okay, so the idea is to use a small amount of CO, kill off some bad guys, and slowly increase. Got it.

Other than the gut issues, I do feel really good when I consume coconut. It's odd how I don't get this reaction from canned coconut milk or dried coconut. Just straight oil and the manna. The manna is the worst, I have to say. Huge digestive upset.

Thanks!.... Tom

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-20T17:38:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/coconut-oil-and-the-trots.1550/post-25698>

Yes, it's the oil that is so powerful a detox agent. I wonder if the manna does it most for you b/c it's so delicious that it enables you to eat more oil than you ordinarily would? (Manna has a lot more oil than milk or plain coconut flesh)

I'm the opposite - I have built up a large tolerance for the oil, but can't yet tolerate the flesh at all, not even in manna. It seems I've pretty much fixed my bacterial imbalance, but still have a lot of physically damaged gut to fix.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### Lyndra — 2012-05-20T05:47:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-tests-ok-or-subpar.1551/post-25646>

> **Patty Cakes;25696 wrote:** Your FT3 should be at the top of the range and FT4 mid-high range plus the TSH looks a little high to me. Also check your rT3, but I got 10.8 which means your FT4 is converting to rT3 instead of FT3. [http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/) This is often caused by low iron or poorly functioning adrenals so you may want to check both of those w/ a full iron panel and a 24 hr saliva adrenal test. Also, I believe a rT3 ratio as low as your means you are leptin resistant in the brain.
>
>
>
> Are you on any thyroid meds now?

Not on thyroid meds. I just did a 24-hour salivary cortisol test but don't have results back yet. I have been on BHRT since January and am feeling a bit more energetic but sleep is still crap (waking every 1-1/2 hours or so).

I just redid a lipid panel and my LDL is up to 167. HDL is great (101), trigs are good (61). I'm wondering if low thyroid is causing LDL to increase (based on the 3rd podcast Chris Kresser did with Chris Masterjohn). I've been on 1000 mcg of iodine since January but am thinking I should increase that. Vit D is fairly low (45) so I plan to increase that, too.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-05-20T19:04:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-tests-ok-or-subpar.1551/post-25735>

Don't forget to make sure you have K2 as that is the balancer for D3. And yes, thyroid probs give way to higher cholesterol in some people. Not me and I'm doing my darnedest to get my cholesterol up! Also sometimes a dab of progesterone will help a sluggish thyroid. Not sure if that would be enough for you as your FT3 is horribly low. Not sure about the iodine as it makes me break out into a rash.

### Lyndra — 2012-05-20T23:01:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-tests-ok-or-subpar.1551/post-25818>

Thanks for the reminder about K2. I stopped all my supplements for a week to see if that would help my sleep and haven't added that back in.

Any idea what effect might DIM have? I've been taking that since January to reduce levels of E2 but I wonder if that, plus all the crucifers I eat, might not be such a good idea. This is Chris Masterjohn's take (from a WAPF article): "The safety of concentrated sources of crucifer-related chemicals such as broccoli sprouts or supplements containing indole-3-carbinol (I3C) and 3-3'-diindolylmethane (DIM) is questionable. These supplements should be avoided until continuing research can further elucidate their risks and benefits."

# Thread: Doctors?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sem — 2012-05-20T18:41:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/post-25722>

How does everyone find good, progressive doctors they can trust? I've wasted so much money and time with doctors who haven't a clue! It is so frustrating. Anyone know of someone good in Los Angeles? Thank you.

### ColdBren — 2012-05-20T20:32:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/post-25756>

i am in LA too, hoping someone answers you. I am considering going to holtorf med. group. they have a lot of info online and seem to be more progressive. They don't take insurance though.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-20T23:14:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/post-25820>

Never trust a doc, conventional or not, if you can help it. Instead, listen and then go think for yourself. Be the one in charge..... is my humble opinion.

### L8F — 2012-05-21T04:23:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/post-25872>

I found mine at Genova Diagnostic site, referral area. Got a bunch of names, looked them all up and chose one that looked right after calling etc. It has been amazing to have a bigger picture view. My personal opinion is that having the experiential knowledge coupled with one's own body of knowledge/personal research etc is the best combo.

I lost quite a bit of trust in the intellectual curiosity of physicians during my recent foray into Paleo and all of this LR stuff, and realizing that there is so much bias and broken-ness in medicine in general. That being said I feel that the knowledge of a good physician who can work with you, and help guide the direction of one's treatment has been something I could never have done on my own. I know a lot of conventional medicine, and now read for 6 months straight on the rest of this stuff, but still was at a loss about the treatments that are out there for a variety of problems. My new doc was great in pulling it all together in the span of an hour and a half, and had seen it all before. That was a huge weight off my chest.

I am a big fan of researching and learning, and directing the avenue to take in one's own care, but at a certain point I needed to have someone else treat me as a patient, and give my trust--with questions every 5 minutes--to them. It was like I could exhale finally. I have made a huge leap in progress with this help. (Not free though, mucho bucks)

Also think another good way to try might be to get a consult with someone like Chris Kresser, or some of the hormone guys Dzugan etc. Or there is the thought of getting oneself to another part of the country with a known quantity. For me that would be out near Austin with a guy named Lane Sebring, a Paleo doc x 12 years. He spoke at Paleo Fx and seems to know his stuff well. (But again, I would try to get an idea by talking to the clinician first, "Have you worked with X Y Z before, and can you rec something other than XYZ that I have tried thus far..." type thing...Most of us here have learned a lot, and tried a lot, and done lots of investigations on our own already. That calls for someone a little more experienced...

### johnnyb — 2012-05-21T05:59:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/doctors.1558/post-25891>

> **Souldanzer;25909 wrote:** Never trust a doc, conventional or not, if you can help it. Instead, listen and then go think for yourself. Be the one in charge..... is my humble opinion.

+1

I'm very fond of my Dr. She's does practice CW but seems very mindful that it's my life and cooperates to that end.

# Thread: HCG for male testosterone levels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/>
**Subforum:** HCG Protocol
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### primat — 2012-05-21T15:50:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-25926>

Google has mostly led me to bodybuilding/steroid-sites, which isn't really helpful for me.

I'm wondering if HCG could be an alternative (or addition) to testosterone. I need to get T-levels up, but still want to keep my fertility. Any guys on here doing long term HCG for this purpose?

I will discuss this with my endo, of course, but I like to be as prepared as possible since getting to talk to him outside scheduled hours is harder than having a seanse with Elvis...

### colleencoble — 2012-05-21T16:49:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-25942>

I know Jan who was on the MDA forum with us used it. I believe he used 300 iu every other day.

### finnite@dccnet.com — 2012-05-21T18:21:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-25987>

> **colleencoble;26033 wrote:** I know Jan who was on the MDA forum with us used it. I believe he used 300 iu every other day.

he posts quite regularly in a forum called \`[all things male](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/forumdisplay.php?2-All-Things-Male)\`and they do discuss using hcg and T replacement

### Huck — 2012-05-21T20:06:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-26034>

> **primat;26017 wrote:** Google has mostly led me to bodybuilding/steroid-sites, which isn't really helpful for me.
>
>
>
> I'm wondering if HCG could be an alternative (or addition) to testosterone. I need to get T-levels up, but still want to keep my fertility. Any guys on here doing long term HCG for this purpose?
>
>
>
> I will discuss this with my endo, of course, but I like to be as prepared as possible since getting to talk to him outside scheduled hours is harder than having a seanse with Elvis...
>
>
> This is part of an answer Jack posted to a question I had.
>
>
>
> ...If you have this issue you can stimulate T using HCG from the pituitaty by increasing LH. The issue is that LH surges stimulate the Testes to make Testosterone.......if your testes are no longer working well.....then HCG wont work. Most people default to Testosterone replacement.......it has its own set of issues so there are tradeoffs but the trade offs are small for most men who need it in my experience.

### primat — 2012-05-21T22:55:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-26106>

> **Huck;26125 wrote:** This is part of an answer Jack posted to a question I had.

MMm I know he would say "Labs will show!"  And be right about it.

I'm planning for the worst and hoping for the best. Since I have low T along with low LH and they seem to follow eachother I think we'll have to do some provocation-test to see if T reacts to LH/HCG. However sad this is, I seem to be homing in on something tweakable after 12 years of being told to eat less and exercise more. The list of low T consequenses over time is not pleasant reading, though - so I'm doubling my life insurance...

### Huck — 2012-05-21T23:03:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hcg-for-male-testosterone-levels.1567/post-26110>

> **primat;26197 wrote:** MMm I know he would say "Labs will show!"  And be right about it.
>
>
>
> I'm planning for the worst and hoping for the best. Since I have low T along with low LH and they seem to follow eachother I think we'll have to do some provocation-test to see if T reacts to LH/HCG. However sad this is, I seem to be homing in on something tweakable after 12 years of being told to eat less and exercise more. The list of low T consequenses over time is not pleasant reading, though - so I'm doubling my life insurance...

I tried to boost testosterone with pregnenolone and dhea and ended up with an enlarged prostate which caused urinary problems, blood in urine, UTI, high PSA, and extremely high dhea. After about 3 weeks of antibiotics and stopping dhea, 7-keto, and pregnenolone, I am back to normal. I mention this just as a reminder to get labs done and keep track of when you start and stop supplements.

For now, my focus is on losing weight and then I will work on boosting testosterone through exercise.

# Thread: The Klinghardt Neurotoxin Elimination Protocol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-klinghardt-neurotoxin-elimination-protocol.1568/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-21T17:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-klinghardt-neurotoxin-elimination-protocol.1568/post-25956>

[http://customers.hbci.com/~wenonah/new/9steps.htm](http://http://customers.hbci.com/~wenonah/new/9steps.htm)

Approved by:

American Academy of Neural Therapy and

Institute of Neurobiology (Bellevue, WA, USA)

Institute for Neurobiologie (Stuttgart, Germany)

Academy for Balanced NeuroBiology Ltd (London, United Kingdom)

This lecture was presented by Dietrich Klinghardt M.D., Ph.D. at the Jean Piaget Department at the University of Geneva, Switzerland Oct. 2002 to physicians and dentists from Europe, Israel, several Arab countries and Asia.

There is also a mercury amalgam one on the page.

### chocolate — 2012-05-21T17:41:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-klinghardt-neurotoxin-elimination-protocol.1568/post-25971>

[http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi\_7396/is\_315/ai\_n42061272/pg\_5/](http://http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_7396/is_315/ai_n42061272/pg_5/)

A sensible approach to eliminating neurotoxins

by Dietrich Klinghardt

Dosage: Take 15 drops of BioPure cilantro tincture in hot water 3 times per day prior to a meal and at bedtime (during the vagus-dominant sleep phase, many detox functions are most active), or 30 minutes after taking chlorella. Cilantro causes the gallbladder to dump bile--containing the excreted neurotoxins--into the small intestine. The bile release occurs naturally as we are eating and is much enhanced by cilantro. As noted, if no chlorella is taken, the abundant nerve endings of the ENS reabsorb most neurotoxins as they travel through the small intestine. Gradually increase dose for full benefit. In the initial phase of the detox, cilantro should be given 5 days on, 2 days off.

The proprietary (or not) protocol is pretty simple.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-21T17:49:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-klinghardt-neurotoxin-elimination-protocol.1568/post-25973>

> **chocolate;26062 wrote:** [http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi\_7396/is\_315/ai\_n42061272/pg\_5/](http://http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_7396/is_315/ai_n42061272/pg_5/)
>
>
>
> A sensible approach to eliminating neurotoxins
>
> by Dietrich Klinghardt
>
>
>
> Dosage: Take 15 drops of BioPure cilantro tincture in hot water 3 times per day prior to a meal and at bedtime (during the vagus-dominant sleep phase, many detox functions are most active), or 30 minutes after taking chlorella. Cilantro causes the gallbladder to dump bile--containing the excreted neurotoxins--into the small intestine. The bile release occurs naturally as we are eating and is much enhanced by cilantro. As noted, if no chlorella is taken, the abundant nerve endings of the ENS reabsorb most neurotoxins as they travel through the small intestine. Gradually increase dose for full benefit. In the initial phase of the detox, cilantro should be given 5 days on, 2 days off.
>
>
>
> The proprietary (or not) protocol is pretty simple.

Thanks Chocolate...you have such good resources. This sounds really interesting! I think I may have to try it for my foggy brain. I wonder could you use psyllium husks and betonite clay instead?

# Thread: garlic is bio active selenium...
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-is-bio-active-selenium.1570/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-21T17:30:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-is-bio-active-selenium.1570/post-25965>

[http://www.cancer.gov/cancertopics/factsheet/prevention/garlic-and-cancer-prevention](http://http://www.cancer.gov/cancertopics/factsheet/prevention/garlic-and-cancer-prevention)

What is garlic?

Garlic is a vegetable (Allium sativum) that belongs to the Allium class of bulb-shaped plants, which also includes onions, chives, leeks, and scallions. Garlic is used for flavoring in cooking and is unique because of its high sulfur content. In addition to sulfur, garlic also contains arginine, oligosaccharides, flavonoids, and selenium, all of which may be beneficial to health (1).

The characteristic odor and flavor of garlic comes from sulfur compounds formed from allicin, the major precursor of garlicâ€™s bioactive compounds, which are formed when garlic bulbs are chopped, crushed, or damaged (2). **Bioactive compounds are defined as substances in foods or dietary supplements, other than those needed to meet basic nutritional needs, that are responsible for changes in health status.**

Do findings from population studies offer evidence that garlic may prevent cancer?

Several population studies show an association between increased intake of garlic and reduced risk of certain cancers, including cancers of the stomach, colon, esophagus, pancreas, and breast. Population studies are multidisciplinary studies of population groups that investigate the cause, incidence, or spread of a disease or examine the effect of health-related interventions, dietary and nutritional intakes, or environmental exposures. An analysis of data from seven population studies showed that the higher the amount of raw and cooked garlic consumed, the lower the risk of stomach and colorectal cancer (5).

The European Prospective Investigation into Cancer and Nutrition (EPIC) is an ongoing multinational study involving men and women from 10 different countries. This study is investigating the effects of nutrition on cancer. In the study, higher intakes of onion and garlic were associated with a reduced risk of intestinal cancer (6).

The Iowa Womenâ€™s Study is a large prospective study investigating whether diet, distribution of body fat, and other risk factors are related to cancer incidence in older women. Findings from the study showed a strong association between garlic consumption and colon cancer risk. Women who consumed the highest amounts of garlic had a 50 percent lower risk of cancer of the distal colon compared with women who had the lowest level of garlic consumption (7).

Several population studies conducted in China centered on garlic consumption and cancer risk. In one study, investigators found that frequent consumption of garlic and various types of onions and chives was associated with reduced risk of esophageal and stomach cancers, with greater risk reductions seen for higher levels of consumption (8). Similarly, in another study, the consumption of allium vegetables, especially garlic and onions, was linked to a reduced risk of stomach cancer (9). In a third study, greater intake of allium vegetables (more than 10 g per day vs. less than 2.2 g per day), particularly garlic and scallions, was associated with an approximately 50 percent reduction in prostate cancer risk (10).

Evidence also suggests that increased garlic consumption may reduce pancreatic cancer risk. A study conducted in the San Francisco Bay area found that pancreatic cancer risk was 54 percent lower in people who ate larger amounts of garlic compared with those who ate lower amounts (11).

In addition, a study in France found that increased garlic consumption was associated with a statistically significant reduction in breast cancer risk. After considering total calorie intake and other established risk factors, breast cancer risk was reduced in those consuming greater amounts of fiber, garlic, and onions (12).

### chocolate — 2012-05-27T05:25:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-is-bio-active-selenium.1570/post-27795>

[http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html)

A garlic tablet a day... It could keep high blood pressure at bay

Read more: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html#ixzz1w25kwVaE](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html#ixzz1w25kwVaE)

> In the trial, researchers from the unversity's general practice department gave capsules containing 960mg of aged garlic extract to patients with high blood pressure.
>
>
>
> Those who took the supplements every day had much lower readings after 12 weeks.
>
>
>
> A high blood pressure reading is one that exceeds 140/90 millimetres of mercury. The first figure, the systolic pressure, corresponding to the 'surge' that occurs with each heart beat.
>
>
>
> Patients in the trial who had a systolic reading above 140mmHg saw it drop 10.2 lower on average than a control group on a placebo or dummy pill.
>
>
>
> This reduced the blood pressure of some patients down to healthy levels.
>
>
>
> Dr Ried said 'This reduction is clinically significant, as a drop in systolic blood pressure by 5mmHg reduces the risk of cardiovascular disease by eight to twenty per cent.'
>
>
>
> Read more: [http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html#ixzz1w26TSBqe](http://http://www.dailymail.co.uk/health/article-1304121/Garlic-tablets-Can-lead-lowering-blood-pressure.html#ixzz1w26TSBqe)
>
>
> Garlic is thought to have an antihypertensive effect because it stimulates production of certain chemical substances called nitric oxide (NO) and hydrogen sulphide (H2S), which helps relax blood vessels.

### chocolate — 2012-05-27T06:23:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-is-bio-active-selenium.1570/post-27801>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6698185](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/6698185)

Hypolipidemic effects of garlic oil in rats fed ethanol and a high lipid diet.

Shoetan A, Augusti KT, Joseph PK.

Abstract

Feeding of ethanol and a high fat-high cholesterol diet to rats markedly increased the total lipids in the liver, and cholesterol and triglyceride levels in the serum, liver and kidneys. However, when ethanol mixed with 0.5% garlic oil was fed to animals maintained on the high fat-high cholesterol diet, these lipid levels were significantly reduced to levels near to those seen in untreated control rats. Garlic oil did not reduce the serum albumin or the total proteins of liver, kidneys or serum when fed along with ethanol. Probably the garlic oil enhances the catabolism of dietary cholesterol and fatty acids.

### chocolate — 2012-07-06T20:33:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/garlic-is-bio-active-selenium.1570/post-35909>

[http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi\_m0887/is\_12\_29/ai\_n56719683/](http://http://findarticles.com/p/articles/mi_m0887/is_12_29/ai_n56719683/)

Antibacterial ability of organoselenium compounds

Selenium (Se) was considered/identified as a toxic compound initially during its discovery. However. in the past, scientist eventually proved that this trace mineral plays a vital role in the regulation of immune system in both human and animals. **In addition, organoselenium is reported to have the better bioavailability and less toxicity than inorganic selenium. Organoselenium compounds have been identified to exhibit antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal. antiparasitic, antihistamine, and anticancer properties.** Extensive studies have also been conducted to synthesize stable organoselenium compounds, which has potential application as antioxidants, enzymemodulators, antitumors, antivirals, antimicrobials, antihypertensive agents, and cytokine inducers. However, limited number of organoselenium compounds were evaluated for its' antimicrobials feature.

Benzoylselenoglycolic acid, 3-\[(phenylcarbonyl)-selenyl\] propanoic acid, and their esters (8 derivatives) were synthesized and screened for antibacterial activity against E. coli. Bacillus subtilis, Staphylococcus aureus, and Salmonella typhimurium by the researchers from Provimi India Innovation Centre. The level of bacterial inhibition was determined quantitatively by minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the newly synthesized compounds.

The two mentioned organoselenium and the eight synthesized derivatives of the novel compound demonstrates moderate to significant antibacterial activity on the pathogenic bacterial species in the report. Significant inhibitory activity was achieved at the low concentration of 0.1 mg/ml against both gram positive and gram negative bacteria by the compound derivatives except S. typhimurium (for which the inhibitory effect was shown in much higher concentration range at 1-10mg/ml).

The study not only successfully synthesized eight novel compounds, the MIC of the organoselenium compounds also showed that they were efficient for application as the antibacterial agent on the pathogenic germs. Further investigation on the potential biological activity of the novel compounds is still in progress at Provimi India Innovation Centre, Bangalore.

Details: P.M. Radhakrishna, Provimi India Innovation Centre, Provimi Animal Nutrition India Ltd., Bangalore 560 106. India. Phone: 91-80-2856-2298. E-mail: [pmradhakrishna@provimi.in](mailto:pmradhakrishna@provimi.in).

COPYRIGHT 2010 Frost & Sullivan

COPYRIGHT 2011 Gale, Cengage Learning

### vlynnb — 2012-05-22T04:53:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/season-eating-figure-out-whats-in-season-where-you-live.1571/post-26187>

Epicurious map, I would describe it as a curiosity. For Idaho in May it lists apples, asparagus, and potatoes. Asparagus, yes. The only other things coming out of the ground is very early lettuce and it is only baby size in Southeastern Idaho. Earliest apples come on in mid July and potatoes are a fall crop. Is it accurate for other areas?

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-22T04:57:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/season-eating-figure-out-whats-in-season-where-you-live.1571/post-26189>

I saw some inaccuracies as well. Most apples aren't mature until aug/sep/early oct.

As for potatoes new potatoes can be harvested now if planted in feb, but are better left until late aug/early sept ( if you eat them. I've fully converted to cauliflower smashers so don't see much reason to eat potatoes). Look around and you'll find what grows regionally.

### vlynnb — 2012-05-25T05:17:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/season-eating-figure-out-whats-in-season-where-you-live.1571/post-27251>

I definitely agree that being aware of what is growing when around you works better than relying on a chart. There may be places in Idaho where some planting can occur in Feb. allowing for new potatoes now but not here. Most things are just being planted and we had a frost again last night, we can have them every month of the year.

I haven't done much gardening in the past but am learning. Too bad I probably won't be able to eat the famous nightshades that grow so well here. Maybe should look into cauliflower.

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-21T18:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-05-02-2012.1572/post-25980>

Labs done on 05/02/2012 Second set of numbers are lab reference ranges. The Functional medicine Chiropractor had a second set of functional medicine ranges that aren't listed here that are obviously a bit different and she did stress that the cholesterol being high wasn'mt a big deal which she should considering shes on the Paleo Physicians network listing.

Cholesterol, Total 201 125-200

HDL Cholesterol 44 >40 m

Triglycerides 137

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-21T19:45:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/labs-05-02-2012.1572/post-26030>

Quick look -taking a break from excel hell...  1st question - have you been CTing??? b/c that can throw testing off!

HDL is low - you want to get it up above 60mg/dl at a minimum

Trigs - too HIGH; you want to get that DOWN! High Trigs and Low HDL usually are an indicator of small pattern LDL (BAD!!!) How long have you been grain free? you need to push 100% paleo to adjust on this. no dairy, ghee allowed; also since your HDL is below 60 you should limit your nightshades.

the other things that pop out to me: your Fasting BG is HIGH - you are diabetic at 138!!! Dr K likes to see FBG below 80mg/dl fwiw! you need to ditch all grains/sugars, diary etc and go KETO until under control! You HbA1C is HIGH as well!

TSH is off the charts! your T3 looks a bit on the low side, while your T4 is mid range. I expect what T4 is being converted is being turned into Reverse T3 instead of T3. recommend next time around you get both free T3 and Reverse T3 tested as well.

Also notice you're don't have an hsCRP - that's a big one you should get - helps tell the picture.

Additionally - did you get any hormones run? if so post those when you get in...

# Thread: Carbs Can Kill website
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-can-kill-website.1573/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-05-21T18:09:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-can-kill-website.1573/post-25981>

I noticed this on my FB newsfeed today...Someone asked this Dr Su about what the minimum carb intake should be. Here is his answer:

Robert Su: There is no minimum intake for carbohydrate in grams per day to stay healthy, theoretically. The intake of carbohydrate is mainly for fibers, with which little digestible carbohydrate is consumed. The daily amount carbohydrate is really ind...ividualized, depending on the state of beta cells with the individual. If the person's beta cells have been severely abused, he should take as little of carbohydrate as possible. Or, if the person's beta cells are still in good size and function, he probably can take much more carbohydrate. The best way to evaluate the circumstance is to use a serial blood glucose tests in conjunction with the amount and types of carbohydrate foods to be consumed.

[http://www.carbohydratescankill.com/93/why-does-everyone-need-annual-series-of-blood-glucose-tests](http://http://www.carbohydratescankill.com/93/why-does-everyone-need-annual-series-of-blood-glucose-tests)

hmm...

### chocolate — 2012-05-22T00:18:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-can-kill-website.1573/post-26119>

this is awesome, now i just have to figure out which glucose meter is the best deal.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T01:26:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs-can-kill-website.1573/post-26131>

Personally I think this is true that carbs are not needed for life I think to say there are no benefits of them to mammals in the warm seasons is just not correct. I think they manner in which they are used today is among the biggest mismatches in our diet that is available. As with all macro nutrients there is a time and place. If you want to broaden the view I think carbs should make a small part of an annual diet for a human and they should be concentrated in the longest light cycles we face. If you live further north or south of the equator you amounts should drop. If you live in the tropics the offset to the higher carb diet that is protective is increase nutrient density found in fish and pastured meats. Palm oil and coconut oil are great replacement sources of calories from carbs in the tropics and they both carry huge metabolic advantages at the mitochondria in how they handled. I also think certain foods like cocoa are very protective in the tropics for a high laden carb diet. It is as if Mother Nature is giving you these foods as well in the warm equatorial areas to offset the sustain damage from a strict carb loaded diet. I think we have adapted to warm from cold.......based upon the KT climate where mammals came from.......so we can handle it 67 million years later but I think we are still best equipped to run off of protein and fats best.

Seriously, that's how people die of hypothermia, just hopping in cold lakes without being adapted slowly (in the house! or by starting in summer warmer water) first!

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-22T17:58:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26345>

> **Birdy;26428 wrote:** "they were eating SAD" is just the stock answer. It is a meaningless throwaway comment designed to shut down any real discussion.

Or, it could be true.  I mean, I'm seeing major fat loss with CT, and so are many others. I haven't heard anyone report fat increase, but am keeping my ears open.

When you eat a summer diet but use a winter temperature, is it ridiculous to imagine there might be some unintended consequences, like fat accumulation?

I do remember a few weeks back reading a scientific paper stating that human fat does not act like blubber (insulating), that it serves an entirely different purpose, and does not warm.

I know I grew up thinking that skinny people were colder than fatter, but is it really true? Looking at my own family, I'm thinner and warmer in cold than I have been in years; my skinny daughter no longer turns blue even the she's just as thin & the water's just as cold; my skinny son & skinny husband have the same experience.

### RobH — 2012-05-22T18:28:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26360>

The difference and bottom line is this: most of the primal / paleo bloggers out there do it because they are standing on the shoulders of the science (Boyd Eaton / Loren Cordain / Robb Wolf, et al compiled it nicely and (LC and RW) mass marketed it) that proves grains / processed foods are bad for you, they have an instinctual knowledge that this is the right way to go from the diet (and lifestyle) standpoint, and they want it to "feel good." My understanding is that Jack arrived at his diet recommendations (which coincide with the much of Paleo community's ideas) through hard research on existing literature (a lot of time in the library), serious thought experiments and evolutionary biology. I think his Factor X theory is brilliant -- along the lines of Relativity and Natural Selection -- and ties so many things together than most of us NEVER think of. And the biggest difference is that he tests, reads, researches, thinks, and most importantly takes care of other people -- interprets those tests, prescribes, retests, operates, etc. In doing so he assumes a direct responsibility for their health -- and I'm not just talking medico legally, but ethically and morally -- and personally. I know he takes that seriously.

I'll put my money on Jack interpreting my hormone panel and telling me which direction to go ANY DAY over most of those other folks in the paleosphere who are trying to beat up on him. Not to sound arrogant or narcissistic here but there is a huge difference between preaching your great ideas or experiences to people and assuming the responsibility to clinically treat them. And it is not just a licensing issue, but a confidence issue. I'm thinking I could show my lab work to any number of Paleo Bloggers jumping on him and they wouldn't have a clue. Mark Sisson has lots of great stuff to say and fine advice but I don't think he'll be making any clinical decisions for ANYBODY anytime soon. The buck stops at the blog.

As a doctor, I know and work with lots of doctors. The ones I admire and respect the most are the neurosurgeons and the cardiothoracic surgeons. Can you all imagine the confidence, competence, and knowledge it takes to cut someone's head or chest open and operate on on the two organs where if you screw up just a little bit you kill someone (never mind just being able to close then incision and make it look good? Literally every time the pick up a scalpel (and the other assorted tools of surgery) they are taking somebody's life in your hands. If they pass out halfway through the operation there is NOBODY else in the room who can save them (not me either). Almost all of the rest of us have a lot more margin for error in our lives and our jobs (I have those kind of responsibilities about 2% of the time I work -- the rest of they time I can fix my errors...), and the thing about neurosurg is they probably don't even know where the errors might show up in the future -- a few inches to the left and someone might never walk again. I respect those who take on that responsibility -- they are directly responsible for a life that they are intimately connected with during that operation (pre-op and post-op too). It changes them and way they think and act.

The whole stupid "attack Jack" thing out there (which I really don't want to waste time with) reminds me of a high school clique thing.

Once I've read, re-read, taken notes on, looked up in textbooks, and understand all his blogs back to time zero, then I'll start worrying about all the citations or needing more. Why would you impede the progress of this flow of information and ideas writing and formatting a bibliography. He's not charging for the work, copyright laws don't apply so who cares? He's not writing for academic grants. And I'll buy and read and re-read the book, etc.

I know who I'll bet myself on, and I know there are plenty of others out there who will and are doing the same.

Regards,

RobH

### Jack Kruse — 2012-05-22T18:36:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26367>

RobH I appreciate your words here since you are a physician......it amazes me the difference in the public standards on the internet and in clinical medicine in our clinics......People do not go to their doctor and ask for the cites for the their listed complication on metformin from Cecil's medicine, but they think it matter here on a blog for some reason? The same this is true with the surgeries that I do, but I need to cite every sentence and point I make here. If you look at my blogs most have copious cites. And still it is not enough?

I guess my sensibilities are just different. I think the blog is here to elucidate the concepts of the theory to help people understand conceptually why things are happening......often in medicine we dont tell people the why about anything we do......we just do it..........

I have never had anyone ask me why we clip aneurysms or ask for cites. This seems to be an internet issue for some reason.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-22T19:04:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26383>

RobH, you make some excellent points that, while I know you don't want to get in the muck, could really help if you were to post them just once to that FTA thread. I'm thinking particularly of the points about neurosurgeons (room for error, responsibility, and whatnot) & clinical experience.

FWIW, I don't want a bibliography, and don't need things spelled out in proper MLA form. Just a "So and So" attached to words taken directly from another person/source, especially when it's a lengthy passage or one that not everyone is familiar with.

(Cuz you know, if I exclaim I HAVE A DREAM! everyone kinda knows who I'm referring to and no one will think I came up with those words myself.)

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-22T19:08:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26388>

Just to clarify, I think you are doing a fine job on the blog posts. You're piling a bunch of information together and do not need to footnote every statement. It's more when you're on FB or offering comments that you can sometimes pull up a really profound thought or give a length explanation for something, and it turns out it's word for word from someone/thing else.

It gives the impression that you're trying to pass those ideas off as your own, and I don't think you are, but it undermines your credibility - and again, I couldn't care less about what the haters think, I'm talking about decent people checking out your information and wanting to learn and get well.

So to clarify, I'm not saying you need to reference every \*idea\* any more than you're already doing. Just that it would be good when you \*directly quote\* from another source to give the name of the source. Nothing more.

### ealachan — 2012-05-22T19:22:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26393>

> **Jack Kruse;26458 wrote:** RobH I appreciate your words here since you are a physician......it amazes me the difference in the public standards on the internet and in clinical medicine in our clinics......People do not go to their doctor and ask for the cites for the their listed complication on metformin from Cecil's medicine, but they think it matter here on a blog for some reason? The same this is true with the surgeries that I do, but I need to cite every sentence and point I make here. If you look at my blogs most have copious cites. And still it is not enough?
>
>
>
> I guess my sensibilities are just different. I think the blog is here to elucidate the concepts of the theory to help people understand conceptually why things are happening......often in medicine we dont tell people the why about anything we do......we just do it..........
>
>
>
> I have never had anyone ask me why we clip aneurysms or ask for cites. This seems to be an internet issue for some reason.

It's because the internet is still very much the Wild, Wild West of modes of communication. Anyone with half a mind to can put together a decent-looking website and start spouting off about whatever the hell they want. If you walk into a doctor's office, you can be fairly confident that they at least know the basics because they were awarded an M.D. from an accredited school, but I could make a website right now, call myself Dr. Yammerpants McHuffington, MD, and start posting about how I have experimented on myself and found that eating only Swiss cheese during the waning moon while Mercury is in retrograde will make you live forever.

I don't disagree with you that haters gonna hate, and that nitpickers gonna nitpick, but I also don't disagree with people wanting cites for information, especially when it comes to trying to make informed decisions about nutrition and supplementation. My bugbear isn't even the cites or lack there-of so much as the sometimes very hard to interpret comments you make in response to your blog posts, and your forum posts. I know, you don't charge for it (well, mostly), and if I don't like it I can get bent. I get that, and I'm still here (82 days and counting on the Reset). I'm just trying to point out that no one here is saying we _don't_ believe what you're saying. We're the ones already following your protocols, and we'd love to see you get the POSITIVE recognition that you deserve, which might be more forthcoming if the general public could make more sense of what you're actually saying sometimes.

### MJ* — 2012-05-22T21:09:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26437>

> **RobH;26451 wrote:** The difference and bottom line is this: ...
>
> Regards,
>

>
> RobH

Thanks this is a wonderful, levelheaded post from a fresh perspective, thanks for taking the time to write it all...I am staying out of the fray, it's not worth the time and I have no association besides. I have a hard time understanding why Dr. Kruse would even care with all he has going for him. Jack the doctor, blogger/surgeon is am outlier, one of 5%, he'll survive  and his work is amazing I think we here all agree...Jack the bear in the Paleo beehive on the other hand, has stirred things up, and maybe, just maybe that's not the worst thing in the world because if even privately it will cause some self evaluation in those who are in a position of influence in giving the advice they give. The other thing though is that when you go after someone else's honey, what are the bees to do? Buzz alot, sting you, and this will die down and the direction I'd like to see Dr. Kruse go in is away from all that, toward the great big world out there with lots of people who could benefit from his work...

I'd site my blog posts ENG 101 style, and on here make mention but I don't think it need to be too formal, just spill your guts before you lose a thought...I try to point back to everything that brings me to specific thoughts so people really can understand where it's coming from to further their own understanding and mine if need be, and if they want/need to follow a thought further...I think it's helpful to do that but only for sake of the people here, and not necessary for those who would be critical, who cares what they think?!

### indigogirl — 2012-05-24T04:08:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-26926>

> **ruby;26983 wrote:** Really? How does that follow?
>
> A brilliant surgeon, with bucketfulls of education, access to latest research, monetary resources to facilitate the highest quality diet etc, iron will and brilliant brain gets himself morbidly obese and this somehow makes him more credible?

Ruby,

I do think it makes him more credible.

Some of us have eating disorders...some of us have cravings that are hard to control. If one hasn't been there one doesn't know what it feels like.

I have an addictive personality( if there is such a thing). I gave up a pack and a half a day smoking habit 33 years ago. My husband could smoke a cigarette (could...hasnt chosen to in forever) once a week and then not smoke again for months. I could never do this!

I still struggle with sugar. My husband just doesn't get it...sugar doesn't call his name.

If I'm going to take advice from someone I want them to walk the talk (which Mark and Jack both do).

I might erroneously think Jack has been where I am/was and because of this is better suited to address my issues. Maybe I'm projecting?

This doesn't mean that conversely someone who is not overweight can't help me.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-24T04:14:19+0300

I don't think the people who go paleo are risk averse or afraid of new ideas. No longer eating the things that are in 90% of what they ate before is pretty daring.

But I really do like the folks on the forum here because you don't have all the young, hot-to-trot foul-mouthed 20 year old guys who don't know how to speak with respect for anybody. And it makes me feel really old writing that, but hey, what makes being rude to people okay? There's a LOT of that at MDA, enough that a lot of people stop entering discussions after a while because of the juvenile people jumping in and internet-shouting.

The intellectual & human quality of the people on this forum is astounding, and I do think that says great things about Dr. Kruse.

And it makes me feel really old writing that, but hey, what makes being rude to people okay? There's a LOT of that at MDA, enough that a lot of people stop entering discussions after a while because of the juvenile people jumping in and internet-shouting.
>
>
>
> The intellectual & human quality of the people on this forum is astounding, and I do think that says great things about Dr. Kruse.

I didn't really read stuff on MDA, except for the monster thread on leptin reset. Within the paleo world, I guess the Krusers are the first to open up to new ideas. I don't think we just got the broken ones, we are also the avid readers, the experimentalists - we ARE more excited about trying out new ideas, the newest ideas, not just the newish ones.

100% me ... I found MDA via a P90X blog. it was a revelation for me! And initially I had no problem loosing the baby weight - but once I lost most of my baby weight from ds, I couldn't get any lower... every one on MDA pushed carb cycling and IF - and neither felt right for me... I did try each - and w/each I gained weight...even tried calorie restriction and that didn't do anything...then suddenly Jack showed up one day - and I figured why not? what's it gonna hurt? I dove in head first and BITCHED the entire 1st month about that BAB! And JAck called me on it too! then suddenly, TOTM returned, and I started seeing LS signals - even though I gained weight... and Jack kept on my back telling me I needed to test - which I resisted...but then I finally broke down and tested - and he was right I was a hot hormonal mess.... at least now by body has the support it needs from nutrition, supplements and BHRT - I'm finally seeing progress, its slow but reversing the damage I've done to myself isn't going to happen over night...

### kathylu — 2012-05-24T17:27:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-27019>

Similar story here. I was metabolically disordered from early childhood (became obese by the age of 7). I was able to lose weight only by extreme measures...I mean really extreme. Not eating for days, exercising to exhaustion all the while fighting intense hunger and cravings. Then when I couldn't do it anymore, I would start binging (which by the way never stopped the hunger or cravings). All the while people just told me I needed more "willpower", just eat less and exercise more! I knew very few people who had more willpower than I did...it's just that whatever I did worked for a few weeks and then my weight loss would come to a screaming halt. No one could tell me what was wrong with me...they just handed me another copy of a 1000 calorie diet. When I told them that was generally all I ate, they maintained it was impossible and that I must be deluding myself (To be fair, when I was in a "binge cycle", I ate about 3000 calories per day).

I got to the point where I felt is was an impossible task to get to a normal weight, so I simply concentrated on being "fat, but fit". I ate low carb, organic, took supplements, did strength training and some cardio and felt pretty good. My labs were always good, great cholesterol, normal blood sugar, etc. This while the "skinny folks" in my family were getting heart disease, cancer, auto-immune problems, gall bladder disease, etc. I tried my best to get them to look at the low carb paleo diet, but was simply met with a disbelieving grin "There she goes again!". Because I was fat. Therefore I couldn't know anything useful. My Ph.D. studies in biochemistry didn't seem to count at all.

Then I found this website. To say I was skeptical would be an understatement. However, I liked Jack's explanation of the function of leptin, bad grammar and all...it squared with what I knew of biochemistry and neuroanatomy (which I've studied). The idea of using the brain's inherent neuroplasticity to rewrire hormonal signaling actually made sense to me.

So, I decided to give it a try. It was hard. The BAB made me feel sick. No exercise made me feel like a slug. I emailed Jack, whose response was basically to smile and say that the more violent my reactions were, the more this was going to help me. So I hung in there and within 3 weeks I could tolerate the BAB and miracle of miracles, my extreme hunger and cravings just disappeared. Gone. Nothing had EVER made that happen before! I spent 12 weeks on the leptin rx because it took that long for me to see signs of LS. I enjoyed my new found freedom from the tyranny of constant hunger and carb cravings. I lost 7 lbs. in 12 weeks, not much, but enough to keep me going.

At the end of March, I added CT, hated it, felt really sick, got hungry again, emailed Jack again. Was told to keep going and "get tested!". I did, found out that my hormones were terribly disordered and started BHRT. All of which I understood and was able to explain to my open-minded doctor because of Jack's explanations! Now, I feel better and I'm losing at the rate of around 3-3.5 lbs per week. All with no changes in my diet (remember, I was LC paleo before I started the reset). I still hate the CT, still have detox symptoms (muscle soreness, headaches). However, I finally see light at the end of the tunnel...I think I will finally get to a reasonable weight and be able to maintain it. So, 40 lbs gone since finding Jack, 60 lbs down from my high weight, 100 still to go.

Jack, I'm in your camp no matter what. Keep doing what you are doing. Forget the idiots. You are helping more than you can possibly know.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-24T17:35:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-reset-and-ct-discussed-on-marks-daily-apple-today.1578/post-27020>

> **ealachan;27111 wrote:** Wow. Would you really say that to an obese person face-to-face, or only on the internet?

Elachan,

Do you really think this is so offensive? I'm sure Ruby did not mean it to be so.

Having been thin until my mid 30's and then yoyoing up and down as much as 90 lbs overweight for the next 20 years I have lived as a "fat" woman and all that entails...

My overeating is emotional. I am smart. I know how to take care of myself...I don't always do it.

Would you take advice from a shrink whose personal life was a mess?

I think listening to an unhealthy person for health advice is analogous.

I hope I have not offended you...I do not mean to be ugly.

This after reading Kathylu's post...I have been very lucky that my weight hasn't horribly affected my health. HORRIBLY is the key word. It has affected my health...allergies, adult onset asthma, always tired, don't sleep well. I have always assumed that really overweight people are not OK with themselves because I do not feel OK with myself when I am overweight. I also assume that overweight people are not healthy. Maybe I am prejudiced against overweight people and dont realize it?

### ealachan — 2012-05-24T17:43:34+0300
