### chocolate — 2012-06-06T22:02:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29767>

[http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm](http://http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm)

Inhibitory effect of beta-thujaplicin on ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in mouse keratinocytes.

Baba T, Nakano H, Tamai K, Sawamura D, Hanada K, Hashimoto I, Arima Y

Department of Dermatology, Hirosaki University School of Medicine, Japan.

**Sunburn cells are thought to represent ultraviolet B-induced apoptotic keratinocytes. It has been demonstrated that enzymatic and nonenzymatic antioxidants effectively suppress sunburn cell formation, indicating that reactive oxygen species may play a role in the progression of ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis. Metallothionein, a cytosol protein, has antioxidant activity, and overexpression of metallothionein has been reported to reduce the number of sunburn cells in mouse skin. We have also demonstrated that overexpression of metallothionein inhibits ultraviolet B-induced DNA ladder formation in mouse keratinocytes.** These findings support the hypothesis that cellular metallothionein may play an important role in the inhibition of ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes through its antioxidant activity. In the present study, we investigated the effects of beta-thujaplicin, an extract from the woods of Thuja plicata D. Don. and Chamaecyparis obtuse, Sieb. et Zucc., on ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes and on metallothionein induction. Topical application of beta-thujaplicin decreased the number of ultraviolet B-mediated sunburn cells and terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase (TdT)-mediated dUTP-biotin nick end labeling-positive cells in mouse ear skin. Incubation with beta-thujaplicin suppressed ultraviolet B-induced DNA ladder formation in cultured mouse keratinocytes. Histochemical analysis showed that topical application of beta-thujaplicin induced metallothionein protein in mouse skin. Northern analysis and western blotting revealed significant induction of metallothionein mRNA and metallothionein protein, respectively, in beta-thujaplicin-treated cultured mouse keratinocytes. These findings indicate that beta-thujaplicin inhibits ultraviolet B-induced apoptosis in keratinocytes and strongly suggest that the inhibitory mechanism is due to the antioxidant activity of metallothionein induced by the agent.

### chocolate — 2012-06-06T22:08:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29770>

[http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm](http://http://www.ithyroid.com/metallothionein.htm)

Cell Mol Biol (Noisy-le-grand) 2000 Mar;46(2):465-88

Metallothionein in human disease.

Simpkins CO

Hamot Research Center and Tri-State Emergency System, Erie, PA, USA. [phippsdian@aol.com](mailto:phippsdian@aol.com)

\[Medline record in process\]

Evidence concerning a role for metallothionein (MT) in human disease is reviewed. Current knowledge of MT is juxtaposed with our understanding of the pathogenesis of disease. MT is known to modulate three fundamental processes: 1) the release of gaseous mediators such as hydroxyl radical or nitric oxide; 2) apoptosis, and 3) the binding and exchange of heavy metals such as zinc, cadmium or copper. The capability to specifically manipulate MT levels in cells and in mice is beginning to provide answers regarding how MT could impact complex disease scenarios. **Associations among MT and several diseases, including cancer, circulatory and septic shock, coronary artery disease, and Alzheimer's disease have been made. Strong evidence exists that MT modulates the immune system. The primary function of MT remains unknown.**

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T00:29:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyrotoxicosis-selenium-analogues-lithium-candida-peliliculosa-menengitus.1634/post-29793>

[http://www.pnas.org/content/81/17/5494.full.pdf](http://http://www.pnas.org/content/81/17/5494.full.pdf)

> The metallothioneins (MTs) are a group of low molecular
>
> weight proteins that bind heavy metal ions belonging to
>
> groups Ib and IIbj such as Cd2+, Zn2+, and Hg2' and Cu',
>
> Ag+, and Au+, respectively. They are characterized by their
>
> high cysteine content and capacity for metal binding; they
>
> exist in several molecular forms and are classified by their
>
> electrophoretic behavior as either MT-I or MT-Il (1). Yet,
>
> each class might represent more than a single protein species.
>
> Their major role is in the regulation of trace metal
>
> (Zn2+, Cu+) metabolism, acting in the storage of these ions
>
> in the liver. However, since the transcription of MT genes
>
> increases dramatically after exposure to heavy metals (2),
>
> they also, can serve as a protective system against heavy
>
> metal toxicity. The control ofMTgene expression by glucocorticoid
>
> hormones (3, 4) is probably involved in the regulation
>
> of plasma Zn2+ concentration (5). Defects in MT gene
>
> expression have been suggested to be the cause for several
>
> disorders of trace metal metabolism in man-i.e., Menkes
>
> kinky hair syndrome (6, 7), Wilson disease (8), and acrodermatitis
>
> enteropathica (9). Yet, no direct involvement of the
>
> MTgenes in any one of these diseases has been demonstrated.
>
> MTgenes also have been suggested to be involved in the
>
> genetic predisposition of mice to the toxic effects of Cd,
>
> such as testicular necrosis (10), and Cd-induced hypertension
>
> (11) in rats.

Has anyone had any adverse reactions to l-glutamine? I've tried a few times to start taking it to follow Dr. K's leaky gut protocol, but each time I seem to have some crazy reaction to it. The first few times I got extremely irritable and just felt really agitated. I stopped it for awhile and just tried it again today and within 15-20 minutes I felt like I had extremely low blood sugar levels. I finally felt like I was having a breakthrough the last few days because I was able to skip lunch and just have a large, early dinner. I felt so terrible after the dose this morning though that I had no choice but to at least eat a little something this afternoon.

Coincidence or could the l-glutamine cause that somehow? I searched for adverse reactions / side effects, but didn't see anything like that.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27412>

I'm thinking I already know the answer here, but thought I'd ask anyway to see what others think. I'm debating whether

I should do my typical 30-35 minutes in a 50-degree tub or spend longer swimming in the pool we just opened. It's currently around 70-72 degrees, so I know I could stay in longer, but it's also got chlorine to deal with. So, which would you go for?

### Shijin13 — 2012-05-25T21:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27420>

Why not BOTH?

Shoot for the 70 degree pool 1st thing in the am working out.

then 1-3x/week do the 50deg tub

### JC2K — 2012-05-25T22:34:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27430>

The 50 degree pool is probably going to be more efficient. But depending on the size, you might not be able to immerse fully. The 70 degree pool will probably be less boring, since you can swim around.

### BJK77 — 2012-05-26T17:41:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27639>

### JC2K — 2012-05-26T18:55:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/50-degree-tub-or-70-degree-pool.1646/post-27659>

The 50 degree water is better, but that doesn't mean 70 degree water is horrible. Just takes longer. You just do what you can with the resources you have, and don't stress over it.

# Thread: What Causes Acid Reflux/GERD?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-25T23:42:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27455>

Hello Everyone,

I've suffered from acid reflux for 25+ years. Low carb paleo definitely helps, no starch and very little sugar is a blessing. But I still get reflux, especially when I drink coffee or wine. I've even done something similar to NAET to get rid of food sensitivities, and that has helped, but reflux is still an issue.

I've experimented a lot, and I know from reading and experimenting that the reflux is immediately caused by the failure of the lower esophageal sphincter to close properly. I also have a small hiatal hernia, so that is a contributing factor.

One interesting theory I've read is that LOW stomach acid will cause the esophageal sphincter to fail to close properly. Hence the use of Betaine HCl. I can temporarily eliminate the reflux by taking HCl, but sometimes I need to take incredibly large quantities to achieve relief (on the order or 5-10 capsules). Given that HCl relieves the reflux, and antacid makes it worse, I'm inclined toward the low stomach acid theory.

So, my real question, then, is WHY would I have such low stomach acid? How does one "goose" the stomach into cranking out HCl? Is there a leptin/hormone issue at work here? I can't help but think that since my thyroid has been working at half speed for most of the past 30 years that THAT has contributed to the issue.

Any thoughts? Thanks!... Tom

### janagram — 2012-05-26T02:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27507>

I don't have an answer, but I m interested; I have this at night. If I bend over to pick up my shoes or something I can feel it coming on...that must be hiatal hernia or some such thing...and if I take an antacid , it helps, so I may not need the betaine hcl, but I have some in an enzyme combo I take at least once a day. And just what do you do about a hiatal hernia?

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T07:04:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27595>

B6 is needed to make stomach acid.

### tes3@mac.com — 2012-05-26T17:50:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27644>

B6, interesting. I keep getting hints that digestive issues are a chicken/egg problem. Poor absorption of nutrients (ie, B6), causes digestive issues, which then cause more digestive issues, etc. I'll do some B6 research. Thanks.

### MJ* — 2012-05-26T18:57:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-causes-acid-reflux-gerd.1649/post-27661>

I used to suffer terribly from this for many years...I never really knew what caused it but I did begin to notice that the later I ate and then also the time between eating and going to bed was a factor. I would only be symptomatic when I was sleeping. On rare occasions, I would wake up feeling like I was choking...like gasping for air that I could not get, and like you were choking on your own vomit. It would scare the begeesus out of me and my husband, because it would wake him up too, and last for a bit, and there was little I could do to stop it, until it passed - it was always very scary... I did some reading and found out that one way to cope with it was to use an incline pillow, this helped, and I also stopped eating late at night - _ever_ because the trade off of the near death breathing episodes was not worth it...changing my diet has eliminated these symptoms completely!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### Raiken3712 — 2012-05-26T00:19:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-bactieria.1651/post-27471>

So anyway I was treading an article on bacteria in the human system and how some are known to have beneficial effects for varying bodily systems. H.pylori played a part with appetite having something to do with Ghrelin, and B.fragilis was good for the immune system.

Are there any good pages out there with info on what foods you can eat or things you can do to get the ideal amount of these beneficial types. Where exactly do you get H.pylori from? There are also probably different types of H.pylori so I guess it would also be important to know where to get the good kind. I know yogurt, and fermented vegetables have some, but they vary depending on type of yogurt or vegetable as to the types.

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T00:21:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-have-a-good-chai-concentrate-recipe-that-uses-stevia-or-isnt-harsh.1652/post-27473>

[http://yourlife.usatoday.com/fitness-food/recipes/recipe/2012/01/chai-tea-concentrate/610942/1](http://http://yourlife.usatoday.com/fitness-food/recipes/recipe/2012/01/chai-tea-concentrate/610942/1)

Ingredients:

4 tsps. each ground ginger and cinnamon

4 tsps. fennel seeds, ground

2 tsps. each ground black pepper, cardamom and coriander

1 tsp. ground cloves

Â¼ cup loose black tea such as Earl Grey

Â¾ cup blue agave

2 Tbs. molasses

4 tsps. vanilla extract

1 Tb. fresh lemon juice

it seems like a good detox and I think the molasses might even be reasonable to use... I just don't know how to use stevia. I know I could just use lemon peel instead of juice.

### janagram — 2012-05-26T03:51:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-have-a-good-chai-concentrate-recipe-that-uses-stevia-or-isnt-harsh.1652/post-27525>

mmm, looks good, but I would avoid the agave and work out the sweetness with stevia added at the moment of serving. Ahhh, I used to make it commercially, years ago, in large quantities.... I used no ground spices, only dried chopped ginger and cinnamon sticks,whole seeds, etc. and steeped it overnight....but I would try this one. (I am a fan of stevia...it's the no-sugar.)

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T04:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-anyone-have-a-good-chai-concentrate-recipe-that-uses-stevia-or-isnt-harsh.1652/post-27541>

# Thread: good news about curry and cumin... pathway
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T02:01:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-27498>

[http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/25/like.curry.new.biological.role.identified.compound.used.ancient.medicine?utm\_source=feedburner&utm\_medium=feed&utm\_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm\_content](http://http://esciencenews.com/articles/2012/05/25/like.curry.new.biological.role.identified.compound.used.ancient.medicine?utm_source=feedburner&utm_medium=feed&utm_campaign=Feed%3A+eScienceNews%2Fpopular+%28e!+Science+News+-+Popular%29&utm_content)

Like curry? New biological role identified for compound used in ancient medicine

Published: Friday, May 25, 2012 - 14:33 in Health & Medicine

Oregon State University scientists just identified a new reason why some curry dishes, made with spices humans have used for thousands of years, might be good for you. New research has discovered that curcumin, a compound found in the cooking spice turmeric, can cause a modest but measurable increase in levels of a protein that's known to be important in the "innate" immune system, helping to prevent infection in humans and other animals.

This cathelicidin antimicrobial peptide, or CAMP, is part of what helps our immune system fight off various bacteria, viruses or fungi even though they hadn't been encountered before. Prior to this, it was known that CAMP levels were increased by vitamin D.

Discovery of an alternative mechanism to influence or raise CAMP levels is of scientific interest and could open new research avenues in nutrition and pharmacology, scientists said.

### janagram — 2012-05-26T02:31:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-27510>

so...seafood curry anyone?

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T03:12:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-28105>

[http://mct.aacrjournals.org/content/8/8/2348.short](http://http://mct.aacrjournals.org/content/8/8/2348.short)

Curcumin blocks prostaglandin E2 biosynthesis through direct inhibition of the microsomal prostaglandin E2 synthase-1

> Abstract
>
>
>
> Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) plays a crucial role in the apparent link between tumor growth and chronic inflammation. Cyclooxygenase (COX)-2 and microsomal PGE2 synthase-1, which are overexpressed in many cancers, are functionally coupled and thus produce massive PGE2 in various tumors. Curcumin, a polyphenolic β-diketone from tumeric with anti-carcinogenic and anti-inflammatory activities, was shown to suppress PGE2 formation and to block the expression of COX-2 and of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1. Here, we identified microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 as a molecular target of curcumin and we show that inhibition of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 activity is the predominant mechanism of curcumin to suppress PGE2 biosynthesis. Curcumin reversibly inhibited the conversion of PGH2 to PGE2 by microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 in microsomes of interleukin-1β–stimulated A549 lung carcinoma cells with an IC50 of 0.2 to 0.3 μmol/L. **Closely related polyphenols (e.g., resveratrol, coniferyl alcohol, eugenol, rosmarinic acid) failed in this respect, and isolated ovine COX-1 and human recombinant COX-2 were not inhibited by curcumin up to 30 μmol/L. In lipopolysaccharide-stimulated human whole blood, **curcumin inhibited COX-2–derived PGE2 formation from endogenous or from exogenous arachidonic acid, whereas the concomitant formation of COX-2–mediated 6-keto PGF1α and COX-1–derived 12(S)-hydroxy-5-cis-8,10-trans-heptadecatrienoic acid was suppressed only at significant higher concentrations.** Based on the key function of PGE2 in inflammation and carcinogenesis, inhibition of microsomal PGE2 synthase-1 by curcumin provides a molecular basis for its anticarcinogenic and anti-inflammatory activities**. \[Mol Cancer Ther 2009;8(8):2348–55\]

### chocolate — 2012-05-29T03:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/good-news-about-curry-and-cumin-pathway.1654/post-28109>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curcumin](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Curcumin)

**A daily dose of 2 grams of Curcuma domestica extract was found to provide pain relief that was equivalent to ibuprofen for the relief of pain associated with osteoarthritis of the knee.**\[9\] An extensive survey of the literature shows a number of other potential uses and that daily doses over a 3 month period of up to 12 grams proved safe.\[10\] Commercial capsules of curcumin contain piperine, a compound found in pepper which aids absorption of curcumin into the blood stream. However, as curcuma is known to inhibit blood clotting, it should be avoided for a two week period prior to major surgery and not used in conjunction with blood thinners such as warfarin and Plavix. It is also known to aggravate gallstone problems.\[11\]

# Thread: Bipolar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bipolar.1658/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T05:32:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bipolar.1658/post-27579>

Bipolar and thyroid

[http://www.psycheducation.org/thyroid/introduction.htm](http://http://www.psycheducation.org/thyroid/introduction.htm)

Here are two reasons to care about thyroid:

1\. **There is a clear connection between the process of thyroid hormone regulation and bipolar disorder. The problem is, this connection is only just now beginning to become evident, and how the connection works is basically a mystery. Two studies recently showed a strikingly high rate of autoimmune-caused thyroid problems in people with bipolar disorder, far more than you would expect to find.Vonk, Kupka Thyroid problems are more common in the complex forms of bipolar disorder (mixed states and rapid cycling) than in classic bipolar manic patients.Chang Signs of thyroid auto-immunity are much more common in people with anxiety and depression, particularly the forms of anxiety which don't easily fit into typical "anxiety disorder" labels.Carta**

2\. Two studies have shown that people with bipolar depression were less likely to get better if they had low thyroid levels, whereas the ones with higher levels responded pretty well.Cole, Frye . The same phenomenon was recently shown even in "unipolar" depression.Gitlin. These three studies are the basis for a treatment approach you could consider, particularly if depression is your main problem: gently pushing your thyroid status over toward the "hyperthyroid" end of normal, if you happen now to be toward the hypothyroid end of normal (the lab testing we use to place you on this spectrum is explained below). Update 4/2008: this approach, using just a little bit of the standard form of thyroid hormone -- T4, explained below -- was recently tested directly. The results were very positive, but it was a preliminary test with no control group.Lojko

Update 10/09: another research team recently concluded that "reduction in thyroid function can exacerbate bipolar symptoms even in euthyroid subjects." Frye In other words, people who are in the normal range ("euthyroid") can see their bipolar symptoms getting worse if their thyroid levels get low, even if that reduction leaves them still in the normal range.

(Finally, you should also know that some people think the standard lab testing for thyroid status does not do a good job of figuring out who's "normal" and who's not.

# Thread: candida albicans filament
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-26T06:12:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/post-27588>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1142434/](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1142434/)

Induction of the Candida albicans Filamentous Growth Program by Relief of Transcriptional Repression: A Genome-wide AnalysisD in Box

Abstract

Candida albicans, the major human fungal pathogen, undergoes a reversible morphological transition from blastospores (round budding cells) to filaments (elongated cells attached end-to-end). **This transition, which is induced upon exposure of C. albicans cells to a number of host conditions, including serum and body temperature (37Â°C), is required for virulence.** Using whole-genome DNA microarray analysis, we describe 61 genes that are significantly induced (≥2-fold) during the blastospore to filament transition that takes place in response to exposure to serum and 37Â°C. We next show that approximately half of these genes are transcriptionally repressed in the blastospore state by three transcriptional repressors, Rfg1, Nrg1, and Tup1. We conclude that the relief of this transcriptional repression plays a key role in bringing the C. albicans filamentous growth program into play, and we describe the framework of this transcriptional circuit.

### chocolate — 2012-05-26T16:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-albicans-filament.1659/post-27623>

I read on several websites that the presence of biotin inhibits the growth of candida albicans, it can't convert to virulent in the gut. The biotin is usually made by the good bacteria.

[http://www.nativeremedies.com/articles/the-roll-of-biotin-in-the-treatment-of-candida.html](http://http://www.nativeremedies.com/articles/the-roll-of-biotin-in-the-treatment-of-candida.html)

[http://www.flora-balance.com/images/biotin\_study\_top.jpg](http://http://www.flora-balance.com/images/biotin_study_top.jpg)

"Mycelial development and chemical alteration of Candida Albicans from biotin insufficiency." Yamaguchi H. Sabouraudia 1974 Nov (left slide - optimal biotin = yeast cell growth, right slide - sub-optimal levels of biotin = systemic candida growth)

I can find the pub meds, but no abstracts... he's done a ton of mitochondrial studies on candida also.

[http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/vitamins/biotin/](http://http://lpi.oregonstate.edu/infocenter/vitamins/biotin/)

Biotin

Biotin is a water-soluble vitamin that is generally classified as a B-complex vitamin. After the initial discovery of biotin, nearly 40 years of research were required to establish it as a vitamin (1). Biotin is required by all organisms **but can be synthesized only by bacteria, yeasts, molds, algae, and some plant species (2).**

So, I wonder if its a quorum signaling fake out. The body doesn't like to digest sugars in the winter according to several nutrition websites and I can't find a source for that.

> The anti-microbial activity observed in the fermented samples containing 33 g/L total acid (7 g/L acetic acid) was significant against the tested gram positive and gram negative pathogenic organisms. **Candida albicans was not inhibited by Kombucha. Tea, at drinkable levels, demonstrated no anti-microbial properties.** The contribution of tea itself to the anti-microbial activity of Kombucha proved to be insignificant in the tested organisms, even at the highest levels tested. As a result, the anti-microbial activity of Kombucha was from the acetic acid composition.

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T20:33:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27684>

My sister just sent me a pdf of a Japanese article about "Senobi breathing" as a treatment for obesity.

It's too big to attach. (1.21 mb) but I think you can download it from this link:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20834183](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/20834183)

It talks about obese people having blunted hormone responses and "neuroadrenergic abnormalities".

Apparently this breathing exercise (which is easy) warms up brown fat and improves growth hormone response.

Has anyone else tried this? Has there been a discussion of it here, which I just missed?

### Melinda — 2012-05-26T21:41:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/senobi-breathing.1664/post-27693>

It was brought up in the comments to CPC#4. Dr. K said he'd look into it. I've been doing it before meals and anytime I feel mental hunger when I shouldn't. I think it helps me feel calm but way too early to quantify.

Thanks, Melinda! I'll go check the comments of CPC#4, which should take awhile, but is sure to yield other interesting ideas. And starting today I'll try this several times a day and see what comes of it.

I think that overcoming metabolic syndrome may end up (for me, at least) like putting together a jigsaw puzzle. One little piece, another little piece -- oops, that's the wrong place, try again -- and eventually the picture begins to emerge in little chunks.

I'm doing the hunt and peck method myself. I know testing is the best plan forward but money and circumstances have that on hold for now. I'm keeping a daily chart of some metrics that are important to me i.e. energy, mental clarity, amount and quality of sleep, CT etc...so if I add something or change something, I can attribute it to a part of the protocol.

It's at comment #147 at CPC#4

### skline@peak.org — 2012-05-26T22:20:40+0300

Thanks, Melinda, that will save me some time.

I keep a log, with daily weight (in spite of everyone dissing the scale, I think I know how to keep from overreacting), BG in the a.m. and sometimes other times of day, roughly what I ate, and other factors which might be affecting sleep, weight, soreness, digestion, etc.

I know that testing would give a lot of numbers and maybe some good information -- on the other hand, one would have to keep doing it to see if one's responses to it were working; it's expensive; and like everything else in life, it has an error rate. So I do a little testing like with the glucometer, but put off the major lab reports.

If I were coming up to an appointment with Doc, I'd test like mad, because he would know what to do about the results. As it is, I try this and that and see how things go. We are analog systems and not digital, after all. I think it's more valuable to try doing without gluten for a month than to get tested for the antibodies to it while still eating it. Results don't lie. .... well, often.

Doc answered that he was trying it with a kettleball, but wasn't getting results. I'm doing it several times a day for about a half minute. I'll increase time over the next week or so. As summer advances, I'll be sure to do it outdoors very early in the morning, so I get the advantage of whatever coolness there is. We're having a very cold and wet spring in western Oregon, which is helpful for me, especially this year, though not for plants like tomatoes.

# Thread: How Long on LR?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NickieG — 2012-05-26T21:39:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-27692>

How long have you folks stayed on the LR diet? How do you know when it is time to do something different?

I have been on the LRx for 3 months now. While I have not lost weight in terms of numbers on the scale, my weight is stable. My clothes fit differently i.e. looser and I need to buy new jeans as all the ones I own are now big on me.

I have been doing CT for about a month. I started with face dunks which I love. Moved to cold showers and have recently begun to fill the tub with cold water and sit then lay in it and read a book. I am not loving this yet and am doing it.

I do love the results. My skin is softer. The skin on my feet has changed tremendously as evidenced by going to get a pedicure. Little dead skin to be cut off. I am sleeping well. No more waking up at 3 or 4 am. However, up evening I got extremely angry at myself about something and didn't sleep well at all. I was able to see a clear correlation between my perceived stress and poor sleep. CTed the next night and slept well.

Not sure how long to keep doing the LRx and would love to hear how others knew it was time to do something different. Thanks you guys!

Nickie

### David — 2012-06-02T14:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-29027>

Nickie,

I have continued the Leptin Rx for about 16 weeks and have been progressing with CT for about half that time. I have lost weight, and I have also experienced many of the changes you have noted. Several times I have experienced a net weight gain for a week or so, always followed by a loss to a lower net weight, so the program has not been a smooth transition to a lower weight for me by any means.

During his first webinar, Dr Kruse noted in passing that one cannot really be Leptin sensitive if still obese, which I interpret as having a BMI over 30, so I will ignore the 6-8 week Rx estimate and stay on LR reset for now. By the way, I changed my entire lifestyle to conform to the Paleo Diet (ala Robb Wolf) last November and have lost about 50 pounds.

I have been doing the LRx since I started last Nov. except for the supplements. This is how I live now and it's great. BAB totally paleo. I haven't lost weight but have lost body fat and gained muscle. Stick with it it gets better everyday.

i did 6 months on the LRx without a missed day. during that time i tried an hcg round and gave it up at day 11 because i was craving more protein/fat then i could get in on the Simeons protoocl. very poor weight loss but very easy to do. then i over ate like crazy for a week and picked up 7 lbs. about 3000 cals protein/fat instead of the 1650 i usually ate. did JUDDD for 6 weeks and dropped 10 lbs. then a 5 week break still doing the LRx. then ended the LRx at 6 months and 1 week. because i had all the signs that were spelled out in the FAQ. i am doing JUDDD again down 2.5 in my first week. still paleo and my first meal is very high protein but not always within 30 mins or even 60 mins of awakening.

Speaking of scale, I have been very stable...to stable, for a good three months, and this week something happened and I've hit two new lows. Usually when I hit a new low it only lasts a day and can take a long time to see it again. This week both came and stuck. We will see what the weekend brings! The only think I'm doing different is I added a little bit of swimming laps in last two weeks now that pool is open. Nothing hard core but the pool is cool...not as cold as I would have liked although everyone around me complains about it being cold!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30272>

MartiD do yourself a big favor......bite the bullet and get a real body comp DEXA and you'll find out just how good/bad off you are. I do them q18 months

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-09T19:55:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30301>

I cannot imagine eating any other way. I gave up grains over a year ago except for oatmeal. Then gave that up the first of 2012. It was at that time I started the LRx. The eating and CT are a way of life and I really don't miss any other the foods I don't eat.

### janagram — 2012-06-13T18:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30912>

I need to tighten up the protocols! I am known to eat rice crackers at a party, drink too much wine....but that's about the extent of my evildoing....

My bp was high at the doc yesterday....140/90, its usually great, so....? Could it be the coffee?

My sleep has slipped a bit and I end up dreaming too much and not getting into deep sleep...also waking repeatedly thru the night...I call it surf-sleeping.

Folks, this site keeps me keeping on, so, thank you all, I really want to perfect my lifestyle to the point where I am healthy and the right weight.

### ealachan — 2012-06-13T18:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30917>

> **janagram;31016 wrote:** I need to tighten up the protocols! I am known to eat rice crackers at a party, drink too much wine....but that's about the extent of my evildoing....
>
> My bp was high at the doc yesterday....140/90, its usually great, so....? Could it be the coffee?
>
> My sleep has slipped a bit and I end up dreaming too much and not getting into deep sleep...also waking repeatedly thru the night...I call it surf-sleeping.
>
> Folks, this site keeps me keeping on, so, thank you all, I really want to perfect my lifestyle to the point where I am healthy and the right weight.

re: blood pressure - mine always reads about 20 points higher in the doctor's office than it does at home, sometimes even higher if they use the wrong size cuff for my arm. Plus, one blood pressure reading is just a snapshot of that particular second. It doesn't mean your BP is running high all the time. If you're concerned about it and have a cuff at home (or have the means to buy one...they're not that expensive), you can try taking it every day at varying times for a few weeks to get an idea of what it's doing on the longer term. If you DO buy a cuff to monitor your BP at home, make sure you measure your arm and get the correct size of cuff, otherwise your readings will be inaccurate.

### janagram — 2012-06-13T19:34:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30932>

yeah, good point. gotta keep it in perspective. thanks.

### NickieG — 2012-06-13T22:28:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-30969>

> **David;29126 wrote:** Nickie,
>
> I have continued the Leptin Rx for about 16 weeks and have been progressing with CT for about half that time. I have lost weight, and I have also experienced many of the changes you have noted. Several times I have experienced a net weight gain for a week or so, always followed by a loss to a lower net weight, so the program has not been a smooth transition to a lower weight for me by any means.

David....thanks for this...I have noticed over the past week that my weight has gone up three to four pounds.

I have also noticed headaches every day. I rarely have headaches so I am assuming I am detoxing something. I have continued to spend some of my shower time in cold water and also spot CTing. I have also been experiencing stress at work with another individual. My strategy has been to say what is on my mind, then be polite when I see her and otherwise steer clear of her. Luckily she is temporary at work.

I will continue to stay with the Leptin RX way of eating. I find it very easy overall. I did go to a wedding last week and the meal was not food I would eat and the timing was off. I chose not to have anything. The most uncomfortable thing was the multiple times I no thank you to someone offering me food, then offering me something else, then something else. I have finally learned to joke about my "weird" way of eating. Seems to work better than simply saying "no thank you".

I appreciate everyones responses....I am learning so much.

Nickie

### janagram — 2012-06-20T03:44:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-long-on-lr.1665/post-32313>

after joking about your weird way of eating, whip out your blublocker wraparound glasses and ask for an ice pack.

# Thread: F-lux on iOS

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis

### pthomaslandry@gmail.com — 2012-05-27T02:01:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27752>

Just a PSA

New release of Absinthe 2.01 jailbreaks the latest iOS, and you can install f.lux via cydia your iPad, iPhone, etc... I'm posting this from a nicely filtered iPad3.

(google absinthe 2.01 and use at your own risk)

### indigogirl — 2012-05-27T06:18:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27799>

I'm afraid to put it on my iPad when it says use at your own risk!!

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-05-27T06:52:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27802>

F.lux is perfectly safe; I've used it for ages on my laptop, and have had it on my iPhone while jail broken. Now that a JB is out for iOS 5.1.1, I fully intend to put it on both my iPhone and iPad.

That said, it's just easier to put on some blue-blocking glasses, as that will block the blue from whatever device, not just one with flux installed.

For those who don't know, f.lux (on the iPhone/iPad) is an app for jailbroken devices, and only jailbreak if you are technically savvy and know what you're doing. For those that do, it's handy to have it available.

### indigogirl — 2012-05-27T06:54:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27804>

Thanks!

I'm not tech savvy so I'll just keep wearing my blue blockers.

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-05-27T07:06:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27806>

Yeah, to tell the truth my blue-blockers have essentially obviated the need for things like f.lux; it's just handy for those moments when I turn over in bed and check my phone without having to first fumble around on the nightstand for the blue blockers.

### hellojtm — 2012-05-27T22:11:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27914>

jesse

### hellojtm — 2012-05-27T22:13:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-27915>

> **August;27946 wrote:** I think you could also go into settings -> general -> Accessibility .... and turn "White on Black" ...ON

It just inverts the display, so you will still be emitting the blue light, wherever there used to be orange light. i have this set to triple-click on the home button.

jesse

### cuivie@me.com — 2012-09-13T21:26:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-51493>

Just wanted to follow up on what I said earlier about f.lux being obviates with blue-blocker glasses. After reading more about Jack's concerns with exposure to \_artificial \_amounts of blue light and how electronic devices produce more blue light than than you'd get with normal midday sun exposure, I decided to start using flux again specifically for daytime use.

Most devices now, including phones and TVs, default to a 6500K color temperature. The midday sun is 5500K. So Instead of using flux for its main purpose of lowering the color temp of the screen after sundown (when I'm wearing blue blockers anyways), I'm now primarily using it to set the screen color temps to 5500K during the daytime to help mitigate some of the excess blue light.

This will make your screen seem yellow at first, but you get used it after a while. If you need to do precision color work based on 6500K, just temporarily disable flux.

### freshveggies — 2012-09-13T21:36:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-51494>

This is exactly what I have done now. Set Flux to night mode during the day too.

### lioness7 — 2012-09-13T23:42:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/f-lux-on-ios.1668/post-51510>

Cuivie, I think you are correct to use i flux again. Though I was first impressed with the brilliance and contrast on my Mac last year, a month later I installed i.flux and liked the way it dimmed the screen over the course of the day. About six months ago, I turned down both the daylight and the night time settings to the lowest it would go and use this all day long whenever I am on - blue blockers of course in the evening.... It took a little bit to get used to, ( like less than an hour) but now I can't stand to look at the screen if it were to be any brighter. It feels so much more relaxing to read off the screen now, which was not apparent to me last year not having anything to compare to at the time.

> **CuiviÃ«;52081 wrote:** Just wanted to follow up on what I said earlier about f.lux being obviates with blue-blocker glasses. # Thread: put your baby outside?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Birdy — 2012-05-27T03:53:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27783>

[http://joannagoddard.blogspot.com/2011/09/motherhood-mondays-prams-in-denmark.html](http://http://joannagoddard.blogspot.com/2011/09/motherhood-mondays-prams-in-denmark.html)I have heard it is common, have your baby sleep in the cold, at least an hour a day. If a baby is cold-adapted, does it confer lifelong good stuff?

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-27T03:57:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27784>

I have a niece who goes to a kindergarten where she's outside all day. They have a tiny little RV in case it totally starts pouring. But usually, they are outside all day

### indigogirl — 2012-05-27T06:06:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27798>

I'm 59 and when I was a baby my mother would bundle me up in my carriage and put me outside for at least an hour each day...so I was told. I think this was common practice.

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T12:59:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27817>

> **Birdy;27881 wrote:** [http://joannagoddard.blogspot.com/2011/09/motherhood-mondays-prams-in-denmark.html](http://http://joannagoddard.blogspot.com/2011/09/motherhood-mondays-prams-in-denmark.html)I have heard it is common, have your baby sleep in the cold, at least an hour a day. If a baby is cold-adapted, does it confer lifelong good stuff?

OMG those pictures made my palms sweat...being a NYer my first thought is someone's going to steal that baby!

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T13:00:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27818>

> **Souldanzer;27882 wrote:** I have a niece who goes to a kindergarten where she's outside all day. They have a tiny little RV in case it totally starts pouring. But usually, they are outside all day

That's awesome! I used to make the time to take my class out everyday unless it was lightening...I could not believe how many teachers didn't want to do this...fresh air feeds the brain...and when the kids know it's coming they don't complain, they look forward to it!

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T13:02:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27819>

> **indigogirl;27896 wrote:** I'm 59 and when I was a baby my mother would bundle me up in my carriage and put me outside for at least an hour each day...so I was told. I think this was common practice.

I remember my mom talking about doing this and she'd tell us to do it with our kids, I'd do it sometimes with my kids but in the backyard, but not in real cold weather...least I forget to bring them in

### Adria — 2012-05-27T15:54:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-27830>

I remember reading about youth training in the old olympic sports schools in the Soviet Union, the young boys would sleep outside in the snow in just their pajamas, with no blanket. Not sure if this is common anymore.

Growing up in cold climate, we'd play all day outside starting as toddlers. We'd come in with ice formed on our eyelashes and our knitted mittens would be caked in ice.

### Birdy — 2012-05-29T18:22:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-28187>

very good question Adria, and I was hoping someone would bite. There are lots of reasons to be and sleep cold in the Soviet Union besides being a future Olympian. Then again, lets say someone had grown up cold due to poverty or dad not paying the fuel bill. Would kids who sleep outside or without blankets become better Olympians? Does it depend on the sport?

### Birdy — 2012-05-29T18:23:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-28189>

I had a rule. If I turned blue I had to come inside. It was usually for a change of clothing and to turn pink, then outside again.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-29T19:43:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-28207>

> **Birdy;28288 wrote:** I had a rule. If I turned blue I had to come inside. It was usually for a change of clothing and to turn pink, then outside again.

hehe, we had the same rule  I don't know how kids can be happy these days... inside all day behind technology. Playing outside was our very favorite thing to do.

### MamaGrok — 2012-05-30T01:17:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-28250>

just sent mine out for the third time today. "No, you can't go in for more TV like the neighbors just did ... again. What gives you the idea I'd say yes?" I do it all winter, and in the rain, too. Lightning's the only reason to stay inside. (Okay, hail, tornadoes, etc.)

### PaulaRichards — 2012-05-30T02:05:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/put-your-baby-outside.1671/post-28257>

My mother and grandmother used to put all the babies outside in their carriages for naps. According to them, we all had the bests naps outside!

### Birdy — 2012-05-30T03:09:59+0300

# Thread: Candida resource page... lots of very common vitamin deficiencies

**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-27T17:32:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-resource-page-lots-of-very-common-vitamin-deficiencies.1674/post-27844>

> According to the conditions it finds, candida can exist in 3 forms; a budding, multiplying yeast, a hyphae form, which penetrates the gut wall in search of nutrition in the blood and as highly invasive spores, which penetrate the gut wall to be transported in the blood stream to other organs.
>
>
>
> Once the yeast has overgrown the gut, malnutrition will result, in the form of vitamin and trace nutrient deficiencies. In addition, the constant presence of a pathogen will stimulate the immune system 24 x 7 x 365, eventually causing immune deficiency (as found in CFS) and severe fatigue. Victims will experience a multitude of symptoms caused by malnutition, an impaired immune system, toxins from the yeast's metabolic processes and from the physical presence of the yeast."

[http://www.gilbertssyndrome.com/candida.php](http://http://www.gilbertssyndrome.com/candida.php)

simple, easy to understand article [http://docakilah.wordpress.com/2011/10/21/the-cause-prevention-and-detoxing-of-candida-yeast/](http://http://docakilah.wordpress.com/2011/10/21/the-cause-prevention-and-detoxing-of-candida-yeast/)

I'm not really sure malabsorption is the right word. Flat out parasitic might be better. The nutrients get stolen and it occurs at night usually, when the candida makes the ethanol, that causes the fatty liver that causes the metabolic syndrome.

# Thread: Are Cravings a Food/Season Mismatch?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-05-27T19:26:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27871>

The title isn't a perfect match for my thoughts, but close enough.

I tried to lose weight by low carbing for almost 10 years. Whenever I had been eating a SAD diet for several months, I could low carb for about 12 weeks (on average) before I would have any cravings. And when cravings started, they continued day after day.

For me, 12 weeks meant I lost about 15% of my starting weight. I started looking at stats of others and found that many people either hit a stall or started having cravings when they had lost between 10%-20% of their start weight. Since I didn't have access to their start date, I made the assumption that if they lost 10%-20% of their weight, that probably took them 2 - 4 months.

For me it was about 12 weeks and in general, it seems it was 8-16 weeks or 12 week average. What is 12 weeks? Approximately 1/4 of a year or 1 season.

Stick with me, almost done.

So if a SAD diet simulates a fall diet and a low carb diet simulates a winter diet, could it be that cravings are telling us low carbers it is time to change to a Spring diet?

### AKMan — 2012-05-27T20:26:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27885>

On the face this makes sense, but I doubt it's the case. Here's why: The normal low-carb diet is filled with O6 and the normal life doesn't account for reduced sunlight as found in Winter.

I think that the typical SAD eater is experiencing an endless Summer--not so much Fall. High carbs, long light cycles, accumulating visceral and sub Q fat, cells membrane filled with O6. An abrupt switch to Atkins, let's say, with lots of "healthy vegetable oil" for fat and lots of bacon and butter and sour cream and lots of store bought red meat and chicken, without less light, no thought to sleep, and with no cold probably doesn't signal "Winter" to your brain. It probably signals, "WTF?"

Now, go from SAD to paleo/primal blueprint type diet, rich in fish, and ketogenic with lots of good grass fed beef/bone broth/fish broth and also adjust lights to match the season, sleep well, move a lot, and get some good cold exposure to give every signal of winter and your body should start to cycle O3 for O6 in the cells, and burn through visceral/sub-Q fat like nobody's business.

So, your basic thought probably has some merit, but the typical low-carb diet doesn't do it justice. I have been telling people who comment on my weight loss to wait until Fall to start trying to lose weight. I tell them to start exercising and lose the sugar, wheat, and vegetable oil right now, but not to go low carb or do anything else differently until it starts getting chilly and the day shortens.

### Huck — 2012-05-27T21:16:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-cravings-a-food-season-mismatch.1675/post-27901>

> **AKMan;27984 wrote:** I have been telling people who comment on my weight loss to wait until Fall to start trying to lose weight. I tell them to start exercising and lose the sugar, wheat, and vegetable oil right now, but not to go low carb or do anything else differently until it starts getting chilly and the day shortens.

That's close to what I was thinking, but Fall is when we fatten up for winter. Wouldn't it make more sense to start a ketogenic diet in Winter?

# Thread: The embarrassment of cold adapting!

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 55

### MJ* — 2012-05-27T20:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27888>

I live in a beach community, really small lots of landâ€¦ for 20somehting years we had essentially no neighbor behind usâ€¦there was an old woman but she never showed herself, she had lots of family in the area but she liked to stay inside, so we had a lot of privacyâ€¦weâ€™re hardly in our yard, just always at soccer games etc., last year we decide to get a pool, letâ€™s be home more with the kids etc., enjoy our privacy â€“ _the old woman dies! One of her grandkids takes the house, cuts down a bunch of the trees, big holesâ€¦their house now has clear views because of their deck that you could never even see before, who knew? And to boot, second floor windowsâ€¦so Iâ€™m letting my dog out, I open the door like I always do, half paying attentionâ€¦still thinking Iâ€™m in privacy landâ€¦forgetting that I have a pair of undies on over my yoga pants to hold in my ice packs so they stay in place, bright white ice packs hanging out of my underwear that is now overwear!!! I look up and lock eyes with the new young couple as they sip their coffee on their deck facing my back door, I have yet to meet them holy \*\*\*\*_â€¦I die because I canâ€™t just take the ice packs out of my underwear in front of themâ€¦I slink down, as if Iâ€™m picking something up and crawl awayâ€¦

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-27T21:47:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27910>

Cavemam - nudity is all around you, you just don't know it. More people than you think enjoy a bit of nudity.

A former landlord used to have a hot tub within sight of the little place I was renting - she informed me once before I signed the lease about the nudity I had to expect and that was that. She granted me the same freedom around the property

I got the same intro before getting a job on a farm a few years back - was informed of the nudity I had to expect around the farm and I can leave now if it bugs me. He also offered the same to me - I passed on this one...

All in Boulder county  I do think I'd feel a little more embarrassed about the ice pack tied to body parts look.

### QuantumRadical — 2012-05-28T02:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27943>

You pick your house. God picks your neighbors!

Don't you just wish people would just mind their own business?

MJ, instead of fight or flight, you could have asked them to join the ice party.

On nudity. Isn't clothing a neolithic thought?

Happy Memorial Day Weekend to you all. Yesterday I did my first three-bagger (48 lbs of ice) in the bathtub. Used my IR gun to see how cold. 30.2degrees F. Got in, total shocker. Went numb within a minute. Stayed in three minutes and had totally bright red skin. Quite a burning experience. Got out, sunbathed for 30 minutes per side to warm up. Got back into the tub again, lasted 5 minutes--water temp was still brutal. Waited about two more hours, got in again for 40 min this time with water tmp about 43. Best CT day ever. Until today. Only a two-bagger (32lbs of ice) and was quite relaxed for 30 minutes, then started mild shivering tremors. Got out at 40 min. Water temp was 40 F. Sunbathed immediately for 25 min each side. Never felt better!

Ohh, now I want to know! I hatehatehate most clothing  I must have missed out somehow on the epigenetic change that makes you think restrictive rubber bands, padding/wires (!!), and belts around your torso must be a good thing. They drive me insane.

### Souldanzer — 2012-05-28T05:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-27976>

Looks like the NY times has a suggestion on what's going on with the missing fur...

[http://www.nytimes.com/2003/08/19/science/why-humans-and-their-fur-parted-ways.html?pagewanted=all&src=pm](http://http://www.nytimes.com/2003/08/19/science/why-humans-and-their-fur-parted-ways.html?pagewanted=all&src=pm)

> It implies we were naked for more than a million years before we started wearing clothes....
>
>
>
> One theory holds that the hominid line went through a semi-aquatic phase -- witness the slight webbing on our hands....
>
>
>
> Humans lost their body hair, they say, to free themselves of external parasites that infest fur -- blood-sucking lice, fleas and ticks and the diseases they spread....
>
>
>
> Among the newly furless humans, bare skin would have served, like the peacock's tail, as a signal of fitness.

I'm sure Dr JK has a different explanation entirely  It certainly would explain why some of the young girls these days appear to think the more skin exposed the better...

### Inger — 2012-05-28T11:37:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-embarrassment-of-cold-adapting.1676/post-28002>

hahaha.. this is a fun thread..

I have to tell what happened to me the other day...

I do my CT in the river behind my parents house where I live these days cause me and hubby are separated.

I always do it naked cause there are no one (usually... I always check out before if anyone is close).

So, this morning I sit there under the small bridge, singing looooud.. I was so happy and all was so beautiful and the river ends right there in a small waterfall that makes noice so I thought no one will hear me sing that loud anyway. Great.. all of a sudden there comes a man (a worker with his working-clothes on) to check out something on the bridge / waterfall (it is a factory far away that takes their water from it).. and he saw me sitting there naked in the river singing loud.. Oh my.. I stopped singing for sure.. I was feeling so ashamed I do not knew where to look honestly. Thanks god he did not stay for long...Things happens in CT life.. haha..

Inger

### MJ* — 2012-05-28T14:10:19+0300

LoL oh my gawd Inger thanks for sharing...first I want to say, I am really happy for you that you could find such a great sense of joy at as I like to say, "being one with nature". The fact that you want to be singing out loud like that and enjoying nature so much that you get lost in it, that is so cool. I think our little Long Island swim group felt some of that joy yesterday! You probably made his day LoL LoL LoL (I can only imagine the story he had to tell from his view point), and at least it's unlikely you'll ever see him again to find out - that or he'll be fixing that bridge a whole lot more now.

Maybe one day everyone will learn to be ok with all of it...for now, that was so funny, I just loved your story!

# Thread: Brothing alternative?  DIY mineral supplements...free ish
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-27T21:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/post-27911>

[http://www.healingnaturallybybee.com/articles/supp2.php](http://http://www.healingnaturallybybee.com/articles/supp2.php)

Calcium Made From Eggshells

Â© Copyright Bee Wilder

Revised February 14, 2011 to clarify the amount of calcium in eggshells, and to include more information on taking calcium and magnesium.

Eggshell Nutrients

Eggshell calcium presents healthy, balanced calcium due to trace amounts of other minerals contained in it. Eggshell calcium is probably the best natural source of calcium, and it is easier for your body to digest and absorb.

Dutch researchers have reported recently a highly positive effect of eggshell calcium (with added magnesium and vitamin D) on bone mineral density in a scientific study (double blind, placebo-controlled). Laboratory test and measures of bone density were carefully made in these studies. The eggshell supplemented group had measurable increases in bone density in their hip bones, after one year.

The ideal bone-building combination of eggshell calcium and vitamin D3 was also well documented in Japanese studies. Researchers at the Japan Women's University, Yokyo studied a combination of vitamin D3 and eggshell powder in animals with osteoporosis. **Not only was the eggshell powder with vitamin D3 able to improve bone mineral density, but it did it without significantly increasing blood calcium levels.

You can use any kind of egg (chicken, goose, duck), but it is best to use organic or certified organic eggs from free-range birds. If the bird does not get proper nutrients the eggshells won't contain the nutrients we need.

How Much to Take?

One whole medium sized eggshell makes about one teaspoon of powder, which yields about 750 – 800 mgs of elemental calcium plus other microelements, i.e. magnesium, boron, copper, iron, manganese, molybdenum, sulphur, silicon, zinc, etc. There are 27 elements in total. The composition of an eggshell is very similar to that of our bones and teeth.

Elemental Amount: Eggshells contain calcium carbonate, and approximately 40% of it is absorbable. The amount absorbed is called the elemental amount.**

People who are intolerant of dairy products require 300 mgs twice a day of calcium, in addition to calcium from other food sources, so you would take a little less than 1/2 teaspoon of the eggshell powder two times a day. Also take 350 mgs of magnesium citrate two times a day at the same time. Calcium and magnesium need to be taken in at least equal amounts, and some people require more magnesium than calcium depending upon how much they obtain from their diet. Do not take more than 500 mgs of calcium at one time because your body is not able to absorb more than that at one time.

However, people who are tolerant of dairy products and/or obtain other high sources of calcium in daily, i.e. sardines or mineral-rich bone broths, may only need to take 300 mg once a day, but they often require more magnesium, so they should start by taking 400 mg of magnesium once a day as well.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-05-28T07:41:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brothing-alternative-diy-mineral-supplements-free-ish.1677/post-27996>

We save our egg shells, clean them with water, then dry them in a low oven. Then we grind them up - we add them to our dog's food.

# Thread: Optimal seafood soup
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaleoDentist — 2012-05-27T23:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/post-27924>

This is a take on a thai coconut soup. it is a great way to get your iodine and seafood in an exotic dish! experiment with other seafood and seaweed. (scallops is a good example)

Ingredients:

1/2 cup sliced, unpeeled ginger

3 lemon grass stalks sliced (sub juice of 2 limes)

1 32 oz container of organic low salt chicken broth (or make your own)

2 cans full fat coconut milk

1/2 lb shrimp cut in bite size pieces depending on size

1/2 lb squid cut into rectangles

1/2 lb white flaky fish (I used cod pieces from Trader Joes)

2 tbsp fish sauce

10 schitake mushrooms sliced thin (sub any mushroom)

1 package of dried wakame seaweed (Whole Foods)

juice of 2 limes

sea salt to taste

Heat chicken stock and add lemon grass and ginger, bring to boil and simmer 20-30 minmin a small pot.

Add remaining ingredients to large pot. strain infused chicken stock into large pot, discard the lemon grass and ginger.

bring to a gentle boil and simmer for 5 min. this should gently cook the seafood without making the squid tough.add salt to taste or more fish sauce if you like the flavor.

I'll post a pic later.

enjoy!

### Melinda — 2012-05-28T00:36:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-seafood-soup.1678/post-27930>

Very nice reading receipt. I made something similar yesterday but didn't have a second can of coconut milk so I poured in about 6-8oz of heavy cream at the time of adding the seafood. I liked the texture better than with just coconut milk.

Thanks for posting it. I look forward to the pictures.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-labs-decrypting-thread.1679/post-27926>

This thread is for all of us who want to understand what each of the labs mean.

The goal here is to go over each lab test that Dr. Kruse recommends and teach all of us what it means as it relates to our health. There a re some physicians here, maybe they can chyme in. Of course it would be great if Dr Kruse makes an appearance.

specifically: what the lab tests for, the normal range and what the highs and lows are. In addition, it would be great to have a discussion how to improve or normalize each lab result.

This would help the non-docs better understand the mubo-jumbo that they stare at when they get their blood work back.

when adding to the info, use the "quote" feature so it is easier to follow.

Recommended Artifacts for a Complete

BodyBio Report

If you decide to obtain a new laboratory blood test, please show this form to your health

care practitioner. Ask your doctor to order your bloodwork so that ALL of the artifacts

listed below are included. Although you may receive a BodyBio Report on just 4

artifacts, this will help you to obtain the most thorough information possible in your next

BodyBio Report.

Eat no food for 8-12 hours (usually over night) prior to your blood draw

The following information must appear on the lab requisition form:

Your Name, Date of Birth, & Gender

The Doctor’s Name, Address & Telephone Number

The Date & Time of the blood collection

BLOOD CHEMISTRY PANEL:

Alanine Transaminase (ALT or SGPT)

Albumin

Alkaline Phosphatase

Asparate Transaminase (AST or SGOT)

Bilirubin, Total

BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)

Calcium

CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

Chloride

Cholesterol, Total

Creatinine

Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)

Globulin

Glucose

HDL

LDL

Iron, Total

Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

Phosphorus

Potassium

Protein, Total

Sodium

Triglycerides

Uric Acid

COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT

w/ MANUAL DIFFERENTIAL:

WBC

RBC

RDW

Hemoglobin

Hematocrit

MCV

MCH

MCHC

Mean Platelet Volume

Platelet Count

Neutrophils

Lymphocytes

Basophils

Eosinophils

Monocytes

.

# Thread: short chain fatty acids
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-28T03:10:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/post-27952>

[http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/61/2/364](http://http://diabetes.diabetesjournals.org/content/61/2/364)

Short-Chain Fatty Acids Stimulate Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Secretion via the G-Proteinâ€“Coupled Receptor FFAR2

Abstract

Interest in how the gut microbiome can influence the metabolic state of the host has recently heightened. One postulated link is bacterial fermentation of â€œindigestibleâ€￾ prebiotics to short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), which in turn modulate the release of gut hormones controlling insulin release and appetite. We show here that SCFAs trigger secretion of the incretin hormone glucagon-like peptide (GLP)-1 from mixed colonic cultures in vitro. Quantitative PCR revealed enriched expression of the SCFA receptors ffar2 (grp43) and ffar3 (gpr41) in GLP-1â€“secreting L cells, and consistent with the reported coupling of GPR43 to Gq signaling pathways, SCFAs raised cytosolic Ca2+ in L cells in primary culture. Mice lacking ffar2 or ffar3 exhibited reduced SCFA-triggered GLP-1 secretion in vitro and in vivo and a parallel impairment of glucose tolerance. These results highlight SCFAs and their receptors as potential targets for the treatment of diabetes.

### chocolate — 2012-05-28T03:18:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-chain-fatty-acids.1680/post-27954>

[http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Short\_chain\_fatty\_acids](http://http://drmyhill.co.uk/wiki/Short_chain_fatty_acids)

DESCRIPTION

Short chain fatty acids are produced in the large bowel as a result of bacterial fermentation of soluble fibre. They can provide over 500 kcals a day of energy which is especially useful when blood sugar levels fall low because mitochondria like to use SCFAs as an energy source. At night we all switch to SCFAs as blood sugar levels fall and this prevents hypoglycaemia at night. Night-time hypoglycaemia is a common cause of disturbed sleep.

This test looks for a hypoglycaemic tendency by measuring levels of short chain fatty acids first thing in the morning before breakfast has been taken. The logic is that when there is hypoglycaemia, the body switches early to using short chain fatty acids for energy supply and these will then deplete. Therefore, low levels of short chain fatty acids indicate a hypoglycaemic tendency. Hypoglycaemia is a common problem for people with a fermenting gut, so the test may indirectly be pointing to a problem with yeast or bacterial overgrowth in the bowel.

Low levels of SCFAs may also be indicative of a low fibre diet. This can be helped by eating foods rich in fibre such as nuts, seeds and vegetables.

[http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml](http://http://candidapage.com/aldehyde.shtml)

The Candida/Aldehyde Detox Pathway And The Molybdenum Connection

Written by Janice Weiss

> Back to Dr. Cooter and Dr. Schmitt: "Molybdenum is chemically responsible for breaking down acetaldehyde into acetic acid. Acetaldehyde cannot be excreted from the body; it accumulates. Acetic acid can be, though, and the body naturally removes it or changes it into acetyl coenzyme A, a major player in the body's energy system.... Acetaldhyde accumulations in tissue are responsible for weakness in muscles, irritation, and PAIN."
>
>
> And from another source comes another connection -- from Dr. Robert Atkins' newsletter: Dr. Atkins is writing about Pantethine which he prescribes to his Crohn's Disease and Colitis patients, with acknowledgement to Dr. Melvin Werbach for Dr. Werbach's study that demonstrated that people with colitis have markedly decreased Coenzyme A activity if the mucosal surface of their colons, even when the blood levels of pantothenic acid are normal. Dr. Atkins concluded, based on his success with these patients of his, that Pantethine bypasses the block in converting Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) to Coenzyme A. **But also, that Pantethine is a growth factor for lactobacillus bulgaricus and bifidobacterium that we know help control yeast overgrowth** (and Dr. Cooter also speaks of it in his book). Candida, according to antibody studies done at the Atkins Center, is involved in more than 80 percent of all cases of Crohn's and Colitis.

# Thread: Fever during pregnancy more than doubles the risk of autism or developmental delay in
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-05-28T22:21:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/post-28078>

[http://www.psypost.org/2012/05/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in-children-11896](http://http://www.psypost.org/2012/05/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in-children-11896)

A team of UC Davis researchers has found that mothers who had fevers during their pregnancies were more than twice as likely to have a child with autism or developmental delay than were mothers who did not have a fever or who took medication to counter its effect.

â€œOur study provides strong evidence that controlling fevers while pregnant may be effective in modifying the risk of having a child with autism or developmental delay,â€ said Ousseny Zerbo, lead author of the study, who was a Ph.D. candidate with UC Davis when the study was conducted and is now a postdoctoral researcher with the Kaiser Permanente Northern California Division of Research. â€œWe recommend that pregnant women who develop fever take anti-pyretic medications and seek medical attention if their fever persists.â€

Published online in the Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders, the study is believed to be the first to consider how fever from any cause, including the flu, and its treatment during pregnancy could affect the likelihood of having a child with autism or developmental delay.

The results are based on data from a large, case-control investigation known as the Childhood Autism Risk from Genetics and the Environment (CHARGE) Study. Another recent study based on CHARGE data found that mothers who were obese or diabetic had a higher likelihood of having children with autism.

Irva Hertz-Picciotto, a professor of public health sciences at UC Davis and principal investigator of CHARGE, pointed out that fever is produced by acute inflammation â€” the short-term, natural immune system reaction to infection or injury â€” and that chronic inflammation, which no longer serves a beneficial purpose and can damage healthy tissue, may be present in mothers with metabolic abnormalities like diabetes and obesity.

â€œSince an inflammatory state in the body accompanies obesity and diabetes as well as fever,â€ said Hertz-Picciotto, â€œthe natural question is: Could inflammatory factors play a role in autism?â€

She explained that when people are infected by bacteria or viruses, the body generally reacts by mounting a healing response that involves the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines from white blood cells into the bloodstream. Some cytokines are able to cross the placenta, and therefore could reach the fetal central nervous system, potentially altering levels of neurotransmitters and brain development.

### BeingVenus — 2012-06-06T16:05:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fever-during-pregnancy-more-than-doubles-the-risk-of-autism-or-developmental-delay-in.1687/post-29701>

Speaking out of left field but perhaps in addition to an inflammatory state this has to do with Fat soluable vitamin levels as well. For the past 20 years women have been told to slather on sunscreen at every oppurtunity. It's stuck in out moisturizers, our makeup. Fever and illness uses up much needed stores and if you aren't actively consuming large amounts of those activators to replenish them, they'll never be recuperated for the rest of the pregnancy. Also, people with low D levels tend to get sick more often in general.

Anyway, I have nothing to back up my argument, though two years ago short birth intervals were associated with a LARGE increase in autism and short birth intervals = low nutrient stores as well. (unless you're again, actively and consciously replenishing them.)
