### MonteD — 2012-06-14T08:18:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31089>

> **bigknitwit;29735 wrote:
>
>
>
> My fasting morning blood sugars have been in the low 90s on the days I've tested. During the day I generally see 100ish, maybe 120ish after a meal.
>
>
>
> Today I ate "a bunch" of watermelon, and tested my blood sugar 30 minutes later. I was horrified to see 167. I waited another 15 minutes and got 176!! At 65 minutes out, it was down to 116. And I haven't tested it since then. Is this a "normal" response? That seems like a really, really big spike to me. Am I glucose impaired/insulin resistant? How do I deal with this, as well as get my am blood sugar down?
>
>
> Dali Dula;29737 wrote:** You are officially diabetic if you didn't know this already.

Your numbers could definitely be improved upon that is for sure. But I wouldn't go so far as to say you are "officially" diabetic. "Officially" diabetic would mean your fasting blood sugar levels would need to be over 126. Pre-diabetic would mean fasting between 110 and 126. Below 90 is considered optimal.

The "watermelon test" is less helpful. You should do a home OGTT. Pure glucose gels and tablets are easy to find at any drugstore.

[http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16](http://http://chriskresser.com/how-to-prevent-diabetes-and-heart-disease-for-16)

I may be woefully misinformed but you don't appear to be even close to a full blown diabetic....

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-14T14:41:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31092>

I was incorrect that you are officially diabetic. I guess i wish I had had a better understanding of diabetes when I was prediabetic and taken steps to deal with it. Although you may not be classified as diabetic with your current numbers please remain vigilant so as not to become diabetic. When I was diagnosed I had a BG of 1100. The chart above is interesting and most of the time I am within normal with no meds and paleo. A spike of 176 may not put you in the official diabetic zone but it is not a good thing. I have read that organ damage begins at 140. I still see spikes in this neighborhood and I don't like it Remain vigilant. Keep testing. Keep tweaking. Good luck.

### kathylu — 2012-06-14T17:29:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31122>

Interesting thread. I am concerned with this issue too. Since going low carb paleo (1 year) and leptin rx since 9/11, my FBG hovers around 110. However, my post prandial BG (after a meal) hardly ever exceeds 130 and my HbA1C is 5.4. I know 5.4 is not ideal, but it's not too bad either. However, no matter what I do, my FBG stubbornly remains around 110. I don't recall ever getting a reading below 98 in the two years I have been tracking blood glucose. So, I don't ever see dips down to 70's and 80's like others have reported (even after CT). Since I am following Jack's recommendations to the letter, the only other thing I could do is take drugs (metformin) I suppose. However, I'm taking a wait and see approach. Maybe it takes more than 9 months on the leptin rx to see the results others are reporting.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T18:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31136>

In my world an A1c of 5.4 with FBG of 110 is pretty bad. A high of 130 isn't something I'm striving for either. Not ever seeing dips under 98.... that would spell a problem for me. I've entirely abandoned CW ranges in this area.... I want it under 85 pretty much all the time.

Dr K somewhere explained what the stubborn high FBG means.... something with the liver iirc. I'd get at least a fasting insulin to get an idea of what's going on with your pancreas.

hth

### kathylu — 2012-06-14T22:26:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31225>

I do consider it a problem, though my doc says it's nothing to worry about. She thinks it is "physiological insulin resistance" induced by ketones (specifically beta hydroxybutarate) from long-time low carbing. Supposedly that's not harmful. I give her some credit for actually understanding that this phenomenon occurs!

My fasting insulin is always less than 2...that indicates that I am not pre-diabetic. I asked Jack about this some time ago and he basically said to be patient, that it takes the liver a long time to "come around" sometimes...

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T22:51:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31234>

I remember that post from Dr K and that it has something to do with the liver not being up to speed. Still.... it means something isn't right yet.

If a CW doc tells me not to worry about something, I tend to do so.... I guess you'd know if she's correct if this was low-carb induced and your had better BGs before that.

### kathylu — 2012-06-15T17:09:44+0300

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-16T00:39:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-31430>

My doctor at the Women to Women clinic in Maine had suggested I take a supplement called Insinase, by metagenics, to help control blood sugar. She said it was the herbal equivalent of metformin. At the time, my fasting insulin was elevated even though my fbg was in the low 90s, she said it meant I was insulin resistant and having blood sugar swings. She was right, as I have since confirmed with a glucose meter. I took the insinase for a month, and noticed my fbg down even at 77 (before I had the meter - I happened to have lab work done that included it), so it must have been helping with the spikes. I have been leptin rxing since then, hoping to fix myself. I recently bought another round of insinase (not cheap!), for times when I may want to take the kids strawberry picking, and other slight deviations off the plan still paleo. Not on an empty stomach, and I'll test to see how it affects my blood sugar.

She also said, like Jack has said, that in my case the insulin resistance is likely due to HPA axis dysfunction. I've increased my Armour dose (which improved the low blood pressure, high heart rate), and started taking DHEA, and will likely start pregnenelone in a few weeks after adapting to DHEA. Maybe with these adjustments, the insulin resistance will improve... I have dropped 15 pounds though, in 3.5 months, doing the leptin rx without eggs/nuts/dairy. Just meat/fish/veggies/occasional small fruit serving.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-19T20:03:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-me-with-figure-out-my-blood-sugar-readings.1765/post-32198>

I'm wondering if the "normal" range on this chart is optimal.... A spike as high as 150, an hour after eating a high carb meal is what this chart calls the edge of normal, and then peacefully back down to between 80-100. My blood sugar rarely seems to drop below 95ish, even going without eating from 5pm (low carb food) until the following morning. It's like my liver wants to maintain roughly 100, and can't account for incoming sugar very well. I do not see hypoglycemic dips, but I do see dips after CTing, especially about 30 minutes after getting out (still shivering, so still burning through sugar I suppose - down to 90, 80, once 70). I guess, like someone else said, this points to leptin resistance at the liver...?

# Thread: H. Pylori Natural Treatments?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-06T05:16:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-29675>

I just found out I have H. Pylori from the Metametrix GI Effects Test (stool). My doc wanted to get the breath test to make sure and then she said we can treat with antibiotics. This doc usually tries to treat with supplements and natural ways but not for this. I found out the breath test is over $300 so that is out for now.

Does anyone have any natural way to treat this that will eradicate the bacteria?

Thanks!

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-06T06:52:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-29686>

Oh my gosh! I've been researching bismuth and there is so much info. I'm assuming CBS, colloidal bismuth subcitrate, is the best choice for this. Although this seems fairly innocuous (I could be totally wrong on that) I'm guessing this is by script only. Now research on where to find this.

Any ideas?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-06T14:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-29690>

pepto bismol actually works.....

I'm going to do more research on Pepto. This sure would be a fairly easy alternative. My doc also suggested Mastic Gum as an alternative but was afraid it may not zap it like the antibiotics would.

Thanks for all the help Dr. K!

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-12T04:41:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/h-pylori-natural-treatments.1767/post-30646>

just a thought, don't know really, but would iodine be helpful?

From Iodine4health.com Dr. Derry:"Iodine appears to have several more roles in the body. Iodine protects against abnormal growth of bacteria in the stomach (helicobacter pylori is the most clinically significant.) Iodine can coat incoming allergic proteins to make them non-allergic, which likely also applies to the internal equivalent called autoimmune disease. Iodine binds softly to the double and triple bond of lipids to protect these bonds while they are being transported to synaptic sites in the brain and blood vessels of the body. As well iodine in the stomach deactivates all biological and most chemical poisons.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-12T05:42:04+0300

Incorky that is interesting you brought that up. I took large amounts of iodine (Ioderal) a couple of years ago and broke out into a rash after a year of working my way up to the larger amounts (50 mg). Every time I have tried to take the pills I get the rash back even in very small amounts.

I have been rinsing my mouth out with a potent iodine rinse of Nascent Iodine and water (about 50/50) to keep the dental surgery incision area clean since I'm not on antibiotics. I'm quite tempted to try the Nascent I and see what would happen. If iodine protects against H. Pylori I wonder if if would also eradicate????? Thanks for the info.

### brent-next — 2017-06-06T12:38:06+0300

What do you mean that you found out that you have h-pylori? Do you mean you had a blood test that was positive for antibodies, or a breath test, or a stool test positive for the antibodies or an endoscopy with a culture that proves that you have it?

Read the earlier posts in this thread on Pepto Bisomol (bismuth). I tested negative for the h-pylori stool antibodies, took bismuth subcitrate tablets anyway and felt a lot better. Pepto Bismol is not available where I am or I would certainly try it. It's not terribly natural, but it has a good and long safety record.

### JanSz — 2022-04-08T22:52:04+0300

Instead of Pepto-Bismol try Potassium bicarbonate.
Try at first one flat teaspoon.
When it helps, but not fully, increase dose, do not use more than 5 teaspoons daily (unless you did the test).
It changed my life.
I use it daily now. One teaspoon in the morning.
I am buying it over internet.

You may want to do the (Potassium RBC) test first, but not really necessary.

When it helps, send good thoughts to [@DrEttinger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/22307/)

Dh has always been a big pasta/rice guy. His grain consumption has dropped off since I went primal and now that I've been pushing 100% paleo he eats bkfst cereals for bkfst every am, he'll eat sandwiches & pizza at lunch & either rice or quinoa at home 3xweek w/dinner

As for labs - dh hasnt been to the doctor since before we got married (~5yr ago) his weigh is about the same ~165/170 he's 5'10" he's very lean/lanky. His back has been bothering him off/on since 2008 but his bitching moaning whining complaining was out of control last fall so I talked the chiro into evaluating him for free, knowing dh wants to avoid surgery dh went to see the chiro the chiro took one look at the X-rays and sent him for an MRI ( this was in dec 2011. He didn't get the MRI until the end of Feb. chiro sent him to a neurosurgeon b/c he didn't like what he saw. Took DH til may to make the apt. He's already got a 2fused vertabare in his neck (this was done right out of college)

Dh also has crappy sleep. On the wkends he stays up and watches the tv shoes he's dvr'd for the week. He comes to bed btwn 3-5am regularly I've actually been up with the boy at 5am when he's coming to bed. He usually falls asleep while reading the kids their bedtime story and I have a hard time getting him up to help w/getting things ready for the next day

Right now I think he's in denial about his health and he's afraid of knowing what's really going on. I'm going to pull out the Dzugan optimal hormone levels for men and have him take a look at them. He's got to want to change but at this rate he's not going to be able to actively participate as the kids get older and become more athletic.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T01:33:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-dhs-labs.1770/post-30007>

Shijin, I know it's hard to understand how someone would just ignore their health like that. I think you pointed out something very important, and that is that he is afraid of the information a doctor's visit and all these tests might bring.

If the fear is the reason he's choosing denial/avoidance then "kicking his behind into action" and "scaring him into action" is not a good approach in my opinion. I was in exactly that place until very recently and really what happens is that you are frozen into non-action, it's a flight/fight/freeze reaction and somehow he is stuck in freeze. I was stuck in freeze b/c every time I sought help I was told that there was nothing to be done about xxxx. I just had to learn to live with every complaint I had and every breakdown of my body. If there is no help then why know about it? So you can live in fear of what you now know will happen sooner or later?

You and I, and most other people on here, appreciate the power that knowledge gives (thank you Lyndra ). We are learning how to figure out what will help us to get better. That might not be your hubby's perspective at this point in time. I know it wasn't mine and I went to great lengths to avoid being presented with bad news.

I wasn't really able to turn this around until I found Dr K's blog. With renewed hope that there might be something to fix and safe, I got a bunch of things tested. But the only reason I was able to break through the freeze into fight was b/c fight had become attractive with outlook of success. I know I would never have gotten my recent MRI had it not be for Dr K being so available - I was so scared. I would have traveled to TN if needed... and I live right next to Boulder Neurosurgical Associates.

I think what he might need is for you to approach this with gentleness instead of pushing. Lots of affirmation of support no matter what the outcome will be. He might need lots of information right now about all the thing that \*can\* be done to make his life better or prevent it from getting worse. He needs to understand that at this point in time knowledge does happen to be power and not being stuck with what is. He needs to know that you will be by his side no matter if he can ride a bike in a few years or happens to be in a wheelchair. That is the number one thing I need to hear from my loved ones right now.... Every.Single.Day.

Now, if he isn't taking action b/c he thinks things are just dandy, that's a different story.

As always, YMMV

### Lyndra — 2012-06-08T05:18:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-dhs-labs.1770/post-30047>

Only you know what your husband will respond to in terms of information, cajoling, etc. I was recently reluctant to order up a 23andme, not sure I wanted to know what my genes might say about my future. However, with recent tests showing BG creeping up, I decided that knowing is far better than not, because then I can choose to do something (or choose not to do something) - but at least have a choice. I know for myself, I have to be in the right place and time before I can make that decision to get information and act on it and to someone who is highly action-oriented, I'm sure my behavior would be beyond irritating. I know you'll use all your knowledge to help him work through this.

366 means you better retest in the AM and be prepared for some bad news.........while testing mid day is a bit inaccurate for men, 366 is awfully low even at that time for a guy your age.........just realize that testosterone correlates with heart disease risk in men......and if you have a low T and a high E2.......that is the cardiac death march.......that slowly happens to most American men silently. Do not let this stand.

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

10 ------- Metabolic Panel (14), Comprehensive

11 ------- Complete Blood Count (CBC) With Differential and Platelet

12 ------- NMR LipoProfileÂ®

13 ------- Iron, Total

14 ------- Ferritin

15 ------- Phosphorus, Serum

16 ------- Uric Acid, Serum

17 ------- Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)\*

18 ------- CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

19 ------- Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

20 ------- RDW

21 ------- Homocysteine, cardio

22 ------- Insulin and C-Peptide, Serum

23 ------- HgA1c

24 ------- C-Reactive Protein (CRP), High Sensitivity (Cardiac Risk Assessment)

25 ------- Thyroid-stimulating Hormone (TSH), T4, FT4, T3, FT3, RT3

26 ------- Lab pls calculate ratios, (Free T3 Ã· RT3) and (T3 Ã· RT3)

27 ------- Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies

28 ------- Thyroid Antithyroglobulin Antibody

29 ------- prolactin

30 ------- IGF-1

31 ------- IGFBP3

\-------------------------------

\===========================

# Thread: Leptin, Hunger,  Cold
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-06T23:43:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29789>

I have been talking about this on Mark's daily Apple and wanted to get ya'alls opinion.

Studies like this one, [http://ukpmc.ac.uk/abstract/MED/10490779/reload=0;jsessionid=nk8EXhvifHEGUw6IXikW.0](http://http://ukpmc.ac.uk/abstract/MED/10490779/reload=0;jsessionid=nk8EXhvifHEGUw6IXikW.0) ,and Jack has been saying all along, that cold kills hunger.

In this study, obese and non-obese women were studied for leptin sensitivity and other autonomic nervous system functions when warm and exposed to cold. The obese women showed less effect from the cold exposure than non-obese women.

My conclusion is that the leptin resistance at the receptor level is interfering with the hunger signals that should be turned off when cold.

Would a good test for LR be that you are hungry when cold?

Another studied showed similar results in mice:

"Leptin receptor gene expression has been measured in arcuate and ventromedial hypothalamic nuclei. Receptor mRNA in both hypothalamic areas was higher in obese mice than in lean littermates. Twice daily leptin administration for 7 days profoundly affected food intake, reduced leptin receptor mRNA in the arcuate nucleus, and had a similar effect on neuropeptide Y gene expression. A single leptin injection was ineffective. Exposure of lean mice to cold for 24 h caused an induction of leptin receptor and NPY mRNA which was normalized when animals were returned to the warm. Regulation of receptor gene expression may be an important component in the reading of the leptin signal."

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T00:43:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29798>

Can't be.......I'm just a nut job.

### AKMan — 2012-06-07T00:56:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29804>

> **Jack Kruse;29900 wrote:** Can't be.......I'm just a nut job.

Yes you are!

So, I'm thinking when people ask, "Am I Leptin resistant?" In addition to the mirror test, you could ask if they are hungry when cold.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T01:45:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29815>

Hunger tells you a lot about leptin levels......a ton in fact. I think the people like Robb Wolf who says CT is hormetic will be hard pressed to explain why cold destroys hunger in obese folks when nothing else does. The other thing it does I have not really talked about publicaly......is in surgery patients that I use CT in that they just do not eat......yet they do amazingly well in recovery. It is like a free pass to IF'ing leangains style. With time we will see if the trainer or the surgeon is right.

### AKMan — 2012-06-07T02:15:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29821>

> **August;29911 wrote:** So, Hungry (hungrier?) when cold indicates leptin resistance . . . and Not Hungry when cold indicates leptin sensitivity, right?
>
>
>
> (and not a nut job. . . .also right?)

I think you are right...about the hunger and cold. The studies, and human experiences, show when one is leptin sensitive, in extreme cold you should have no hunger as the leptin receptors are taken out of the equation. If leptin resistant, you will still be hungry in extreme cold because the leptin receptors are compromised to begin with.

This shouldn't be confused with the propensity to eat more in cold weather, however. Exposure to cooler temperatures long-term lower leptin levels and actually increase hunger. This is to compensate for the increased demand on metabolism in winter. Staying warm takes more calories.

### vkiernan — 2012-06-07T14:52:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29874>

> **AKMan;29906 wrote:** Yes you are!
>
>
>
> So, I'm thinking when people ask, "Am I Leptin resistant?" In addition to the mirror test, you could ask if they are hungry when cold.

Hungry when cold or cold when hungry? I was cold when I would get hungry a while back but not hungry when I was cold. Interesting that you brought this up. I think Jack made a comment about it after reading something I wrote it and said that's not good. I sort of forgot all about it. Now I will have to make sure that isn't the case. Pretty sure not and this change happended after I started CT'ing.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T15:18:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29879>

When your in cold your hunger and your fat should go down.......that is the normal response in humans......when it does not it means something is out of order in you.

### bigknitwit — 2012-06-07T20:04:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29942>

> **Jack Kruse;29981 wrote:** When your in cold your hunger and your fat should go down.......that is the normal response in humans......when it does not it means something is out of order in you.

You're talking about hormones, right? I'm curious what are the more typical "out of order" hormone profiles, or degenerative conditions, that result in the inverse response to cold exposure (increased hunger/increased fat). And then, will continued cold exposure eventually turn the boat around, or is the cold in this kind of scenario making things "worse?"

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-07T20:19:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-29947>

I believe any type of hormonal imbalance will cause some weird eating habits. I had/have estrogen dominance and for 2-1/2 months the CTing (cold baths) made me hungrier and gain 5#. I was spilling out lots of the excess estro and had symptoms to show for it. But now it seems to have settled down and I actually have lost all the weight I gained. I actually don't think my ED is completely balanced but it is getting closer.

### janagram — 2012-06-09T04:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-hunger-cold.1774/post-30226>

I remember being a kid and after a day in the ocean or pool I would be FAMISHED!!!! dunno if that means anything. My own kids were too!

### indigogirl — 2012-06-15T23:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31407>

The cervix is involved in orgasmic function. Studies have shown women who have their cervixes removed don't orgasm as well. I don't have the study...

My uterus wasn't prolapsed...just a fibroid the size of a grapefruit and menopause was not in sight. Wish I knew then more of what I know now about estrogen dominance.

### chocolate — 2012-06-16T01:23:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31446>

[http://www.modernmedicine.com/modernmedicine/Modern+Medicine+Now/Beyond-Kegels-When-do-gynecologic-problems-call-fo/ArticleStandard/Article/detail/751427](http://http://www.modernmedicine.com/modernmedicine/Modern+Medicine+Now/Beyond-Kegels-When-do-gynecologic-problems-call-fo/ArticleStandard/Article/detail/751427)

Beyond Kegels: When do gynecologic problems call for physical therapy?Many gynecologic problems require multidisciplinary treatment. Physical therapy may play a significant treatment role in achieving successful outcomes and improving patients' quality of life.

> Most gynecologic problems require multidisciplinary treatment. An often-overlooked modality is formalized physical therapy provided by individuals trained in the treatment of gynecologic problems.
>
>
>
> Although physical therapy for gynecologic ailments is not typically taught in medical school, physical therapists are able to offer new approaches to many gynecologic problems that are traditionally difficult to treat, including pelvic pain and disorders of the pelvic floor. This article reviews common gynecologic disorders for which referral for physical therapy should be considered and can make a difference.
>
>
>
> The role of physical therapy
>
>
>
> Physical therapy is commonly employed for rehabilitation after acute or chronic musculoskeletal injury. Pelvic muscles can require the same type of rehabilitation. The techniques employed by physical therapists for pelvic pain or dysfunction include treatment modalities similar to those for back pain or joint rehabilitation, including taking a detailed history, evaluating posture, stretching the muscles, applying manual massage, electrical stimulation, and exercises to increase control and strength of the pelvic floor, pelvic stability training, having patients keep diaries of pain or incontinence, and instructing the patient in behavior or voiding modifications. This list is not all-inclusive, but specialized physical therapy can contribute much toward improving the function and condition of a patient's pelvic floor.
>
>
> Disorders of pelvic function
>
>
>
> Pelvic organ prolapse (POP)
>
>
>
> The cross-sectional, national health survey conducted by the National Centers for Health Statistics of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention surveyed 1,961 women about symptomatic POP and reported a prevalence rate of 2.9%.11 The effectiveness of pelvic floor muscle training in reversing POP and alleviating symptoms was studied in a randomized trial of 109 women with prolapse ranging from mild to severe. The group randomized to pelvic floor training showed statistically significant elevation of the bladder (3.0 mm) and the rectum (5.5 mm) after treatment and demonstrated reduced frequency and bother of symptoms compared with controls.12 Because of the statistically significant but small measured change in reduction of the prolapse, symptomatic stage 1, 2, and early stage 3 prolapse patients are most likely to benefit from pelvic floor strengthening.

I hope to never need depends.....it could happen though....My kids have already advised I'll be going to a home the minute I become annoying. I'm still here. They have a high tolerance.

nice article.....

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-16T06:54:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31504>

Thank you so much! I agree, I plan on keeping my organs!! And chocolate, I'm glad your kids are keeping u around! Lol

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-16T18:55:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-31566>

LOL...the reason I'm here on these boards, came to PaleoFX, eat CO oil daily, do the CT baths....about 3 years ago, my OB/GYN recommended "immediate total hysterectomy" after I complained of painful periods and a sonogram showed fibroids, I ran out of there so fast and started doing my own research. Today, I have all my parts, and a Paleo diet, CT, etc., to thank for it. Oh, and the krill oil has done wonders.

I, too, learned that the cervix lessens or obliterates orgasm. Ain't nobody doing that to me.

The fact that these doctors think we have these removeable parts just astounds me. About 30 years ago, they wanted to perform a total hysterctomy on my mother. She asked for a 2nd opinion. No joke...the doctor picked up the phone, called another doctor, told him her symptoms, and hung up and said, "OK I just got a 2nd opinion. He agrees". She went back to her favorite doctor, who told her she was fine. 30 years later, and she still has all her parts.

Please help your mother by doing a lot of your own research. There have got to be other things they can do. My research found that there are thousands of unnecessary hysterectomies performed.

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-19T17:15:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/advice-on-hysterectomy.1778/post-32141>

She is very uncomfortable and she has a low tolerance. She hasn't followed thru w the exercises and getting her to make dietary changes is hard. I had such hopes for her trying some healthy and gluten free Sugar free foods while at my house but she wouldn't even taste them! The only thing she tried was the Zevia pop. Its sad because I think she thinks it's "too late" for her. Her mom had osteoporosis and Alzheimer's. My mom already has osteoporosis and migraines. I've been begging her to at least give up gluten. She is trying. Now I know why Dr K says adapt or die. She isn't willing to adapt in many ways. I made some strawberry vinaigrette salad dressing but she wouldn't even try it. Insists on her Newmans Own olive oil dressing. Which would be great if it really was only olive oil but it has canola oil! But I digress. We were working on my yard and she said she'd get preen. I said no chemicals. I don't want them in my yard and told her she shouldn't go near them either and she said "it's too late for me." So sad. I really need to work on my dad. If he did it, she wOuld follow.

# Thread: Anyone in Korea:  Try Cold Thermogenesis in Public Bathouses
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### mambo — 2012-06-07T05:54:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/post-29855>

I was in Korea for 2 weeks and I tried Dr. K's CT in Korea's many public bathouses. There are some excellent cold temperature pools there, with temperatures ranging from 39-41F and 55-63F. Some places with 3 or more cold bathtubs with different temperatures: cold, colder, coldest.

The one in Pusan (Haundae Spa) was the best at around 39F. It seemed to be just above freezing, the coldest bathtub I ever experienced. No one could stay in for more than 30 seconds.

After about 2 weeks of CT, I could feel my temperature rising. Also, I began to sweat profusely (I'm sublicinically hypothyroid and have had problems sweating for the last 3 years). Another wild thing: my metabolism speeded up and I seemed to be burning fat real quickly. When I weighed myself, I was close to 153. I left the country at 161. I seemed to have acquired the metabolism of those legendary sherpas of the Himalayas!

My conclusion: there is definitely something to Dr. K's CT.

### MJ* — 2012-06-07T06:48:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/post-29860>

> **mambo;29957 wrote:** I was in Korea for 2 weeks and I tried Dr. K's CT in Korea's many public bathouses. There are some excellent cold temperature pools there, with temperatures ranging from 39-41F and 55-63F. Some places with 3 or more cold bathtubs with different temperatures: cold, colder, coldest.
>
>
>
> The one in Pusan (Haundae Spa) was the best at around 39F. It seemed to be just above freezing, the coldest bathtub I ever experienced. No one could stay in for more than 30 seconds.
>
>
>
> After about 2 weeks of CT, I could feel my temperature rising. Also, I began to sweat profusely (I'm sublicinically hypothyroid and have had problems sweating for the last 3 years). Another wild thing: my metabolism speeded up and I seemed to be burning fat real quickly. When I weighed myself, I was close to 153. I left the country at 161. I seemed to have acquired the metabolism of those legendary sherpas of the Himalayas!
>
>
>
> My conclusion: there is definitely something to Dr. K's CT.

Great testimonial...how long were you able to stay in the cold water? If you continue to adapt to the cold you could go back and shock them all!

I live on Jeju Island and I'm going to start CT this week.

Thank you for posting this! I was also thinking about the saunas for the bath since my little place doesn't have one.

Paisley

### mambo — 2012-06-12T03:40:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anyone-in-korea-try-cold-thermogenesis-in-public-bathouses.1781/post-30633>

I tried to stay for at least 30 minutes each time. For every visit, I used the cold pool twice, beginning and end. So that's about 60 minutes total. I could do 30 minutes no problem without prep in 55-63F. When it gets to 39F, I couldn't obviously.

In Korea, you can also stay overnight in such places, called Zzimjilbangs. The charge is for 12 hours at around 10,000W, equivalent to about $8.50. For my 11 day stay, I stayed 8 nights in Korean spas in 5 different cities and had my fill of Cold Thermogenesis. These spas also have "ice rooms" but I mainly stuck to cold pools. I mainly followed the guide provided by this blog:

[http://grrrltraveler.com/countries/asia/korea/fave-tips-stays-for-korea/siloam-spa/](http://http://grrrltraveler.com/countries/asia/korea/fave-tips-stays-for-korea/siloam-spa/)

I agree with Grrrltraveler: Siloam has the best sleeping rooms in Seoul; but in Pusan, you have to try [Haundae Spa Center](http://http://grrrltraveler.com/the-grrr/traveling-solo/night-jjimjilbang/) with its ice cold pool. In Daegu, you have to try [Goongjeon Lavender](http://http://www.goongjeon.com/sub2-8.html) with its cavern-like cold pool.

In the U.S. you can also visit Korean spas but Russian spas also have good cold pools, from what I hear. You can use this search site to look for such places.

[http://saunascape.com/find-sauna/brc-delphi-sauna](http://http://saunascape.com/find-sauna/brc-delphi-sauna)

If you live in the NYC area, the place to visit is [King Sauna](http://http://kingsaunanj.com/) near Fort Lee, N.J. and [Spa Castle](http://http://www.nyspacastle.com/eng/info/info01.php) near Flushing, Queens. If you're near Philly, [Southhampton Spa](http://http://www.southamptonspa.com/) gets high marks. I don't know too much about Onsens or Japanese spas.

> **MJ\*;29962 wrote:** Great testimonial...how long were you able to stay in the cold water? If you continue to adapt to the cold you could go back and shock them all!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: 4:16 AM sunrise, 10 pm sunset--evolutionary coping in the Northern latitudes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### thyme — 2012-06-07T13:30:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/post-29869>

The local mammals seem to go with it--they're up and eating with sunrise and down and sleeping at sunset. How do I know?

Because I'm waking with the bloody sunrise. And while naturally awakening with the sun at a slightly earlier time every day was a lovely example of circadian rhythms in springtime, it's proving to be a royal pain in the arse now that we're in summer. I live on the sea, in the country, and the only light that enters my bedroom is moonlight and sunlight peeping through slight cracks in the heavy curtains.

I wear a silk eyemask.

I'm eating seasonally.

I have no artificial lights at night.

I am falling asleep at 11 (no lights--that's when it finally gets dark outside, a good hour after sunset). and waking at 4:30, when the sun rises, and we aren't yet to the solstice.

How does the nightly sleep hormonal process adapt to 5.5 hours of darkness--and, presumably, sleep?

And other than grumbling at the sun and world in the morning before pulling a pillow over your head and going back to sleep for a couple more hours, how do you cope?

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-07T14:46:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/4-16-am-sunrise-10-pm-sunset-evolutionary-coping-in-the-northern-latitudes.1782/post-29873>

I go to bed a lot earlier than 11.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-07T16:32:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-29896>

I began LR on 02/25/12, Friday. The day after I had my annual biometrics done at work and found out that my healthy juicing had caused me to gain 12.5 lbs in 7 months. I began snorkeling on 03/26/12. I tried to go too fast w/ the CT & wound up staring at an empty tub for 3(?) weeks. I'm back to spot icing: 6 frozen liter bottles (clavicle to pubis) and baggies of ice on supraclavicular, interscapular & L5/S1 (disc bulge that aches unless iced).

I've lost 15 lbs as of this morning. I slept 8 hours last night for the first time in a very long time.

I would love to say everything's rosy on my wee journey to Optimal, but this week it's not. My tinnitus has been worse for the past week plus I've been looking at [www.stopthethyroidmadness.com](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com) to get a handle on my thyroid lab results. As it turns out I have several of the symptoms listed. My PCP emailed me that thyroid is normal so we're going to have to have a conversation about symptoms when I go for my follow-up visit this coming Monday.

What's been keeping me going is, I remember some smart person posting that when sleep kicks in, hormones aren't far behind. I know that CT will help thyroid once the sex hormones are under control. So I'm going to see what my PCP wants to do about the sex hormones. For the record, I subscribe to Dr. John Lee (physiologic doses) not TS Wylie (pharmacologic doses).

If anyone chooses to comment on my labs, I'd be grateful for any assistance. While I can read the results, I have no idea of what to do with the information.

Elin

PS: After reading others' posts about eggs I cut them out last Sunday, and my wheezing has almost completely disappeared. That may be why my hsCRP was so high when labs were done. Thanks to all who weighed in on eggs!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-08T12:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30081>

Night #2 sleeping 8 hours without waking. I feel like Wonder Woman!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:12:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30085>

The CRP was a HS CRP? If so that is why your hormone panel is a mess........Free T3 is real low so dont be fooled by a decent TSH......most people use TSH to trend thyroid recs and that is a bad method in my view.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-08T15:36:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30108>

> **Jack Kruse;30187 wrote:** The CRP was a HS CRP? If so that is why your hormone panel is a mess........Free T3 is real low so dont be fooled by a decent TSH......most people use TSH to trend thyroid recs and that is a bad method in my view.

Yes, it was hsCRP. That result is from before I quit eating eggs & cheese, which were giving me a huge histamine reaction. I'm going to talk w/ my PCP on Monday about desiccated thyroid and topical sex hormones. Then I plan to re-test in October or November.

Slept through the night again last night. Hoping that it gets to be a habit.

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-09T15:34:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30260>

Ah well... last night I woke up once about 3:15am to void my bladder. Even though I didn't sleep all the way through, I feel good. I go to bed @ 9pm and have an alarm set for 5am. This morning being Saturday my alarm was off and I slept until 6:15.

Good sleep is better than any drug I've ever heard of.

In my world Saturdays are for errands. I'm off and running...

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-10T14:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30393>

Went to bed @ 10 last night; woke up @ 440 this morning. It's not 8 hours but I'll take it.

I ice every evening from 7pm-8pm. Last night I noticed 2 things:

1- Whereas I haven't been able to feel any cold in my lower abdomen at all since I began this CT journey, I was very aware for the first time that my uterus was cold - not uncomfortable, just an awareness of cold.

2- I put a sandwich-size baggie of ice on either side of my neck when icing to hit the BAT that lives there and for the past 3 nights it's been more water than ice when I'm done. Also, it's no longer painful at the 15 minute mark. I'm really not aware of it's being cold and have to re-adjust the baggies' positions several times to make sure they're where they should be.

Today is my cook-for-the-week, do laundry, get ice day. Git 'er done!

Elin

### Tizawee — 2012-06-11T22:31:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-30591>

Saw the PCP today re thyroid/adrenal issues. She's decided that she wants to fix one at a time so we know what's working. We agreed on estrogen/progesterone via sublingual liquid or troche. We'll re-assess in 3 months. So, I'll also be going to the store for Rhodiola, lubricarion and Eleuthrococcus ginseng (not panax though).

My "homework" is to bring my stress levels down. Gonna have to put on my thinking cap on that one.

Slept 8 hours last night. Still icing for an hour a night.

e

PS: Almost forgot. I lost another 3 pounds! Yay!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-14T15:58:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31100>

Picked up my hormone Rx from the compounding pharmacy yesterday after work. It's in troche form. Each troche contains Estriol 0.2mg, Estradiol 0.5mg, Progesterone 40mg & DHEA 5mg. I am dosing w/ half a troche twice a day. Pharmacy says it should take 6 - 8 weeks to reach optimal blood levels.

Fingers crossed.

e

### indigogirl — 2012-06-14T16:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31108>

Hi Elin,

You seem to be chugging along! Good work!

I use troches, too. Seems most women on here doing BHRT are using creams or lotions. I wasn't absorbing the lotion so we went to a troche and now I'm testing better levels. I'm not familiar with Lee's method...have heard of him...will have to look into it.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-15T02:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31273>

LOL, chugging along... well put, indigogirl! That's exactly what I'm doing: BAB, work, dinner, ice, sleep, start all over again the next morning.

This is my first experience with troches. (Used OTC cream for a few years but the new PCP doesn't like 'em.) I'm concerned that the estrogen is too high, but I'm willing to be patient while my body adjusts. Are you having good results? Did you have to wait long to feel any change(s)?

Dr. John Lee began treating women with BHRT in his clinical practice in the mid-70's before anyone knew what it was. He wrote "What Your Doctor May _Not Tell You About Menopause" and several other books all about balancing hormones. He advocated physiologic doses (equivalent to normal body function) instead of pharmacologic doses ("considerably greater" than what the body produces). His books contain almost as many biochem lessons as Dr. Kruse' blogs! One of his books, "What Your Doctor May Not_ Tell You About Breast Cancer" was co-written with Dr. David Zava, owner of ZRT Lab, the lab Dr. Kruse recommends for saliva testing.

e

### indigogirl — 2012-06-15T03:00:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31274>

Learning about hormones is so confusing...thankfully there is info out there, but who to trust? I started to look at Dr Uzzi Reiss' info online, have looked at Dzugan (makes sense to me, too pricey), am now reading an e book by Dr. Daniel Kalish.

I'm on Biest and progesterone. For the first 4-6 weeks on the troches I had breast tenderness, which I didn't have on the lotion, so I felt that it was working (meaning I was absorbing it) and lab worked showed I am. My GYN thinks my levels are good. Colleen Coble (posts here) thought they were in the toilet...and I trust her knowledge. Trying to figure out where I go from here!

### Tizawee — 2012-06-15T04:40:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-31284>

I am not familiar with Reiss or Kalish & have only heard about Dzugan from this forum. Knowing who to trust is why I stuck with Dr. Lee. His approach makes sense and doesn't cost an arm and a leg. I only know Colleen Coble from her posts here.

Perhaps, like your tenderness, my swollen fingers are a sign that my body is doing something. I hope so. Don't want to blow up like a balloon.

e

### Tizawee — 2012-06-21T02:35:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-32558>

Apparently I'm not as far along with CT as I thought. Grrr. Now that my female hormones are getting some help, I'm down to 20 - 30 minutes of icing before shivering starts. Most times I can make it to 45 minutes before I have to stop but it really depends on how tired I am.

Tinnitus hasn't stopped and now my hands get cold in the afternoons at work. Both are thyroid signs, I believe. My PCP is going to check my labs again on Sept 21st... so I only have 13 weeks to wait. Woo Hoo.

I had a bad week last week. On Friday, June 8th, a friend died very suddenly. His memorial was Saturday, the 16th then Father's Day was the 17th. My second father's day without a father. No easy feat for a Daddy's girl. I found out that I'm an emotional eater: I just had to have some chips so I got a bag of sweet potato chips and ate the whole thing. I don't feel physically bad but my weight loss has stalled. And there's no telling what kind of damage that canola oil did. To repeat myself: Grrr.

Okay, enough with the poor me's. Back in the saddle, Elin.

Off to CT.

e

### Tizawee — 2012-07-02T13:08:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-34973>

I "borrowed" this format from two ladies who frequently post in the forums. Thank you both!**Week 18, Day 1 (Sunday, 07/01/12)

**Waking Temperature: 97.3F

**FBG: 93 (high; maybe from too much coffee yesterday?)

**Weight: 152

12 oz Coffee

**Breakfast: ~ 8 oz cod left over from last night (BG 1 hour postprandial: 111)

**Within 30 minutes of waking? Yes

**Lunch: 12 oz bag o’ broccoli (heated up, of course) w/ 3T CO + salt/onion powder

Dinner:** Cod baked w/ CO, salt, turmeric

Within 4 hours of bedtime**?** yes

Cravings?** No

Snacking?** No

Morning Musings:** Today is day 125 on LR. It just looks easier to track doing week/day so I’m going to be using that format from now on.

This is the summer I’ve been waiting for for two years: oral surgery is scheduled for Saturday, 07/14/12. I’m finally going to get my snaggle-teeth taken care of and get a partial denture so I have a full mouth of straight teeth again.

(I have what’s commonly referred to as Meth Mouth: I was on OMG doses of pain management meds for 7 years. I got myself off the meds in May ’09 and have finally saved enough $ for oral surgery + partial dentures.)

Tomorrow @ 0630 I turn 55. I woke myself up @ 0600 on my 13\[sup\]th\[/sup\] birthday just to see what it would feel like to become a teenager… No change. I still had mild muscle/joint aches all over and felt a certain amount of hopelessness about the world. I could never understand how, if life was full of pain and hopelessness, people could manage to be so happy and successful. It would take me 28 more years to discover I have celiac disease and another 13 years to find and eliminate almost all allergens from my diet. It wasn’t until my friend (Bugsy in this forum) showed me this web site that I figured out I have a leaky gut. It was just a few weeks ago I discovered I have a histamine reaction to eggs(!). I know I won’t feel much different at 0630 tomorrow morning but it will undoubtedly be the best birthday I’ve ever had.

Earlier this week Dr. Kruse offered Optimal Living logo shirts on his FB page. In celebration of this most excellent birthday, I ordered one. Mrs. Kruse had it made and in the mail within 2 days. I chose a polo shirt so I can wear it to work, and it’s one of the nicest I’ve ever seen. The logo looks great! I’m waiting to wear it on Wednesday, my birthday party, and will post pics. Once I get down to goal, I’ll order another, more flattering shirt.

I work in an incoming call center for one of the largest insurance carriers in the nation quoting claim status and benefits to doctors and hospitals. We bid for shifts on a quarterly basis. We just did shift bid, and beginning a week from tomorrow I’ll be starting an earlier shift. It’s important to me because it means I’ll be able to eat dinner before I leave the office, which means 4 hours before bed. (I’ve lost over 20 lbs on the Reset + CT even though I’ve been eating as late as 1900 most evenings. My bed time is 2100 so, not good.) Now I’ll be able to eat and CT according to protocol. I’m hoping that’ll make a difference in my results.

Evening Observations:** I made an appointment w/ Dr. Kruse for Wed a.m. It has to be by phone because I don’t skype. I hope I’ll be able to take notes fast enough! LOL! So excited to be hacked by the Master Dot Connector himself and to have stumbled into a bargain to boot! C’mon Wednesday!

That’s enough for now.

### Tizawee — 2012-07-02T16:14:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tis-a-wee-optimal-journey.1783/post-34990>

For my birthday I got myself an Optimized Living polo shirt & a one-hour consultation with Dr. Kruse. Life is good!

[](<http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/35150=296-OL Polo.jpg>)

Hi!

I know this thread is very limiting as this technology is currently only available in like one place in the country (I think the Hawaii one is being rebuilt)

Just wanted to see who on here has done this and what you have found. Thought it would interest others as well since this exercise appears to be 'Kruse Approved' (forgive me Doctor Kruse if I'm incorrect)

I am going for my 11th session in a few minutes. Its been hard getting back in the groove as I was on vacation for 2 weeks but so far so good.

Anyone else care to chime in or if anyone has any questions as to my personal experience, fire away!

### jayjha1@gmail.com — 2012-06-12T06:56:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vasper-training-participants.1785/post-30662>

Well, after my fattening vacation, I threw down 5 sessions last week and then one this morning

I am finding I am getting stronger for sure - this on pretty low calories and definitely very low carbs

I do mostly bodyweight exercises, so my chinups have gone up as well as my pushups (I do them on a Perfect Pushup with a weighted vest)

I am trying to shed about 20lbs over the next 2 months, so I will be updating this thread whenever I have something to say or if anyone has any questions

And what about when you are hip and thigh heavy? What does that mean.

Thanks

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-29958>

### Llanabanana — 2012-06-11T04:56:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-30486>

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-11T22:41:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-30594>

Just reading Dr JohnLee's hormone book. He says excess fat around the hips, buttocks, and thighs is estrogen dominance. Excess Fat around middle is usually a sign of insulin resistance (too much sugar and refined carbs, too little exercise). A poochy tummy (which is different from weight gain around middle and different from saggy tummy caused by pregnancy) can be caused by excess cortisol (stres) and/or estrogen deficiency and/or constipation.

### ealachan — 2012-06-11T23:12:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-30597>

> **Jennifer;30698 wrote:** Just reading Dr JohnLee's hormone book. He says excess fat around the hips, buttocks, and thighs is estrogen dominance. Excess Fat around middle is usually a sign of insulin resistance (too much sugar and refined carbs, too little exercise). A poochy tummy (which is different from weight gain around middle and different from saggy tummy caused by pregnancy) can be caused by excess cortisol (stres) and/or estrogen deficiency and/or constipation.

What's the difference between a poochy tummy and weight gain around the middle? I have two very distinct areas of fat (well, more than two, but we're just talking abdomen right now, hahaha) - one above my waist and one below my waist. My actual waist is quite a lot smaller than my "upper belly", and my "lower belly" is lower, like around the widest part of my hips, 4-5" below my actual waist. I've also got really heavy thighs and a big butt, but I'm curious what my shape in the front might mean. Other than that I'm fat, of course...

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-07-29T18:31:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-41801>

Quote taken from Ask Jack re: IGF-1 deficiency. I asked about testing IGF-1 directly. Also, it was because of this thread that Jack mentioned a Kardashian behind is from IGF-1 deficiency.

IGF1 is the best way to test or you can use that is safe in my opinion. The DEA is forcing manufactures to push physicians to get patients to do Stimulation testing but those test can carry some risk. Moreover, they are difficult to find a place who is willing to do them.

GH stimulation tests help to diagnose GH deficiency and hypopituitarism. For a stimulation test, a sample of blood is drawn after 10-12 hours of fasting. Then, under close medical supervision, a person is given an intravenous solution of insulin or arginine. Blood samples are then drawn at timed intervals, and GH levels are tested in each to see if the pituitary gland was stimulated by the insulin (or arginine) to produce expected levels of GH. Other GH stimulants include clonidine and glucagon.

GH suppression tests help to diagnose GH excess, which, in addition to other blood tests and imaging scans, can help identify and locate pituitary tumors. For a suppression test, a sample of blood is drawn after 10-12 hours of fasting. A person is then given a standard glucose solution to drink. Blood samples are drawn at timed intervals, and GH levels are tested in each to see if the pituitary gland is sufficiently suppressed by the dose of glucose.

Often other blood tests that reflect pituitary function, such as free T4, TSH, cortisol, FSH, LH, and testosterone (in men), are also ordered. These tests are usually performed prior to GH testing to make sure that they are normal and/or controlled with medication before GH testing is done. For example, hypothryoidism must be treated prior to testing for GH deficiency in children; otherwise, a falsely low GH result may be seen. Glucose levels are run on the samples collected during the GH suppression test, both to track glucose levels and to make sure that the person's system is sufficiently challenged by the glucose solution.

Since exercise normally causes a temporary increase in GH levels, some doctors may perform an initial evaluation of growth hormone deficiency by measuring GH after someone exercises vigorously for a period of time.

### Marccus — 2012-09-18T12:45:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/back-fat-and-heavy-in-the-hips-and-thighs.1787/post-52313>

Cortisol is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands that helps regulate blood pressure and cardiovascular function, as well as the body's use of proteins, carbohydrates and fats. Cortisol secretion increases in response to physical and psychological stress during the fight or flight response, which is why it's sometimes called "the stress hormone."

# Thread: Why can't I fix this friggin' leaky gut?!?!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx

### BJK77 — 2012-06-07T22:10:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-29962>

I'm having one of those days and am just beyond frustrated. I've been on a low-carb paleo / GAPS diet for 2 years now. I've been doing CT since March. My HDL is good @ 106, so well over the 60 Dr. K says he likes to see. My vitamin D is decent @ 66 though it could obviously be a bit better. It was 90 in February and dropped to 66 in May after I decreased my supplementation, so I'm upping it again to 10,000 IU/day. Trigs are good at 45 and hsCRP is even good at 0.05.

I just cannot figure out why I can't fix this leaky gut. I do have hypothyroidism, but not Hashi's. My hormones are all in the toilet, but I'm supplementing with DHEA and Preg. I feel like I'm just ending up with more and more AI diseases (Rosacea, Raynaud's) as time goes on and I can't for the life of me understand why. I am super clean with my diet - total AI protocol (no nuts, seeds, dairy, eggs, chocolate, etc). I have about 2 cups of bone broth per day and only eat organic, grass fed beef and high quality seafood. I don't do any processed/cured meats or conventional meats at all. I also have organic sauerkraut with every meal.

My BF every morning is a combo of beef and seafood, so I have at least 1 serving of seafood daily - wild Alaskan salmon, sardines, shrimp, scallops, etc. My veggies are all organic, but I'm even super limited in those that I can tolerate - pretty much kale, zucchini, small amounts of mushrooms and maybe a bit of cabbage and broccoli here and there.

I follow the leaky gut protocol as much as I can, but I don't tolerate the l-glutamine and I can't take high dose vitamin C due to oxalate issues. Other than that, I'm doing the best I can with it.

What the heck gives?!?! After 2 years I would think I'd be able to tolerate more foods and be in a much better state of health. Continuing to struggle with constipation that started after I went low-carb isn't exactly helping things either.

Anyone see anything glaringly obvious that I'm missing because I'm really not sure what else to add/take away to make some progress here.

BJK77, mh586 is spot on with his advice. Check the supplements that you are taking and see how many of them contain magnesium stearate. This is a vegetable based ingredient that is added to supplement ingredients to help them flow in the manufacturing process. The magnesium stearate coats the small intestine so that absorption of nutrients is not possible. Other non-nutritional additives to supplements are corn oil, Titanium Dioxide and others. Do as Souldanzer suggests and get your magnesium from epson salt baths. Your

body may not take internal consumption of magnesium, but it should have no problem absorbing what it needs through the skin.

Also, double up on your use of CO. Dr. K says CO is the best thing for fixing a leaky gut.

Keep on trucking, you will eventually get there.

BJK77, I am only just reading this thread and my experience may not be helpful but I'll share it just in case. I was having a similar problem to what you describe... keeping diet squeaky clean, labs showing good things, but food intolerances/allergies increasing. I started to get really worried I was getting worse. Then, one day while walking the dog, I was listening to a random Sean Croxton podcast with Paul Chek. It was a Q&A thing. (I'm not a Paul Chek devotee, but find a good bit of what he says thought-provoking.) In any case, someone called in and basically said what you are saying: their food intolerances are getting worse, not better. He asked about Betaine HCl and the woman said she wasn't taking much/any. He then said "I have seen countless cases of people going from no food intolerances to over 100 food allergies by not taking Betaine HCl when they need it." He said you only need it temporarily, but it gives your gut the rest it needs to start producing sufficient amounts on its own. If you don't have sufficient HCl, then whole food particles get into the blood and food allergies and autoimmune issues start to show.

Like I said, this may not apply to you. Perhaps you're taking heaps of it already. But I wasn't. So I started. And, lo and behold, my allergy symptoms (I was having sneezing allergies as well as swollen fingers) and my inability to eat foods I had eaten successfully before all went away.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-cant-i-fix-this-friggin-leaky-gut.1788/post-34313>

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16777920](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16777920)

**Zinc carnosine, a health food supplement that stabilises small bowel integrity and stimulates gut repair processes.**

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Zinc carnosine (ZnC) is a health food product claimed to possess health-promoting and gastrointestinal supportive activity. Scientific evidence underlying these claims is, however, limited.

AIM:

To examine the effect of ZnC on various models of gut injury and repair, and in a clinical trial.

METHODS:

In vitro studies used pro-migratory (wounded monolayer) and proliferation (\[(3)H\]-thymidine incorporation) assays of human colonic (HT29), rat intestinal epithelial (RIE) and canine kidney (MDCK) epithelial cells. In vivo studies used a rat model of gastric damage (indomethacin/restraint) and a mouse model of small-intestinal (indomethacin) damage. Healthy volunteers (n = 10) undertook a randomised crossover trial comparing changes in gut permeability (lactulose:rhamnose ratios) before and after 5 days of indomethacin treatment (50 mg three times a day) with ZnC (37.5 mg twice daily) or placebo coadministration.

RESULTS:

ZnC stimulated migration and proliferation of cells in a dose-dependent manner (maximum effects in both assays at 100 micromol/l using HT29 cells), causing an approximate threefold increase in migration and proliferation (both p

How would a person do this? I hear of people using magnets on their liver for the heavy metal chelation. Would you make a pyramid and sunbathe in it with your gut brain axis housed in the magnetic field would you point them south toward the inside or outside? would you just lay them on your liver and fast? Ideas, please? I would appreciate anything.

[http://gizmodo.com/5495947/magnets-and-metal-nano+particles-can-build-you-a-new-liver](http://http://gizmodo.com/5495947/magnets-and-metal-nano+particles-can-build-you-a-new-liver)

> Magnets and Metal Nano-Particles Can Build You a New Liver
>
>
>
> Magnets and Metal Nano-Particles Can Build You a New LiverBetween alcohol-fueled holidays and everyday health issues, some of us might eventually wind up needing new livers or some other organs. Turns out scientists could build us those new organs using metal nano-particles suspended in a magnetic field.
>
>
>
> The basic idea is that while researchers have been able to "build" organs in the past, they've been doing it on a 2D surface. Since this can lead to abnormal behavior in cells, researchers had to build in 3D: By injecting cells with a metallic gel, the researchers have succeeded in suspending cultured cells in a three-dimensional magnetic field. With this magnetic scaffolding, organs can be grown in the right shape, and with no foreign material.
>
>
>
> Now don't get too excited and run off to celebrate this news with too many drinks just yet, this is early research and intended for those with life-threatening need for organs—not lushes with no self-control. \[Technology Review via Pop Sci\]

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:14:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29970>

[http://www.technologyreview.com/view/418056/sculpting-tissues-with-magnets/](http://http://www.technologyreview.com/view/418056/sculpting-tissues-with-magnets/)

Sculpting Tissues with Magnets

Cells treated with magnetic nanoparticles can be held in tissue-like 3-D shapes.

Being able to grow more realistic liver, heart, and other tissues in the lab could provide a new lease on life for patients waiting on the transplant list--and lead to more realistic systems for testing drugs. But tissue engineers have found that mimicking these complex, three-dimensional structures in the lab is difficult. Part of what's holding them up are flat, two-dimensional tissue culture systems that grow cells in an environment very different from that inside the body.

**Now researchers at Rice University and the MD Anderson Cancer Center in Houston have developed a simple way to make cells form 3-D structures. They developed a gel made up of a polymer, iron oxide nanoparticles, and engineered viruses called phage. When cells are added to this mixture, the phage cause them to absorb the magnetic particles. The Houston group showed that they could use a weak magnet to hold magnetized brain cancer cells in a 3-D suspension. Gene-expression studies showed that these suspended cells behave more naturally than a control group grown on a conventional flat surface: the cancer cells were producing a mix of proteins very similar to what they produce in the body. These results are described in Nature Nanotechnology this week.**

The magnetizing gel has been licensed to a startup company, Nano3D Bioscience, which will run tests to compare the technology other methods for making 3-D tissues.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:48:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29976>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/fg124m22330t2g80/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/fg124m22330t2g80/)

Abstract

Individual ferritin molecules can be sensitively detected using magnetic sample modulation (MSM) combined with contact mode atomic force microscopy (AFM). To generate an oscillating magnetic field, an alternating current (AC) was applied to a solenoid placed within the base of the AFM sample stage. When a modulated electromagnetic field is applied to samples, ferromagnetic and paramagnetic nanomaterials are induced to vibrate. The flux of the AC electromagnetic field causes the ferritin samples to vibrate with corresponding rhythm and periodicity of the applied field. This motion can be detected and mapped using contact mode AFM with a soft, nonmagnetic cantilever. Changes in the phase and amplitude of the periodic motion of the sample are sensed by the tip to selectively map vibrating magnetic nanomaterials. Particle lithography was used to create nanopatterned test platforms of ferritin for MSM measurements. Regularly spaced structures of proteins provide precise reproducible dimensions for multiple successive surface measurements at dimensions of tens of nanometers.

### chocolate — 2012-06-07T23:51:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29980>

Influence of 50 Hz magnetic fields on circadian rhythm of the suprachiasmatic nucleus activity

[http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/login.jsp?tp=&arnumber=746203&url=http%3A%2F%2Fieeexplore.ieee.org%2Fiel4%2F6018%2F16110%2F00746203.pdf%3Farnumber%3D746203](http://http://ieeexplore.ieee.org/xpl/login.jsp?tp=&arnumber=746203&url=http%3A%2F%2Fieeexplore.ieee.org%2Fiel4%2F6018%2F16110%2F00746203.pdf%3Farnumber%3D746203)

Abstract

Extremely low frequency (ELF) magnetic fields (MF), such as 50 Hz or 60 Hz magnetic fields, have been shown to suppress the nocturnal production of pineal melatonin. The amount of melatonin produced in the pineal gland changes according to the circadian rhythm. The suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) of anterior hypothalamus are supposed to be the site of a master circadian pacemaker in mammals. The sympathetic neurons that innervate the pineal gland are driven by the intrinsic activity of SCN. The study reported here is the first attempt to investigate whether the 50 Hz magnetic field exposure has any effects on the circadian rhythm generated in SCN itself. **The results of this study indicated that 50 Hz magnetic field could influence the circadian rhythm generated in the SCN only when the direction of the field was in the horizontal plane of the rat brain**

### chocolate — 2012-06-08T00:08:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29983>

[http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/early/2012/03/12/rspb.2012.0324.full](http://http://rspb.royalsocietypublishing.org/content/early/2012/03/12/rspb.2012.0324.full)

Are stress responses to geomagnetic storms mediated by the cryptochrome compass system?

> A controversial body of literature demonstrates associations of geomagnetic storms (GMS) with numerous cardiovascular, psychiatric and behavioural outcomes. Various melatonin hypotheses of GMS have suggested that temporal variation in the geomagnetic field (GMF) may be acting as an additional zeitgeber (a temporal synchronizer) for circadian rhythms, with GMS somehow interfering with the hypothesized system. The cryptochrome genes are known primarily as key components of the circadian pacemaker, ultimately involved in controlling the expression of the hormone melatonin. Cryptochrome is identified as a clear candidate for mediating the effect of GMS on humans, demonstrating the prior existence of several crucial pieces of evidence. A distinct scientific literature demonstrates the widespread use of geomagnetic information for navigation across a range of taxa. One mechanism of magnetoreception is thought to involve a light-dependent retinal molecular system mediated by cryptochrome, acting in a distinct functionality to its established role as a circadian oscillator. There is evidence suggesting that such a magnetosense—or at least the vestiges of it—may exist in humans. This paper argues that cryptochrome is not acting as secondary geomagnetic zeitgeber to influence melatonin synthesis. **Instead, it is hypothesized that the cryptochrome compass system is mediating stress responses more broadly across the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis (including alterations to circadian behaviour) in response to changes in the GMF. Two conceptual models are outlined for the existence of such responses—the first as a generalized migrational/dispersal strategy, the second as a stress response to unexpected signals to the magnetosense.** It is therefore proposed that GMS lead to disorientation of hormonal systems in animals and humans, thus explaining the effects of GMS on human health and behaviour.

### chocolate — 2012-06-08T00:12:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnets-on-the-liver-to-fake-out-the-circadian-clock-and-chelate-the-vitamin-d-recept.1789/post-29985>

[http://www.nature.com/ncomms/journal/v2/n6/full/ncomms1364.html](http://http://www.nature.com/ncomms/journal/v2/n6/full/ncomms1364.html)

Human cryptochrome exhibits light-dependent magnetosensitivity

Humans are not believed to have a magnetic sense, even though many animals use the Earth's magnetic field for orientation and navigation. One model of magnetosensing in animals proposes that geomagnetic fields are perceived by light-sensitive chemical reactions involving the flavoprotein cryptochrome (CRY). Here we show using a transgenic approach that human CRY2, which is heavily expressed in the retina, can function as a magnetosensor in the magnetoreception system of Drosophila and that it does so in a light-dependent manner. **The results show that human CRY2 has the molecular capability to function as a light-sensitive magnetosensor and reopen an area of sensory biology that is ready for further exploration in humans.**

I guess we can complain about our eyes all we want.... its gonna be awhile.

# Thread: Need Some Help with Labs...

### mmuskopf@gmail.com — 2012-06-08T05:05:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30042>

Alright, I am a Type 1 Diabetic with an A1C of 5.8....I'm 6'1" and about 230lbs...but muscular build, so a little chubby but not really fat per se. Anyway, got some lab work done and I wasn't too happy with my lipid panel AND for some reason I have elevated liver enzymes!?!?! Anyone have any advice/info they can share on this???

Thanks all!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T05:12:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30045>

i love your labs.........but you need to check your hormones.....your LDL is up because I will bet your Free T3 is low and your a glycator due to your disease. But for a T1D youre doing great.

### mmuskopf@gmail.com — 2012-06-08T06:28:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30074>

Jack, thank you. I appreciate you taking the time to read and respond. I also appreciate the praise and feel like I am heading in the right direction here but still have a few things to sort out.

Ok, I was thinking a thyroid test was in order as well and actually already emailed the doctor about it. She agreed so much that she went and ordered a TSH before I could make my case for the Free/Total T3/T4/Rt3 tests....uggghhh. Oh well, I will get it straightened out.

So, I suspect the labs will indicate a deficiency in T4 to T3 conversion... I've been doing high protein breakfasts for probably 3-4 months now and eat no more than prob 30-50g carbs per day (a la Bernstein + Leptin Rx). I kind of feel like I am doing everything right in terms of diet and exercise (heavy weights mostly) so I'm wondering if, as a T1D, the remedy will be simply more time on the Leptin Rx than most OR using medication (ie dessicated thyroid perhaps?).

Dr. Bernstein says that something like +70% of his diabetic patients with elevated LDL end up on thyroid meds and that fixes the LDL problem...but I can't help but wonder if that can be avoided...right now, only meds I take are insulin and I've always been so proud of that...lol.

I know you can't give specific medical advice, but are meds going to be the end of the road for T1Ds with apparent thyroid issues? In other words, am I wasting my time continuing to read the blog posts for "nuggets" to sort this out? I know I am on the right track here, but since it's hard to tell what advice does or does not apply to T1Ds, I can't tell if I've reached the end of the trail or not...

Btw, that question isn't necessarily only directed to Jack, anyone please feel free to chime in.

Thanks again!

M

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:28:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30088>

I think T1D have a real thyroid issue because glycation causes major LDL receptor protein changes.......so keeping your BG in control is vital......as you age as a type 1 it become harder. the other thing to follow is your DHEA level because this is a sign of immuno incompetance from glycation and lipid peroxidation as you age it is behind the poor sleep and poor immune function in diabetics.

### mmuskopf@gmail.com — 2012-06-09T09:00:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30254>

Ok, I am following you. Thanks again for the info. I'll get the thyroid labs and see what they say and take meds if necessary. Still working on getting the A1C down to true normal range...I will get it eventually and am sure it will help with everything

Thanks again!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:56:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30271>

Every diabetic should consider always getting a full thyroid panel anytime their lipids are checked........the linkage is missed by most modern docs.....but if you open internal medicine textbooks form 50-80 yrs ago......this was standard treatment for high LDL in those who glycate.

### JanSz — 2012-06-12T19:47:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/need-some-help-with-labs.1791/post-30719>

> **Jack Kruse;30373 wrote:** Every diabetic should consider always getting a full thyroid panel anytime their lipids are checked........the linkage is missed by most modern docs.....but if you open internal medicine textbooks form 50-80 yrs ago......this was standard treatment for high LDL in those who glycate.

Thank you for this info.

I context of high LDL dr Patricia Kane worry a lot about Fatty Acids and cell membrane integrity.

Vitamin C

Chromium

Phosphatidylcholine

Evening Primrose Oil

Fish Oil HIGH EPA (EPA/DHA(3:1)

EFA 4.1 Oil (ALA/LA(4:1)

Carnitine

Increase Protein Intake

.
