# Thread: Nosebleeds?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T05:17:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30046>

Why would I get sudden nosebleeds, three in less than 24 hrs, and two more over the week before? I don't usually get nosebleeds... ever.

Anyone? This is weird.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T06:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30069>

Again. Number 4 in less than 24hrs....

### Melinda — 2012-06-08T06:34:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30076>

I'm not sure but when I got my Omega six/three ratio too low, i got lots of unexplained bruising. Taking K2 allows me to eat lots of salmon and still not get bruising. I've also increased the amounts of nuts I eat. For awhile, I was completely adverse to eating any PUFA's and I think I thinned my blood too much. Nose bleeds are like bruises.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T07:04:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30078>

> **Melinda;30178 wrote:** I'm not sure but when I got my Omega six/three ratio too low, i got lots of unexplained bruising. Taking K2 allows me to eat lots of salmon and still not get bruising. I've also increased the amounts of nuts I eat. For awhile, I was completely adverse to eating any PUFA's and I think I thinned my blood too much. Nose bleeds are like bruises.

That's a really good point. I remember you saying, Souldanzer, that you were eating so much fish you were growing gills. And I saw a comment on one of the blogs (sorry can't remember which) that Dr K had to put some O6's into his diet, too, to balance out his ratio.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:25:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30087>

It could be too high an 03 level......too much vit E too......or it could be related to higher BP, or a bleeding dyscrasia. If it continues please get it looked at. It is not normal.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-08T15:04:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30099>

> **Souldanzer;30148 wrote: Why would I get sudden nosebleeds, three in less than 24 hrs, and two more over the week before? I don't usually get nosebleeds... ever.
>
>
>
> Anyone? This is weird.
>
>
> I get them around seasonal changes. particularly as fall turns to winter and winter to summer - doesn't matter where I live - for me it has something to do with the allergies, and change in moisture in the air. I never got them when I lived in Colorado or Idaho, but here in NoVa and Okinawa I'd get them... no issues in NJ or Guam.
>
>
>
> Jack Kruse;30189 wrote:** It could be too high an 03 level......too much vit E too......or it could be related to higher BP, or a bleeding dyscrasia. If it continues please get it looked at. It is not normal.

I wonder if my O3 during those time periods is closer to 1:1 considering I'm already at a 2:1????? interesting...

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-08T17:21:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30128>

I agree - get it checked. Didn't you recently have head trauma or concussion??

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:04:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30193>

Thanks, guys. Your input is so appreciated. This AM the other nostril started to bleed. I don't even blow my nose, just use a tissue to gently clean it.

@ Jennifer: Yep, I hit my head pretty badly a couple months ago. I'm not sure this would still be an issue but that thought did cross my mind...

@ Shijin: We actually are having some really humid days in CO right now, which really should help prevent nosebleeds - I thought.

@ Melinda/Kiwi/Dr. K: Score on O3 and vitamin E....

Can high O3 create cravings for O6.... I've got such a hard time leaving the nuts alone? Maybe I'll go pick up some bacon later and see if that helps.

I really don't feel like going in to see someone about it today. I'm done with strangers questioning and touching me for this week

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:20:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30194>

BP is 104/66. Doesn't appear to be the issue.

### Shijin13 — 2012-06-08T23:21:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30195>

> **Souldanzer;30295 wrote:**
>
> @ Shijin: We actually are having some really humid days in CO right now, which really should help prevent nosebleeds - I thought.
>
> (

I only get them at sea level as we transition to a warmer humid climate...don't get them at altitude or in dry environments...

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-09T21:15:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30307>

I think it's improving. Still a little bit of bleeding whenever I clean my nose but not the soaking tissues with blood kind anymore. Dropped the vitamin E supplement and am balancing out my O3's a bit more. Hopefully this will fix the problem. If it doesn't, I'll address it next week with my doc.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-11T03:30:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30475>

I've been to the ER with a nosebleed that wouldn't stop. I saw an ENT who cauterized some blood vessels, which has mostly done the trick. I had to go back for an additional cauterization, and even now, I can't actually blow my nose. You may have ruptured a blood vessel, and each time you even barely blow your nose, it bleeds again.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-11T20:12:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30556>

Thanks, PrimalPam. My nosebleeds stop... but they were starting again all day long. This appears to be improving significantly. Still a little bit of bleeding when I clean my nose but nothing major any longer. I think this was an o3/vitamin E/K2 problem.

### kcorcoran@comcast.net — 2012-06-12T04:16:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-30641>

Weak collagen. You need vitamin c, K1 (for liver to make clotting factors) and dietary collagen from skin, bone broths, chicken feet and/or gelatin. Great for skin, hair and nails too. I get nose bleeds too if I don't include k1 with my high dose cod liver oil supplement from green pastures.

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-24T22:50:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-33346>

Ever since I started "absolute Paleo" (i.e. no cheating!), and CT, my nosebleeds have completely stopped. I was still getting minor ones from nose-blowing; now, I no longer have those.

### IceBarbie — 2012-06-25T22:08:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-33512>

You can quickly stop most nosebleeds by applying ice to the back of your neck. I can't remember if the ice should be closer to the base of the skull or the down a little further so just use an icepack that covers that 3-4" area.

### purplemonster — 2012-06-28T04:42:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nosebleeds.1792/post-34096>

Probably has got to do with the changing weather?

# Thread: A Salmon Question for the Aussie's or Kiwi's.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### sums-sums — 2012-06-08T05:28:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30054>

I am an American expat living in Sydney and went to buy my weekly Salmon fillets from the fish market. I asked the owner, ' is this wild caught or farm raised.' He informed me that 90% of the Salmon sold in the Southern Hemisphere is farm raised. Oh no, now what do I do? I love Salmon and I don't know if I should now not eat it due to it being farm raised.

Do you eat the Salmon or is there another fish I could substitute for it?

Thanks in advance for your help.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-08T05:44:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30060>

Yep, we get almost all farm-raised too in NZ. I don't tend to eat/buy it. Our local fish shop occasionally gets wild caught (it is labelled as such) and when she gets it I always buy it! If it isn't in, I buy other wild caught fish which is usually in pretty good variety. Not sure what you get in Oz, but in NZ we get a pretty good range and we regularly get things like deep sea cod, too. I basically just turn up, ask what was caught this morning and double check it's wild. Also, when Bluff oysters are in season, eat em up!! And if you can get whitebait (only get the NZ stuff) at certain times of the year, buy it...it's super good for you.

### sums-sums — 2012-06-08T06:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30067>

> **KiwiLauren;30162 wrote:** Yep, we get almost all farm-raised too in NZ. I don't tend to eat/buy it. Our local fish shop occasionally gets wild caught (it is labelled as such) and when she gets it I always buy it! If it isn't in, I buy other wild caught fish which is usually in pretty good variety. Not sure what you get in Oz, but in NZ we get a pretty good range and we regularly get things like deep sea cod, too. I basically just turn up, ask what was caught this morning and double check it's wild. Also, when Bluff oysters are in season, eat em up!! And if you can get whitebait (only get the NZ stuff) at certain times of the year, buy it...it's super good for you.

Thanks KiwiLauren. I had a feeling that I have to say goodbye to the farm raised Salmon...so sad. I love Salmon not only for the taste, but all the healthy fats it contains as well. I will start asking for fresh wild caught fish, I appreciate the advice about whitebait.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-08T13:35:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30090>

Eat the fattiest southern polar fish you can find......it will make up for it.

### primat — 2012-06-12T12:40:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-30671>

Wild caught is hard to get in Norway too, I have take time off work if I want to get to the fish market - which is insanely expensive too.

I always get wild cod etc when it shows up in my local store, otherwise I tell myself that farmed fish is better than no fish :

/rant

### sums-sums — 2012-06-18T12:16:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-31901>

Thanks Dr. Kruse for your response. It prompted me to have a talk with our local fish guy. I basically asked him what are some of the fattiest southern polar fish that are wild caught and not farm raised. He basically told me, 'none.' He said even a fatty fish he would recommend as a substitute is also farm raised. He then tried to explain to me how big the farms are out in the middle of the ocean, that it issn't a big deal and that in a year or so all the fish sold in the world will be farm raised....(i can see your eyes rolling in the back of your head). He then proceded to tell me that everything is farmed raised, chicken, cattle, ect....

So I am stuck eating low fat wild caught white fish for now.

Primat -- I started thinking like you...maybe farm raised fish is better than no fish.

### primat — 2012-06-19T14:34:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/a-salmon-question-for-the-aussies-or-kiwis.1793/post-32111>

> **sums-sums;32007 wrote:**
>
> Primat -- I started thinking like you...maybe farm raised fish is better than no fish.

Guess we have to go fish our own dinners...  To bad the fish farming industry is severely polluting the local environments for wild fish too. The amount of food pellets and antibiotics (and fish feces) below the farms are a big problem to local faunas.

As far as I know, tiny fish like [bristlings](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sprattus_sprattus) aren't farmed, and while I do enjoy a [100g box of them canned in ev olive oil](http://http://www.kingoscar.com/products-by-market/usa/usa-sardines/brisling-sardines-in-olive-oil-5.html) a few times a week it's not suitable for dinner. Also I feel I'm missing out since they are beheaded  Guess I'm becoming more primal hehe

# Thread: Alternative anti-inflammatories
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-06-08T18:03:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30156>

Chocolate started a great discussion about this, but since it was an "Ask Jack", it was answered and promptly closed.

Jack said he's "Not an aspirin fan at all.....love turmeric, reseveratrol, krill oil, and fish oils for anti inflammatory effects that are natural."

I really like using krill oil for headaches, as I've mentioned before. And I'm using turmeric daily in my cooking.

But recently someone suggested Bromelain for muscle inflammation. I tried it yesterday for a sore back, when ice just wasn't fixing all the pain, and I'm quite impressed. You have to take it on an empty stomach, between meals, or the body will use the bromelain as a digestive enzyme. So I have taken it anywhere from an hour after eating to an hour before eating. 1200 mg, 3x per day.

After I took a dose, I remembered that I'm allergic to raw pineapple; it makes my tongue and lips swell. But I've had no issues with the bromelain. Amazing what stomach acid can do.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-08T23:31:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30198>

> After I took a dose, I remembered that I'm allergic to raw pineapple; it makes my tongue and lips swell. But I've had no issues with the bromelain. Amazing what stomach acid can do.

That might mean that you are having trouble with \*unripe\* pineapple. Most people eat their pineapple way before they're ready... they need to have a dark golden color, preferably almost some brown spots when you cut them, and a bit of softness when you touch them. The leaves on top should almost be wilted and come off easily without pulling really.

Once you cut the top off your ripe pineapple, turn it over and put it cut side down on a plate. Then put in fridge for 24hrs. This will distribute the sugars evenly for a tasty, juice, sweet pineapple.

Don't try this if whatever you're experiencing allergy-wise could be dangerous

### TerrierMom — 2012-06-09T00:04:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30203>

I'm wondering the same thing, especially when a headache erupts...so used to reaching for Advil, aleve, aspirin, Tylenol.... Now not sure I should be!!!

### ATL_Paleo — 2012-06-09T00:26:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-30206>

Two possible solutions. I have used both, and had good results. They lean more to relieving muscle soreness, but can help headaches.

White Willow Bark -> [http://www.iherb.com/Nature-s-Way-White-Willow-Bark-100-Capsules/2063](http://http://www.iherb.com/Nature-s-Way-White-Willow-Bark-100-Capsules/2063)

[http://altmedicine.about.com/od/completeazindex/a/willow\_bark.htm](http://http://altmedicine.about.com/od/completeazindex/a/willow_bark.htm)

Arnica Montana ... homeopathic solution -> [http://www.iherb.com/Hyland-s-Arnica-Montana-30X-250-Tablets/3788](http://http://www.iherb.com/Hyland-s-Arnica-Montana-30X-250-Tablets/3788)

### Glen PDQ — 2012-09-29T06:24:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55163>

Bromelain was found to increase phagocytosis by 100%. I don't remember the dose but Jack Kruse says 11pm-2am is the time when the body is most efficient at apoptosis and protein recycling. I can't remember if phagocytosis takes place in that time slot, but if so, perhaps taking it an hour before going to bed would make renewal more efficient.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-29T16:35:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55203>

+1000 on the Arnica Montana. We have found that to work great on DH's back when he gets muscle spasms and/or other joint pains.

Peppermint oil works great for headache relief. Lavender can be helpful too.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-29T17:13:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55208>

How do you "take" the peppermint oil (or lavendar)? My sons are plagued with frequent headaches.

### Endless — 2012-09-29T17:41:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55222>

> How do you "take" the peppermint oil (or lavendar)? My sons are plagued with frequent headaches.

It's aromatherapy...peppermint oil on the temples is good...make sure to test iton your skin, itcan be strong and diluting with a carrier oil is a good idea. A sachet of either near your head while sleeping can help as well.

### BJK77 — 2012-09-29T17:49:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55226>

> **bigknitwit;56208 wrote: How do you "take" the peppermint oil (or lavendar)? My sons are plagued with frequent headaches.
>
>
> Endless;56222 wrote:** It's aromatherapy...peppermint oil on the temples is good...make sure to test iton your skin, itcan be strong and diluting with a carrier oil is a good idea. A sachet of either near your head while sleeping can help as well.

Yes, definitely dilute with a carrier oil if you put it directly on the skin, but it is WONDERFUL for headaches if applied on the temples. Even just taking a few deep breaths from the open bottle can work wonders.

Peppermint tea can also be helpful. My daughter gets headaches from the bus ride home from school many days and a cup of peppermint tea usually calms them right down.

### bigknitwit — 2012-09-30T15:00:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/alternative-anti-inflammatories.1798/post-55369>

Peppermint oil in the toilet got me to finally be able to pee after delivering one of my babies! I never thought of it for headaches. I will pick some up. Thanks for the info.

# Thread: Zinc With Oral Rehydration Therapy Reduces Stool Output and Duration of Diarrhea in H
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-with-oral-rehydration-therapy-reduces-stool-output-and-duration-of-diarrhea-in-h.1800/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-08T23:29:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zinc-with-oral-rehydration-therapy-reduces-stool-output-and-duration-of-diarrhea-in-h.1800/post-30197>

[http://www.idpas.org/pdf/2689Zinc%20With%20Oral%20Rehydration%20Therapy%20Reduces%20Stool%20Output%20and%20Duration%20of%20Diarrhea%20in%20Hospitalized%20Children%20A%20Randomized%20Controlled%20Trial2689.pdf](http://http://www.idpas.org/pdf/2689Zinc%20With%20Oral%20Rehydration%20Therapy%20Reduces%20Stool%20Output%20and%20Duration%20of%20Diarrhea%20in%20Hospitalized%20Children%20A%20Randomized%20Controlled%20Trial2689.pdf)

Zinc With Oral Rehydration Therapy Reduces Stool Output and

Duration of Diarrhea in Hospitalized Children: A Randomized

Controlled Trial

> ABSTRACT
>
> Objectives: The authors evaluated the effect of zinc treatment
>
> as an adjunct to oral rehydration therapy on stool output and
>
> diarrheal duration in children with acute noncholera diarrhea
>
> with dehydration.
>
> Methods: This double-blind, randomized, controlled trial was
>
> conducted at two urban hospitals in New Delhi. A total of 287
>
> dehydrated male patients, ages 3 to 36 months, with diarrhea
>
> for 72 hours were enrolled. They were assigned to zinc or
>
> placebo by a randomization scheme stratified by age ( or >12
>
> months) and weight for height (65%â€“80% or >80% National
>
> Centre for Health Statistics median). Participants in the zinc
>
> group received 15 mg (12 months) or 30 mg (>12 months)
>
> elemental zinc daily in three divided doses for 14 days. The
>
> main outcome measures were stool output and diarrheal
>
> duration.
>
> Results: Zinc treatment reduced total stool output (ratio of
>
> geometric means, 0.69; 95% confidence interval \[CI\]: 0.48,
>
> 0.99) and stool output per day of diarrhea (ratio of geometric
>
> means, 0.76; 95% CI: 0.59, 0.98). The risk of continued diarrhea
>
> was lower (relative hazards, 0.76; 95% CI: 0.59, 0.97) and
>
> the proportion of diarrheal episodes lasting 5 days (odds
>
> ratio, 0.49; 95% CI: 0.25, 0.97) or 7 days was less (odds
>
> ratio, 0.09; 95% CI: 0.01, 0.73) in the zinc group.
>
> Conclusions: This study demonstrates a beneficial effect of
>
> zinc administered during acute diarrhea on stool output, diarrheal
>
> duration, and proportion of episodes lasting more than 7
>
> days. **_The effects are large enough to merit routine use of zinc
>
> during acute diarrhea in developing countries._** JPGN 38:34â€“40,
>
> 2004\. Key Words: Acute diarrheaâ€”Randomized controlled
>
> trialâ€”Stool outputâ€”Zinc. Â© 2003 Lippincott Williams &

I don't get the developing countries part.

# Thread: Zeliq article
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-06-09T07:27:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30250>

[http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-procedure-fat-cells-woman-surgery.html](http://http://medicalxpress.com/news/2012-06-procedure-fat-cells-woman-surgery.html)

The instrument clamps to the body and gets cold. It doesn't freeze but gets cold enough that it fatally damages fat cells just beneath the skin.

"It doesn't have to be very cold; the skin doesn't freeze or frostbite," Ridenour said. "Except for the tugging on the skin, it's painless."

He couldn't say what the effective temperature was. The manufacturer, Zeltiq Aesthetics Inc., won't reveal that information.

I'll bet i know the effective temperature--it's the same as a baggie full of ice and water...$1500, please.

### Owl — 2012-06-09T08:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/zeliq-article.1802/post-30253>

You've made a typo in the name, it's Zeltiq.

From [http://inyourface.ocregister.com/2009/12/17/lipo-rival-zeltiq-aims-to-freeze-away-fat/13699/](http://http://inyourface.ocregister.com/2009/12/17/lipo-rival-zeltiq-aims-to-freeze-away-fat/13699/)

â€œIt chills the fat to about 5 degrees Centigrade \[41 degrees Fahrenheit\]. This is enough to cause frozen crystalline formation within the fat cells,â€￾ said Dr. Christopher Zachary, chairman of the UCI Department of Dermatology. â€œKeep them like this for 30-60 minutes and this induces â€˜programmed cell deathâ€™ a gradual process whereby the fat cells just quietly disappear.â€￾

From [http://www.americanhealthandbeauty.com/articles/?article=3138](http://http://www.americanhealthandbeauty.com/articles/?article=3138)

"It's very controlled freezing of fat cells and then the fat cells don't rupture, there's no immediate death to the fat cells, it triggers apoptosis which is a programmed cell death so they die gradually over a period of three or four months. There is a specific process that the cell undergoes as it dies away." Dr. Fitzpatrick explains, "Initially it sets up an inflammatory reaction so if you biopsy the skin â€“ the fat cell is surrounded by an inflammatory infiltrate so there are white blood cells that move into the area and actually the area feels tender, it feels kind of like a bruise if you push on it but as that resolves, the fat cells then are just gradually reabsorbed. So they go into a programmed cellular death where that's how your body gets rid of cells that are no longer useful so the cold doesn't immediately kill the cell it goes into a stage where it dies gradually."

The treatment is completely painless and is pretty simple. As Dr. Fitpatrick describes, "We have an applicator cup... with a vacuum and it pulls the skin and fat up into the cup and there are cooling plates on both sides of the cup that drop the temperature down and that controlled drop in temperature freezes the fat cells and actually once we finish we keep them frozen for about an hour â€“ when we take the applicator cup off there's actually like a frozen stick of butter there, the skin is very firm like a little frozen brick and it is red at that point. Now it thaws over period of 5 or 10 minutes or so and then there's some redness that the skin has afterwards which is just related to increased blood flow immediately post treatment. Occasionally the patient will get a bruise much like hickey I mean so that's kind of secondary to the suction of the vacuum. If there is a bruise it will generally fade over a period of about a week or so and as I mentioned if you biopsy the skin a few days after you will see an inflammatory reaction around the fat cells and that is accompanied clinically by just some tenderness in the area. Dr. Fitzpatrick says that it is very easy to predict how the fat will be lost and that after the cells die, they are reabsorbed into the body. "You lose that layer of fat cells that's right underneath the surface of the skin. It's a very predictable process, it's good for spot reduction but right underneath the outer layer of the skin, right underneath the dermis there's a layer that will be lost that's about at the most about a half inch in thickness. That layer will be the same no matter how much fat is underneath it, you cannot make it take out more fat if there's a big thick layer. So, sometimes if you have a thick layer you may want to go back after about three of four months and retreat the same spot but in most patients the single treatment works really well.

Around 25% of the unwanted fat can be removed in a single treatment. Slight contour differences are visible after three weeks but dramatic results may take between 3 to 6 months to be seen. This is the period of time the body needs to naturally eliminate the fat. Some patients may actually need a second treatment, which can be done after two to four months, reducing another 20% of fat."

### Greg rx — 2012-06-11T23:13:22+0300

Ok, I am dying to share this. I have been spot cting my abdomen for the last 8 or 10 weeks along with air adapting by riding my bike in shorts and short sleeves. This started back in March here in Michigan with temps in the thirties. Two relative points 1) I reached my halfway goal this morning and it appears I lost all 30 pounds from my stomach. 2) On my way down the stairs after weighing myself, I caught sight of my calf and ankle muscles in the mirror at the bottom of the stairs . Even though I am still quite obese, this part of my body looks ripped and well defined. Keep in mind the extreme cold (and exercise ) this part of my body was exposed to. Now I just have to figure out how to get full body ct.

# Thread: The power of self directed adaptation.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 20
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Dali Dula — 2012-06-09T16:24:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30264>

It has now been one year since I decided to take full responsibility for my health care. Dropped the diabetes meds and went paleo. I had to adapt to a new diet which I was able to do. I kept tweaking and adapting further adding another baby step each time I became comfortable with the changes. Then I started leptinrx and kept tweaking (adapting) and then the huge adaptation of CT in March 2012. Each of these changes has brought significant improvement in my life.

After working through whatever initial discomfort these changes brought I have come out the other side an improved human. So now I am starting to be in awe the power of self directed adaptation. When you adapt to your environment or situation the rewards of that adaptation far outweigh the effort or fleeting discomfort to achieve that adaptation.

This is changing the way I approach life now . So I would like to offer a thread to discuss this concept and hear if other people are experiencing anything like this. **The less you try to control and the more you adapt the better life is.**

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:28:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30265>

This is an awesome idea for a thread.......this should become the Monster Thread on here.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-09T16:34:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30266>

Self Directed Adaptation to me:

If you aren't willing to sacrifice your comfort, you just don't have what it takes to make a difference. This maybe harsh but you never can become optimal if you allow things to always subvert you. you must retrain how you think to become the person you were born to be.

People want to be inspired. Leaders use the language of possibility, while behaving they are on their way to immortality. Greatness has a purpose that few others see initially but all seem to feel right away. Invest in you and become your own guru of inspiration. We need more starfishes these days.

Unconscious incompetence is always the first step on the way to acceptance. Historically, most folks wont be aware of this fault in thinking.

I'm no longer allowing me to be my built in excuse for anything any longer. **Lead yourself to your Optimal self.** When you follow the crowd it directs you to a place called average..........

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-09T17:57:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30285>

One of the tings I am trying to figure out is are there universal principles that can be applied toward any direction of adaptation not just those that directly affect our physical health. Jack developed his protocols so that although there may be some initial discomfort they are not painful or harmful but also accessible. This is truly the genius of Jack to me. So how do we figure out how to design protocols for whatever issues we may face? This is **power**. I want to apply these principles to every aspect of my life.

Dali Dula,

Thanks for this thread! The comments here by you and Jack are just what I need for re-inspiration!

I see all these ultra hyped ads for stuff like Protandim, and here Jack is offering a real system, based on science....for freeee! Thank you, Jack!

And your question about designing protocols for whatEVER issues we face ....is brilliant! This thread rocks!

### johnnyb — 2012-06-09T22:03:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30313>

This fits better with my road to optimal. Some of the things I want are out do my reach. Today I move towards them so that I can reach them.

### AKMan — 2012-06-09T23:37:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30328>

Here's one of the many things I've done. I'm a retired military guy and have access to VA and military hospital (tricare). These two, working together, had me on 5 different medicines and headed down a path of constant medication. After going primal and ditching all the meds, I thought I would never go back to these quacks.

After hearing Jack harp on labs and testing, I now use these guys to my advantage. The VA and the military hospital will do bloodwork every 6 mo. I have it timed so I can get bloodwork done every 3 months between them.

They are famous for just testing the minimal amount, but now when I go in, I'll say, "do you suppose you could check my Vit D?, or Testosterone? or hsCRP?" They give me funny looks, but usually will add it to the lab sheet. I also go and get the results and read them myself, as they will only follow-up if something is wrong--in their eyes.

I will never again put my health in the hands of others.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-10T01:16:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30348>

When my previous doctor would not order an HS-CRP test, I ordered one myself, online. I gave the results to my new, low-carb friendly doctor, and he included the test on the battery of tests he ordered. He didn't include a hormone panel, so I went online again and ordered those tests. I'll give the results to him next visit.

I will obtain optimal.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-10T15:49:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30399>

Another aspect of implementing these changes is the unexpected. My motivation for going paleo, leptin rx, and ct has been to deal with a disease (t2d), but each of these adaptations has yielded one or more unexpected bonuses. After going paleo besides improving my bg levels I no longer sun burn and I pee like a race horse and i don't need Viagra. After the leptin reset all carb cravings disappeared and body comp improved dramatically along with increased energy. Then there is CT and the circadian cycles which brought about sleeping like a log all night, incredible body comp, almost zero systemic inflammation, increased vo2, better mood and amazing labs. These are only the things I can think of now.

So now when I make changes, no matter what my goal, I have come to expect bonuses. Truly expect the unexpected. Thanks Jack

### nosnhojjennifer@yahoo.com — 2012-06-10T16:44:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30403>

Jack developed his protocols so that although there may be some initial discomfort they are not painful or harmful but also accessible. This is truly the genius of Jack to me. So how do we figure out how to design protocols for whatever issues we may face? This is **power. I want to apply these principles to every aspect of my life.
> agreed and I think you have summed it up well here:
>
>
>
> Dali Dula;30366 wrote:** The less you try to control and the more you adapt the better life is.**

I spent a lot of time trying to make my body, life, people, situations conform to what I want and all I found was struggle, a constant war. Now, when I see a pattern that I don't want in my life, I start asking different questions. In stead of figuring out how I will change the outside, I look on the inside and see what can I do. I can only control my own actions, thoughts, choices. And to my surprise, I get good results with this. I'm so much happier! There is a saying, if you look in the mirror and the face you see is dirty, you don't wash the mirror, you wash your face! I've stopped trying to change the mirror.

### SeaHorse — 2012-06-10T17:00:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30406>

One of the benefits of leaving the middle of the road is that I find I can see what I need to do more clearly. It was only when I allowed myself to consider that only eating meat/fish/fats was ok, that I saw how histamine and oxalates were really an issue for me. This has cut down my veg. intake enormously, and also means I have to be careful with what kinds of meat and fish I eat. So, for now I don't have the huge variety that has been the cornerstone of what I had believed was healthy. But I'm healthier now, so off to the extremes I go...happy to leave the middle and explore the edges.

### Barry — 2012-06-11T01:36:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30449>

my 2 cents:

Create a new Normal

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-11T02:21:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-30458>

### JanSz — 2012-06-15T18:03:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-power-of-self-directed-adaptation.1803/post-31349>

> **AKMan;30431 wrote: Here's one of the many things I've done. I'm a retired military guy and have access to VA and military hospital (tricare). These two, working together, had me on 5 different medicines and headed down a path of constant medication. After going primal and ditching all the meds, I thought I would never go back to these quacks.
>
>
>
> After hearing Jack harp on labs and testing, I now use these guys to my advantage. The VA and the military hospital will do bloodwork every 6 mo. I have it timed so I can get bloodwork done every 3 months between them.
>
>
>
> They are famous for just testing the minimal amount, but now when I go in, I'll say, "do you suppose you could check my Vit D?, or Testosterone? or hsCRP?" They give me funny looks, but usually will add it to the lab sheet. I also go and get the results and read them myself, as they will only follow-up if something is wrong--in their eyes.
>
>
>
> I will never again put my health in the hands of others.**

**

Thank you for serving my Country.

You can get a boatload of information by doing few basic tests, 3 panels and 5 individual items.

See post #7, link below.

Most doctors start with those tests, so the question is really in how one reads them.

Get copy of your tests from your doc's office. If you have most of the input, post it (on separate thread, Optimal Labs section).

I have made a spreadsheet that should give one good portion of the answer that otherwise you can get by ordering

BodyBio Report

\-------------

I have made a collection of information that may be called JackK+

It is all on post #1 here:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?20061-Dr.-Patricia-Kane&p=171942#post171942)

\-------------

\===========

\===========

• **Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (14)

BLOOD CHEMISTRY PANEL:

Alanine Transaminase (ALT or SGPT)

Albumin

Alkaline Phosphatase

Asparate Transaminase (AST or SGOT)

Bilirubin, Total

BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)

Calcium

CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

Chloride

Cholesterol, Total

Creatinine

Globulin

Glucose

HDL

LDL

Potassium

Protein, Total

Sodium

Triglycerides

• Individual Tests**

o Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)

o Iron, Total

o Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

o Phosphorus

o Uric Acid, serum

• CBC with Differential and Platelet

COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT w/ MANUAL DIFFERENTIAL:**

WBC

RBC

RDW

Hemoglobin

Hematocrit

MCV

MCH

MCHC

Mean Platelet Volume

Platelet Count

Neutrophils

Lymphocytes

Basophils

Eosinophils

Monocytes

..

# Thread: minerals and light exposure
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:08:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30293>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/82343x46733r6x44/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/82343x46733r6x44/)

Effects of light on trace elments and triiodothyronine levels in plasma of mirror carp ( Cyprinus carpio )

Abstract

The changes of copper, zinc, iron, magnesium, manganese, and triiodothyronine as a result of short- and long-term isothermal exposure to light were determined in the plasma of mirror carp (Cyprinus carpio). Two groups of fish were subject to a 3-mo treatment that consisted of (1) 8h of light followed by 16 h of darkness and (2) 16 h of light followed by 8h of darkness at 25Â°C. No significant changes were recorded between the two groups at the beginning of the study. After 3 mo, the triiodothyronine and elements levels as well as the growth rates of fish in the long-time exposure period were significantly higher than those in the short-time exposure (p

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:12:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30295>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/37874716631m7lp5/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/37874716631m7lp5/)

Selenium and zinc in patients with acute and chronic uveitis

Abstract

Patients with chronic inflammation often show decreased serum levels of trace elements. This study aimed to investigate serum levels of selenium and zinc in patients with uveitis. Twenty-four patients (13 female, 11 male) with a mean age of 40.6Â±15.5 yr were included in this study. According to their underlying disease, they were divided into acute (n=13) or chronic (n=11) uveitis. Selenium and zinc determination was performed by atomic absorption spectroscopy in EDTA blood samples. Patients with acute or first-time uveitis showed selenium and zinc concentrations within the normal range. In contrast to this, patients with chronic recurrent uveitis tend to result in decreased selenium and zinc levels. Especially selenium showed a remarkable reduction in serum concentration below the normal range. Furthermore, there was a tendency to decreased trace element concentrations with increasing age. A sex dependency could not been found. Especially patients with chronic, recurring uveitis show remarkable decreased selenium concentration in the EDTA-blood. Further studies should investigate possible positive effects of therapeutic selenium and zinc supplementation in patients with chronic, recurrent uveitis.

Index Entries Zinc - selenium - uveitis - atomic absorption spectroscopy

Fulltext Preview

### chocolate — 2012-06-09T19:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minerals-and-light-exposure.1806/post-30296>

[http://www.springerlink.com/content/3279288n73732628/](http://http://www.springerlink.com/content/3279288n73732628/)

Selenium, iron, copper, and zinc levels and copper-to-zinc ratios in serum of patients at different stages of viral hepatic diseases

Abstract

Viral hepatic diseases, especially those induced by the hepatitis B virus, can progress into more serious pathological outcomes and eventually to hepatocellular carcinoma. A growing body of evidence indicates that many trace elements play important roles in a number of carcinogenic processes that proceed through various mechanisms. To examine the status of trace elements during the development of hepatic carcinoma, we determined the selenium, iron, copper, and zinc levels and copper-to-zinc ratios in the serum of patients at different stages of viral hepatic disease. We observed significant changes in the selenium, iron copper, and zinc levels in the serum of patients having hepatocellular carcinoma, relative to those of healthy controls (p

A case-control study on selenium, zinc, and copper in plasma and hair of subjects affected by breast and lung cancer

Abstract

The purpose of our study was to investigate the relationship between plasma and hair levels of Se, Zn, and Cu, and cancer. We selected a total of 66 patients affected by either breast (38) or lung (28) cancer. They entered into the study at the onset of disease, and before any chemical or radiotherapy. Controls were randomly selected among healthy people and were matched for sex, age, smoking habits, and residence. In the group of breast cancer, a significant decrease in hair Se was found compared to controls (p

# Thread: How do you pass the time while CT'ing?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PrimalPam — 2012-06-09T22:37:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30316>

Just started CT'ing. Did the face dunks and after all the resistance I put up, I just...got in. Got the water down to the mid-50's in the tub, and I enjoyed it! I think I stayed in around 20 mins. I'll start using a timer soon.

But...I was bored! What do you all do to pass the time?

### indigogirl — 2012-06-09T22:56:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30320>

I listen to music on my iPhone. I use the songs to push myself to stay in longer...as in can't get out for another song, etc, etc...and I choose long songs!

### AKMan — 2012-06-09T23:21:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30326>

Music here, too.

I also do something weird called 'oil pulling'. It's where you swish sesame (or coconut or sunflower) oil in your mouth for 20 minutes. It really helps your teeth and has some effect on leptin sensors. When you do it, you need in a quiet place and focus on breathing. I found the CT bath a perfect place to do it.

Otherwise, I find the really cold bath very relaxing, makes me somewhat catatonic. I really look forward to it. Especially if I had a serious workout earlier that day.

### Lyndra — 2012-06-10T00:03:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30333>

I listen to a podcast. Chris Kresser, Abel James, Jimmy Moore, etc. Listening to a conversation helps the time pass plus I learn a few things. Because I am working on healing arthritis in my thumbs, I need to keep my hands in the water, so reading a book isn't an option. I did try that my first time but I shook so much I could hardly focus on the pages but now I'm much more comfortable.

### David — 2012-06-10T00:12:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30335>

> **AKMan;30429 wrote:** Music here, too.

I couldn't even get comfortable without music.

### dkenward@yahoo.com — 2012-06-10T00:45:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30341>

I use a portable DVD player to catch up on all the DVD boxes I've bought over the years, thinking I would want to watch them some day. It's entertaining and makes the time fly by. It's also a good way to keep track of the time. A typical half-hour comedy is 22 minutes. A drama is 45. So depending on my schedule I usually aim for 45 minutes during the week and an hour+ on the weekends.

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-10T00:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30344>

Pandora........is how I roll

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-10T01:08:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30347>

Thanks...you guys are awesome! Firing up my ipod for tomorrow...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-10T03:24:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30363>

I listen to podcasts, read on the kindle or even an audiobook works great. For me the mind has to be going and thinking.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-10T05:29:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30378>

When I started CT the pond was cold enough so I would swim to pass the time. Swimming in >50f is hard. Now I use a cast iron tub in the yard. I make up ipod play lists for my ever increasing time and get out when the music ends. I also read, but I don't retain much. I really can't wait for autumn when the native waters get cold. When you can swim and frolic the time passes quickly. I also do fire breathing in the tub. Firebreathing is a yoga thing where you engage the core muscles and breath in and out rapidly through the nose for like a hundred times.

### PaleoDentist — 2012-06-10T05:57:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30385>

I read Paleo Books and listen to podcasts.

### Nymue — 2012-06-10T16:07:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30401>

> **indigogirl;30423 wrote:** I listen to music on my iPhone. I use the songs to push myself to stay in longer...as in can't get out for another song, etc, etc...and I choose long songs!

I do this, too. At first I tried reading or listening to podcasts, but would still get distracted by the cold. Now I put on the most hardcore music that I have, a playlist that I would work out to, for instance, and use the intensity to drive myself. And will use "one more song" as a goal, and then will usually say, "ok, one more"!

### Kpcst — 2012-06-10T17:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30411>

I listen to podcasts. Or watch tv.

Sent from my DROID RAZR using Tapatalk

### PrimalPam — 2012-06-10T18:56:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30423>

I just made a couple of 35-minute playlists. Just finished a CT session...surprisingly, the playlist ended before I was ready to get out!

### Lanie — 2012-06-11T07:20:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30503>

I watch back seasons of Grey's Anatomy on Netflix by propping up my iPad outside the tub.

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-11T14:43:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30510>

Lanie, I checked out your blog. It's great. I sometimes have that morning sleepiness you talk about. I have had this happen and have found it accompanied by BG spikes. Next time you feel like that check BG.

### omlh@todmi.com — 2012-06-11T20:26:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-30561>

I read books - the more gripping the better so that I don't feel the cold!

### Optimalbound — 2012-06-14T21:34:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31213>

I've downloaded Dr. Kruse's entire blog onto my Kindle reading tablet. Reading it makes my head hurt so much that my body feels comfortable by comparison.

### Arthur Morris — 2012-06-15T23:42:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31415>

Books on tape on my Bose.... I actually look forward to the time.

### Dan in Utah — 2012-06-16T00:32:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31425>

Summer in Utah = cold streams with fly rod in hand. I don't use waders anymore...ha, ha. Just shorts and a beanie. (Too bad I can't go more often. Beats a cold tub any day.) Helps with stress, too. I'm headed out tonight. Midges are coming off just before sunset. If I'm lucky, I'll have some coconut oil fried trout for dinner. Wahoo!

### Dali Dula — 2012-06-16T14:46:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31523>

> **Dan in Utah;31531 wrote:** Summer in Utah = cold streams with fly rod in hand. I don't use waders anymore...ha, ha. Just shorts and a beanie. (Too bad I can't go more often. Beats a cold tub any day.) Helps with stress, too. I'm headed out tonight. Midges are coming off just before sunset. If I'm lucky, I'll have some coconut oil fried trout for dinner. Wahoo!

Nice. Wild is good.

### Caroline Cooper — 2012-06-16T19:24:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-31570>

> **PrimalPam;30418 wrote:** Just started CT'ing. Did the face dunks and after all the resistance I put up, I just...got in. Got the water down to the mid-50's in the tub, and I enjoyed it! I think I stayed in around 20 mins. I'll start using a timer soon.
>
>
>
> But...I was bored! What do you all do to pass the time?

Hi PrimalPam,

I don't find the cold very comfortable at this point. I am just a beginner but this is how I past the time in the tub:

1\. I face dunk and hold my breath throughout the time in the tub.

2\. I meditate and try to quiet my mind. I find the cold tough to take and work at excepting the cold and not fighting it.

3\. I listen to the aerial sounds around my tub. (I would like to do my tub outside.) I keep the window open and listen to the birds, wind, etc. I find it all very stimulating.

This morning I could hear the crows. The young crows have just left the nest and are harassing their parent's for food because they are not very good at getting food for themselves yet. I could hear the wings of one crow, woosh, woosh, woosh, and land on the roof, his nails scratching around on the metal. The local song birds took to the attack because crows steal their eggs and offspring. It was quite a fight.

### jmathisen — 2012-06-28T05:13:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-pass-the-time-while-cting.1808/post-34106>

> **August;33718 wrote:** May I ask how you like your kindle? Is it by change one of the black and white ones? I was thinking that might be a \[cough\] better solution for reading after dark . . . I think you can reverse things so that it is white on black, right? Do you know if the B&W kindles also offer rudimentary web browsing/internet . . and if so, if you can do the white on black thing for that too? Is it readable with the blue blocking glasses? (I understand most models will also 'read to you' . . that would be nice)

I put on some opera, and read a book on my nook. The new nook with Glowlight has a nice gentle glow to it, so I can turn off the lights, sit in the near dark, and think cold and dark thoughts while I read. It is very similar to the kindle, although a barnes and noble product instead of amazon, but much similarity in terms of features.

The glowlight makes it awesome. Check it out.

# Thread: excess protein and bone loss
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-06-10T01:02:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-30346>

[http://www.betterbones.com/bonenutrition/protein/benefits.aspx](http://http://www.betterbones.com/bonenutrition/protein/benefits.aspx)

> Several worldwide surveys document that the countries with highest animal protein intakes are those with highest hip fracture rates. The proposed explanation of this relationship between animal protein and hip fracture incidence relates to the fact that animal protein is rich in acid-forming, sulfur-containing amino acids and low in base-forming precursors (such as vegetable sources of potassium citrate). Further, the contemporary cultures consuming a high animal protein diet also tend to under-consume vegetables, fruits, nuts and seeds, food high in base-forming precursors. This combination contributes to chronic low-grade metabolic acidosis and subsequent bone weakening. The growing body of literature documenting the association between chronic, low-grade metabolic acidosis and bone loss bolsters the argument that high dietary protein, if not balanced with high base-forming precursor intake, can have a detrimental impact on bone.
>
>
> Dietary protein is best considered in the context of the entire diet of each individual, particularity in terms of the balance between acid-forming and base-forming foodstuffs and overall mineral intake. In simple terms this can be seen as the relationship between protein and potassium intakes, as documented by Dr. Lynda Frassetto at the University of California, San Francisco. In this regard, insights from our evolutionary past might be helpful. **Anthropologists speculate **that our human ancestral diet during the _Paleolithic Period 100,000 years ago_** was more nutrient dense than contemporary diets not only in protein, but also in calcium, potassium, magnesium and zinc, while low in sodium chloride, sugars and void of refined carbohydrates. For example, calcium, potassium and protein intakes were two to three times higher than current intake levels, while sodium intake was seven times lower.** These diets were balanced high protein, high mineral, high phyto-nutrient, alkalizing diets. Overall, in fact, researchers report that 87% of likely prehistoric diets provided an excess of base, while contemporary Westernized diets provide an excess of acid. Our diet-induced low-grade metabolic acidity could well be the most significant, and least well-recognized, of all modern bone-depleting risk factors.

### Michael — 2012-06-21T09:31:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32634>

> **Joann;32622 wrote:** What to do??? keto diet would be only protein, right?
>
>
> No, a ketogenic diet would be adequate in protein and relatively high in fat.
>
>
>
> maybe a mediteranian diet would be a better choice fro osteo?

Likely a low carb paleo diet would be best for this. Dr. Rosedale, whose regimen for diabetes patients is not unlike low carb paleo, reckons that the protein matrix in bone is the key factor. If you teach yourself to be a sugar burner, then you down regulate your fat burning enzymes. He says this means that when you sleep, and there's no sugar around because you're in the fasted state, your body robs out protein from muscle and bone to convert it to glucose to burn.

That would be consistent with Jack's comment that it's processed carbs that really correlate with osteoporosis.

Not doing any exercise, it may not have to be much, seems also to be a big no-no, since you're sending biological signals to your body that you don't need strong muscles and bones.

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/)

### Destiny — 2012-06-21T13:22:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32644>

> **Michael;32758 wrote:** No, a ketogenic diet would be adequate in protein and relatively high in fat.
>

>
> Likely a low carb paleo diet would be best for this. Dr. Rosedale, whose regimen for diabetes patients is not unlike low carb paleo, reckons that the protein matrix in bone is the key factor. If you teach yourself to be a sugar burner, then you down regulate your fat burning enzymes. He says this means that when you sleep, and there's no sugar around because you're in the fasted state, your body robs out protein from muscle and bone to convert it to glucose to burn.
>

> That would be consistent with Jack's comment that it's processed carbs that really correlate with osteoporosis.
>

> Not doing any exercise, it may not have to be much, seems also to be a big no-no, since you're sending biological signals to your body that you don't need strong muscles and bones.
>
>
>
> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2011/06/25/ron-rosedale-healthy-bones/)

I got to the link and found this article at:

[http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/)

It was too long, so here is a couple of tidbits:

ON KRAUSS

The punchline is that we expected that because these diets have low carbohydrate, when we fed the high saturated fat level along with the red meat, we would see a pretty benign metabolic risk profile. Just as we did with low saturated fat and red meat.

But that’s not what happened!

RON KRAUSS

This is one of the surprises that keep life interesting for us for us as researchers and also for the world out there who happens to be looking over our shoulder. In this case, the surprise was that the combination of the high beef diet and the high saturated fat diet caused very serious increases in all of the cholesterol related risk factors we had been measuring, including total particle numbers, small LDL, total LDL cholesterol, inflammation, whatever we looked at, we saw an adverse effect.

Why would the iron in red meat, when coupled with saturated fat, increase risk factors for inflammation, for small particle LDL, for higher blood sugars, and all the rest?

RON KRAUSS

This is just an idea. This is in our whole discussion the one area where we do’t have data . . . yet. But it’s an intriguing hypothesis. Because it’s known from genetic, metabolic and population studies that the iron content of the liver, which stores most of our iron for our needs for red blood cell production and many other metabolic processes . . . if that amount of iron within the liver is excessive, it can lead to impaired sugar metabolism and predispose even to diabetes in the extreme case. High levels of iron in the liver also can be associated with abnormal lipid profiles, with higher amounts of small particle LDL. So we already have evidence that high iron levels in the liver are a potential determinant of things that can influence risk for diabetes and heart disease. And since iron comes from what we eat, it could be related to the dietary response that we studied. Why beef should have a particularly high effect on hepatic iron is a possibility we’re interested in, along with the question of why beef would create this risk for us, in particular, when we consume saturated fat. So while we don’t have data yet, we do have an intriguing collection of clues that maybe would lead to definitive conclusions from Sherlock Holmes.

### Michael — 2012-06-21T16:06:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32682>

> **Destiny;32769 wrote:** I ... found this article ... :
>
> [http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/](http://http://www.meandmydiabetes.com/2012/04/17/ron-krauss-saturated-fat-red-meat-it-depends/)
>
>
>
>
> I hadn't seen that. It's an interesting read. It seems very much at the hypothesis stage.
>
>
>
> The thought seems to be that:
>
>
>
>
> > saturated fat could potentially increase the amount of iron coming in from beef
>
>
>
> But high levels of iron can be problematic:
>
>
>
> ... if that amount of iron within the liver is excessive, it can lead to impaired sugar metabolism and predispose even to diabetes in the extreme case.

One of the potential adverse effects of high hepatic iron is "impair\[ment of\] sugar metabolism". Ron Krauss's "low carbohydrate diet" is, he says, 26% carbohydrate. So there's still a lot of glucose to be metabolized on that.

As a paranthetic comment, Drs. Phinney and Volek suggest that a diet can't really be characterized as "low carbohydrate" unless it's less than 15% carbohydrate; also, I can't help wondering whether hunter-gatherers in many parts of the world _could get up to that level of carbohydrate for much of the year.

Maybe high saturated fat may not be a problem in itself but could be in the context of high carbohydrate_. I think Jeff Volek has indicated that he thinks that may be true. Here would be a mechanism, the mediating factor being iron, if the hypothesis is correct.

In the meantime, maybe watch those iron levels, and ring the changes in protein choices with some seafood and poultry?

### Michael — 2012-06-21T19:46:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/excess-protein-and-bone-loss.1810/post-32758>

As an addendum to this one: If excess iron does turn out to be a problem in this, or other, contexts, then one has to ask whether it was sensible of the U.S. Government to mandate "enrichment" of flour with iron, and then on top of that to tell the population to consume more flour-based products.

In any event, it does rather look as though people who should probably know better have been deciding that some food components are "good" or "bad". The "good" ones are then over-promoted - calcium, anyone? Alternatively, they're added to nutritionally-denuded, and in any case fairly worthless, foods that probably shouldn't be consumed anyway in whole-society non-voluntary dosing experiments - folic acid, anyone? It comes across as a very crude way of thinking.

It's also a strange irony that it's the U.S. that does this. Americans tend to pride themselves on what they believe to be their individualism. Yet the U.S. Government makes decisions for its citizens in ways that would probably not be tolerated in many European countries.

# Thread: Does Leaky Gut Include Kidney Issues?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T04:11:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30367>

My other half is a type 2 diabetic, and his urine albumen levels are still rising. The lab's reference range for normal is below either 23 or 30, and his is **145 this time around. We got his A1C down to 7 fro something above 9, sometimes lower, and I'm not seeing an improvement in THIS number, it seems to be getting worse. Also, the reference level for creatinine is .6 - 1.4 mg/dL, and his level is 147**

What can we do about it? The doctors don't seem to care, but I do. I want him to have working kidneys thank you very much. Just because he's over 60, doesn't mean life's over for crying out loud!

Does this qualify as leaky gut?

### chocolate — 2012-06-10T04:18:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30368>

[http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/42689095/ns/health/t/low-carb-high-fat-diet-could-replace-dialysis/](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/42689095/ns/health/t/low-carb-high-fat-diet-could-replace-dialysis/)

there was a really great study done on ketogenic diet and kidneys.... If I find it I'll post... I need to see it again myself.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-10T04:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30372>

Hi MaggieC. Sounds like your hubby is cutting way back on sugars, based on his A1C progress. Is he also cutting back on wheat and corn? Don't worry about the fact that he is over 60, he can beat this. The doctors just want to manage his illness(es).

When I cut out eating sugars, I would still occasionally get cloudy urine. When I cut out wheat, that stopped entirely. I think I still might have a leaky gut, though perhaps it is a bit less leaky. Hope this helps.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T06:10:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30387>

Thank you for the information! I'm already doing a ketogenic diet, and he sees how little I'm suffering. He only eats carbs/starches one meal per week now, which always includes dessert.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T10:19:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30390>

Here's an interesting study on curcurmin and effect on renal failure in rats. [http://ajprenal.physiology.org/content/296/5/F1146.full](http://http://ajprenal.physiology.org/content/296/5/F1146.full)

### MaggieC — 2012-06-10T23:07:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30442>

Having done more research, I am furious. According to dh's urine albumin and creatinine levels, he is in stage 3 kidney disease, and just hasn't started exhibiting symptoms. What I'm mad about is that I go to his doctor's appointments, and NONE of his doctors has mentioned how important this is. Because he has heart issues, and is diabetic, that's all that's important. Yes, they said his kidneys aren't filtering stuff as well as they should, and that's it. I was reading literature last night, that claimed it wasn't reversible. Just do your best to keep it from getting worse. AT LEAST 3 doctors aren't raising an alarm that THIS IS IMPORTANT. I wonder how they would feel about having to be on dialysis for the rest of THEIR lives. The brochures say "moderate" carbs to protect kidneys. Sigh. I'm sorry, I'm preaching to the choir here. I'm just furious. Yeah, I have health problems too, and I'm working on them. DH is 13 years older than I am and I want him around as long as possible, and the medical profession (present company excluded, thank heavens) isn't helping a bit.

Dear Jack,

I would love for you to do a blog on kidneys. I'm guessing I'm not the only person dealing with this issue! Also, does it count as leaky gut, or is it a separate issue?

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-10T23:36:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30444>

my kidney function has been down the last few years and nobody takes issue with it.... b/c it's not bad enough to do medicate it....yet. Effed up world. Makes me just as furious.

### chocolate — 2012-06-10T23:51:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30445>

souldanzer, that 's where the vitamin d receptors are. Also an issue with my cupcake.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-11T02:31:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30461>

receptors as in uptake/making vit D or receptors as in unable using the vit D that's already there?

I guess what I'm asking is would that make my D level drop or my body just can't use it?

Always touches my heart talking to a parent

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T22:10:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30738>

Oh, MaggieC, sorry to hear about that. It would make me furious also. That must be why he is slow to respond to his lowered carbs. One carby meal per week sounds very good, considering. I wonder if cold (CT) might help his kidneys. I'd definitely throw out all the ibuprophen in the house.

### MaggieC — 2012-06-13T09:59:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30863>

I can't throw out the ibuprophen, I can choose not to use it though. My best and only option is to lead by example, and we've made it to here so far. I need to show major changes in my health before my family will come along too.

### nonchalant — 2012-06-13T16:53:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/does-leaky-gut-include-kidney-issues.1811/post-30893>

In his situation, cutting out ALL carbs would be best, of course. But until then, perhaps he could try substituting krill oil for the ibuprophen. It takes effect in a little less than an hour, like ibuprophen, and seems to last about 4 hours. Even my parents agree that it works remarkably well, for muscle/joint pain as well as headaches. I've also recently found that bromelain also works quite well, at least for muscles, but it has to be taken on an empty stomach.

He's lucky to have you on his side.

On a 2-4day of cycle do a blood draw in LabCorp, do this tests (add LH & FSH):

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry Test Number: 140707

2 ------- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) − Comprehensive Screen, Test Nr: 500175

3 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

10 ------- Metabolic Panel (14), Comprehensive

11 ------- Complete Blood Count (CBC) With Differential and Platelet

12 ------- NMR LipoProfileÂ®

13 ------- Iron, Total

14 ------- Ferritin

15 ------- Phosphorus, Serum

16 ------- Uric Acid, Serum

17 ------- Gamma-Glutamyl Transpeptidase (GGT)\*

18 ------- CO2 (Carbon Dioxide)

19 ------- Lactate Dehydrogenase (LDH)

20 ------- RDW

21 ------- Homocysteine, cardio

22 ------- Insulin and C-Peptide, Serum

23 ------- HgA1c

24 ------- C-Reactive Protein (CRP), High Sensitivity (Cardiac Risk Assessment)

25 ------- Thyroid-stimulating Hormone (TSH), T4, FT4, T3, FT3, RT3

26 ------- Lab pls calculate ratios, (Free T3 Ã· RT3) and (T3 Ã· RT3)

27 ------- Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Antibodies

28 ------- Thyroid Antithyroglobulin Antibody

29 ------- prolactin

30 ------- IGF-1

31 ------- IGFBP3

//////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

On day 19-21 of cycle do first 9 lines of above list (add LH & FSH).

////////

Note:

Correlate, correct, adjust, report from Essential & Metabolic Fatty Acids Analysis (EMFA)

using

Why 4:1 Ratio Oil?

do

performed

at Kennedy Krieger Institute - Johns Hopkins University

# Thread: Hormones insanity from CT, but I think my NMR looks great - any hacks?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormones-insanity-from-ct-but-i-think-my-nmr-looks-great-any-hacks.1814/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### imdreaming — 2012-06-10T18:40:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormones-insanity-from-ct-but-i-think-my-nmr-looks-great-any-hacks.1814/post-30421>

Brief recent background: I've been low carbing for about 4 years but still struggling with my weight. I did one round of homeopathic HCG and one round of RX HCG. Both times I struggled with keeping the weight off- it always comes back right before my TOM, and it doesn't like to leave. Started the LR in July 2011, and started getting labs done in August 2011. My doc started me on Armour thyroid and adaptogens for high cortisol. Gained 5-8 pounds after starting the reset, but thrilled to be free from cravings! Weight stabilized, but in December, I started having anxiety and panic attacks and went into a downward spiral. Doc took me off Armour and switched me to GTA (over the counter T3 only). Anxiety was debilitating, heart rate increased from 60 bpm to over 80 bpm, started having sleep problems, losing weight rapidly (down 7 pounds in 4 weeks) and at one point, I did not sleep for 5 consecutive days. Long story short, I realized it was the GTA causing problems and stopped taking it. (oh, also- back on 50 mg Zoloft. I've been on and off it for about 15 years. I would like to get off of it permanently, but the anxiety was threatening my family and job) Took a month break from thyroid meds and felt awful, went back to Armour and worked my way back up to 90 mg. Rapidly regained the 7+ pounds. Started CT in mid-March. I am currently taking a cold shower, 10-15 mins daily, 30 min bath (about 58 degrees currently) 2-3 times per week, plus daily spot treatment, minimum 1 hour each. Every night I fall asleep with a cold pack between my shoulder blades, plus I sit on ice packs at work and do spot icing on my hips. Since starting CT, I am up an additional 7-10 pounds. Dr Kruse told me at one point, back in the monster thread on MDA that my problem was hormones. I'm sleeping good again, feeling good- I feel like my cortisol is pretty much under control, anxiety and panic attacks have ceased. My biggest complaint is the weight gain! I'm eating less than ever before, though I had gotten a little sloppy recently. I have just re-committed to strict LR, no dairy, no fruit and no alcohol.

I've posted my hormone tests: first set of numbers is the range, second set is results from 9/12/2011, third set is my recent test on 5/30/12. If there is only one result number, it is from the September test- no new test result.

Test: Range 9/12/2011 5/30/2012

Estradiol (55.8 - 214.2) 56.3 159.3

FSH ( 1.5 - 9.1) 2.9

Luteinizing Hormone (0.5 - 16.9) 1

Progesterone ( 4.44 - 28.03) 5.32 20.65

Prolactin ( 2.8 - 29.2) 3.5

Testosterone, total ( 14 - 76) 37 37

Dihydrotestosterone ( 5 - 30) 4

Estradiol jumped sky high- I suspected that before the test- I'm sure at least half of my extra weight is in my breasts  I'm taking DIM, Estro Factors and NOW Liver detox/regenerator. Another complication for me- I do not have my gallbladder, and I have read that makes detoxing excess estrogen more difficult. My doc wants me to keep taking the supplements and re-test in 8 weeks.

I'm very pleased with my NMR though:

Range 4/21/2012

LDL (=40) 107

Triglycerides (

**

Better (less $) to order thru your doctor.

For those who cannot:

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

\=========================

The test in question is:

**SpectraCell’s micronutrient testing

[http://www.spectracell.com/mnt/](http://http://www.spectracell.com/mnt/)

It tests for:

**VITAMINS

Vitamin A

Vitamin B1

Vitamin B2

Vitamin B3

Vitamin B6

Vitamin B12

Biotin

Folate

Pantothenate

Vitamin C

Vitamin D

Vitamin K

**MINERALS

Calcium

Magnesium

Manganese

Zinc

Copper

**Amino Acids

Asparagine

Glutamine

Serine

**Antioxidants**

Alpha Lipoic Acid

Coenzyme Q10

Cysteine

Glutathione

Selenium

Vitamin E

Spectrox™**

FOR Total

Antioxidant Function

Carbohydrate Metabolism**

Chromium

Fructose Sensitivity

Glucose-Insulin\* Metabolism

Fatty Acids**

Oleic Acid

Metabolites**

Choline

Inositol

Carnitine

///////////////////////////////////////

Correlating Micronutrient Testing with Common Pathologies**

http://www.spectracell.com/clinical-education-center/education-center-webinar-library/

///////////////////////////////////////////

SpectraCell’s Patented Technology

SpectraCell’s patented, chemically-defined control media contains the minimal amount of each essential micronutrient that is needed to support optimal lymphocyte growth or mitogenic response. The functional intracellular status of micronutrients involved in cell metabolism is evaluated by manipulation of the individual micronutrients in the media followed by mitogenic stimulation and measurement of DNA synthesis.

The same technology also provides a total antioxidant function test (SPECTROX™) which assesses the ability of cells to resist damage caused by free radicals and other forms of oxidative stress. Due to the considerable number of cellular antioxidants with extensive interactions, redundancies, repair and recharging capabilities, measuring total function is the most accurate and clinically useful way to assess your patients’ capacity to resist oxidative damage.

Since lymphocytes are produced in the bone marrow and stored in the peripheral locations for long periods of time (the average life span of a lymphocyte is approximately four to six months), SpectraCell’s measurements provide a powerful portrait of each patients’ long-term nutrient status. This is analogous to the use of a glycosylated hemoglobin test to evaluate blood glucose levels over a one to three month period.

///////////////////////////////////////////
