I was wondering if you would be willing to take a look at my labs. Any suggestions would be appreciated. If not, i totally understand. Thanks for you time and consideration.

A little background.40 year old female with hashimotos and history of adrenal fatigue. Last year in july i did a round of hhcg. I haven’t had tom since. I lost weight, struggled to maintain and had bad low blood sugar issues. Did a second round hhcg in january. Started leptin reset in march but have had some setbacks (ie cheating). I have regained 15 pounds. I am kind of desperate to get tom back (i have no kids). Working on leaky gut and adrenal rx. I feel better now (mood wise) but still no tom and the belly flab is unwelcome. I’m working with a func med doc and she has me on an elimination diet for leaky gut. I’m on estradiol for gyn and supplementing otc progesterone. Recently (after bloodwork) switched from 50 mcg synthroid to 90 mg armour.

dhea is 141 (range 102-1185).

left side=reason, following by what to do about:

The way I see it, dr Kruse's advice (on Krill oil) is generally good advice, good for general population,

but Ed/Patricia Kane advice is specific, and should be guided by (highly detailed) Fatty Acids Analysis.

While I am at it, with above mind set,

is valuable analysis,

Blood test shows that you are not taking enough of any of the three.

I suggest that you:

stop progesterone supplementation.

Get all theree E1, E2, E3 tested and get some kind of triest cream or vaginal, titrate dose using blood testing (not saliva).

Think of thyroid as in intermediate phase of care, use enough Armour Thyroid or (bought from internet, Amazon, Thyroid-S), enough to keep TSH(0.5-2.5)

\-------------------

At LabCorp do this tests,

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry Test Number: 140707

2 ------- Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia (CAH) − Comprehensive Screen, Test Nr: 500175

3 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate

4 ------- SHBG

5 ------- Dihydrotestosterone

6 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide Test Number: 500881

7 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549

8 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive 140244

9 ------- Estrone, Serum

You want to do 3-6 cycles of above tests.

When you get results, adjust dose, up or down, depending on results.

Right now you do not menstruate, so you want to be on new protocol for a month,

have script waiting, when month passes, do blood testing.

Will take sometime 2 weeks before you get results.

I will always help you in analyzing results and figuring new protocol.

On new protocol you want to be at least a month,

but

if you start menstruating, other than waiting one whole month you will have another condition to satisfy.

Blood draw have to be 3-5 day of cycle.

Practically, you will be doing those blood tests every 2+ months.

Eventually you will stabilize.

You need to make a spreadsheet that will track your

protocols

and

resulting blood testds

similar to this one:

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?15433-JanSz-Labs-amp-Tests&p=166492#post166492](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?15433-JanSz-Labs-amp-Tests&p=166492#post166492)

Highly likely your very low progesterone is result of your low pregnenolone.

Assume that it is true.

You want to supplement pregnenolone to

achieve good pregnenolone level

but

you may even go somewhat higher if need be,

if

you missing on progesterone

you want at the minimum

progesterone=1.5 ng/mL

You will also be monitoring

Cortisol AM/PM(7:20AM & 11:59AM)

aiming at Cortisol AM~20

if need be, you will attempt manipulating progesterone level (via pregnenolone pills) aiming at good Cortisol.

when you get back your menses, you want progesterone=1.5 ng/mL in first few days, then much much more.

Your hope is, that your body will take over and will produce more progesterone latter on in cycle.

At first, you will maintain your pregnenolone dose constant throughout the cycle.

Depending on blood tests results in day 19-21, yo may need to do additional tweaking.

lets set ranges on desirable goals:

on day 3-4 your E2=~(20-60)pg/mL

on day 19-21 your E2=~(100-160)pg/mL

on day 3-4 your progesterone=~(1.4-1.5)ng/mL

on day 19-21 your progesterone=~(15-20)ng/mL

\--------

Your testosterone is low, but we have not touched p/e and DHEAs, hold on testosterone

\--------------

DHEAs

you have not tested

DHEA is 141 (range 102-1185) low

I suggest that you buy dozen bottles of pregnenolone and 3 bottles of 7-ketoDHEA 100mg pills from lef.org

Pregnenolone Micronized Lipid Matrix MLM 150mg tabs, scored, from Nutricology.

Start with

Estradiol + Estrone/wakeup (figure something, increase current dose 3x)

pregnenolone 300mg/wakeup

7ketoDHEA 100mg/wakeup

Armour Thyroid (1/2 dose)/wakeup

pregnenolone 150mg/noon

7ketoDHEA 100mg/noon

Armour Thyroid (1/2 dose)/noon

/////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////////

> Working on leaky gut and adrenal RX.

leaky gut----->Dr Jack Kruse protocols and diet

but say good bye to ALL grains and maybe nightshades (forget about incremental eliminating)

adrenal RX-----> I am leery about, whatever you do, consider saving $$$ & time

also thyroid care

\----------------

instead do this tests and follow them up with corrections

repeat those tests 2x/year

Spectracell Comprehensive Nutritional Panel

[http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular\_vitamin\_analysis\_spectracell.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/intracellular_vitamin_analysis_spectracell.html)

Elemental Hair Analysis by Genova Diagnostics

[http://www.healthremedies.com/elemental\_hair\_analysis\_genova\_diagnostics.html](http://http://www.healthremedies.com/elemental_hair_analysis_genova_diagnostics.html)

Maintain spreadsheet to see your progress.

\-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

> Vit D is 44 (range 30 - 100)
>
>
> Use 10000iu/day
>
> \----------------------------------------------------------------------------
>
> selenium 153 (63-160)

1 dropperfull/day

Lugol's Iodine Family Pack -- 6 (2 fl. oz.) bottles

[http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW](http://http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW)

You have thyroid antibodies.

There are schools of thought that say to not use iodine in presence of antibodies.

The other school says that antibodies are the result of low iodine.

Additionally excess of iodine displaces undesirable hallides.

Use of iodine requires plenty of selenium, you seem to have plenty.

I see no reason why you should not make a good try of iodine.

If you react negatively, just stop.

Highly unlikely (statistically speaking).

[http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?19800-Low-Dose-Naltrexone-(LDN)](http://http://www.musclechatroom.com/forum/showthread.php?19800-Low-Dose-Naltrexone-\(LDN\))

Buy it, try it, it is cheap.

### JanSz — 2012-10-29T00:18:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/jennifer-jansz.1815/post-60646>

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Omega6/Omega3=2(1.3-12)

AA/EPA=0.2(0.2-7)

AA=0.4(0.3-2.3) you want to be close to top range

This may be due to very high EPA(first) and very high DHA(second)

Waiting out, eat

1gelcap/day NowFoods Primrose Oil (that is GLA which hopefully will convert to more AA)

also

B3, B6, C, biotin,Zn, Mg

Better yet do Spectracell Analysis, prevent (futile) guessing.

On end of 3-4 months do Fatty Acid Analysis,

but get it from Genova Diagnostics (or better, more detiled)

EPA=2.4H(46)

LDL=120(>
Abstract:

The active secosteroid hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin-D (1,25D) often reaches excessive levels in normocalcemic patients suffering from chronic Th1 inflammatory illnesses, including sarcoidosis and rheumatoid arthritis. This is due to unregulated production of 1,25D in the mitochondria of activated macrophages. Phagocytic cells parasitized by cell wall deficient (CWD) L-forms of bacteria drive this dysfunction of vitamin D metabolism. The paracrine levels of 1,25D rise and the level of substrate 25-D falls. If studies measure only the 25D precursor, a low 25D may be misinterpreted as indicating the patient requires vitamin D supplementation. Our data show that active 1,25D hormone may be elevated, even with a low level of 25D substrate because of the inflamed macrophagesâ€™ hyperactive conversion to the active hormone. In sarcoidosis, for example, this dysregulated vitamin D conversion can mean that even a moderate intake of vitamin D through ingestion or solar exposure can cause the 1,25D hormone to become high enough to stimulate osteoclastic action, and bone resorption. Data presented here suggest that this extra-renal synthesis of 1,25D is more widespread than previously thought and because it leads to vitamin D hypersensitivity, has important implications for research, diagnosis and treatment of chronic disease. The relationship of our data to past research on the role of vitamin D in several diseases is discussed. The assay of both the active 1,25D and inactive 25D metabolites will lead to additional clinical data, potentially improving both diagnosis and care of a variety of autoimmune and other Th1 illnesses. The high recovery rate using a new antibacterial protocol (initially developed for treating sarcoidosis) and the normalization of 1,25D levels, subsequent to such treatment, emphasizes the importance of measuring both the active 1,25D hormone and the 25D substrate. It should be noted that serum must be properly handled and transported frozen in order to obtain accurate 1,25D test results. Further, an increase in 25D levels induced by vitamin D supplementation may lead to long-term disease progression by facilitating proliferation of the intracellular CWD bacterial pathogens.

//////////////////////////

Abstract:

The active secosteroid hormone 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin-D (1,25D) often reaches excessive levels in normocalcemic patients suffering from chronic Th1 inflammatory illnesses, including sarcoidosis and rheumatoid arthritis. This is due to unregulated production of 1,25D in the mitochondria of activated macrophages. Phagocytic cells parasitized by cell wall deficient (CWD) L-forms of bacteria drive this dysfunction of vitamin D metabolism. The paracrine levels of 1,25D rise and the level of substrate 25-D falls. If studies measure only the 25D precursor, a low 25D may be misinterpreted as indicating the patient requires vitamin D supplementation. Our data show that active 1,25D hormone may be elevated, even with a low level of 25D substrate because of the inflamed macrophages’ hyperactive conversion to the active hormone. In sarcoidosis, for example, this dysregulated vitamin D conversion can mean that even a moderate intake of vitamin D through ingestion or solar exposure can cause the 1,25D hormone to become high enough to stimulate osteoclastic action, and bone resorption. Data presented here suggest that this extra-renal synthesis of 1,25D is more widespread than previously thought and because it leads to vitamin D hypersensitivity, has important implications for research, diagnosis and treatment of chronic disease. The relationship of our data to past research on the role of vitamin D in several diseases is discussed. The assay of both the active 1,25D and inactive 25D metabolites will lead to additional clinical data, potentially improving both diagnosis and care of a variety of autoimmune and other Th1 illnesses. The high recovery rate using a new antibacterial protocol (initially developed for treating sarcoidosis) and the normalization of 1,25D levels, subsequent to such treatment, emphasizes the importance of measuring both the active 1,25D hormone and the 25D substrate. It should be noted that serum must be properly handled and transported frozen in order to obtain accurate 1,25D test results. Further, an increase in 25D levels induced by vitamin D supplementation may lead to long-term disease progression by facilitating proliferation of the intracellular CWD bacterial pathogens.

Data presented here suggest that this extra-renal synthesis of 1,25D is more widespread than previously thought and because it leads to vitamin D hypersensitivity, has important implications for research, diagnosis and treatment of chronic disease. The relationship of our data to past research on the role of vitamin D in several diseases is discussed. The assay of both the active 1,25D and inactive 25D metabolites will lead to additional clinical data, potentially improving both diagnosis and care of a variety of autoimmune and other Th1 illnesses. The high recovery rate using a new antibacterial protocol (initially developed for treating sarcoidosis) and the normalization of 1,25D levels, subsequent to such treatment, emphasizes the importance of measuring both the active 1,25D hormone and the 25D substrate. It should be noted that serum must be properly handled and transported frozen in order to obtain accurate 1,25D test results. Further, an increase in 25D levels induced by vitamin D supplementation may lead to long-term disease progression by facilitating proliferation of the intracellular CWD bacterial pathogens.

//////////////////////////

Other things to consider - this is your DNA not your epigenetics. To get a full understanding of your body you will need a methlyation panel to assess your current biochemistry. # Thread: Is my panic a hyper or hypo response to cortisol?   thoughts?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 22
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColdBren — 2012-06-11T04:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30485>

**A striking pattern of cortisol non-responsiveness to psychosocial stress in patients with panic disorder with concurrent normal cortisol awakening responses.**

Petrowski K, Herold U, Joraschky P, Wittchen HU, Kirschbaum C.

Source

Institute of Psychotherapy and Psychosomatic Medicine, School of Medicine, Technische UniversitÃ¤t Dresden, Fetscherstr. 74, 01307 Dresden, Germany. [katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de](mailto:katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de)

Abstract

BACKGROUND:

Subtle and inconsistent differences in hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity have been reported for patients with panic disorder. While these patients show little or no alterations in basal ACTH and cortisol levels, it has been hypothesized that HPA hyperresponsivity was a trait in panic patients when exposed to novel and uncontrollable stimulation.

METHODS:

Thirty-four patients (23 females, mean age 35 yrs) diagnosed with panic disorder were compared to 34 healthy controls matched for age, gender, smoking status, and use of oral contraceptives. Both groups were exposed twice to a potent laboratory stress protocol, the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) on consecutive days. Free salivary cortisol levels and heart rate responses were repeatedly measured before and following the TSST. In addition, the cortisol awakening response (CAR) was assessed to further investigate HPA reactivity in PD patients.

RESULTS:

While the TSST induced similar heart rate stress responses in both groups, cortisol responses were clearly absent in the panic patients with normal responses in the controls (F(1.96, 66)=20.16; p

### nonchalant — 2012-06-12T17:59:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30707>

Hi Bren, I hope you don't mind what might be a completely off-topic comment, but I used to wake up at night in a panic. My heart would be pounding, my mind would be confused, and I'd be in a cold sweat. As soon as I woke up, however, I'd know what the problem was. I would roll onto my side, and in a few seconds, all would be well. I was pregnant, you see, and the baby was pressing on my portal vein, cutting off circulation a bit. I think the doctor said it was the portal vein. I tried never to lay on my back, or even use the recliner, but once asleep who knew where I would end up.

I'm ever so happy that my cortisol awakening response did its job, on numerous occasions. This was from 4 months up to childbirth, with both of my pregancies. It was a bit unnerving.

I hope you find the solution to your panic attacks soon.

\-Sally

### chocolate — 2012-06-13T00:08:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30750>

[http://www.longecity.org/forum/blog/61/entry-362-what-supplements-influence-cortisol-levels/](http://http://www.longecity.org/forum/blog/61/entry-362-what-supplements-influence-cortisol-levels/)

> Cortisol and 5-HTP
>
>
>
> L-5-hydroxytryptophan induced increase in salivary cortisol in panic disorder patients and healthy volunteers.
>
> Psychopharmacology (Berl). 2002 Jun;161(4):365-9.
>
> Department of Psychiatry, Institute of Brain and Behaviour, Maastricht University, AB Maastricht The Netherlands
>
> Hypersensitivity of brain serotonin receptors has been proposed as a causal mechanism in the pathophysiology of panic disorder. This theory can be tested, using serotonergic stimulation of the HPA axis. Up to now, plasma cortisol has generally been used as the outcome measure in such studies. Assessment of salivary cortisol is a non-invasive alternative to measure HPA axis activity. Salivary cortisol levels were measured in 24 panic disorder patients and 24 healthy volunteers, following ingestion of 200 mg L-5-hydroxytryptophan or placebo. A significant rise in cortisol was observed in both patients and controls following ingestion of L-5-hydroxytryptophan. No such effects were seen in the placebo condition. **The results show that L-5-hydroxytryptophan stimulated salivary cortisol is a useful probe of serotonin function in healthy volunteers as well as panic disorder patients, and provide some evidence against a serotonin receptor hypersensitivity in panic disorder.**

I was trying to remember which vitamin helped with cortisol. It is b-5 and pregnenolone. Anyway, I ran into this and thought it might help you. The whole page is pretty simple and good, I think. Not rat studies. It seems Aspbergers have a chronic cortisol problem and need to not things up. It is good information to me.

### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T00:48:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-30993>

> **ColdBren;30589 wrote: A striking pattern of cortisol non-responsiveness to psychosocial stress in patients with panic disorder with concurrent normal cortisol awakening responses.**
>
> Petrowski K, Herold U, Joraschky P, Wittchen HU, Kirschbaum C.
>
> Source
>
> Institute of Psychotherapy and Psychosomatic Medicine, School of Medicine, Technische UniversitÃ¤t Dresden, Fetscherstr. 74, 01307 Dresden, Germany. [katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de](mailto:katja.petrowski@mailbox.tu-dresden.de)
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
> Subtle and inconsistent differences in hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity have been reported for patients with panic disorder. While these patients show little or no alterations in basal ACTH and cortisol levels, it has been hypothesized that HPA hyperresponsivity was a trait in panic patients when exposed to novel and uncontrollable stimulation.
>
> METHODS:
>
> Thirty-four patients (23 females, mean age 35 yrs) diagnosed with panic disorder were compared to 34 healthy controls matched for age, gender, smoking status, and use of oral contraceptives. Both groups were exposed twice to a potent laboratory stress protocol, the Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) on consecutive days. Free salivary cortisol levels and heart rate responses were repeatedly measured before and following the TSST. In addition, the cortisol awakening response (CAR) was assessed to further investigate HPA reactivity in PD patients.
>
> RESULTS:
>
> While the TSST induced similar heart rate stress responses in both groups, cortisol responses were clearly absent in the panic patients with normal responses in the controls (F(1.96, 66)=20.16; pthe present results do not support the hypothesis that PD patients show a trait HPA hyperresponsiveness to novel and uncontrollable stimulation. In contrast, the data provide support for a hyporesponsive HPA axis under emotional stress in PD patients.

This study doesn't suggest that you have a problematic reaction \*to\* cortisol. It says that your problem might begin earlier in the processing in your brain b/c people with panic disorder don't show the expected cortisol release when under emotional stress. They call this a hyporesponsive HPA axis when under emotional stress.

Cortisol isn't the problematic part according to this study. The problem might be that it's missing. It also says that it is specific to emotional stress b/c the subjects had a normal cortisol reaction in the morning (when cortisol rises naturally), so it's not like their cortisol response is broken in general, just in this specific case (emotional stress).

### Sistagrok — 2012-06-28T19:54:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34195>

> **ColdBren;33823 wrote:** Yes, to both

Ok, I ask those questions as that is exactly what I've been dealing with for the past few years also. I have however lessened the episodes considerably and went from 3-5 times per week down to 2-3 times per month, which has been a lifesaver for me. I had started to think I was going to lose my mind from lack of sleep, as the episodes just kept repeating themselves for hours into the night. And I woke up in the morning quite a wreck.

The reason your sleep apnea test came back fine is because the sleep techs are trained to wait until after you go past the sleep onset stage of sleep to start counting apneic events due to "normal" ventilitory control fluctuations at sleep onset.

I know mine to be central apnea events though, as when I woke(from the adrenalin rush) on very bad nights, I had visual disturbances(black spots) and dementia type issues , along with a drugged, over all very ill feeling, for the few minutes that I had gotten woken due to lack of oxygen. Sometimes though, I'd just get the adrenalin rush, along with myoclonus or both of them together.

After a minute of waking, my CO levels would even out all the symptoms went away, and right back to sleep I'd go, until a few minutes later where it all repeats itself. All of this happening in a very groggy state too.

That adrenalin rush is your body telling you to wake up and breath.

I discovered melatonin. It does not take away the episodes at all. But...it makes you cross the thresh hold of sleep onset to sleep, much faster. The episodes don't repeat as long and as many times. Melatonin literally saved me I think. For me, the repeating went from maybe 4-5 hours of misery to less than an hour of it. Now my sleep is unbelievably good! If your interested I can go into this more with you, as for me, I have a reason why I get these episodes, and have therefore been able to control them, making them occur hardly at all anymore. Feel free to message me and ask anything you may be interested in, as I don't want to go on and on here in this forum about it. I've done more research on this than just about anything though, as for me I felt it to be a matter of life and death to fix it.

### ColdBren — 2012-06-29T20:46:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34428>

Sista grok, this is exactly what is happening. I do use melatonin now and it does help in exactly the way you explain....but if I take more than just .5 gram, I have nightmares and visual disturbances. I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

If it is a form of apnea or compromised airways, there are sleep dentists that offer functional orthodontic appliances (sort of like retainers) such as Homeoblock and the DNA Appliance that can change the structure of the airways over time through causing expansion/growth in the maxilla and mandible, thus actually curing it

### ColdBren — 2012-06-29T22:52:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-34451>

That is something I will look into, a million thanks!!

### Sistagrok — 2012-07-02T23:32:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-my-panic-a-hyper-or-hypo-response-to-cortisol-thoughts.1819/post-35123>

> **ColdBren;34587 wrote:** Sista grok, this is exactly what is happening. I do use melatonin now and it does help in exactly the way you explain....but if I take more than just .5 gram, I have nightmares and visual disturbances. I think the false suffocation alarm theory explains this problem best, but healing it is where I really hope the genious of the people here can help. Sorry you have it too, but glad to have someone who gets it....would love to hear any of your thoughts on it all.

Your actually the first person that describes anything like what I have , that's why I knew to let you know about mine. I only take approx 1 gram of the melatonin myself. It is very dose dependent for everyone. If I do more of it, it doesn't work as well. Just a sprinkle of the powder from the capsule will make me fall right to sleep, and right past the episodes if I have one. I have dreams from it too, but they don't seem to sway towards nightmares , they are mixed really.

I will message you to let you know what I have discovered about my own issue with this, and how I helped mine almost disappear. Unfortunately it was all on my own, with no doctors help as they just thought I was having nocturnal panic attacks!

Oh, and if you have central apnea, the devices probably won't work for us, as we don't have obstructive apnea, our brains are just not telling us to automatically breath right at that moment, like it should.

# Thread: Grottenolm's Lab sketchbook
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 33

### Grottenolm — 2012-06-11T05:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-30491>

Hi All! Going to start posting my latest lab work here in this thread. I am not specifically asking for any help just yet until I get more comprehensive lab work done. I am off on holiday soon and I will take care of subsequent testing when I get back in about a month.

I recently went to the doctor for a routine cervical smear, and the doctor said "lets throw in a blood test while we're at it!" and I was like "Meh, why not?" As I was put on the spot I didn't ask her for all the specific things that I should be testing for, since becoming enlightened by Dr K, these forums and you fine people.

Also I just popped my multivitamin just before these tests, thats why the b12 is ridiculous

So as I post more and more labs, we will begin to sketch out the whole picture of my health.

**What my main concerns are at this point

\- Low energy and sometimes a blah, unmotivated mood despite life being perfect

\- Cycle is erratic since coming off the pill in February 2010. Sometimes its 27 days, other times its 40 days, sometimes there are a few months between periods!

\- Still have a soft puffy fatty midsection, although spot CT helps a bit. It seems to be comprised of a lot of water retention as some days I can even discern a faint line down my midsection, while other days it is one puffy uni blob, lol!

\- Extremely hard time losing body fat despite strict dairy free paleo and moderate activity (bodyweight stuff + walking)

About me

**

~ Female, will be 30 years old in December. 5'4 and currently 139 pounds. Never been pregnant.

~ Went on the contraceptive pill, Diane 35-ed for acne at age 16. Developed IBS at this time.

~ Went on a crash diet at age 18 - Weight Watchers, 18 points a day. Was stupid low calories and I was dumb and young, lol!

~ Managed to keep my weight down at 128 pounds until I turned 25. Then ballooned up to 165 when I moved in with my fiancee. This was partially due to overeating with him. Energy went in the crapper when I gained this weight and I simply did not have the stamina to do ANYTHING - it was found that I was anemic. Also, I started eating a lot of things that I never did until I moved out - namely pasta, bread and oats. And more dairy products. More IBS symptoms.

~ Went off the Diane 35 pill in Feb 2010, at age 27. I felt that it was screwing with my body and IBS.

~ Went paleo-primal in Feb 2010. Weight gradually came down to 125 pounds over the period of 1.5 years (I lose weight at a glacial pace) Energy was up! It was great! But terrible IBS persisted and subsequently took over my life. Also issues with appetite control. Body composition was also crap, quite flabby, big potbelly still there (can you tell that the potbelly is the bane of my existence? It follows me everywhere, lol! Or should I say it heralds my arrival everywhere I go) Only doing walking and body weight exercises at this point.

~ September 2011 - decided to do Leptin RX, until about mid November. Cured all IBS COMPLETELY, woohoo! Brought my weight down to 118 pounds.

~ January 2012 until Present - have moved onto post RX way of eating. But I have ballooned back up to 139 pounds. But my body composition seems better, ie more muscular. I can still fit into the majority of the clothes I wore when I weighed 125 pounds. I don't really care about scale weight numbers to be honest, just bodyfat percentage.

~ Added spot CT on February 26 2012 - ice packs on the belly. The persistent distended abdomen that I have had my whole life is slowly going down as a result of CT. But I have a lot of mushiness in the midsection that I cannot get rid of for the life of me. Would like to get rid of it at some point in my life, because I am a vain mofo who wants to rock a bikini!

~ Sleep is WONDERFUL - In bed by 10.30 and up by about 8am. Lots of gnarly dreaming and I only wake up in the middle of the night if my stupid neighbour blasts his music downstairs.

~ Currently I eat VLC Keto paleo, about 30 carbs a day, because it's winter here. Lots of lamb, pet cow who was pasture raised, salmon, sardines, mackerel, greens, other non starchy veg, coconut oil, eggs, bone broth and gross organs. I drink wine about every 2 weeks - a glass or 2 at a time. No dairy, as it really makes me retain fluid, especially around my midsection (which I have a lot of trouble with - fluid retention. My rings and shoes are either tight or loose depending on weather Venus is in the 7th house of aries or somesuch variable) Drinks are black coffee, green tea, other herbal teas and coco powder in hot water. I can easily go for months and months eating strict paleo with absolutely no sugar or nasty additives/chemicals. I am fortunate that all my family and friends cook good food with real ingredients. I eat this way because it makes me feel great. I don't have any problem with cravings or "falling off the wagon." My doctor is always surprised that I have the mushy body composition that I do, because she remarks "wow! You eat so strict?" and wonders how come...

I am on a myriad of supplements at the moment - Krill oil, Vitamin C, Vitamin d3, K2, Magnesium Malate, B vitamin complex (will drop this one) and Now foods Liver detoxifier and regenerator. I want to wean off supplements over the next few months and perhaps only keep the D3 and Magnesium

Well done if you read my tale up until this point! You get 3 lamb chops with marrow bone, mmmm....Onto my latest test...

**05 Jun 2012, Iron Studies

Serum Iron: 18 umol/L ( 10 - 30 )

Transferrin: 2.1 g/L ( 1.7 - 3.4 )

Transferrin Saturation: 0.34 ( 0.15 - 0.50 )

Ferritin: 179 ug/L ( 20 - 170 ) H

Comment: Normal transferrin saturation usually excludes causes of a high ferritin such as infection, inflammation and hereditary haemochromatosis. Other causes to consider include excess alcohol, chronic iron ingestion, or rarely, hyperthyroidism.

**05 Jun 2012, Thyroid Function Tests

Free T4: 17.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )

TSH: 2.5 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 )

Free T3: 2.7 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 ) L

Comment: Likely to be euthyroid - as judged by TSH.

05 Jun 2012, Complete Blood Count

**

Haemoglobin: 148 g/L ( 115 - 155 )

RBC: 5.07 x10e12/L ( 3.60 - 5.60 )

HCT: 0.46 L/L ( 0.35 - 0.46 )

MCV: 90 fL ( 80 - 99 )

MCH: 29.2 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )

Platelets: 259 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )

WBC: 5.6 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )

Neutrophils: 2.62 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )

Lymphocytes: 2.19 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )

Monocytes: 0.63 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )

Eosinophils: 0.15 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )

Basophils: 0.02 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

05 Jun 2012, Serum B12 And Folate

**

B12: 1178 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 ) HH

Folate: 42.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )

Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170

Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

Comment: If not on vitamin B12 replacement the high vitamin B12 may be due to use of multivitamins. Otherwise in a healthy person this is not significant. Repeat B12 assay is not necessary.

**05 Jun 2012, Diabetic Profile

**

HbA1c: 36 mmol/mol ( 20 - 40 )

Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result virtually excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.

If diabetic and treated with insulin/sulphonylureas, the risk for hypoglycaemia is increased. Suggest repeat HbA1c in 6 - 12 months.

Will post more when I come back from holiday - Looking forward to visiting the States and Mexico, yay!

### nonchalant — 2012-11-16T04:38:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64639>

Hi Grottenolm, you are female but the only hormone tested was testosterone?

Your high LDL might be because you aren't converting its cholesterol to steriod hormones (progesterone, pregnenolone, DHEA, estrogens, etc.) This would cause lethargy. Your first Free T3 was low, and T3 is needed also to create the steriod hormones.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-16T05:13:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64644>

+1 to what Nonchalant said. I'd also worry about both estrogen levels and E metabolism given the hx of oral contraceptives. You can get saliva hormone testing done through PathLab in Auckland ([www.pharmaceutical.co.nz](http://http://www.pharmaceutical.co.nz)). I'd be wanting to know P, E1, E2, E3, DHEA, and an ASI with melatonin. Hormone imbalances can cause some of the symptoms you're experiencing. Are you eating seaweeds? I'd be thinking about iodine deficiencies, too. Good luck.

### Grottenolm — 2012-11-17T04:02:56+0200

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-17T04:39:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-64891>

Re: the pharmaceutical.co.nz folks... you'll have to pay on your own but you need a MD script/referral. Given that it's just saliva testing and you self-pay, it shouldn't be too hard (esp in Auckland) to find a doc willing to write the script.

Don't forget about pre-loading with Se before taking iodine, esp in our country where soil is Se deficient!

Re: ESR, this is just from google, but maybe it's helpful?

The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is an easy, inexpensive, nonspecific test that has been used for many years to help detect conditions associated with [acute](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/acute) and [chronic](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/chronic) [inflammation](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/inflammation), including infections, cancers, and [autoimmune diseases](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/conditions/autoimmune). ESR is said to be nonspecific because increased results do not tell the doctor exactly where the inflammation is in the body or what is causing it, and also because it can be affected by other conditions besides inflammation. For this reason, the ESR is typically used in conjunction with [other tests](http://http://labtestsonline.org/understanding/analytes/esr/tab/faq).

ESR is helpful in diagnosing two specific inflammatory diseases, [temporal arteritis](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/tempart) and [polymyalgia rheumatica](http://http://labtestsonline.org/glossary/polymyalgia). A high ESR is one of the main test results used to support the diagnosis. It is also used to monitor disease activity and response to therapy in both of these diseases.

240-450 Equivocal
\>=450 Recent Streptococcus pyogenes infection likely
aDNase B: <200 Recent Streptococcus pyogenes infection unlikely
200-680 Equivocal
\>680 Recent Streptococcus pyogenes infection likely

Interpretation of streptococcal antibody titres are age dependant. Lower
levels are seen in the very young and in patients >15yrs. Please refer to
the national health foundation guidelines:
[http://www.heartfoundation.org.nz/uploads/Guide for diagnosis](http://www.heartfoundation.org.nz/uploads/Guide%20for%20diagnosis%20)
of%20ARF(4).pdf
A two fold increase in tests performed 10-14 days apart is indicative of
recent infection. Please call clinical microbiologist with any queries.

**14 Feb 2013, Blood Group Only
**
BLOOD GROUP ONLY: BLOOD BANK SPECIMEN: BLOOD

BLOOD GROUP

Group: O Rh(D) Positive

**14 Feb 2013, Thyroid Function Tests
**
Free T4: 15.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )
TSH: 1.1 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 )
Free T3: 3.3 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 )

Comment: Consistent with euthyroidism. Suggest repeat TSH in 2 years if clinically indicated. FT3 is not indicated when screening for thyroid disease or monitoring thyroxine replacement.

**14 Feb 2013, Reproductive Hormones
**
Progesterone: 44.8 nmol/L
Oestradiol: 745 pmol/L

Interpretation: Oestradiol
Follicular 50 - 600
Mid-cycle
Luteal 160 - 770
Post Menopausal < 200

Interpretation: PROGESTERONE
Adult Female <6 nmol/L - Ovulation unlikely
6 - 25 nmol/L - Ovulation possible, depending on
timing of sample
\>25 nmol/L - Ovulation confirmed
Post Menopausal Females  nmol/L

**14 Feb 2013, 25 Hydroxy Vitamin D
**
25 Hydroxy Vitamin D: 160 nmol/L ( 50 - 150 ) H

Reference range:
<25 Moderate to severe vitamin D deficiency
25 - 50 Mild deficiency
50 - 150 Optimal target range for bone health
\>250 Vitamin D intoxication has been reported at this level,
but is rare at levels <500nmol/L

**14 Feb 2013, Dhea Sulphate
**DHEAS: 2.1 umol/L ( 1.2 - 10.3 )

### Grottenolm — 2013-02-28T02:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-78193>

Thanks for stopping by Dr K! Yes, I totally failed the DHEA test, I get an 'F', lol - My Holistic GP is starting me on DHEA supplementation next week, and we want to get my DHEA levels up into the top quartile of the range. So I will get some regular DHEA, not 7-keto, and go from there. I am ecstatic about this as I suspected low DHEA for a long time....We will get the levels up and retest in 1.5 months time. Then maybe if needed, sort out the thyroid. But hopefully getting DHEA up will have a downstream effect on the other hormones.

She also wants me to supplement with **Vitamin E (mixed tocopherols) and a B-complex**. Also, she recommended eating heaps of liver, beetroot and cruciferous veg.

We are also concerned at my liver's ability to clear out estrogens, so I am considering taking Metagenics Estrofactors.

Can anyone recommend a good b-complex brand? I have been researching on iHerb.com, but there are so many to choose from!

### Grottenolm — 2013-03-03T23:31:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-78815>

Alright! now that I have a great picture of my hormones, courtesy of my latest blood + Saliva test results (below) I can now formulate a more productive plan of action.

Anyone reading this, please please listen to Kruse and GET TESTED - I can't stress this enough! It makes everything so much easier to deal with and I know a lot of people IRL who flounder around with supplements, spinning their wheels not knowing exactly what is wrong with them, just because they haven't been tested properly.

**Saliva test results (cycle day 20)
**

**CORTISOL**

8am 16.50 (Range 6.00 - 42.00) nmol/L
Noon 3.10 (Range 2.00 - 11.00) nmol/L
4pm 2.80 (Range 2.00 - 15.00) nmol/L
8pm 2.20 (Range 1.00 - 5.00) nmol/L

**PROGESTERONE (P4)**

389 (Day 20 of cycle) pmol/L

**DHEAS**

15 (Range 2.5 - 25.0) nmol/L

**TESTOSTERONE**

63 (Range 25 - 190) pmol/L

**ESTROGENS**

Estradiol (E2) 3.0 pmol/L
Estrone (E1) 17 (Range 9.6 - 20.0) pg/mL
Estriol (E3) 12 (Range 0.0 - 29.0) pg/mL
E3/\[E2+E1\] 0.6 \*L (>1.00)
P4/E2 Ratio 129.7 \*h (Range 4.0 -108.0)

From my results, my little pea brain gathers the following -

~ Depressed cortisol throughout most of the day
~ Low testosterone
~ Estrogen/progesterone Dominant? I have a hard time with ratios and don't know what the P4/E2 ratio means......

**My Plan of action
**
~ Start taking some adaptogens - Maca, Vitamin C powder, Rhodiola, Holy Basil Extract, liqorice tea
~ Follow everything that is mentioned here - [http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/](http://www.jackkruse.com/what-might-casey-anthony-and-oj-have-in-common/)
~ Carry on with my current supplements - Magnesium, Vitamin D, Vitamin K2, Selenium, Kelp,
~ Carry on with amino acids which currently help with energy and sleep - Glutamine powder, Tyrosine, L-Theanine
~ Add a good B-complex + Vitamin E mixed tocopherols, glutathione
~ Dr will start me on DHEA, but I am not sure what dose to start with?

**Questions for anyone reading
**

~ What would be a good starting dose for DHEA? Is lower better at first, and then titrate up? I will be getting all my tests re done in 2 months time.
~ What do you make of the Estrogen/Progesterone situation? I still don't understand that fully.

Thanks for reading!

### diane — 2013-03-04T00:13:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-78830>

I just re-read the DHEA blog, and it recommends starting with 10mg and going up from there. [http://www.jackkruse.com/hormone-cpc-1-dhea/](http://www.jackkruse.com/hormone-cpc-1-dhea/)

I think the ratio is progesterone to Estradiol (E2), which is low. I've seen somewhere it should be 400-500. So you may need to increase progesterone as well.

### Grottenolm — 2013-05-04T07:25:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-85424>

Hiyas! I have been taking a DHEA supplement since early March, and man I am feeling like a new person. I feel as if I am slowly coming back to the world of the living.

~ Energy is still a little low and I only have stamina to do leisurely walking and callanetics/barre style workouts.

~ Still have random depressive episodes every now and then, for no reason at all.

~ Currently following a high fat ketogenic style of eating as it makes my brain feel the best. Daily calories are 78% ~ 80% fat. Lost 4 pounds since March.

~ But my motivation and drive have really improved since starting DHEA, and work has been so much easier. Also been more inspired to create more art for pleasure recently.

Here are my latest labs since starting on DHEA. But my DHEA levels are still low at 50mg supplementation per day (Lower than my previous test!), and my free t3 is still low. I stopped taking my DHEA supplement 24 hours before getting tested. Does it mean that I am not absorbing the DHEA? Also my Testosterone levels appear to be the same as before. I thought the increased motivation, couple of pimples on the chin were indicative of increased testosterone levels. But I don't know if blood Test levels are a reliable indicator.

I want to take another saliva test in August.

> \=====================================================
> Always state when blood was drawn in relation to menstrual cycle.
> Now you have to:
> find that out,
> go to this chart
> [http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikiped...terone,\_LH\_and\_FSH\_during\_menstrual\_cycle.svg](http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Hormones_estradiol%2C_progesterone%2C_LH_and_FSH_during_menstrual_cycle.svg)
> go and see where your estradiol is in relation to average thick blue line for the cycle day that the blood was drawn.
>
> Do the same for progesterone, except that you really want large excess there.
> Progesterone: 56.5 nmol/L= 17.8 ng/dL
> Oestradiol: 392 pmol/L = 106.8 pg/mL
> using this calculator
> P/E=143
> well
> P/E ratio is really molar ratio, and you happen to have E2 and pg in molar units,
> that does not happens often
>
> so 56500/393=144
>
> Dr Lam wants P/E around 200
> Dr Kruse wants P/E around 500
>
>
>
> **19 Apr 2013, Reproductive Hormones
> **
> Progesterone: 56.5 nmol/L
> Oestradiol: 392 pmol/L
>
> Interpretation: Oestradiol

> Post Menopausal < 200

> timing of sample
> \>25 nmol/L - Ovulation confirmed

>
> DHEAs is very important steroid hormone.
> If you take all major steroid hormones in drop of blood,
> DHEAs participation is 95% by weight
>
> so it is big deal when you are missing on it
>
> and the little DHEAs that you already have made alaedy such a good impression on you.

I went thru your journal, fantastic, but not much details on labs.

Generally speaking.
When working on thyroid (and not only) it is good idea to not use supplemental thyroid hormones until thyroid friendly background is prepared first.

We usually do that by implementing dr K's advice, generously provided by him.
But, dr K reserves very important part of puzzle for dr/patient interaction.

That sounds nice until one realizes that it will take long time until more doctors catch up with this way of looking at health issues, because currently is is super hard to find doctor who would provide satisfactory help.

I was actually hoping for more activity on dedicated section of this forum:

Optimal Labs

Discuss labs and places to have your labs drawn

\-------------------

I am not a doctor, but like to learn about this important health topic.

..

### Grottenolm — 2013-05-09T03:19:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-85687>

I misread JanSz's Progesterone graph - actually my progesterone is really HIGH - the doctor said it is similar to someone that is supplementing it! So I am stopping the Maca supplement.

She is going to put me on a very low dose of dessicated Thyroid. Unfortunately we can't get T3 only here in New Zealand. However am going to be travelling to LA/Mexico in June, and she gave me a script that would enable me to pick up a 3 month supply while I am overseas. I will look into that....

So for now we are carrying on with the 50mg DHEA per day because it does feel good. Another thing - when I took the last blood test, I stopped taking the DHEA 24 hours prior. She said next time try 12 hours instead so there is till some left floating around the blood....

As for fasting insulin - I need to start exercising more in order to rectify this. Once my FT3 is up I can hopefully muster the energy to do some sprints  She is happy with my 75% - 80% diet to manage blood sugar levels and is very supportive of fasting when my body lets me.

### Grottenolm — 2013-05-20T08:53:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-86520>

Just an update - my dr started me on dessicated thyroid and I have been taking it for about 4 days now. It might be too early to tell but I think I'm feeling a lot better and my mood has been great over the weekend since commencing! She gave me 200x 16.25 mcg pills to play around with. I am charting Basal body temperature and other signs, and will go for blood testing again in a few weeks time.

At the moment I have started on 3x 16.25mcg pills per day and will be titrating it up bit by bit until my waking temperature becomes "normal" (pre ovulation) At the moment my waking temps are between 35~36 degrees, and we are aiming for 36.75.

I am not sure when to stop titrating up the dose of thyroid, perhaps keep an eye out for palpitations, anxiety, loose bowels, and general hyper-ness.

### caroline — 2013-05-20T10:24:43+0300

> I am not sure when to stop titrating up the dose of thyroid, perhaps keep an eye out for palpitations, anxiety, loose bowels, and general hyper-ness.

Consider little different approach.

1\. Work on Circadian Rhythm (Jack Kruse's whole Project) so you get proper cortisol/DHEA/melatonin profile and levels on saliva tests
2\. optimize steroid hormone panel on days 3-4
3\. take as much thyroid hormones as it takes to get to TSH~1.5 (1-2) (and leave the rest for latter
4\. do Spectracell analysis and follow up (plus iodine)
5\. do Fatty Acids Analysis and follow up

Now (about year latter, you may be ready for thyroid tweaking (and benefit from it).

....

### Grottenolm — 2013-10-30T03:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grottenolms-lab-sketchbook.1820/post-99398>

Hiya! So I've been on DHEA and Dessicated thyroid for a few months now, and this is my latest bloodwork. (100mg of DHEA and have titrated up to 4 grains of thyroid, per day)

Currently waiting on saliva test results, and will post those as well.

Still Following circadian protocal - meal timing, getting light on the retina first thing, no eating after sunset. Drink alcohol about once a week, couple of shots of the hard stuff mostly. Eating a ketogenic diet and feeling wonderful - the thyroid and DHEA have made me feel normal for the first time in years!

My doc says I need more folate/folic acid - so more liver? Or supplementation....

10 Oct 2013 - fasted AM test

**Thyroid Function Tests
**
Free T4: 18.0 pmol/L ( 10.0 - 20.0 )
TSH: < 0.01 mIU/L ( 0.30 - 4.00 ) LL
Free T3: 5.5 pmol/L ( 3.0 - 6.5 )

**Reproductive Hormones
**
Progesterone: 2.6 nmol/L
Oestradiol: 126 pmol/L

Interpretation: Oestradiol
Follicular 50 - 600
Mid-cycle
Luteal 160 - 770
Post Menopausal < 200

Interpretation: PROGESTERONE
Adult Female <6 nmol/L - Ovulation unlikely
6 - 25 nmol/L - Ovulation possible, depending on
timing of sample
\>25 nmol/L - Ovulation confirmed
Post Menopausal Females  nmol/L

**Serum B12 And Folate
**
B12: 520 pmol/L ( 170 - 600 )
Folate: 13.0 nmol/L ( > 7.0 )
Comment: Borderline range B12 110 - 170
Borderline range Folate 7.0 - 10.0

**Lipid Tests
**
Fasting status: Fasting
Cholesterol: 7.5 mmol/L ( < 5.0 ) H
Triglyceride: 0.6 mmol/L ( < 2.0 )
HDL Cholesterol: 2.45 mmol/L ( > 1.00 )
LDL cholesterol: 4.8 mmol/L ( < 3.4 ) H
Chol/HDL Ratio: 3.1 ( < 4.5 )

Comment: For most patients non-fasting lipids are acceptable for CVD risk assessment, as Chol/HDL ratio is not affected.
For established CVD risk (including diabetes) NZGG optimal levels are Cholesterol < 4.0, LDL < 2.0 and Chol/HDL ratio < 4.0.

**Renal Function Tests
**
Sodium: 140 mmol/L ( 135 - 145 )
Potassium: 4.8 mmol/L ( 3.5 - 5.2 )
Creatinine: 50 umol/L ( 45 - 90 )
eGFR: > 90 mL/min/1.73m2 ( > 90 )

Iron Studies
Ferritin: 40 ug/L ( 20 - 190 )

**Liver Function Tests
**
Total Bilirubin: 8 umol/L ( < 25 )
Alk. Phosphatase: 60 U/L ( 40 - 100 )
GGT: 14 U/L ( < 50 )
ALT: 45 U/L ( < 45 ) H
Total Protein: 69 g/L ( 66 - 84 )
Albumin: 41 g/L ( 38 - 52 )
Globulin: 28 g/L ( 18 - 38 )

**Dhea Sulphate
**
DHEAS: 6.0 umol/L ( 1.2 - 10.3 )

**Cortisol**

Serum Cortisol: 571 nmol/L

Comment: A serum cortisol higher than 400 nmol/L, in an unstressed person, excludes adrenal insufficiency in the vast majority of cases. If there is clinical concern, contact an endocrinologist.
Cortisol (Morning Peak) 200 - 700 nmol/L

**Testosterone**

Testosterone: 2.0 nmol/L ( 0.5 - 2.6 )

Diabetic Profile

HbA1c: 39 mmol/mol ( < 41 )
Comment: If used as a screening test, HbA1c result likely excludes diabetes. No need to repeat until next scheduled CVD risk assessment.
If diabetic and treated with insulin/sulphonylureas, the risk for hypoglycaemia is increased. Suggest repeat HbA1c in 6 - 12 months.

**Complete Blood Count
**
Haemoglobin: 145 g/L ( 115 - 155 )
RBC: 5.02 x10e12/L ( 3.60 - 5.60 )
HCT: 0.44 L/L ( 0.35 - 0.46 )
MCV: 88 fL ( 80 - 99 )
MCH: 28.9 pg ( 27.0 - 33.0 )
Platelets: 235 x10e9/L ( 150 - 400 )
WBC: 7.0 x10e9/L ( 4.0 - 11.0 )
Neutrophils: 3.64 x10e9/L ( 1.90 - 7.50 )
Lymphocytes: 2.21 x10e9/L ( 1.00 - 4.00 )
Monocytes: 0.89 x10e9/L ( 0.20 - 1.00 )
Eosinophils: 0.20 x10e9/L ( < 0.51 )
Basophils: 0.01 x10e9/L ( 0.00 - 0.20 )

# Thread: 10 lbs loss, finally getting there!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/>
**Subforum:** Success Stories
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Destiny — 2012-06-11T16:45:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30518>

I really had a very hard time going from 152 lbs to 142 lbs. It took me over 8 months with many frustrating moments (going up and down). During the whole process, I found out that my female hormones were off, my FBG was higher than it should and my TSH was higher as well. I got some BHRT from a not so competent doctor and still have to find somebody for my thyroid issue. Not a smooth ride, but a progress is being made. I still need to lose 10 more and I know it will probably take more than 8 months.

I just wanted to share this with you since I am sure that I am not alone. Just keep going and don't give up!

### Lyndra — 2012-06-11T17:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30524>

Congrats! Great to hear you have had success and are persevering.

### Mery — 2012-06-12T12:39:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30670>

Well done, i'll carry on see where it takes me, there is light at the end of the tunnel.

### Destiny — 2012-06-12T14:05:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-30673>

> **Mery;30774 wrote:** Well done, i'll carry on see where it takes me, there is light at the end of the tunnel.

I guess it is hard to understand that many people are able to lose fat fast while my weight loss has been so slow. My husband has been eating a way more carbs than me and he lost over 40 lbs. In addition, his initial weight loss was very quick and substantial, then it slowed down but continued at a steady pace every month. I know I cannot compare myself to a guy but still it is not fair .

### KiwiLauren — 2012-06-18T00:19:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31825>

> **Destiny;30777 wrote:** I guess it is hard to understand that many people are able to lose fat fast while my weight loss has been so slow. My husband has been eating a way more carbs than me and he lost over 40 lbs. In addition, his initial weight loss was very quick and substantial, then it slowed down but continued at a steady pace every month. I know I cannot compare myself to a guy but still it is not fair .

I'm in the same boat! Husband getting slimmer and fitter by the moment (and not doing CT except on the rare occasion... AND eating the occasional pizza to boot!) and I'm sticking to all the protocols, doing CT, perfectly obeying the light cycles, and the weight is so s...l...o...w.... to move. Hang in there

### Patty Cakes — 2012-06-18T05:22:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31882>

Destiny, if your thyroid is not being treated optimally you will have the hardest time loosing weight. It sounds like if you can find a doc who will treat you properly with BHRT and thyroid meds (if needed, I can't remember) your last 10 will be so much easier. I have been lucky to be slim most of my life but when thyroid disease hit I gained 25# and when trying to drop a few LBs I couldn't even lose more than 2# to save my life!

You're doing great to have lost 10#. Keep it up and you will lose the last 10! Good luck and know many of us are rooting for ya as we are in the same boat.

### Destiny — 2012-06-18T13:30:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31905>

> **Patty Cakes;31988 wrote:** Destiny, if your thyroid is not being treated optimally you will have the hardest time loosing weight. It sounds like if you can find a doc who will treat you properly with BHRT and thyroid meds (if needed, I can't remember) your last 10 will be so much easier. I have been lucky to be slim most of my life but when thyroid disease hit I gained 25# and when trying to drop a few LBs I couldn't even lose more than 2# to save my life!
>
>
>
> You're doing great to have lost 10#. Keep it up and you will lose the last 10! Good luck and know many of us are rooting for ya as we are in the same boat.

Thanks, Patty, for the encouragement. My weight used to be anywhere between 127-132. I really gained when I had my H Pylori problems and treatments. Maybe it was coincidence, not sure.

I made an appointment with an endocrinologist at the beginning of July. Hopefully, she will be open to help me.

### Tizawee — 2012-06-18T14:41:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-31907>

Way to hang in there, Destiny! I've just started BHRT myself (today = day 6) and will look at thyroid meds at my 3-month check-up. It took me 2 months to drop any weight at all. I just console myself with the fact that, even though the scale doesn't show something happening, I'm getting healthier by the day.

### Destiny — 2012-08-01T15:20:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-42238>

I am 5 lbs up to 147 lbs for no apparent reason. This is so frustrating ....

### Destiny — 2013-02-19T13:44:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-76447>

>
> Hi Destiny,
> Congratulations and agree with you that we must keep going and don't give up. If you give up, pick up where you left off and remember why you have started working out. Have a supportive team and stay close to people who motivates you.

Thank you, Algarnon. As you said, we must keep going ..... My 10 lbs loss is now 30 lbs loss .... My quest changed to regaining my health not just a weight loss.

For that reason, I am still not working out. I just do natural walks.

### Brice — 2013-03-13T17:01:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/10-lbs-loss-finally-getting-there.1823/post-80358>

You have succeeded in your these efforts, so congratulation for your this success.
You should carry on your these weight loss efforts for the permanent fitness.

# Thread: Leptin Rx Journal- Getting Serious
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 92
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### allison09 — 2012-06-12T03:11:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30632>

Hi everyone

Today was my first day of the Leptin Rx. I tried to start yesterday, but snacked after dinner-so today is Day 1! A bit of background:

I'm 27 years old, 5'6", 128 lbs. And I like long walks on the beach. Kidding...writing my "stats" makes me feel silly

I've been primal/paleo for a little more than a year, but before that I did the Specific Carbohydrate Diet for about a year to deal with some intense SIBO. Before THAT I was a vegetarian for years, and vegan for 2 years. Typical story, eh? Be healthy! Go veg! Ruin your health! Everyone always leaves out that last part though....

Moving on....like several others around here, I've struggled with an eating disorder for longer than I care to admit. The past 4 years of my life have been tough-I've been severely stressed out (moving, ending a relationship, grad school) and pretty depressed. My bulimia resurfaced-gradually at first- and it's recently been out of control, even though I've moved to a better place and things are generally better for me. I'm not sure about the relationship between emotions and eating and all of that-I'm not confusing correlation with causation, I'm just saying this all happened around the same time. I'd tried a bunch of things to end this cycle- The Diet Cure, strict primal/paleo, Leangains fasting, prozac and Topamax (not together), and therapy. Nothing has helped. Actually, the Topamax worked brilliantly for 2 weeks-then it was back to binging and purging. I don't like medication and it made me feel crappy, so I'm currently off all meds. So...I've found my way to the Leptin Rx. I am hopeful because I've been lurking around here for the past few days reading as much as I can, and it seems like many have found this to be helpful (Momagrok I'm lookin' at you!). I just want to be free of this, I want my brain back. I want my health back, and I want my life back.

I'm currently following the auto-immune Rx because of the SIBO (hmmm...carb binges don't help with that? SHOCKING!) and a suspected candida issue. Ugh. I LOVE eggs and this kind of makes me want to cry. I'll also miss the dairy, but nuts are HUGE triggers for me, so it's better that they are not in my life at all.

I have a million questions about this whole things too, so hopefully some of you seasoned pros would be so kind as to share your experiences and insights with me

1\. Is it ever a good idea to use whey protein in the BAB? I know Dr. K says certain people should/shouldn't-anyone know of any protein powders that make the cut? I'll even spring for the Bulletproof stuff if I know for sure it won't set me back.

2\. Today I had pork spare ribs, ground beef, and sound sauerkraut for my BAB- I finished by 7:45am and wasn't hungry again till like 6pm. Even then, it was a hint of hunger-did I over do it? Too much fat?

3\. I want to lose the few pounds I've recently gained due to all the bingeing/being lazy and not working out. Is it still recommended to eat a bunch of coconut oil? Even though I've been paleo for a while, the idea of eating oil in order to lose weight (as recommended by Dr. K) still doesn't make sense in my brain.

4\. Would it be a huge setback to workout before 5pm once or twice a week?

I have several other questions, but this is turning into a novel, so I'll just wait on them. Plus I've had a killer headache ALL day and I need to get off the computer

I'm excited to start this program-I hope it's as effective for me as it has been for so many others. Any advice is welcome and appreciated!

### Lanie — 2012-06-12T04:22:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30642>

Welcome, Allison! There are a lot of very knowledgable and supportive people hanging around here. I'm still learning myself. The basics aren't hard. Sounds like you've mastered the BAB! Try not to expect perfection from yourself. If you have a snack, just learn from it. Be kind to yourself. One of the best things I've gotten out of the LRx is I've been able to get out of that awful diet mindset that always resulted in me beating myself up. Many people here are binge-free now...even in the face of stress.

Good luck with your journey.

### PaulaRichards — 2012-06-12T18:02:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30708>

Welcome Allison! I'll say you're definitely in the right place. I have a long history of sugar addiction and binging. This is the best binge-free journey I've been on. I'm not binge-free yet but managed a 77 day stretch, which ended last Monday for a few hours. I consider the BAB and the leptin reset to be the only way to success for me. In the past a binge would have surely lasted at least a week, if not several! I see the mistakes I made leading up to my setback last week and I'm using that knowledge on my road to success!

I know it's hard to sometimes wrap your head around the amount of protein and fat in our BAB but it's the only way to break the binge cycle. I've learned my BAB's need to be between 65-75 grams of protein (with lots of fat...Yum!) so that I don't have cravings. If I eat this amount I rarely have cravings & if I do they are a fleating thought! It's amazing really!

Welcome, good luck & keep us posted!!!

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T05:15:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30822>

Thanks Lanie and Paulalynn! It's so nice to be in a place where people understand where I've been and where I want to be. Paulalynn- 77 days!!! That's so amazing!!! I'd love to be able to say that! One day...one day

So Day 2 went well for me. I had a BAB of canned BPA-free salmon, a little tuna to bring up the protein, a few pieces of bacon, and some lacto-fermented sauerkraut to help with digestion. I didn't feel as full as I did yesterday, I think because there was less fat today. It was nice. So, I was full pretty much all day, but I ate around 2 anyway for some reason. I had made plans to go running with a friend, and I knew I was going to an intense HIIT class tonight, so I thought I should eat something before the 7pm deadline. I at a lot of coconut manna. I know Dr. K promotes that stuff, and I freakin love it, but it's been a total trigger for me in the past. I make homemade manna, and put 1 Tbsp in mini muffin cups, then refrigerate it. They are so amazing! Buuuut I eat lots of them. So, after polishing off way to many this afternoon, I got rid of the rest of the manna and I'm not going to get anymore. At least not for a while. I think I need to be further in the Rx before I can have stuff like that around all the time. Sigh. Oh well.

So, I was thinking about the BAB today, and even after only 2 days I'm noticing a difference. I mean, it could be placebo effect because I'm all amped about this right now, but I don't know. I went food shopping today and didn't really feel any temptations. I didin't buy any foods I shouldn't have, and I didn't sample from any bulk bins! Hahah I'm being so serious about the no snacking rule! I think it's key though- the whole eating all day thing is NOT good for me, and it leads to crappy digestion and binges. Also, I like the BAB because I feel like I've been fighting my urge to eat right when I get up for so long! I'd always try to put off breakfast, fast for long periods of time, and it would just lead to food obsessions and binges. No more. Leangains isn't for everyone apparently.

I've also been thinking about the exercise component of this. I've decided that I'm not going to stop exercising. I need to move, I need to be active-it helps with my mental/emotional well being. I've been fighting some depression lately, and I think exercise really helps me with that. I do not want to fall down the rabbit hole of depression again- I'm finally on the way out! If it slows down my Leptin reset progress, then so be it I guess. More time to eat a BAB Seriously though, I'm going to make every effort to workout after 5pm. And if I can't fit it in after 5, I'll at least make sure that I get my BAB first, and wait a bit before exercising after. I don't think it's the worst concession to make-esp since I'm committed to the no snacking and the AI protocol. But ohhh man did I want some eggs and coffee today. It's cool, I got through it

I hope everyone had a good day

PS- I just made a batch of homemade turkey sausage patties for breakfast tomorrow and I didn't eat any of it!!! I'm actually not even tempted! Man I hope this lasts!

### Jack Kruse — 2012-06-13T05:20:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30824>

The key to the BAB......is it re yokes your gut hormones to the hypothalamus while increasing your GH levels........get some.

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T05:45:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30832>

Thanks for the comment Dr. K! Am I doing something wrong regarding the BAB, or are you just encouraging me to keep eating big in the morning? I hope it's the latter, because I'm excited for my sausage!

### Lanie — 2012-06-13T06:04:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30836>

> **Jack Kruse;30928 wrote:** The key to the BAB......is it re yokes your gut hormones to the hypothalamus while increasing your GH levels........get some.

I think he was just explaining the rationale for the BAB...why it's so important. It sounds like you're doing great! There's no reason this can't last.

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T14:59:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30876>

Ok, that's what I thought! Thanks Lanie

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T15:37:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30882>

Holy Moly!!! My BAB clocked in at 64 grams of protein today! I may never eat again! My sausages were obviously delicious, I ate three patties

### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-06-13T16:52:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30891>

Welcome Allison!

I've dealed with bulimia with a little more than 4 years. Going paleo helped me to lower the frequency of binges, but they were still there. I came here to help improve my sleep, and I discovered that I was in such a mess!! As soon as I started the reset, cravings went away and let me alone for the first time in my life!! I felt of the badwagon twice since Febuary, but now I'm 81 days binge-free. I still have a hard time to believe it!!  Since cravings and binging are under control, now I can work on other stuff to bring myself on optimal level. Not there yet, but I see improvement in myself (and in my thinking) every day.

I also had to cut dairies, egss () and nuts. You feel so much better pretty soon, trust me, its worth it.

To answer your questions, 1) I personnaly don't thing anyone should drink their meal. But the only way to know if its ok for you is to tale blood sugar level after you have any, and to see how it affects you (we want to limit BS swings).

2) You'll have to adjust over time. Dr K says that eventually we should have two meals a day. Try to back off a little on the protein and fat and see if you are hungry at night. And what you are doing one day doesn't mean you'll be doing the same a month later...Its constent adaptation.

3)yes! have plenty of it! you probably have a leaky gut, so its good for you! Don't count calories, the reset is not a diet, its a way to heal your body

4)That, I don't know. I've done anything else but walking is the last 3 months. I wouldn't do it, but once again, it depends of your goal (being optimal or not), and without testing, its hard to answer that for you.

Most important right now: BAB, plenty of fat, no panic if you gain weight, and get tested!!

And if you have any more questions, reach out!!

K

### allison09 — 2012-06-13T18:08:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30909>

Thanks for your advice AbetterME, I've spent a bit of time reading your journal over these past few days-I feel like we definitely have a lot in common!

I'll be honest and say I'm really afraid of gaining weight while doing the reset...but I feel like even with the BAB I'm eating less. And if there are no binges due to feeling satisfied-then I feel like there will be less caloric intake overall? I don't know. I'm just afraid to gain weight, especially because I was in a place where I felt lean and strong not too long ago. I just want to get back to that, I don't really care what the scale says.

I'll try to incorporate more CO into my diet, although now that I'm out of bacon fat it's all I use! I'll have to pick up some lard too.

I'm kind of overwhelmed with all the info regarding being "optimal". ### kathiebu@hotmail.com — 2012-06-13T22:41:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-30972>

Actually, that might be too much for you...The HIIT part I mean...Read this blog: [http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/](http://http://jackkruse.com/why-is-oprah-still-obese-leptin-part-3/), it might help you undertstand what I mean. If you have suffered from bulimia for several years, it's most likely that your body/hormones/gut is broken, and it might take a while to fix it all.... It depends what you want: losing a few pounds or becoming healthy? The good news it that you can have both, but health will have to come prior to losing weight. Or maybe not. Some are losing pretty quick here, but generally it takes longer for women (b/c of hormones). How is your sleep? Where do you carry fat? How's your mood, your energy?

Don't get overwhelmed. Master one thing at the time (BAB, no snacking, circadian rythm) and see how you feel. If you do all that and don't lose weight, then you may think about adding CT and/or supplements. CT would be the best thing to do to your body along with a keto paleo diet, its speeds everything up. Get there when you're ready, no stress about it. And keep reading the blog. That's a lot of information, but the more you understand, the easier is it to follow the protocol. And don't hesitate to ask questions if you have any concern!!

### allison09 — 2012-06-14T02:44:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31015>

Thanks AbetterME I appreciate the input. I'm familiar with that post on Oprah, but I just re-read it. I definitely know that my gut is broken, and that my hormones are probably a bit wonky (I went 8 months without a period, but got it last month), but unfortunately I don't have testing to know for sure. I think the loss of my period was due to being constantly stressed/anxious. I had a horrible living situation, was going to grad school, and a bit depressed. I moved back to the town I used to live, and within 5 days I got my period! Hahah I don't know how it's all related, but I got a kick out of it

To answer your questions, my sleep is crappy, and has been for a while. Especially these last few nights. I thought this protocol was supposed to improve sleep!! I carry fat in the love-handle and belly region, but I'm not overweight. I'm just not at the weight I want to be at. My mood and energy is not consistent, one day I'll be happy and energetic, and the next I'll be tired and mopey. There are a lot of variables, but right now I'm just sick of my digestive system having a mind of it's own. For example, (and I'm sorry in advance for the graphic nature of what is about to come next), but for the last two days I've had diarrhea, and I don't know why! I'm not eating anything I'm not supposed to-jeez, I'm pretty much eating perfectly. Meat, greens, and fat. That's it! I'm thinking maybe it's from all the junk that I ate last week when I was still binging? Is it because I've been really trying to drink more water? Is it from the enzymes I've been taking? The Kevita and sauerkraut? UGH! Troubleshooting my uncooperative body is so difficult.

I'm going to start writing my daily food intake on here for a record, and also so more experienced people can offer their insight. Some things are not as obvious to me as they are to others.

B-7:45a- 6oz homemade turkey sausage, 5.5 oz broiled tilapia, 2oz spinach sauteed in 1/2 TB CO, Kevita

L- 2p- zucchini "noodles" from a while zucchini (8oz), 2 large mushrooms (4 oz), onion (1.5oz), 4oz pork spare ribs, 1oz kalamata olives, all sauteed together in 1 TB CO, Kevita

Supplements after lunch- enzyme, multi, B's, 2000iu D

40 min gentle yoga before dinner, I'm sore from my HIIT class yesterday

D-7p- 6oz turkey sausage, 2 oz spinach, 4 olives, 1/4 cup sauerkraut, Kevita

I drank one Kevita over the course of the day.

Totals according to Fitday- Totals 1411cal 94g fat 26g carbs 129g protein

The D happened after lunch-any ideas?

I pretty much had to make myself eat dinner tonight. I was only at 1000 calories for the day, but I felt like I should eat something. Although, yesterday I ate at 2pm, went to the HIIT class, didn't eat again till this morning, and I was only the slightest bit hungry. I thought I'd be starving by breakfast, so I probably could have just skipped dinner tonight. I'm not sure what the best thing to do is in this case.

I'd like to try taking magnesium because I'd like to be able to sleep better. I did some digging today for Dr.K's recommendation, and I came up with either Mag malate, which he recommends for sleep, and Mag glycinate, which he recommends for people with a leaky gut. What do you all take? I just don't want it to lead to more diarrhea!

Alright, so I feel like I have a million more questions to ask about this whole thing, but right now I should probably go do some HW. Stupid summer class

### PaulaRichards — 2012-06-14T03:38:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31029>

Hey thanks Paula! I'm actually not new to CO, I've been using it for a long time, and I eat a ton of it, so I don't know if that's the problem or not. I'm hoping it's just some kind of detox or something...although I'm always hesitant to attribute things to "detox".

I thought about getting the Natural Calm stuff. I actually work in the Wellness Department at a natural foods co-op, so I'll have to scope out what we've got tomorrow at work. I'll probably just pick something up even if it's not the Threonate. I'd love to sleep like a rock!

I never feel like I get any kind of endorphin rush, even after heavy exercise. Maybe that's also why I have zero sex drive? Anyway, are there any supplements that can help with that? ### Souldanzer — 2012-06-14T14:47:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leptin-rx-journal-getting-serious.1832/post-31093>

B complex vitamins will make your urine yellow leaky gut or not. 5-htp should never be taken alone. Always combine with tyrosine in 1:10 ratio and use co-factors (b-complex, calcium, vitamin c, cysteine). It takes about 5 days to reach neurotransmitter levels at one dose. Do not take more than 300mg of 5-htp without testing neurotransmitter levels. You have a lot of signs of an NT problem.

Listen to this re: happy chemicals and b/p:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;cVtd85zZyjg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cVtd85zZyjg\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;cVtd85zZyjg)

[https://www.box.com/shared/ky3r0leemy](http://https://www.box.com/shared/ky3r0leemy)

[http://kalishresearch.com/](http://http://kalishresearch.com/)
